THE  YEAR'S  WORK  IN  FIGURES 

The  following  statistics  exhibit  the  work  of  the  Li- 
brary during  the  year  1918  as  compared  with  1917. 
A  substantial  gain  is  shown  in  every  department  ex- 
cept that  of  reference  work,  where  there  was  a  decrease 
quite  obviously  due  to  the  withdrawal  of  large  num- 
bers of  our  readers  from  their  ordinary  pursuits  to 
serve  the  nation,  the  practical  suspension  of  colleges 
and  universities,  and  the  many  preoccupations  of  peo- 
ple generally  in  war  activities  of  various  kinds. 

1918  1917 

No.  of  Vols.  in  Library. .      933,369  859,544 

Home  Circulation  6,620,653  vols.  6,042,344  vols. 

Reference  Use 731,280  vols.     834,363  vols. 

Total  Use 7,351,933  vols.  6,876,707  vols. 

No.  of  Borrowers 350,778 

No.  of  Branches 44

braries    6  6

The  Book  Bulletin 

of  the 

Chicago  Public  Library 

Volume  9  January,    1919  No.  1 

Annotations  in  Library  Book  Bulletins 

WHEN  a  Library  prints  a  list  of  the  books  it  has  purchased 
during  a  given  period  it  not  only  gives  the  public  a  convenient 
index  to  such  purchases  but  it  says,  in  effect:  "Here  are  the  books 
we  have  bought  with  your  money  during  the  past  month,  quarter 
or  year.  They  represent  our  best  judgment  in  selecting,  from  all 
the  books  offered  by  the  publishers,  those  that  we  —  having  in  mind 
our  limitations  and  your  tastes  and  desires  as  experience  has 
revealed  them  to  us  —  think  we  should  have  bought.  We  think  they 
are  good  books  within  their  several  fields,  and  that  they  will  add 
to  your  pleasure  or  profit,  or  both,  if  you  will  read  them,  and  to  the 
usefulness  of  the  Library  by  standing  on  its  shelves.  If  we  did  not 
think  so  they  would  not  be  here." 

Thus  the  Library  is  prepared  to  "stand  behind"  its  book  purchases, 
and  is,  or  should  be  able  to  explain  or  defend,  if  need  be,  the  pres- 
ence of  each  separate  book  in  its  lists.  And  since  the  inclusion  of 
each  separate  book  in  the  lists  is  the  result  of  a  separate,  conscious, 
mental  process  on  the  part  of  some  one  or  more  persons  charged 
with  the  business  of  book  selection,  and  represents  consideration 
and  deliberation,  and  a  decision  reached,  perhaps  in  a  few  minutes, 
but  sometimes  only  after  a  much  longer  period,  it  follows  that  this 
attitude  of  standing  behind  its  books  is  not  baseless  presumption 
but  is  founded  upon  a  legitimate  conviction  duly  and  conscientiously 
arrived  at. 

This  being  true,  it  seems  that  something  of  value  might  be  added 
to  the  lists  if  the  deliberations  of  the  Library  in  its  book  selecting 


581682 


YMKBU 

ji  U'  viiniJ5nr.il 

[<{  ?  ;_;  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


activities  could  be  shared  with  the  public.  By  thus  taking  the  latter 
into  its  confidence  the  Library  would  not  only  be  better  able  to  jus- 
tify its  expenditures  of  book  funds  but  might  also  afford  to  its 
readers  information  that  would  be  welcomed  as  a  means  of  stim- 
ulating interest  in,  or  providing  additional  information  about,  a 
given  book  or,  better,  about  all  of  the  books  in  the  lists.  It  has 
been  found  that  this  can  be  accomplished  very  satisfactorily  by 
means  of  annotations — the  addition  to  each  title  of  a  concise  little 
note  framed  to  convey  the  thoughts  indicated  above. 

Book  annotations  in  Library  Bulletins  must  be  brief,  pointed,  hon- 
est and  convincing.  Being  designed,  in  the  first  instance,  not  to 
induce  book  buying  but  book  reading — not  always  concomitant  ob- 
jects— they  need  not,  or  should  not,  savor  too  much  of  the  untemp- 
ered  enthusiasm  of  the  publisher's  or  publicity  man's  eulogy  aptly 
described  by  a  remarkable  term  of  trade  jargon  as  the  "blurb."  On 
the  other  hand  they  should  be  so  framed  as  to  answer,  as  com- 
pletely as  possible  in  a  very  small  space,  the  double  query  as  to 
"Why  we  bought  this  book  and  why  you  should  read  it."  From  the 
information  compiled  by  the  book  selecting  officials  of  a  Library 
before  the  book  was  bought,  it  should  be  possible  to  evolve  such  an 
answer  to  the  satisfaction  of  every  reasonable  inquirer.  Fre- 
quently such  information  will  be  best  conveyed  in  the  words  of 
others  who  have  said  just  what  we  'wished  to  say  and  have  said  it 
much  better.  In  that  event  quotations  should  be  used  without 
compunction  and  with  no  fear  of  a  latent  implication  of  evading 
responsibility.  We  are  still  standing  behind  the  book — once  re- 
moved. Sometimes  the  note  may  be  wholly  subjective,  reflecting 
the  mental  reactions  of  the  reader;  at  other  times  a  mere  descrip- 
tion will  suffice,  while  not  seldom  nothing  can  be  said  about  a  book 
that  is  not  already  apparent  from  its  title. 

At  all  events  it  has  seemed  wise  to  introduce  annotations  into 
the  Book  Bulletin  and,  beginning  with  this  issue,  the  practice  will 
be  continued  at  least  long  enough  to  afford  a  fair  trial  as  to  its 
value  from  the  point  of  view  both  of  the  Library  and  of  its  pa- 
trons. While  it  will  increase  somewhat  the  cost,  and  will  add  con- 
siderably to  the  labor  of  preparation,  these  burdens  will  be  justified 
and  cheerfully  borne,  and,  eventually,  will  disappear  if,  as  we  con- 
fidently believe,  the  annotations  will  enhance  the  usefulness  of  this 
publication  and  contribute  to  a  better  realization,  on  the  one  hand, 
of  the  policies  and  purposes,  of  the  Library  in  buying  some  books 
and  in  not  buying  other  books,  and,  on  the  other  hand,  of  the  re- 
sults of  our  labors  by  our  patrons  for  whom  they  are  performed.

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.     *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Philosophy 

Spaulding,  Edward  G.  The  new  rational- 
ism; the  development  of  a  constructive 
realism  upon  the  basis  of  modern  logic 
and  science,  and  through  the  criticism  of 
opposed  philosophical  systems.  1918 

L  13835 

The  most  ambitious  work  yet  put  forth  by  a  mem- 
ber of  the  aggressive  American  group  of  Neo-Real- 
ists.  It  undertakes  a  criticism  of  philosophic  method 


and  a  survey  of  all  important  philosophic  problems, 
the  refutation  of  outstanding  rivals  in  the  meta- 
physical field,  and  the  solid  enseatment  of  a  con- 
structive realism. — H.  B.  Alexander  in  The  Dial.

other   sacred  writings,   their    origin,   con- 
tents, and  significance.     1918.  M  6668 
(Biblical   introduction  series). 

A  discussion  of  the  origin,  subject  matter,  and 
value  of  the  books  called  "The  writings"  in  the 
Hebrew  Bible.  To  anyone  wishing  a  concise,  encylo- 
pedic  tabulation  of  critical  pros  and  eons,  and  con- 
clusions in  the  study  of  any  of  these  Old  Testament 
books,  this  volume  will  prove  reliable  and  highly 
serviceable. — The  Dial.

Economics 

Clark,  J.  Maurice  and  others,  ed.  Readings 
in  the  economics  of  war.  1918.  L  16613 
Falls  roughly  into  three  divisions — the  economic 
background  of  war  in  general,  the  economic  reor- 
ganization required  in  view  of  the  necessities  of4a 
world-war,  and  the  economic  questions  involved  in 
the  reorganization  of  the  industrial  system  at  the 
end  of  the  present  conflict. — Preface.

Howe,  John  B.  The  eve  of  election;  facts 
and  hints  for  voters  new  and  old.  1918. 

L 15936 

Includes  a  sketch  of  the  governmental  system — 
federal,  state  (New  York),  and  city;  a  brief  review 
of  the  Constitution,  and  a  historical  summary  of 
parties  in  the  United  States. 

MacNeill,  J.  G.  Swift.  The  constitutional 
and  parliamentary  history  of  Ireland  till 
the  union.  1917.  L  18022 

West,  Henry  L.  Federal  power:  its  growth 
and  necessity.  1918.  L  18030 

Historical  review  of  the  development  of  central- 
ized federal  government  in  the  United  States,  con- 
cluding with  the  statement  of  the  necessity  for 
devising  a  new  system  to  reconcile  democratic  gov- 
ernment with  the  increased  exercise  of  federal 
power.

1918.  L 14275 

Lectures  dealing  with  "The  kinship  of  patriotism 
and    religion,"    "The    moral    values    of    patriotism," 
Religion   and   war,"   and  "The   service  of  religion 
to  patriotism."

Foreign  Relations 

Tyau,  Minchien  T.  Z.  The  legal  obligations 
arising  out  of  treaty  relations  between 
China  and  other  states.  1917.  L  16320 

Analysis  of  the  foreign  relations  of  China  as  de- 
fined in  various  treaties.  The  author  is  lecturer  on 
international  law  at  Tsing  H'ua  College,  Peking. 

Wise,  Jennings  C.  Empire  and  armament; 
the  evolution  of  American  imperialism 
and  the  problem  of  national  defence.  1915 

L 16326 

Pictures  the  United  States  as  an  aggressive  nation, 
outlines  the  development  of  American  imperialism, 
and  advocates  armament  necessary  for  the  mainte- 
nance of  such  a  position. 

National  Characteristics 

Cooper,  Clayton  S.  Understanding  South 
America.  1918.  1 11139 

An  attempt  to  make  possible  a  better  understand- 
ing by  North  Americans  of  their  southern  neighbors 
through  a  sympathetic  interpretation  of  the  South 
American  people — their  standards,  traits,  ideals,  and 
ways  of  looking  at  things.  The  book  contains  much 
incidental  information  concerning  South  American 
commerce. 

Koebel,  William  H.     South  America.     1918. 

L 20112 

Survey  of  the  commefcial  and  industrial  field  in 
South  America  which,  although  written  primarily 
for  those  interested  in  British  commerce,  contains 
much  information  which  will  be  of  value  also  to 
business  men  in  the  United  States.  Two  chapters 
deal  with  the  needs  of  South  America  and  one  with 
the  currency,  weights,  and  measures  of  the  various 
countries. 

Rodrigues,  Gustave.  The  people  of  action, 
an  essay  on  American  idealism.  1918. 

I  4310 

Study  by  a  Frenchman  of  American  institutions 
and  ideals,  published  first  in  France  shortly  after 
America  entered  the  war.

Reconstruction 

Mawson,  Thomas  H.  An  imperial  obliga- 
tion: Industrial  villages  for  partially  dis- 
abled soldiers  and  sailors.  1917.  L 19705 

'Companion  volume  to  the  author's:  Afforestation 
and  the  partially  disabled.  K  24125. 

One  of  the  first  books  to  discuss  the  future  of 
the  disabled  soldier.  The  author,  an  Englishman, 
advocates  the  formation  of  industrial  villages  where 
the  men  may  live  with  their  families  and  find  occu- 
pation in  various  activities. 

The  book  does  not  deal  with  methods  of  rehabili- 
tation or  industrial  education. 

Language 

English  Language 

Chew,  Tobias  O.,  comp.  Practical  high 
school  speller.  1914.  J  4568 

The  desk  Standard  dictionary  of  the  Eng- 
lish language,  designed  to  give  the  or- 
thography, pronunciation,  meaning,  and 
etymology  of  about  80,000  words  and 
phrases.  1917.  *R2372 

Fernald,  James  C.  Expressive  English. 
1918.  J7172 

A  rhetoric  which  seeks  to  make  the  subject  inter- 
esting by  emphasizing  the  possibilities  of  the  English 
language — its  simplicity,  power,  and  the  resources  of 
its  vocabulary.  In  addition  to  the  usual  subjects 


it  includes  a  chapter  on  the  use  of  the  English  dic- 
tionary and  one  on  the  enlargement  of  one's  vocabu- 
lary.

Machinery 

James,  Walter  H.  and  Mackenzie,  M.  C.

(The  Wiley  technical  series  for  vocational  and 
industrial  schools). 

Intended  for  use  as  a  text-book.  Does  not  in- 
volve higher  mathematics. 

Nachman,  Henry  L.  Elements  of  machine 
design.  1918.  K  22544 

From  chapter  one,  which  is  a  brief  outline  on  the 
strength  of  materials,  are  developed  the  equations  for 
the  design  of  the  more  common  machine  elements. 
It  is  stated  that  empirical  formulae  and  rule  of 


thumb  methods  have  been  avoided  as  far  as  pos- 
sible. Courses  in  machine  drawing  and  elementary 
mechanics  are  pre-supposed. — New  Technical  books, 
New  York  Public  Library.

appliances.  1918.  K  24592 

Simple  text  and  many  excellent  illustrations

nical books,  New  York  Public  Library.

Poetry 

Davies,  Mary  Carolyn.  The  drums  in  our 
street;  a  book  of  war  poems.  1918. 

E  10868 

Poems  of  war  as  it  is  felt  by  those  who  stay  at 
home. 

"He  laughed  and  died;  .     • 

Ana  something  died  to  me 
In  greening  countryside, 

In  grass   and  bud  and   tree. 
Color  died  from  the  world, 
And  all  the  sky  was  dim; 
And  something  in  each  soul 
I  meet,  died,  too,  with  him." 

— For  a  young  soldier. 

Downey,  June  E.  The  imaginal  reaction  to 
poetry;  the  affective  and  the  aesthetic 
judgment.  1911.  L  13995, 2 

(University  of  Wyoming.  Department  of  Psy- 
chology). 

Foxcroft,  Frank,  ed.     War  verse.     1918. 

E  10436 

Anthology  of  poems  on  the  war  by  soldiers  and 
civilians,  containing  in  addition  to  many  of  the 
oft-printed  favorites  several  poems  not  before  pub- 
lished. 

Frankau,  Gilbert.  The  other  side,  and  other 
poems.  1918.  E  10942 

Poems  by  a  soldier  emphasizing  the  horror  of  war. 
"Lord,  if  I'd  half  your  brains,  I'd  write  a  book: 
None  of  your  sentimental  platitudes, 
But  something  real,  vital;  that  should  strip 
The  glamour  from  this  outrage  we  call  war, 
Shewing  it  naked,  hideous,  stupid,  vile — 
One  vast  abomination.     So  that  they 
Who,  coming  after,  till  the  ransomed  fields 
Where  our  lean  corpses  rotted  in  the  ooze, 
Reading  my  written  words,  should  understand 
This  stark  stupendous  horror,  visualize 
The  unutterable  foulness  of  it  all." 

— From  "The  other  side." 

Gibbons,  Herbert  A.,  comp.  Songs  from 
the  trenches,  the  soul  of  the  A.  E.  F. 
1918.  E  10437 

Collection  of  verses  written  by  American  soldiers 
in  camp,  on  transport,  or  in  the  trenches  in  France. 

Guiterman,  Arthur.    The  mirthful  lyre.   1918. 

E 10996 
Light  verse  reprinted  from  various  periodicals. 

McClure,  John  P.,  ed.  The  stag's  hornbook. 
1918.  E 10438 

A  pocket  companion  for  the  dry  days  that  are 
upon  us,  a  various  company  of  gay  songs  for  every 
temper  and  degree  of  man  whether  an  exile  from 
Bacchus  or  his  slave. — New  Republic. 


Phelps,  William  L.  The  advance  of  English 
poetry  in  the  twentieth  century.  1918. 

J6328 

Sassoon,  Siegfried.  The  old  huntsman,  and 
other  poems.  1918.  E  11565 

Perhaps  no  poet  who   has  written  of  the  war  has 
excelled  the  grim  beauty  of  some  of  the  poems  in 
this  volume. 
"Light  many  lamps  and  gather  round  his  bed. 

Lend  him  your  eyes,  warm  blood,  and  will  to  live. 

Speak  to  him;  rouse  him;  you  may  save  him  yet. 

He's  young;  he  hated  war;  how  should  he  die 

When  cruel  old  campaigners  win  safe  through? 

But  Death  replied:    'I  choose  him!    So  he  went, 

And  there  was  silence  in  the  summer  night; 

Silence  and  safety;  and  the  veils  of  sleep. 

Then,  far  away,  the  thudding  of  the  guns." 

Shorter,  Mrs.  Dora  S.  (Mrs.  Clement 
Shorter).  The  sad  years,  by  Dora  Siger- 
son.  1918.  E 11539 

Last  poems,  written  during  the  years  of  the  war, 
from   the  heart  of  an  Irish  poet,  sad  with  the  sor- 
rows of  her  native  and  adopted  lands. 
"Sick  I  am  and  sorrowful,  how  can  I  be  well  again 
Here  where  fog  and  darkness  are,  and  big  guns 

boom  all  day, 

Practising  for  evil  sport?     If  you  speak  humanity, 
Hatred  comes  into  each  face,  and  so  you  cease 
to  pray. 

If  I  saw  the  wild  geese  fly  over  the  dark  lakes  of 

Kerry 
Or  could  hear  the  secret  winds,   I  could  kneel 

and  pray. 
But  'tis  sick  I  am  and  grieving,  how  can  I  be  well 

again  • 

Here  where  fear  and  sorrow  are — my  heart  so 
far  away." 

— From  "Sick  I  am  and  sorrowful." 

Villon,  Francois.  Poems  of  Frangois 
Villon.  Biographical  and  critical  essay 
by  Robert  Louis  Stevenson.  1917.  E  8839 
John  Payne's  translation,  with  his  notes.  Except 
for  some  omissions,  indicated  when  they  occur,  the 
text  follows  the  revised  translation,  printed  for  the 
Villon  Society,  1892.  The  study  by  Stevenson 
appeared  in  his  "Familiar  studies  of  men  and  books" 
(1st  edition,  1881)  under  title,  "Frangois  Villon, 
student,  poet,  and  housebreaker,"  and  has  also  been 
published  separately.

Bergengren  Ralph  W.  The  comforts  of

Contents:  Thoughts  while  getting  settled. — Praise

Gay  and  whimsical  essays,  delightful  for  reading

Alaska 

Gordon,  George  B.     In  the  Alaskan  wilder- 
ness.    1917.  1 4700 

Record  of  a  twelve  hundred  mile  canoe  trip  into 
parts  of  Alaska  hitherto  unknown.  The  author  is 
Director  of  the  University  of  Pennsylvania  Museum 
and  his  book  contains  much  of  scientific  interest; 
but  it  will  -be  good  reading  for  lovers  of  adventure 
and  exploration  as  well.

Brooke,  Rupert.  Marsh,  Edward  H.  Rupert 
Brooke;  a  memoir.  1918.  C  20995 

Memoir  of  the  English  soldier  poet  by  one  of  his 
intimate  friends. 


Cohen,  Rose.    Out  of  the  shadow.     1918. 

C  21305 

Autobiography  of  a  Jewish  girl  who  emigrated 
from  Russia  to  the  United  States.  The  struggle  of 
herself  and  her  family  to  adapt  themselves  to  their 
new  environment  is  simply  and  graphically  told.

Schapiro,    J.    Salwyn.      Modern    and    con- 
temporary European  history.     1918. 

A  5941 

Excellent  book  for  the  general  reader  who  desires 
in  comparatively  small  compass  the  political,  social, 
and  economic  history  of  Europe  from  1815  to  the 
time  of  the  entrance  of  America  into  the  present 
war.

A  6840 

Thrilling  account  by  a  scout  in  this  famous  Scotch

Church,  James  Robb.  The  doctor's  part, 
what  happens  to  the  wounded  in  war. 
1918.  A  6849 

These  impressions  of  a  military  observer  are  the 
results  of  over  two  years  spent  by  Colonel  Church 
on  the  western  front  as  an  observer  with  our  allies 
and  later  on  the  staff  of  the  commanding  general  of 
the  American  expeditionary  force. — 'Foreword. 


Curtin,  D.  Thomas, 
sands.     1918. 


The  edge  of  the  quick- 
A6850 


Contents:  A  thousand  differences. — How  the  Ger- 
mans are  governed. — The  phonograph  man. — Smoke 
clouds  of  democracy. — The  Wilson  wedge. — The 
secret  of  German  resistance. — The  decisive  weapo.n. 
— The  invisible  army. — Our  prisoner  extraordinary. 
— Footlight  warfare. — A  dusty  volume  in  Berlin. — 
The  mothers  across  the  sea. — The  dug-outless  front. 
— The  frightfulness  moon. — Thou  shalt  kill. — The 
quicksands. 

Another  book  by  the  author  of  "The  land  of  the 
deepening  shadow"  dealing  with  the  German  gov- 
ernment and  people  as  seen  through  the  eyes  of  an 
American  journalist  who  had  access  to  the  Central 
Powers  during  the  earlier  years  of  the  war.  Much 
of  its  timeliness  has  been  destroyed  by  developments 
since  its  composition.

cradle  of  the  world.     1918.  A  6853 

Combination    of    facts,    impressions,    and    pictures 
of  the  war  in  the  East — particularly  in  Mesopotamia.

A  6857 

Contents:  A  retired  gentleman. — The  fumes  of  the

and  the  English  as  seen  through  the  eyes  of  Asia. 

Morgenthau,  Henry.    Ambassador  Morgen- 
thau's  story.     1918.  A  6855 

An  inside  picture  of  the  part  played  by  Germany 
in  Turkey  during  the  time  between  the  beginning  of 
the  war  and  the  entrance  of  the  United  States.  The 
importance  and  interest  of  its  disclosures  are  greatly 
enhanced  by  the  clear  and  incisive  manner  in  which 
they  are  told. 


Miihlon,  Wilhelm. 
1918. 


The  vandal  of  Europe. 
A  6854 


Entries  from  a  diary  kept  by  a  director  at  Krupps 
during  the  period  between  August  1  and  November 
1,  1914,  in  which  he  recorded  the  occurences  of  that 
.  eventful  time,  his  conversations  with  people  in 
authority,  and  his  lack  of  sympathy  with  the  part 
played  by  his  country.

A  clever  detective  story. 

London,  Jack.    The  red  one.    1918.     F  14789 

Contents:  The  red  one. — The  hussy. — Like  Argus 
of  the  ancient  times. — The  princess.

The  first  part  of  this  comprehensive  book  deals

tion of  our  entrance  to  the  European  war.

BOOK    BULLETIN 

of  the 

Chicago  Public  Library 


THE    'SPLENDID    MILE.'      MICHIGAN    BOULEVARD    SOUTH    FROM    THE 

PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


The  Book  Bulletin 

of  the 

Chicago  Public  Library 

Volume  9  February,  1919  No.   2 

Beautiful  Books 

The  following  list  was  prepared  by  Miss  Margaret  E.  Ely,  librarian  of  Lake 
View  High  School  Library  (a  branch  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library},  for  the  meet- 
ing of  the  National  Council  of  Teachers  of  English,  February  27th  and  28th.  It  is 
included  here  in  the  belief  that  the  list  will  be  of  permanent  value  and  interest  to 
lovers  of  beautiful  books  in  general. 

INTRODUCTION 

THIS  is  a  selected  list  of  editions  for  high  school  library  purchase.     The  titles 
included  are  chosen  from  the  standpoint    of    availability,    attractiveness    and 
usableness.    No  edition  listed  exceeds  five  dollars  in  price  and  most  of  the 
titles  are  far  less  expensive.     This  makes  it  possible  for  practically  any  high  school 
library  to  secure  many,  if  not  all,  of  the  editions  suggested.    No  edition  has  been 
included  unless  its  illustrations  distinguish  it  from  other  well  bound  and  perfectly 
usable  editions.    The  distinction  between  a  good  edition  and  an  illustrated  edition 
has  been  closely  observed.     Poor  binding  and    heavy    makeup    have    excluded    an 
otherwise  attractive  edition  from  inclusion  on  the  list. 

Since  every  title  in  the  list  has  been  personally  observed  by  the  compiler,  there 
is  some  omission  of  editions  which  might  well  be  acceptable.  The  exhaustiveness 
of  the  list  should,  however,  offset  this  omission  and,  for  the  most  part,  the  editions 
omitted  are  merely  further  editions  of  titles  already  included  on  the  list. 

All  publishers  and  prices  have  been  verified  to  date,  M.  E.  E.

Addison,  Joseph.  Days  with  Sir  Roger  de 
Coverley.  Macmillan,  $1.50. 

Attractively  illustrated  by  Hugh  Thomson,  fa- 
mous for  his  wonderful  delineations  of  old  English 
life.

by  Arthur  Rackham.

$2.50. 

Jessie  Willcox  Smith  has  illustrated  this  favorite,

of  the  story.

teresting to  children.  It  is  translated  by  Mrs. 
Lucas  and  illustrated  in  black  and  white  by  the 
Robinson  brothers.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Newton,  A.  Edward.  The  amenities  of 
book-collecting  and  kindred  affections. 
1918.  J  2844 

Contents:  Book-collecting  abroad. — Book-collect- 
ing at  home. — Old  catalogues  and  new  prices. — 
"Association"  books  and  first  editions. — "What 
might  have  been." — James  Boswell — his  book. — A 
light^blue  stocking. — A  ridiculous  philosopher. — A 
great  Victorian. — Temple  Bar  then  and  now. — A 
macaroni  parson. — Oscar  Wilde. — A  word  in  mem- 
ory. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


A  book  to  delight  the  'bibliophile.  The  author, 
whose  hobby  is  the  collecting  of  books  made  pre- 
cious by  their  associations,  discusses  rare  books, 
their  rising  values,  the  second-hand  book  stores 
where  they  may  be  found,  and  the  dealers  who  buy 
and  sell  them. 


Philosophy 

Columbia  University.  Dept.  of  Philosophy. 
Studies  in  the  history  of  ideas.  1918. 

L  12933 

Spiritualism 

Cameron,  Margaret  (Mrs.  H.  C.  Lewis). 
The  seven  purposes;  an  experience  in 
psychic  phenomena.  1918.  L  13488 

The  author  recounts  her  experiences  with  auto- 
matic writing.  The  seven  purposes  are  the  funda- 
mental truths  which  she  alleges  were  imparted  to 
her  from  the  spirit  world.

L  14105 

Material  originally  used  as  a  series  of  lessons  in

Reconstruction 

Benn,  Ernest  J.  P.  The  trade  of  tomorrow. 
t918.  L 20081 

A  contribution  to  the  subject  of  industrial  re- 
construction in  England.  The  author  describes  his 
book  as  a  "plea  for  the  organization  of  the  trades 
of  this  country  in  such  a  way  that  the  parties  in- 
terested— the  state,  workman,  and  capitalist — may 
develop  them  to  their  fullest  extent." 

Bradley,  Harriett.  The  enclosures  in  Eng- 
land; an  economic  reconstruction.  1918. 

L  6589,  80 

(Columbia  University  studies  in  history,  eco- 
nomics and  public  law). 

Coffin,  Henry  S.  In  a  day  of  social  re- 
building; lectures  on  the  ministry  of  the 
church.  1918.  M  8809 

(Lyman  Beecher  lectureship  on  preaching  in  Yale 
University). 

Addressed  primarily  to  theological  students,  these 
lectures  contain  a  wealth  of  advice  on  practcial 
problems  of  worship,  organization,  preaching,  pas- 
toral care,  and  evangelism.  Since  such  problems 
are  subordinated,  however,  to  the  vaster  problems 
of  the  church's  relationship  to  the  regeneration  of 
the  entire  social  order,  it  is  a  book  for  the  layman 
quite  as  much  as  for  the  clergyman. — Adapted  from 
the  Nation. 

Friedman,  Elisha  M.,  ed.  American  prob- 
lems of  reconstruction;  a  national  sym- 
posium on  the  economic  and  financial 
aspects.  1918.  L  16670 

Twenty-eight  articles  dealing  with  the  economic 
and  financial  problems  of  reconstruction,  by  special- 
ists in  their  various  fields.  The  first  comprehensive 
survey  from  the  American  point  of  view. 


I  Economics 

Laboring  Classes 

Grant,  Percy  S.  .Fair  play  for  the  workers; 
some  sides  of  their  maladjustments  and 
the  causes.  1918.  L  16938 

An  earnest  plea  by  a  prominent  New  York 
clergyman  for  understanding  on  the  part  of  society 
and  wealth  of  the  dissatisfaction  and  demands  of 
the  workers.  Analyzes  the  programs  of  various 
labor  organizations  including  the  I.  W.  W. 

Tead,  Ordway.  Instincts  in  industry,  a  study 
of  working-class  psychology.  1918. 

L 16971 

An  analysis  of  the  ten  basic  instincts  "whose 
functioning  throw  light  upon  human  behavior  as  it 
is  revealed  in  industry."  Like  Marot's  "Creative 
impulse  in  industry"  (L  19574),  is  indicative  of 
the  new  disposition  to  consider  an  understanding  of 
the  psychology  of  the  worker  an  essential  prelim- 
inary to  the  readjustment  of  industrial  conditions.

Political  Science 

Cannan,  Gilbert.    Freedom.    1917.        L  15734 

(The  new  commonwealth  books). 

Contents:  The  need  of  freedom. — Of  govern- 
ment.— Of  capital  and  labor. — Of  the  man  in  the 
street. — Of  machinery. — Of  love  of  country. — Of 
the  freedom  of  women. — Of  children. — Of  the  ideal. 

Ely,  Richard  T.    The  world  war  and  leader- 
ship in  a  democracy.     1918.  L  15907 
Pleads  for  a  democracy  in  which  the  emphasis  is 
to   be   placed   upon   the    leadership   of  the   expert. — 
Henry   Neumann  in   the  Survey. 

Fitzpatrick,  Edward  A.  Budget  making  in 
a  democracy,  a  new  view  of  the  budget. 
1918.  L 18059 

The  author  proposes  a  budget  system  of  govern- 
mental appropriation  and  expenditure  based  on  a 
critical  review  of  systems  hitherto  employed.

Criminology 

Figgis,  Darrell.     A  chronicle  of  jails.    1917. 

L 18971 

The  author  was  arrested  for  participation  in  the 
Sinn  Fein  rebellion  of  1916.  This  book  is  an  ac- 
count of  his  experiences  in  Irish  and  English  jails.

1918. 


L  18887 


(The  new  commonwealth  books). 

By  an  English  judge  and  deals  principally  with

reformatory  and  industrial  schools,  the  illegitimate 
child,  and  social  experiments  in  settlements  for 
children. 


Woods,  Arthur. 


Crime  prevention.     1918. 

L  18916 


Articles  by  an  ex-police  commissioner  of  New 
York,  making  clear  what  a  trained  police  force, 
inspired  by  good  leadership,  can  and  cannot  ac- 
complish toward  preventing  crime  and  hinting  at 
the  aid  to  be  derived  from  proper  legislation,  — 
The  Nation.

Chemistry 

Robertson,    T.     Brailsford.      The     physical 
chemistry   of   the    proteins.      1918. 

K  19875 

This  complete  re-writing  of  a  work  which  appeared 
in  German  about  six  years  ago  gives  the  latest 
knowledge  concerning  the  physico-chemical  be- 
haviour of  this  class  of  colloids,  and  in  some  meas- 


ure contribute  to  an  analysis  of  the  properties  and 
behaviour  of  colloids  in  general.  The  appendix 
deals  with  the  technique  of  electrical  measurements 
in  protein  systems. — New  Technical  books,  New 
York  Public  Library.

rence.

Military  Science 

Auld,  Samuel  J.  M.  Gas  and  flame  in  mod- 
ern warfare.  1918.  K  31068 

Freytag-Loringhoven,  Hugo  F.  P.  Freiherr 
v.  A  nation  trained  in  arms  or  a  militia? 
Lessons  in  war  from  the  past  and  the 
present.  1918.  K  31067 

The  author  is  a  member  of  the  German  Imperial 
Staff.  H'is  book,  a  companion  volume  to  his  "De- 
ductions from  the  world  war"  (A  6714),  deduces 
from  the  great  war  and  past  wars  that  a  militia 
organization  is  inadequate  to  safeguard  a  country 
from  its  enemies.  Will  serve  as  propaganda  for

"An   old   courtyard 
Hidden    away 
In    the    afternoon. 
Grey    walks, 
Mossy    stones, 

Copper    carp    swimming    lazily, 
And    beyond, 

A    faint    toneless    hissing   of   the   rain 
That    tears    at    my    heart." 

— Japan.

Of  wild  windy  gulls  whose  joy 
Is    never   to   alight! 

Above  us  is  the  June  sun, 
And    higher    still    the    Blue — 

And  God,  like  a  dream,  dear. 
The  whole   world  through!  ' 

Ridge,  Lola.    The  Ghetto,  and  other  poems. 
1918.  E  11443 

Her  longest  poem  "The  Ghetto"  is  beyond  doubt 
the  most  vivid  arid  sensitive  and  lovely  embodiment 
that  exists  in  American  literature  of  that  many- 
sided  transplantation  of  Jewish  city  dwellers  which 
vulgarity  dismisses  with  a  laugh  and  a  jeer. — Fran- 
cis Hackett  in  the  New  Republic.

Rackham,  Edward  Gosse  has  collected  the  poems

are  descriptive  of  child  life.

Russia 

Poole,  Ernest.  The  village;  Russian  im- 
pressions. 1918.  I  10538 
Even  more  than  in  his  former  book,  "The  dark 
people,"  Mr.  Poole  turns  from  the  contending 
politicians  of  •  the  cities  to  the  peasants  of  the  vil- 
lages as  the  hope  of  future  Russia.  His  new  book 
describes  life  in  a  typical  Russian  village  and  the 
character,  ideals,  and  needs  of  its  inhabitants.

Life  and  exploits  of  the  French  ace  of  aces  re-

Hudson,  William  H.  Far  away  and  long 
ago,  a  history  of  my  early  life.  1918. 

C  22529 

Memories  of  an  English  naturalist  whose  child- 
hood was  spent  on  the  Argentine  pampas  in  the 
days  of  the  tyrant  Rosas.  The  natives  of  the 
pampas,  the  plant  and  animal  life,  the  picturesque 
squalor  of  Buenos  Aires,  the  English  family  in  the 
midst  of  such  unusual  surroundings,  and  the  soul 
of  the  boy  himself  are  exquisitely  pictured.

his  life  and  work  for  the  empire.  1917.

service  of  Melville  E.  Stone  as  general 
manager  of  the  Associated  Press.  1918. 

*C  24394 


32 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


Ward,  Mrs.  Humphry.  A  writer's  recollec- 
tions. 1918.  2v.  C  24751 

Crowded  with  pictures  of  figures  prominent  in  the 
literary  and  political  life  of  England  during  the 
latter  nineteenth  century. 

Wcstinghouse,  George.  Leupp,  Francis  E. 
George  Westinghouse;  his  life  and 
achievements.  1918.  C  24807 

The  career  of  the  inventor  of  alternating  current 
machinery,  the  air-brake  in  use  on  railroads,  and 
many  of  the  things  which  make  life  safe  and  com- 
fortable. The  author  gives  an  intimate  picture  of 
the  man  as  well  as  a  history  of  his  inventions  and 
methods  of  work. — The  Booklist. 

William  II,  emperor  of  Germany.  Hill, 
David  Jayne.  Impressions  of  the  Kaiser. 
1918.  C  24884

Ancient  History 

Leffingwell,  Georgia  W.  Social  and  private 
life  at  Rome  in  the  time  of  Plautus  and

Teggart,  Frederick  J.    The  processes  of  his- 
tory.    1918.  A  2334 
The    author    attempts    by    an    application    of    the 
methods   of  science   to   the   study   of   history   to   ex- 
plain   "how   man   everywhere   became   what  he   is." 


Webster,   Hutton. 


Ancient   history.      1913. 
A  2415 

A  readable  high  school  text  book  in  which  much 
emphasis  is  placed  upon  social  and  cultural  devel- 
opment. 


European  War 

Abbey,  Edwin  A.  An  American  soldier. 
1918.  A  6872 

Letters  from  a  nephew  of  the  American  artist, 
an  engineer  who  enlisted  with  a  Canadian  regiment 
and  was  killed  at  Vimy  Ridge.  They  contain  little 
of  the  details  of  fighting  but  much  of  a  ibrave,  fine 
spirit. 

Bowe,  John.     Soldiers  of  the  Legion.    1918. 

A  6865 

Rather  miscellaneous  impressions  and  informa- 
tion concerning  the  Foreign  Legion  by  one  of  their 
number. 

Cheradame,  Andre.  The  essentials  of  an 
enduring  victory.  1918.  A  6874 

The  author  argues  that  the  Allies  must  impose 
such  terms  of  peace  that  in  twenty  years'  time  the 
relative  positions  of  France  and  Germany  stall  not 
be  such  that,  through  the  greater  losses  of  the 
former,  Germany  will  be  the  eventual  victor  of  the 
world  war. 

Cobb,  Irvin  S.  The  glory  of  the  coming; 
what  mine  eyes  have  seen  of  Americans 
in  action  in  this  year  of  grace  and  allied 
endeavor.  1918.  A  6871 

Well  written  articles  founded  upon  personal 
observation  and  dealing  chiefly  with  phases  of 
American  participation  in  the  war. 


Dexter,  Mary.    In  the  soldier's  service.    1918. 

A  6864 

Letters  of  an  American  girl  describing  her  ex- 
periences as  nurse  and  dietitian  with  the  Red  Cross 
in  England  and  Belgium,  as  ambulance  driver  in 
the  French  army,  and  as  a  student  of  psycho- 
analysis in  a  London  clinic. 

Dorr,  Mrs.  Rheta  Childe.  A*  soldier's  mother 
in  France.  1918.  A  6875 

Driggs,  Laurence  La  Tourette.  Heroes  of 
aviation.  1918.  A  6866 

Recounts  the  exploits  of  the  aces  of  the  Allies — 
French,  English,  American,  Italian,  and  Belgian. 

Duncalf,  Frederic.  A  brief  history  of  the 
war.  1918.  A  6847 

(University  of  Texas  bulletin). 

Gauss,  Christian  F.  Why  we  went  to  war. 
1918.  A  6861 

Popular  account  of  the  relations  existing  between 
the  United  States  and  Germany  between  the  out- 
break of  the  war  and  the  entrance  of  the  United 
States,  showing  the  inevitability  of  the  course  taken 
by  the  United  States  as  much  because  of  inherent 
national  characteristics  as  because  of  the  acts  of 
Germany. 

Gibbons,  Floyd  P.  "And  they  thought  we 
wouldm't  fight."  1918.  A  6870 

Adventures  of  an  American  war  correspondent 
who  managed  to  see  more  action  than  many  of  the 
actual  soldiers — beginning  his  war  career  by"  being 
torpedoed  on  the  Laconia  and  ending  it  by  receiv- 
ing three  wounds  in  fifteen  minutes. 

Kauffman,  Reginald  Wright.  Our  navy  at 
work;  the  Yankee  fleet  in  French  waters. 
1918.  A  6876 


Mackenzie,  Donald  A. 
1918. 


From  all  the  fronts. 
A  6842 


Miscellaneous  collection  of  war  stories,  biogra- 
phies, and  incidents  from  the  various  fronts. 

Morris,  Robert  T.  The  way  out  of  war; 
notes  on  the  biology  of  the  subject.  1918. 

L  14276 

(Tomorrow's    topics    series). 

Morse,  Edwin  W.  The  vanguard  of  Ameri- 
can volunteers  in  the  fighting  lines  and  in 
humanitarian  service  August,  1914 — April, 

1917.  1918.  A  6862 

Brief  sketches  of  the  leading  American  youths 
who  i  pined  the  Foreign  Legion  or  the  Lafayette 
Escadrille,  fought  with  British  regiments,  aided  with 
American  ambulances  in  France,  or  served  in  relief 
work  in  Belgium  or  Serbia  before  the  United  States 
entered  the  war. 

Palmer,     Frederick.       America    in     France. 

1918.  A  6863 
Survey  of  America's  part  in  the  war  written  by  a 

well-known  war  correspondent  who  was  a  member 
of  Pershing's  staff.  The  first  complete  account  in 
book  form  of  the  battles  in  which  American  troops 
participated.

A  6867 

An  account  of  the  work  of  Switzerland  in  the 
repatriation  of  interned  and  evacuated  civilian!, 
written  by  a  worker  in  the  field.

Whitaker,  Herman.  Hunting  the  German 
shark;  the  American  navy  in  the  under-

Wright,  Jack  M.  A  poet  of  the  air;  letters

Russia 

Bryant,  Louise.  Six  red  months  in  Russia, 
an  observer's  account  of  Russia  before 
and  during  the  proletarian  dictatorship. 
1918.  A  6051 

A  series  of  articles  recording  the  author's  obser- 
vations of  the  working  of  the  present  government 
of  Russia,  including  a  clear  explanation  of  the 
political  parties  and  a  description  of  the  leaders. 
She  believes  that  the  Bolshevik  government  is  the 
form  desired  by  and  most  appropriate  for  the  mass 
of  the  Russian  people. 

Buchanan,  Meriel.  The  city  of  trouble.    1918. 

A  6052 

The  daughter  of  the  former  British  Ambassador 
to  Petrograd  who  worked  as  a  nurse  in  a  Russian 
hospital  in  the  early  days  of  the  war  describes  the 
moments  of  highest  interest  in  her  three  years'  stay 
in  Russia,  and  gives  a  vivid  picture  of  Russian 
life.  She  regards  the  rise  of  the  Bolsheviki  to 
power  as  a  calamity. 

Petrunkevitch,  Alexander  I.  and  others.  The 
Russian  revolution.  1918.  A  6061 

Contents:  The  role  of  the  intellectuals  in  the 
liberating  movement  in  Russia,  by  A.  Petrunkevitch. 
— Forces  behind  the  Russian  revolution,  by  S.  N. 
Harper. — The  Russian  revolution,  by  F.  A.  Colder. 
— The  Jugo-Slav  movement,  by  R.  J.  Kerner. 

Rudnitsky,  Stephen.  Ukraine,  the  land  and 
its  people;  an  introduction  to  its  geogra- 
phy. 1918.  A  6050 

An  attempt  to  establish  the  claim  of  the  Ukraine 
to  independent  national  existence  by  proving  it  a 
geographic  and  ethnological  unit.  The  author  is  a 
geographer  of  the  University  of  Lemberg. 

Shel'king,  Eugenii  N.  Recollections  of  a 
Russian  diplomat.  1918.  A  5965 

Reminiscences  of  diplomatic  life  in  Russia  and 
Germany  by  the  former  Secretary  of  the  Russian 
Embassy  in  Berlin,  interesting  for  their  intimate 
pictures  of  William  II  of  Germany  and -Nicholas  II 
of  Russia. 

Thompson,  Donald  C.  From  Czar  to 
Kaiser,  the  betrayal  of  Russia.  1918. 

A  6053 

A  pictorial  record  of  the  Russian  revolution.

Spain 

Altamira  y  Crevea,  Rafael.  A  history  of 
Spain  founded  on  the  Historia  de  Espana 
y  de  la  civilizacion  espanola  of  Rafael 
Altamira,  by  Charles  E.  Chapman.  1918. 

A  40 14 

Introduction  by  Rafael  Altamira  from  whose 
"Historia  de  Espana  ..."  (published  in  4 
vols.,  Barcelona,  1906-11)  this  work  is  a  selection. 
Chapters  xxxn.  ("Charles  in  and  England") 
xxxix  ("The  growth  of  liberalism")  and  XL  ("The 
dawn  of  a  new  day")  are  entirely  by  Chapman. 

Doubtless  the  best  one  volume  history  of  Spain 
in  English.  Condensed  and  adapted  from  the  four 
volume  Spanish  history  by  Altamira,  it  covers  the 
subject  from  primitive  times  to  the  present,  empha- 
sizing throughout  the  growth  of  Spanish  civiliza- 
tion and  institutions  rather  than  political  events.

Wissler,  Clark.    North  American  Indians  of 
the  plains.    1912.  B  8511 

Handbook   series.      No.    1. 

The  first  of  a  series  of  handbooks  published  by 
the  American  Museum  of  Natural  History  which, 
although  designed  as  guides  to  museum  collections, 
have  little  in  common  with  the  conventional  guide- 
book. 

This  volume,  written  by  the  curator  of  anthro- 
pology, furnishes  a  summary  of  facts  and  interpre- 
tations making  up  the  anthropology  of  the  Indians 
of  the  plains.  Clearly  written,  well  illustrated,  a 
convenient  book  for  the  young  or  adult  student.

"The   'rimes'    have   the   real   Mother   Goose   spirit 
with  a  nice  inconsequential  humor  and  jingle.     The 
pictures  too  are  full  of  imagination  and  humor  and 
things  dear  to  children." — Booklist.

Adventures  which  befell  Running  Fox,  son  of  a

H  4305 

A   story   of   four    children   whose    father   went    to

in   caring   for   an   adopted    French   family    make    an 
amusing  narrative.     Suitable  for  children  from  6-10.

the  birds  said.

1918.  H3514 


Eight  stories  based  on  Irish  tales  with  quaint,

Delano,  Edith  Barnard.      Two  alike.     1918. 

H7809 

A  lively  account  of  two  very  real  giris  who  visit 
a  plantation  in  Maryland  where  they  have  all  sorts 
of  fun.  In  one  of  their  escapades  they  stumble 
upon  the  trail  of  an  old  mystery,  which  they  help 
to  solve.

A  search  for  a  lost  gold  mine  in  Central  America,

two  boys  who  followed  in  the  trail  of  the  mysterious 
"Cloven  Foot."

A  City's  Highway  By  Earl  H.  Reed 

From  the  original  etching  by  courtesy  of  the  artist 


A  City's  Highway 

TJPON  the  busy  river,  day  by  day, 

The  crowding  vessels  shoulder  as 
they  pass; 

The  toil  prosaic  goes  upon  its  way, 

Grouping  itself  in  struggling, 
straining  mass. 

But  when  the  burning  sunset  in  the  west 

Dims  into  tender  twilight's  mystic  gray, 

The  prose  of  life  to  poetry  is  blest — 

And  softly  glows  the  city's  great  highway. 

Carolyn  D.  Tyhr. 


The  Book  Bulletin 

of  the 

Chicago  Public  Library 

Volume  9  March,   1919  No.  3 

Wanted:  More  Books  for  Soldiers. 

/T~"SHE  supply  of  gift  books  for  the  soldiers  and  sailors  has  been 
suddenly  cut  off.  With  the  conclusion  of  the  armistice  the 
American  public  has  apparently  lost  interest  in  its  boys  in  the  serv- 
ice. Over  a  million  and  a  half  are  still  under  orders — in  France, 
in  demobilization  camps  in  this  country,  in  hospitals.  More  than 
ever  their  leisure,  with  the  great  preoccupation  of  war  removed,  re- 
quires wholesome  employment.  Classes,  schools,  study  courses  are 
being  conducted  under  government  auspices  in  France,  in  Belgium, 
in  Germany,  wherever  American  boys  are  stationed  to  finish  the 
task  they  went  over  for.  Individually  they  are  sending  their 
thoughts  back  home  ahead  of  their  own  longed  for  return,  seeking 
to  determine  the  kind  of  work  there  may  be  for  them  to  do  when  the 
khaki  and  blue  shall  have  been  finally  put  away,  and  trying  to  fit 
themselves  through  study  and  investigation  to  take  the  places 
awaiting  them  or  to  make  places  for  themselves.  The  American 
Library  Association  is  spending  every  cent  of  its  funds,  received 
through  the  United  War  Work  Campaign  last  fall,  for  technical,  vo- 
cational and  educational  books.  These  are  going  to  meet  the  urg- 
ent call,  most  of  them  overseas  and  a  good  portion  to  the  numer- 


38  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


cms  hospitals  in  Chicago,  and  the  money  to  buy  them  with  is  fast 
running  out. 

There  is  no  money  to  buy  recreational  books  with;  just  novels, 
stories,  picture  books,  jokes.  They  must  come  through  gifts. 
Millions  of  such  books  are  needed,  for  the  sick  and  wounded  in 
the  hospitals  here,  and  for  the  homesick  and  lonely  million  and  a 
half  still  overseas.  Why  have  the  American  people  stopped  their, 
gifts  of  books? 

Please  send  all  good  books  you  can  spare,  especially  fiction,  to 
Dispatch  Office,  American  Library  Association,  70  E.  Randolph 
street,  or  call  Central  5822  "Dispatch  Office"  to  have  books  sent  for. 

And  please  continue  sending  your  good  magazines  to  the  camps,  by 
putting  a  one  cent  stamp  on  the  cover  and  dropping  magazine  in  the 
nearest  mail  box. 


Some  Books  Concerning  a  League  of  Nations 

• 

This  list  comprises  merely  a  few  of  the  books  pertinent  to  the  phases  of 
the  question  most  vital  at  the  present  moment — the  arguments  for  and 
against  the  formation  of  an  international  league,  discussion  of  details  of  or- 
ganization, and  consideration  of  the  effect  of  such  a  league  upon  the  United 
States.  To  what  an  extent  would  its  sanction  involve  abrogation  of  the  Mon- 
roe Doctrine;  and  would  that  abrogation  be  a  desirable  or  regrettable  factor? 
There  has  been  no  attempt  to  make  it  even  approximately  exhaustive  of  the 
material  in  the  library  bearing  directly  or  indirectly  upon  the  formation  of  a 
league  of  nations. 

Those  desiring  periodical  and  pamphlet  material  will  find  it  of  easy  ac- 
cess in  the  Reference  and  Civics  Departments  of  the  Main  Library  building. 
An  extensive  bibliography  on  the  subject  prepared  by  the  Division  of  Bibliog- 
raphy of  the  Library  of  Congress  is  on  file  in  the  Civics  Department,  and  one 
is  included  in  "Selected  articles  on  a  league  of  nations"  compiled  by  E.  M. 
Phelps  and  listed  below. 

Bassett,  John   Spencer.     The  lost  fruits  of      Brailsford,   Henry   Noel.     A   league   of  na- 
Waterloo.     1918.  A  6702          tions.     1917.  L 17963 

Traces  the  disastrous  results  of  the  short-sighted  First    published    in    1914    and    issued    in    revised 

peace  terms  concluded  by  the  European  powers  in  form   three  years   later.      H.    G.   Wells   calls   it   the 

1815    and    advocates    the   formation    of   an    interna-  "classic     of     the     movement     in     England."       The 

tional    league    as    the    means    of    preventing    their  author,   an   Englishman   of   liberal    tendencies,    is   a 

repetition  in  the  present  crisis.  strong  advocate  of  a  league.

Dickinson,  G.  Lowes.    The  choice  before  us. 
1917.  L 14248 

The  purpose  of  this  book  is,  first,  to  expose  the 
ruin  that  lies  before  the  world  unless  this  war  shall 
indeed  end  war.  Next  to  discuss  some  of  the  prob- 
lems of  that  league  of  nations  the  creation  of 
which,  with  the  United  States  as  a  principal  mem- 
ber, is  the  only  way  to  a  lasting  peace. — Preface 
to  the  American  edition. 


.Goldsmith,    Robert, 
peace.     1917. 


A    league    to    enforce 
L 14252 


Discussion  of  the  program  of  the  organization  for 
a  league  to  enforce  peace,  of  which  ex-President 
Taft  is  the  head. 

Kallen,  Horace  Meyer.     The  league  of  na- 
tions.    1919.  L 18004 

The  most  recent  book  on  the  subject.  After  a 
prefatory  chapter  arguing  that  "the  league  of 
nations  must  be,"  the  author  suggests  and  defends 
a  protocol  for  a  league  of  nations  based  upon  the 
principles  set  forth  and  implied  in  the  various 
statements  of  President  Wilson.  Although  the 
details  of  the  organization  are  not  identical  with 
those  provided  for  in  the  constitution,  of  the  pro- 
posed league  now  made  public,  the  discussion  of 
the  various  points  is  most  pertinent. 


Latane,  John  Holladay. 
leadership.     1918. 


From  isolation  to 
L 16301 


A  survey  of  America's  foreign  policy,  the  trend 
of  which  points  to  the  inevitability  of  the  accept- 
ance by  the  United  States  of  a  leading  position  in 
world  affairs.

entitled  "The  Monroe  doctrine"  the  author  contends

that  in  joining  an  international  league  the  United

The  first  volume  gives'  a  history  of  the  movement

Volume  two  explains  and  analyzes  the  principles

L 15864 


Outline  and  discussion  by  the  Professor  of  Con- 
stitutional and  International  Law  at  the  University

of  nations  patterned  after  the  federal  government

1919.  L  18006 


The  articles  in  this  volume  chosen  from  addresses

Mr.  Wells  has  hidden  under  this  unfortunately

league  of  free  nations.

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.   *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

1918.  L 13491 


40 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


The  author  is  secretary  of  the  Society  for  Psy- 
chical Research  and  has  been  a  professor  of  philos- 
ophy at  Columbia.  His  book  is  a  scholarly  review 
of  psychical  beliefs  held  by  mankind  from  the  time 
of  the  earliest  savage  records  to  the  present  day. 
He  .assumes  that  the  fact  of  survival  after  death 
has  been  proved  and  limits  his  discussion  to  its 
nature. 

Martin,  Alfred  W.  Psychic  tendencies  of 
to-day,  an  exposition  and  critique  of  new 
thought,  Christian  science,  spiritualism, 
psychical  research  (Sir  Oliver  Lodge)  and 
modern  materialism  in  relation  to  im- 
mortality. 1918.  L 13540 
The  author  assumes  a  position  of  "entire  neu- 
trality toward  all  psychic  theories  and  institutions." 
He  refrains  from  the  tone  of  ridicule  which  char- 
acterizes "The  question"  by  Clodd  (L  1348S)  and 
examines  the  movements  discussed  in  a  fair  and 
sympathetic  manner,  rejecting  alike  howover  the 
conclusions  of  them  all.

Bosanquet,  Bernard, 
ethics.     1918. 


Some   suggestions   in 
L 14096 


Must  a  man  be  selfish  because  he  does  not  live 
for  others?  Can  morality  be  hostile  to  beauty  or 
vice  versa?  Is  it  true  that  retributive  punishment 
is  a  mere  survival  of  vindictiveness?  Have  we  any 
right  to  be  stupid?  On  difficulties  of  this  kind  I 
thought  some  suggestions  might  be  helpful. — Au- 
thor's preface. 

Calkins,  Mary  W.     The  good  man  and  the 
good;   an  introduction   to  ethics.     1918. 

L 14084 

Hocking,  William  E.    Human  nature  and  its 
remaking.     1918.  L 13908 

Reflections  of  a  scholarly  mind  upon  the  prob- 
lems of  making  human  nature  over.  Closely  rea- 
soned, illustrated  by  fine  feeling,  and  presented 
with  an  engaging  lucidity  and  literary  grace,  the 
book  will  be  found  especially  readable  in  these 
days  of  interest  in  reconstruction. — The  Survey.

fucius, the  prophet  of  politics.- — IV.  Buddha,  the

Mohammed,  the  prophet  of  Arabia.

The  disabled  soldier. 
L  19707 


A  survey  of  the  theory  and  practice  developed  in 
European  countries  and  the  United  States  in  re- 
educating and  placing  industrially  soldiers  disabled 
in  the  war.  The  author  is  director  of  the  Red 
Cross  Institute  for  Crippled  and  Disabled  Men  and 
his  book  will  doubtless  remain  the  most  authorita- 
tive work  to  be  published  in  the  United  States.

gust, 1917.  1918.  L  16651

reconstruction.  —  Arts  and  crafts  in  relation  to  re- 
construction, ed.  by  Dudley  Heath.

The  new  commonwealth  books. 

Proposes  as  a  stage  in  the  world  organization,  in-

Empire  and  the  United  States  lead  the  way  in  the

Journal  of  Ethics.

ship, a  review  of  American  foreign  policy. 
1918.  L 16301 

Of  special  timeliness  is  this  illuminating  survey 
of  American  foreign  policy,  the  trend  of  which 
points  to  the  inevitability  of  our  country's  relin- 
quishing its  earlier  policy  of  isolation  and  assuming 
a  leading  position  in  world  affairs.

Zimmern,  Alfred  E.     Nationality  and  gov- 
ernment, with  other  wartime  essays.    1918. 

L  15873 

The  author  believes  that  no  scheme  of  interna- 
tional government  will  succeed  unless  the  people  of 
the  various  nations  possess  the  finest  ideals  of  na- 
tionalism. These,  to  him,  are  the  ideals  underlying 
the  British  Commonwealth,  which  he  contrasts,  oti 
the  one  hand,  with  the  Prussian  ideal  and  on  the 
other  with  that  of  the  Bolsheviki. 


Contents:  Introduction.  —  War  instincts.  —  War 
and  the  struggle  for  life. — Selection  by  means  of 
war. — The  chosen  people. — How  war  is  being 
metamorphosed. — How  the  army  has  been  trans- 
formed.— Wherein  patriotism  is  rooted. — 'Different 
species  _of  patriotism. — Unjustifiable  chauvinism. — 
The  legitimate  individualism  of  nations. — Altruism. 
— How  war  may  be  abolished:  The  evolution  of  the 
idea  of  the  world  as  an  organism.  _  The  world  as 
an  organism.  The  transformation  in  human  judg- 
ment. War  and  religion.

Outlines  and  defends  clearly  and  forcefully  a

an  inquiry  into  the  basis  for  a  sound  in- 
ternationalism. 1918.  .  L 16652 

A  consideration  of  the  economic  phases  of  inter- 
national reorganization  based  on  the  peace  terms  ad- 
vocated in  the  Inter-Allied  Labor  War  Aims.  Fa- 
vors the  creation  of  international  commissions  simi- 
lar _  to  those  called  into  existence  among  the  Allied 
nations  by  the  necessity  of  the  war  whose  func- 
tions should  be  to  regulate  the  economic  problems 
which  have  caused  warfare  between  nations  in  the 
past.

K 25887 


Worst,  Edward  F. 
ing.     1918. 

The  descriptions  given  are  for  the  amateur 
weaver  who  will  find  them  more  easily  understood 
than  those  given  in  the  more  technical  books  on  the 
subject.  —  Introduction. 

Profusely  and  clearly  illustrated  not  only  with 
numerous  pattern  designs,  but  with  illustrations 
showing  the  mechanism  of  the  loom,  how  to  design 
and  construct  one,  and  how  to  use  it.  Contains  also 
a  chapter  on  dyes  and  dyeing.

literature.      1916. 


A  history  of  American 
J6405 


Couch,  Sir  A.  T.  Q.     Studies  in   literature. 
1918.  J6329 

Discourses,  scholarly  but  by  no  means  heavy,  on 
literary  subjects — the  commerce  of  thought;  pa- 
triotism in  English  literature;  Charles  Reade; 
Matthew  Arnold;  the  origin  of  ballads;  the  poetry 
of  Meredith,  'Hardy,  Swinburne,  Coleridge,  and  the 
seventeenth  century  poets;  the  Horatian  model  in 
English  verse;  and  the  distinction  between  the 
terms  "classical"  and  "romantic."

of  the   Saturday   club,   1855-1870.    "1918. 

B8008 

Chronicle   of   the   first   fifteen   years   of  a   Boston 
tlub    which    included    among    its    members    Agassiz, 
Emerson,   Lowell,   Motley,   and   a  host  of   other  dis- 
tinguished names. 


Poetry 

Cook,  Howard  Willard.  Our  poets  of  to- 
day. 1918.  J  6404 
Contents:  Amy  Lowell. — Sara  Teasdale. — Witter 
Bynner. — Robert  Frost. — Edwin  Arlington  Robin- 
son. —  Percy  Mackaye.  —  Edgar  Lee  Masters.— 
Vachel  Lindsay. — Henry  Van  Dyke. — Edwin  Mark- 
ham. — Cale  Young  Rice. — Conrad  Aiken. — Robert 
Service. 

My  purpose  in  this  book  is  simply  to  present  our 
American  poets  of  to-day,  to  tell  something  of  their 
lives,  their  writings,  what  they  have  done  and 
what  they  bid  fair  to  do. — Foreword. 

Dearmer,  Geoffrey.     Poems.     1918.     E  10872 

Carefully  wrought  poems  of  war  by  an  English 
soldier. 

"Five  million  men  are  dead.     How  can  the  worth 
Of  all  the  world  redeem  such  waste  as  this? 
And  yet  the  spring  is  clamorous  of  birth, 
And  whispering  in  winter's  chrysalis 
Glad  tidings  to  each  clod,  each  particle  of  earth. 
So  the  year's  Easter  triumphs.     Shall  we  then 
Mourn  for  the  dead  unduly,  and  forget 
The  resurrection  in  the  heart  of  men? 
Even  the  poppy  on  the  parapet 
Shall  blossom   as   before   when 
Summer  blows  again." 

— Resurrection. 

Eastman,  Max.     Colors  of  life.     1918. 

E  10889 

Poems,  songs,  and  sonnets,  and  three  prefaces  in 
which    the    author    deals    with    American    ideals    in 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


49 


poetry,    with   sonnets,    and    with    the    philosophy    of 
his  own  early  poems. 

"A  hut,  and  a  tree 
And  a  hill  for  me, 
And  a  piece  of  weedy  meadow. 

I'll  ask  no  thing, 

Of  God  or  King, 

But  to  clear  away  his  shadow." 

— Diogenes. 

Eaton,  Walter  Prichard.  Echoes  and  reali- 
ties. 1918.  E  10893 
The  echoes  here  are  from  the  facts  of  existence, 
as  Mr.  Eaton  has  observed  and  been  moved  by  the 
war,  the  spectacle  of  city  life,  and  the  grim  charac- 
teristics of  New  England  life  and  scenery.  The 
realities  lie  on  the  other  side  of  his  experience,  on 
the  inner  and  imaginative  side. — W.  S.  Braith- 
waite  in  The  Boston  Transcript. 

Foley,    James    W.     Friendly     rhymes,     old 
friends  in  joyous  verse.     1918.         E  10933 
Verses  which  make  good  "pieces  to  speak." 

Frothingham,    Robert,    cd.      Songs    of   men, 
an   anthology.      1918.  E  10410 

Collection  of  poems  which  "run  the  gamut  of 
man's  emotions  in  so  far  as  they  can  be  stimulated 
by  wanderlust,  camp  and  trail  pioneering,  seafaring, 
sport,  battle  afloat  and  ashore,  gold  seeking,  vaga- 
bonds, animals,  the  great  war,  the  joy  of  accom- 
plishment and  the  bitterness  of  failure." — Preface. 

Most  of  the  poets  represented  are  modern.  The 
collection  contains  the  complete  authorized  version 
of  the  much  quoted  "Fifteen  men  on  a  dead  man's 
chest." 


Hofmannsthal,    Hugo    v. 

1918. 


Lyrical    poems. 
El  1046 


Translated  from  the  German  by  Charles  W.  Stork 
who  has  also  written  a  critical  estimate  of  Hof- 
mannsthal as  a  lyric  poet  as  an  introduction  to  his 
volume. 

Meynell,  Alice  C.  A  father  of  women,  and 
other  poems.  1917.  E  11324 

A  collection  of  new  poems  from  the  most  sensi- 
tive and  reticent  of  living  poets — spiritual  poems 
appealing  to  our  conscience  as  well  as  to  our  emo- 
tional and  aesthetic  feelings. — Padraic  Colum  in 
The  Bookman. 

Warren,  Lansing  and  Donaldson,  Robert  A. 
En  repos  and  elsewhere  over  there,  verses 
written  in  France,  1917-1918.  1918. 

E  11703 

Verses  by  two  members  of  the  American  Field 
Service  which  testify  to  the  ability  of  the  American 
soldier  to  cling  bravely  to  his  sense  of  humor  even 
in  the  face  of  the  battle-fields  of  France. 

Wattles,  Willard  A.  Lanterns  in  Gethsem- 
ane;  a  series  of  Biblical  'and  mystical 
poems  in  regard  to  the  Christ  in  the 
present  crisis.  1918.  E  11704 

Wild,  Payson  S.  Idylls  of  the  Skillet  Fork. 
1918.  E  11706 

Verses  in  rural  dialect  descriptive  of  the  life 
about  the  country  of  the  Skillet  Fork — a  river  of 
southern  Illinois.  Many  of  them  are  familiar  to 
readers  of  B.  L.  T.'s  Line  o'  type.

Essays 

Cabell,  James  Branch.     Beyond  life;  dizain 
des  demiurges.     1919.  E  2153 

A  consideration  of  the  qualities  which  give  to 
good  books  a  "life  beyond  life"  which  is  chiefly 
concerned  with  the  part  played  in  life  and  litera- 
ture by  romance.  There  is  much  delightful  inci- 
dental comment  on  authors  and  books;  and  those 
interested  in  contemporary  letters  will  especially 
enjoy  the  essay  entitled  "Which  concerns  the  con- 
temporary." 

Kettle,    Thomas    M.      The    day's     burden; 
studies,    literary    &   political,    and   miscel- 
laneous essays.     1918.  E4125 
Essays   on    political   and    literary    subjects   by   the 
author  of  "The  ways  of  war" — an  Irish  writer  who 


died  a  lieutenant  in  the  Dublin  Fusiliers. 

The  political  studies  deal  for  the  most  part  with 
Ireland.  The  literary  essays  include  "a  new  theory 
of  Hamlet,  a  criticism  of  Anato_le  France's  work, 
and  a  chat,  full  or  irony  and  aspiration,  on  Francis 
Thompson's  'Health  and  happiness."  "  —  London 
Times.

Plowman,  Thomas  F.  In  the  days  of  Vic- 
toria; some  memories  of  men  and  things. 
1918.  C 23796 

The  record  of  a  man  who  has  been  closely  in 
touch  with  a  great  many  interests — books,  pictures, 
journalism,  education,  politics,  the  stage,  church- 
going,  volunteering— on  all  of  which  subjects  he 
has  something  to  say  that  is  worth  saying. — The 
North  American  Review. 


Record  of  ninety  active  years  with  some  ac- 
count of  the  author's  efforts  to  secure  commercial 
opportunities  for  women,  her  work  with  the  "As- 
sociated artists"  which  led  to  her  appointment  as 
director  of  the  Woman's  building  at  the  Columbian 
exposition,  and  her  memories  of  famous  people  of 
America  and  Europe. — The  Booklist.

row   Wilson,   an   interpretation.      191$. 

C  24906 

An  English  correspondent's  estimate  of  "the  char- 
acter and  motives  of  President  Wilson  as  revealed 
by  his  speeches,  writings  and  statesmanship." — 
Preface.

European  War 

Aldrich,  Mildred.  The  peak  of  the  load; 
the  waiting  months  on  the  hilltop  from 
the  entrance  of  the  Stars  and  stripes  to 
the  second  victory  on  the  Marne.  1918. 

A  6886 

The  third  volume  in  the  series  of  letters  that  be- 
gan with  "The  hilltop  on  the  Marne,"  A  5805,  and 
"On  the  edge  of  the  war  zone,"  A  6450.  Writ- 
ten during  the  period  between  the  entrance  of  the 
United  States  into  the  war  and  the  second  Battle 
of  the  Marne,  they  contain  an  interesting  account 
of  the  author's  experiences  during  the  days  of  the 
last  German  offensive. 

Beck,  James  M.  The  reckoning;  a  discus- 
sion of  the  moral  aspects  of  the  peace 
problem,  and  of  retributive  justice  as  an 
indispensable  element.  1918.  A  6887 

A  hastily  written  book  in  which  the  author  ad- 
vocates as  one  of  the  peace  terms  the  separation  of 
Prussia  from  the  other  German  states.  He  also 
discusses  in  a  half-hearted  manner  President  Wil- 
son's fourteen  points. 

Bryce,  James  Bryce,  viscount.  Essays  and 
addresses  in  war  time.  1918.  A  6884 

Papers  that  embody  alike  Lord  Bryce's  views  on 
the  merits  of  the  war  and  his  proposals  for  the 
avoidance  of  similar  calamaties  in  the  future. — 
London  Times. 

Collins,  Francis  A.  Naval  heroes  of  to-day. 
1918.  A  6882 

True    stories    of    brave    deeds    performed    in    the 
•  course  of  the  war. 

Huard,  Frances  Wilson.  With  those  who 
wait.  1918.  A  6885 

Stories  and  incidents  in  the  war-time  lives  of 
civilians  in  the  neighborhood  of  Soissons,  Chateau 
Thierry,  and  the  author's  Paris  home.  The  sacri- 
fices, devotion,  and  patience  of  "those  who  wait" — 
the  old  men,  women,  and  children — are  pictured 
with  all  their  pathos  an.d  humor. 

The  author,  who  has  previously  written  "My 
home  on  the  field  of  honor,"  A  6246,  and  "My 
home  on  the  field  of  mercy,"  A  6554,  is  an  Amer- 
ican who  has  lived  in  France  for  some  years. 

Hunt,  Edward  Eyre.  The  Red  Cross  on 
the  front  line  in  the  great  battle  of  1918. 
1918.  A  6806

Powers,  Harry  H.     The  great  peace.     1918. 

A  6881

ternationalism; the  latter  half  to  a  consideration  of 
the  demands  of  the  various  countries  involved  in 
the  peace  settlement.

Canada 

Porritt,  Edward.  Evolution  of  the  Do- 
minion of  Canada;  its  government  and  its 
politics.  1918.  B  9033 

Government    handbooks. 

Deals  informatively  with  the  area,  physical  fea- 
tures, and  population  of  Canada,  the  influences 
which  brought  about  confederation,  the  distribution 
of  powers  between  the  Dominion  and  Provisional 
Governments,  and  the  national  policy  of  the  Domi- 
nion.— The  Athencrum. 


United  States 

Abbott,  Willis  J.  Soldiers  of  the  sea;  the 
story  of  the  United  States  marine  corps. 
1918.  B  5500 

A  history  of  the  United  States  marine  corps  from 
its  earliest  days  through  its  achievements  in  the 
late  war. 

Becker,  Carl  L.  The  eve  of  the  revolution; 
a  chronicle  of  the  breach  with  England. 
1918.  B  5086, 11 

The  chronicles  of  America  series. 

One  of  a  new  series  which  when  complete  will 
consist  of  fifty  volumes,  each  by  a  different  author 
and  dealing  with  a  phase  or  period  of  American  his- 
tory, but  so  related  as  to  present  a  complete  sur- 
vey of  the  development  of  the  country  from  the  be- 
ginning to  the  present  day. 

The  separate  narratives  so  far  published  give 
every  promise  of  achieving  the  editor's  purpose  "to 
make  the  traditions  of  our  nation  more  real  and 
vivid  to  those  of  our  citizens  who  are  not  in  the 
habit  of  reading  history."  They  are  graphic,  full 
of  color  and  romance,  eminently  readable,  and  un- 
usually attractive  in  physical  make-up. 

Farrand,  Max.  The  development  of  the 
United  States  from  colonies  to  a  world 
power.  1918.  B  5657 

The  general  reader  will  with  difficulty  find  a  more 
interesting  and  readable  brief  history  of  the  United 
States  than  this.  Avoiding  dates  and  details,  the  au- 
thor succeeds  in  picturing  the  evolution  of  a  peo- 
ple in  a  fashion  which,  although  its  occasional 
iconoclasm  may  disturb  some  readers,  will  on  the 
whole  prove  stimulating  to  student  and  general 
reader  alike. 

Fossum,  Andrew.  The  Norse  discovery  of 
America.  1918.  B  5098

Sauer,  Carl  O.  and  others'.  Starved  Rock 
state  park  and  its  environs.  1918. 

*B  3053,  6 

This  bulletin  has  been  prepared  for  the  benefit 
of  those  who,  in  their  visits  to  Starved  Rock,  are 
interested  in  knowing  more  about  the  region  than 
can  be  gathered  in  the  short  time  commonly  spent 
in  the  park.- — Foreword. 

Part  I,  by  C.  O.  Sauer,  deals  with  the  geography 
of  the  region  and  includes  a  section  on  its  explora- 
tion, settlement,  and  development.  Part  II,  by 
G.  H.  Cady,  presents  an  outline  of  its  geology,  and 
Part  III,  by  H.  C.  Cowles,  its  botany. 

Schlesinger,  Arthur,  M.  The  colonial  mer- 
chants and  the  American  revolution,  1763- 
1776.  1918.  L  6589, 78 

Columbia  University  studies  in  history,  econom- 
ics and  public  law. 

A  significant  contribution  to  the  history  of  the 
Revolution  based  entirely  upon  source  material  and 
dealing  with  the  part  played  by  colonial  trade  in- 
terests in  causing  the  rupture  between  England  and 
the  colonies. 

Taylor,  Charles  H.,  ed.  History  of  the 
Board  of  Trade  of  the  city  of  Chicago. 
1917.  3  v.  *V3175 

v.   1-2.     Historical. 
3.     Biographical.

F 13242 

A  story  of  the  wheat  country  in  the  Northwest

1919. 


The  curious  quest. 
F 15764 


A  young  millionaire  gives  up  his  fortune  to  prove 
to  his  friend  that  he  can  earn  his  own  living  for 
a  year,  and  succeeds  beyond  his  expectations. 


Stabatini,  Rafael.     The  historical  nights'  en- 
tertainment.     1918.  F 17570 

Contents:  The  night  of  Holyrood:  the  murder  of 
David  Rizzio. — The  night  of  Kirk  o'  Field;  the 
murder  of  Darnley. — The  night  of  betrayal:  An- 
tonio Perez  and  Philip  II  of  Spain. — The  night  of 
charity:  the  case  of  the  Lady  Alice  Lisle. — The 
night  of  massacre:  the  story  of  the  St.  Bartholo- 
mew.— The  night  of  witchcraft:  Louis  XIV  and 
Madame  de  Montespan.  The  night  of_  gems:  the 
affaire  of  the  queen's  necklace. — -The  night  of  ter- 
ror :  the  drownings  at  Nantes  under  Carrier. — The 
night  of  nuptials:  Charles  the  Bold  and  Sapphira 
Danvelt. — The  night  of  stranglers:  Giovanna  of 
Naples  and  Andreas  of  Hungary. — The  night  of 
hate. — The  murder  of  the  Duke  of  Gandia. — The 
night  of  escape:  Casanova's  escape  from  the  Piombi. 
— -The  night  of  masquerade:  the  assassination  of 
Gustavus  III  of  Sweden.

Clarke,  Charles  R.  The  boys'  book  of  chem- 
istry; a  simple  explanation  of  up-to- 
date  chemistry,  together  with  many  easily 
made  experiments.  1918.  K  19808 

A  simple  explanation  of  chemistry  with  direc- 
tions telling  how  to  carry  on  experiments,  with 
simple  but  adequate  apparatus  and  laboratory. 

Cowles,  Julia  Darrow.    Indian  nature  myths. 

1918.  H  7731 

Explanation    stories   from   Indian    folklore — "How 

the   seasons    came   to   be,"   "Why   the   wind   wails," 

and  many  others  told  with  simple  and  poetic  charm. 

Fabre,  Jean  Henri  C.  Our  humble  helpers, 
familiar  talks  on  the  domestic  animals. 
1918.  K 24198 

A  nature  book  which  discusses  in  an  intimate  and 
lively  manner,  the  subject  of  domestic  animals. 
Written  for  older  boys  and  girls. 

Homerus.  The  adventures  of  Odysseus  and 
the  tale  of  Troy.  1918.  H  4288 

For  the  first  time  the  Odyssey  and  Iliad  have 
been  told  as  one  story,  in  simple,  spirited  language. 
The  pictures  in  color  and  black  and  white  by  Willy 
Pogany  are  charming;  The  black  and  white,  follow- 
ing the  old  Greek  line  drawings  in  style  but  having 
plenty  of  action  to  make  them  interesting. 

Lisle,  Clifton.  Boy  scout  entertainments. 
1918.  E 12536 

Contents:  Hints  for  boys  who  give  entertain- 
ments, by  C.  Lisle. — A  scout's  honor,  a  "hiking" 
play,  by  C.  Lisle. — Fair  play,  a  football  play,  by 
C.  Lisle. — On  the  Greenboro  nine,  a  baseball  play, 
by  W.  Macauley. — Jerry's  job,  a  play  showing  a 
boy  scout  in  business,  by  R.  M.  Robinson. — The 
Daniel  Boone  pageant,  by  C.  Lisle. — A  semaphore 
flag  drill,  by  N.  S.  Hallowell. — List  of  plays, 
sketches,  etc.,  for  boys. 

Entertainments  including  plays,  and  pageants  for 
both  in  and  out-of-doors,  with  general  suggestions 
as  to  their  production. 

Marten,  William  S.  Manual  training — play 
problems;  constructive  work  for  boys  and 
girls  based  on  the  play  interest.  1917. 

K  25948 

Practical,  tested,  _  construction  problems,  which 
appeal  to  the  child's  interest  and  play  instinct. 

Nicolay,  Helen.  The  book  of  American 
wars.  1918.  B  5908 

Pape,  Lee.  The  bravest  thing  in  the  world; 
a  comedy  of  childhood  in  one  act.  1918. 

E12718 

A  play  for  children  from  eight  to  nine.  There 
are  seven  characters,  time  required  is  thirty-five 
minutes. 

Tappan,  Eva  March.  Our  European  ances- 
tors; an  introduction  to  United  States  his- 
tory. 1918.  H6711 

The  Tappan-Kendall  histories. 

"A  brief  survey  of  European  history  to  the  be- 
ginning of  the  seventeenth  century  and  the  dis- 
covery of  the  new  world.  The  language  is  easy 
enough  for  the  average  sixth  grade. — The  Booklist.

Eighteen  authentic  stories  taken  from  struggles  in

in  a  manner  to  stimulate  love  of  country. 

Williams,  Archibald.  How  to  make  things. 
1913.  K 25955 

A  book  for  older  boys.  Gives  directions  for 
making  articles  from  a  cabinet  of  match-boxes  to  a 
horizontal  slide  valve  engine.  Not  requiring  a  high 
degree  of  mechanical  skill  or  expensive  tools.

.entertainments  for  children  from  five  to  ten  years

Stories 

Cheley,-  Frank  H.  Camping  with  Henry; 
stories  for  the  camp  fire.  1918.  H  7693 
Hunting  was  rather  a  sad  experience  for  Henry 
until  he  learned  to  distinguish  between  a  tame 
turkey,  worth  fifty  dollars,  and  the  wild  variety. 
But  the  way  he  learns  makes  an  interesting  story. 

Faulkner,  Georgene.     Red  cross  stories  for 
children.     1917.  g|  H  3828 

Stories  of  the  high  courage  and  noble  devotion 
of  those  who  have  served  under  the  Red  cross. 
The  "Ambulance  driver"  and  "Cap,  the  Red  cross 
dog,"  are  splendid  stories  of  heroism  and  faithful- 
ness, told  by  the  "Story-lady." 

Lippincott,  Joseph   Wharton.     Bun,  a  wild 
rabbit.     1918.  H  4834 

The  story  of  a  little  Georgia  cottpntail's  long 
journey  to  the  North  and  his  acquaintance  with 
men  and  animals,  gained  through  many  hairbreadth 
escapes. 

Macmillan,  Cyrus.     Canadian  wonder  tales. 

1918.  H  5203 

Stories  from  the  folklore  of  the  Canadian  Indian's. 

Especially    interesting   to    boys   and   girls   of   ten   or 

twelve. 

Perkins,  Mrs.  Lucy  F.     The  Spartan  twins. 
1918.  H  5661 

The  pranks  and  merry  times  of  two  little  children 
of  ancient  Sparta;  of  their  journey  to  Athens  and 
the  undreamed  of  things  which  befell  them  while 
there. 

Phillips,  Walter  S.   (El  Comancho).    Three 

boys  in  the  Indian  hills.     1918.          H  5690 

The  life  and  adventures  of  two  white  boys  and  a 


Blackfoot  Indian  boy,  who  lived  on  the  northern 
plains  in  the  days  of  buffaloes  and  wild  game.  There 
are  thrilling  encounters  with  wild  animals  and 
enemy  Indians,  showing  the  cunning  of  the  Black- 
foot  and  bravery  of  the  white  boys. 

In  the  Thomas  Hughes  Room 

Addison,  Joseph.    The  Sir  Roger  de  Cover- 
ley  papers,  from  the  Spectator,  with  ques- 
tions    and     suggestions     for     study,     by 
Homer  K.  Underwood.     1911. 
Electic   English  classics. 

Brooke,   L.    Leslie.     Johnny    Crow's    party; 
another  picture  book.     1909. 

At  Johnny  Crow's  party  the  "Bear  sang  a  senti- 
mental air,"  and  all  the  animals  had  the  j oiliest 
time.  Just  the  sort  of  picture  book  the  four  or  five 
year  old  will  love.

Illustrated  by  Arthur  Rackham.     1915. 

The  artist  has  recreated  with  marvellous  insight 
the  real  Dickens'  characters.  No  ghosts  were  ever 
quite  so  real  as  these  and  surely  there  will  be  only 
one  Scrooge  for  us  hereafter. 

Dodge,  Mrs.  Mary  Mapes.  .  Hans   Brinker; 
or,     The     silver     skates,     illustrated     by 
Maginel  Wright  Enright.     1918. 
A  finely  illustrated  edition. 

Hawthorne,   Nathaniel.     Tanglewood   tales. 
With  illustrations  by  Milo  Winter.     1913. 


Pittenger,  L.  A.,  ed.  A  collection  of  short- 
stories.  1918. 

A  book  essentially  for  the  high  school  student. 
Each  selection  is  followed  by  a  very  brief  biography 
of  the  author,  criticism  of  the  story,  and  bibliog- 
raphy of  general  references  and  of  collateral  read- 
ings. 

Skinner,  Ada  M.  and  Skinner,  E.  L.,  comp.

of  autumn,  Hallowe'en,  and  Thanksgiving. 
1918.

Useful  for  teachers  and  story-tellers.
