CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Rules  and  Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person 
residing  in  Cook  County  having  permanent 
employment  in  the  city,  may  enjoy  the  priv- 
ileges of  the  Library  by  having  a  resident 
of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear  in  the 
city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon 
application  and  compliance  with  the  rules 
and  regulations  of  the  Board  of  Directors 
a  card  good  for  three  years  will  be  issued, 
which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges 
upon  application. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and 
may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period. 
Books  may  be  renewed  but  once.  Books 
of  recent  purhase  labeled  Seven-day  books 
cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed.  The  card 
must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or 
Delivery  Stations  may  be  returned  at  the 
Main  Library,  but  books  cannot  be  applied 
for  at  the  Main  Library  and  delivered  to 
Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  hab- 
its may  use  the  various  Reading  and  Refer- 
ence Rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is 


required  to  use  the  books  on  the  open 
shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported 
to  the  Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books 
drawn  on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  pre- 
sented, and  for  all  fines  incurred.  If  a  card 
is  lost,  immediate  notice  must  be  given  to 
the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be  issued 
thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of 
non-fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each 
work  of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound 
in  one  or  more  volumes,  which  is  not  re- 
turned within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents 
is  made  for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return 
of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  de- 
livered to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until 
it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the 
call  number  is  crossed  off  the  order  slip 
whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited, 
and  all  injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable 
wear  and  all  losses  must  be  promptly  ad- 
justed to  the  satisfaction  of  the  Librarian. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  De- 
partments, third  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Cata- 
logue, fourth  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m., 
Sundays  and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
fourth  floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed 
on  Sunday. 


Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and 
newspapers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays 
and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington 
street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulle- 
tin are  issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and 
August,  and  are  distributed  free  of  charge 
in  all  departments  of  the  Main  Library  and 
in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be  mailed  to 
any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published 
in  January  of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten 
cents  per  copy.

BOOK  BULLETIN 


69 


to  love."  Senlin  is  a  poem  of  unusual  beauty  de- 
picting the  mystery  of  man's  life  in  a  series  of

Cronyn,  George  W.,  ed.  The  path  on  the 
rainbow,  an  anthology  of  songs  and  chants 
from  the  Indians  of  North  America.  1918. 

E 10811 

Translations  of  Indian  songs.  The  book  contains 
an  introduction  by  Mary  Austin  who  calls  it  "the 
first  authoritative  volume  of  aboriginal  American 
verse." 

Sassoon,  Siegfried.  Counter-attack  and 
other  poems.  1918.  E  11566 

Sassoon  depicts  war  with  fiercer  realism  and 
greater  power  than  any  other  poet  of  the  day. 
Four  years  of  active  service  in  the  British  army 
have  changed  a  poet  of  prettiness  and  a  lover  of 
nature  into  a  poet  of  savage  irony  and  a  hater  of 
war  and  those  who  create  it. 

"Does  it  matter?  .  .  .  Losing  your  sight? 
There's  such  splendid  work  for  the  blind; 
And  people  will  always  be  kind, 
As  you  sit  on  the  terrace   remembering 
And  turning  your  face  to  the  light. 

Do  they  matter?  .  .  .  Those  dreams  from  the  pit? 

You  can  drink  and  forget  and  be  glad, 

And  people  won't  say  that  you're  mad, 

For    they'll    know    that    you've    fought    for    your 

country, 
And  no  one  will  worry  a  bit." 

— From  "Does  it  matter?"

Sheldon. 


The    open-air    theatre. 
E  11910 


Comprehensive  survey  of  the  open-air  theater

Lewis,  B.  Roland.  The  technique  of  the 
one-act  play;  a  study  in  dramatic  con- 
struction. 1918.  E  12541 

The  author  is  Associate  Professor  of  English  at 
the  University  of  Utah  where  he  has  conducted 
courses  in  the  writing  of  the  one-act  play.  His 
book  considers  the  dramatist  and  his  audience, 
stage  directions  and  settings,  and  questions  of 
theme,  plot,  structure,  characterization,  and  dia- 
logue. A  list  of  contemporary  one-act  plays  is  ap- 
pended.

Baird,  S.  F.  Dall,  William  Healey.  Spen- 
cer Fullerton  Baird;  a  biography,  includ- 
ing selections  from  his  correspondence 
with  Audubon,  Agassiz,  Dana,  and  others. 
1915.  C  20726 

Belasco,  David.     Winter,  William.     The  life 

of  David  Belasco.     1918.     2  v.         C  20816 

At   once  a  valuable   history  of  certain   aspects   of 

the   American   stage  and  a   record   of  a   life  full   of 

adventurous  and  colorful  variety.  —  The    Dial. 

Hale,  Susan.  Letters  of  Susan  Hale,  ed.  by 
Caroline  P.  Atkinson.  1919.  C  22271 

Vivacious  letters  by  a  sister  of  Edward  Everett 
Hale,  written  from  her  New  England  home,  from 
Egypt,  the  Holy  Land  and  various  parts  of  Europe 
and  the  United  States.  Abounding  in  humor  and 
high  spirits,  they  record  an  eventful  life  and  re- 
flect a  delightful  personality. 

Irving,  Washington.  Letters  of  Washington 
Irving  to  Henry  Brevoort,  ed.  by  George 
S.  Hellman.  1918.  C  22574 

Companion  volume  -to  Letters  of  Henry  Brevoort 
to  Washington  Irving,  C20977.  Both  Collections 
are  of  interest  for  the  biographical  material  they 
contain  and  the  picture  they  afford  of  contempo- 
rary American  life. 

Lincoln,  A.  Robinson,  Luther  Emerson. 
Abraham  Lincoln  as  a  man  of  letters. 
1918.  C  23064 

Covers  a  phase  of  Lincoln's  career  that  has  not 
been  treated  at  length  before.  An  appendix  con- 
tains extracts  from  addresses,  state  papers,  and  let- 
ters and  samples  of  his  verse. 

An   autobiography.

character,  foibles,  and  problems.

China 

Wheeler,    W.     Reginald.       China    and     the 
world-war.     1919.  A  4952 

The  author  is  a  member  of  Hangchow  College. 
His  book  traces  the  recent  development  of  the  coun- 
try, indicates  its  problems,  and  advocates  on  the 
part  of  the  large  powers  in  their  dealings  with 
China  "a  course  of  action  based  on  international 
justice  and  the  development  of  a  democracy  in  the 
far  east  that  will  be  safe  for  the  world."

contains  useful  and  interesting  information  on 
countries  and  their  chief  features  not  always  to  _be 
found  in  similar  books.  Profusely  illustrated  with 
Burton  Holmes  photographs. 

Mulcts,  Lenore   E.     Sunshine  lands  of  Eu- 
rope.    1918.  H5141 
Contents:      Sancho,    the    Portuguese. — Fernando, 
the    Spaniard. — Jacques    and    Odette    of    France. — 
Amerigo,   the   Italian. 

Roosevelt,    T.     Hagedorn,    Hermann.      The 
boys'  life  of  Theodore  Roosevelt.     1918. 

C  23992 

Sympathetic  portrayal  of  the  life  and  character 
of  the  man  of  whom  Mr.  Hagedorn  writes:  "There 
has  never  been  anybody  like  him  in  the  past,  and, 
though  the  world  wait  a  long  while,  there  will 
never  be  anyone  like  him  in  the  future  .  .  .  For 
Theodore  Roosevelt  is  the  epitome  of  the  Great 
Hundred  Million.  .  .  .  He  is  the  typical  American." 
For  eighth  grade  and  high  school  age. 


Smith,   E.   Boyd. 
the  ark.     1918.

H6520 


Synon,  Mary.     My  country's  part. 


A  picture  book.  Continuation  of  the  author's: 
The  story  of  Noah's  ark.  H6511. 

A  picture  book  of  the  animals  which  were  in 
the  ark,  showing  what  they  did  after  the  flood. 
Children  will  appreciate  the  humor  of  the  pictures 
if  too  young  to  understand  the  text. 

1918. 
H6665 

A  very  clear  statement  of  America's  position  in 
the  war  and  the  events  leading  up  to  our  entrance 
into  it.  Written  in  language  simple  enough  for  the 
grades. 


In  the  Thomas  Hughes  Room 

Bartruse,  Grace.  The  children  in  Japan, 
pictured  by  Willy  Pogany,  rhymes  and 
story  by  Grace  Bartruse.  1915. 

Jack  and  Jill  make  a  visit  to  Japan,  and  are 
much  pleased  with  the  kindly  people  and  strange 
customs.  The  book  contains  exquisite  illustrations, 
in  soft  colors,  and  also  large  print,  making  it  very 
attractive  to  the  small  child. 

The  child's  first  book.     1918. 

"Formerly  issued  in  two  volumes  as  The  begin- 
ners' primer  and  The  beginner's  first  reader." 

A  primer  and  reader  in  one  volume  leading  the 
child  by  easy  stages  from  simple  word  combina- 
tions to  real  stories  and  poems.  Children  will  ac- 
cept it  as  a  story  book  because  of  the  attractive 
binding  and  illustrations. 


Mother  Goose.     Little   Mother   Goose,   pic- 
tured by  Willy  Pogany.     1915. 

A  handy  little  edition  of  the  Jessie  Willcox  Smith 
Mother  Goose.  An  attractive  size  for  small  chil- 
dren. 


Stories 

Branch,  Mrs.  Mary  L.  B.     Guld,  the  cavern 
king.     1917.  H  7487 

The  story  of  Guld  and  how  he  proved  himself 
worthy  to  become  ruler  of  the  Underground  people. 
Down  among  the  dark  caverns  he  served  with  his 
subjects  the  kobolds,  bringing  joy  and  light  into 
their  dreary  lives. 

An  unusual  and  fascinating  fairy  tale  for  chil- 
dren from  nine  to  twelve  years. 


Burgess,  Thornton  W. 


Happy  Jack.     1918. 
H7531

Green  Forest;  of  Shadow  the  Weasel  who  was  his 
enemy  and  of  Farmer  Brown's  boy  who  saved 
Happy  Jack  from  the  weasel.

Robin  Hood,  illustrated  by

The  illustrations  in  rich  colors  are  lovely.  The 
Greenwood  is  a  place  of  wonderous  light  and  shade 
and  Robin  Hood  and  his  Merry  Men  satisfy  all  our 
most  romantic  imaginings.

mination to  win.

Snell,  Roy  J.    Captain  Kituk.     1918.    H  6587 

Kituk  wanted  to  become  owner  of  a  schooner  so 
that  he  might  trade  with  his  people,  the  Eskimos, 
who  were  cheated  by  the  white  traders.  This  is  the 
story  of  how  he  succeeded  in  his  schemes.

per.     1918.

First  City  Hall  after  the  Fire,  s.  e.  cor.  La  Salle  and  Adams  sts. 

Known    as   the    "Rookery." 

The  Public  Library  ivas  first  opened,  Jan.  1st,  1873,  in  the  round  Water  Tank  behind  this 

building 


LIBRARIANSHIP  AS  A  VOCATION 


Young  women  with  the  requisite  educational  background  are  invited  to  con- 
sider the  opportunities  for  useful  and  attractive  occupation  open  to  Trained 
Librarians. 

The  Chicago  Public  Library  conducts  a  Training  Class  offering  free  instruc- 
tion in  the  fundamentals  of  library  methods  and  practice,  and  preparing  candi- 
dates for  employment  in  this  Library  under  the  rules  of  the  Chicago  Civil 
Service  Commission. 

The  next  class  will  open  Monday,  September  8th,  continuing  in  daily  session? 
for  about  eight  months. 

Entrance  Examination,  Saturday,  June  14th. 

(Completed  High  School  course  is  the  Minimum  Educational  Requirement 
for  Admission  to  Training  Class.) 

For  particulars  consult  Miss  Adah  F.  Whitcomb, 

Fourth  Floor,  Central  Library  Building. 

Director  of  Training  Class. 

HOURS :    Daily  from  9  a.  m.  to  1  p.  m. 


The  Book  Bulletin 


of  the 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume  9  MAY  No.  5 


Reconstruction 
Economic  and  Industrial 

The  following-  list  of  references  in  this  bulletin  covers  those 
phases  of  the  question  of  economic  reconstruction  which  are  receiv- 
ing the  most  attention  at  the  present  time.  It  was  found  that 
space  would  permit  listing  only  books,  some  documents,  and  a 
few  pamphlets. 

These  have  been  selected  from  a  full  bibliography  on  the  sub- 
ject, which  is  in  card  form  in  the  Civics  Department.  The  subjects 
in  the  latter  are  minutely  divided  in  order  to  bring  out  the  various 
phases  of  the  problem.  All  references  to  articles  in  the  periodicals 
in  the  library  are  given,  and  in  numerous  cases  references  to  im- 
portant sections  in  books  are  made.  This  card  bibliography  is  ac- 
cessible for  reference  use  in  the  Civics  Department. 

Pamphlets  (indicated  "Pam")  and  documents  (indicated 
"Doc"),  unless  otherwise  marked,  circulate  from  the  Civics  Depart- 
ment. Those  marked  *  do  not  circulate,  but  may  be  had  for  refer- 
ence use  in  the  Civics  Department.  *Ser  represents  serials  which  do 
not  circulate.

*Ser.

Program   adopted   at   the   convention   of   the    Fed-

tion.    1917-1918.    2v.                            L  16732 
1917:     Reconstruction  proposals,   p.    136-141. 
1918:     Resolutions    relating   to    reconstruction,    p. 
235-237. 

Benn,  E.  J.  P.  The  tr-ade  of  to-morrow. 
1918.  L 20091 

A  contribution  to  the  subject  of  industrial  recon- 
struction in  England.  The  author  describes  his 
book  as  a  "plea  for  the  organization  of  trades  in 
this  country  in  such  a  way  that  the  parties  inter- 
ested— the  state,  workman,  and  capitalist — may  de- 
velop them  to  their  fullest  extent." 

Trade  parliaments  and  their  work.    1918. 

*Pam. 

Discusses  the  formation  of  a  trade  parliament  or 
Whitley  council,  as  advocated  by  the  Whitley  re- 
port, which  will  act  as  a  link  between  the  govern- 
ment and  the  trade. 

Chapman,  S.  J.,  ed.  Labour  and  capital  af- 
ter the  war.  1918.  L  16733 
A  frank  discussion  of  the  after-the-war  relations 
of  employers  and  employed  by  various  authors, 
some  of  whom  are  members  of  the  British  Recon- 
struction Committee. 

Cole,  G.  D.  H.  Self-government  in  in- 
dustry. 1917.  L 16731 
The  author  advocates  democratic  organization  in 
industry  and  claims  that  the  national  guilds  offer 
the  most  promising  solution  for  the  complex  prob- 
lems involved  in  the  ownership  and  control  of  in- 
dustry. 

Friedman,  E.  M.  Labor  and  reconstruction 
in  Europe.  1919.  L 16735 

"Deals  with  the  effects  of  the  war  and  with  pro- 
grams of  industrial  reconstruction,  and  describes 
the  means  set  up  in  si-xteen  countries  to  deal  with 
reconstruction.  A  book  of  great  value  to  employ- 
ment managers,  directors  of  corporations,  and  stu- 
dents of  labor  problems." 

Great  Britain.  Commission  of  Enquiry  into

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.   *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Overlach,  Theodore  W.     Foreign   financial 
control  in  China.     1919.  L 17734 

Reviews  the  financial  activities  of  the  six  leading 
powers  in  China  during  the  past  twenty  years  and 
looks  forward  to  a  more  unselfish  international 
co-operation  in  the  future  that  shall  better  regard 
the  interests  of  China  itself.

Negro 

Brawley,  Benjamin  G.     Africa  and  the  war. 
1918.  L  16163 

The  author  believes  that  after  the  peace  settle- 
ment Africa  will  be  governed  by  an  international 
protectorate  and  that  under  this  government  un- 
precedented opportunity  will  be  given  to  the  Ameri- 
can negro  for  work  along  the  lines  of  colonization, 
missions,  education  and  practical  development  of 
the  country.  His  book  includes  a  plea  for  better 
schools  to  train  the  American  negro  for  this  work.

City  Planning 

Moody,  Walter  D.  What  of  the  city?  Amer- 
ica's greatest  issue — city  planning,  what  it 
is  and  how  to  go  about  it  to  achieve  suc- 
cess. 1919.  B  7844 

A  popular  exposition  of  the  methods  of  city 
planning  as  developed  by  the  Chicago  Plan  Com- 
mission of  which  the  author  is  managing  director. 
In  addition  to  its  interest  to  city  planners  else- 
where, it  will  make  a  special  appeal  to  Chicago  read- 
ers for  the  information  it  contains  of  the  cultural 
and'  commercial  life  of  the  city. 

The  details  of  the  plan  for  a  more  beautiful  and 
practical  Chicago  are  presented  at_  greater  length 
in  the  author's  previous  book,  Wacker's  manual 
of  the  plan  of  Chicago,  B  7865.  The  present  work 
lays  emphasis  upon  the  promotional  work  necessary 
to  the  plan's  realization.

Peace 

Dawson,  William  Harbutt.     Problems  of  the 
peace.     1917.  A  6894 

Writing  before  the  close  of  the  war  and  assuming 
an  allied  victory,  the  author  considers  from  an 
English  standpoint  the  settlements  to  be  made  at 
the  peace  conference,  advocating  a  moderate  and 
unselfish  policy  but  not  deeming  a  league  of  nations 
possible.

of  intrigue,  Vienna,  1815.  1919.  A  5949 
Compilation  of  extracts  from  diaries  and  memoirs 
of  several  of  the  men  and  women  who  took  part 
in  the  negotiations  and  gay  social  life  of  the  con- 
ference that  determined  the  peace  terms  after  the 
Napoleonic  wars. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


81 


Howe,  Frederic  C.    The  only  possible  peace. 
1919.  L 15863 

A  plea  for  a  peace  settlement  that  will  end  in- 
dustrial imperialism.  To  this  end  the  author 
advocates  a  league  of  nations  that  will  establish 
free  trade  throughout  the  world,  prevent  economic 
exploitation  of  backward  peoples,  and  maintain  inter- 
national control  of  vital  trade  routes.

The  University  of  Minnesota.  Studies  in  chemistry. 

Mason,  William  Pitt.  Examination  of  wa- 
ter, chemical  and  bacteriological.  1917. 

K  19851 

Small  authoritative  volume  on  water  examination 
which  assumes  a  knowledge  of  quantitative  analysis. 
Since  the  first  edition  in  1899  it  has  been  repeatedly 
revised  to  conform  to  the  recommendations  of  the 
American  public  health  committee  and  to  general 
scientific  advance. — The  Booklist. 

Schoch,  Eugene  P.  Chemistry  in  the  high 
school.  1914.  K 19877 

Scott,  Wilfred  W.  and  others,  ed.  Standard 
methods  of  chemical  analysis;  a  manual 
of  analytical  methods  and  general  refer- 
ence for  the  analytical  chemist  and  for 
the  advanced  student.  1918.  2d  ed.,  rev. 

K  19876 


Highly  valuable  compilation  of  methods  of  chemi- 
cal analysis,  contributed  by  research  chemists  con- 
nected with  American  companies,  and  by  other 
authorities. 

Part  I  furnishes  methods  for  the  quantitative 
determination  of  the  elements:  Part  II  covers  spe- 
cial subjects — acids;  water  analysis;  fixed  oils,  fats, 
and  waxes;  analysis  of  paints;  cements;  analysis  of 
alloys;  coal  analysis;  gas  analysis;  assaying:  Part 
III,  Tables  and  useful  data. — New  technical  and  in- 
dustrial books,  Pratt  Institute  Free  Library.

Primarily  a  text  for  advanced  students,  but

Trelease,    William.      Winter    botany.      1918. 

K  21473 

Pocket  size  guide,  companion  to  the  author's 
Plant  materials  of  decorative  gardening,  K  21472. 
The  key  of  the  present  volume  js  based  on  leaf-scar 
and  bud  differences;  and  there  is  less  differentiation 
in  species  and  varieties.  Deals  with  more  than  three 
hundred  genera  belonging  to  ninety-three  families, 
conifers  being  excluded. — The  Booklist.

"Quiet   miles  of  golden   sky, 

And  in  my  heart  a  sudden  flower. 
I   want    to   clap   my   hands   and    cry 

For   Beauty   in    her   secret   bower. 
Quiet   golden    miles    of   dawn — 

Smiling   all    the    East   along; 
And  in  my   heart   nigh   fully   blown, 

A     little    rose-bud    of    a    song."

come  across  a  delightful  description  of  the 
cathedral  of  St.  Front  in  Perigeux  of  the  Perigord; 
a  charming  portrait  of  a  child;  a  child's  questionings 
as  to  fairyland;  an  exquisite  sketch  of  the  signifi- 


Galsworthy,  John.     Another  sheaf. 


Scollard,    Clinton.      Footfarings. 


cance  of  sacramental  things;  bits  of  real  poetry;  and 
a  tale  that  holds  in  it  all  the  sense  of  home. — North

1919. 
E3072 

Contents:  The  road. — The  sacred  work. — Balance

87 


Asia  Minor 

Hawley,   Walter   A.      Asia    Minor.      1918. 

18434 

A  very  readable  travel  book  in  which  "an  attempt 
is  made,  after  briefly  sketching  its  physiography  and 
history,  to  give  a  general  idea  of  the  character  of 
the  scenery  of  this  part  of  Asia,  to  show  the 
present  primitive  condition  of  its  agricultural  and 
industrial  development,  and  to  indicate  some  of  the 
possibilities  of  its  future.  The  people  also  are 
described  as  they  appear  in  their  secular  affairs  and 
religious  observances;  and  the  classic  ruins  depicted 
as  they  appear  today." — Preface. 


United  States 

Shackelton,  Robert.    The  book  of  Philadel- 
phia.    1918.  14880 

A  well  illustrated  description  of  the  present-day 
city  and  its  life — its  churches,  public  buildings  and 
mansions,  and  the  historical  associations  by  which 
they  are  surrounded. 

Uniform  with  The  book  of  Boston,  I  4829,  and 
The  book  of  New  York,  I  4915,  by  the  same  author.

zons; notes  of  some  months  spent  among

Individual  Biography 

Bochkareva,  Maria  L.  Yashka,  my  life  as 
peasant,  officer  and  exile.  1919.  C  20910 

Few  more  thrilling  tales  have  come  out  of  the 
war  than  that  of  this  Russian  peasant  girl  who 
escaped  from  e-xile  in  Siberia  to  become  the  leader 
of  the  Battalion  of  Death. 

Clemenceau,  Georges  E.  Hyndman,  Henry 
M.  Clemenceau,  the  man  and  his  time. 
1919.  C  21287 

The  author,  a  well-known  English  socialist,  is  a 
staunch  friend  and  admirer  of  the  man  of  whom 
he  writes.  He  depicts  Clemenceau's  public  career 
against  the  background  of  French  political  life  dur- 
ing the  last  half  century,  devoting  two  especially 
vivid  chapters  to  the  part  he  played  in  the  Dreyfus 
affair. 

Fielding,  Henry.  Cross,  Wilbur  L.  The  his- 
tory of  Henry  Fielding.  1918.  3  v. 

C  21905 

An  authoritative  nad  exhaustive  biography  which 
substitutes  a  new  conception  of  Fielding's  character 
for  the  traditional  one  so  long  accepted. 

Foch,  Ferdinand.  Atteridge,  A.  Hilliard. 
Marshal  Ferdinand  Foch,  his  life  and  his 
theory  of  modern  war.  1919.  C  21946 

Subordinates  biographical  details  to  an  analysis  of 
military  theories  and  achievements,  presented,  how- 
ever in  a  readable,  non-technical  style. 

Gilchrist,  Mrs.  Anne.  The  letters  of  Anne 
Gilchrist  and  Walt  Whitman.  1918. 

C 22095 

Letters  now  published  for  the  first  time  which 
reveal  the  passionate  admiration  of  an  Englishwoman 
of  letters  for  the  American  poet. 

Keeling,  Frederic  H.  Keeling  letters  &  rec- 
ollections. 1919.  C 22724 

Interesting  letters  by  a  young  Englishman  who 
might  well  have  been  the  hero  of  a  Wells  novel. 
They  reflect  the  writer's  life  at  Cambridge  where 
he  was  an  active  Fabian,  his  work  for  social  better- 
ment, and  as  an  editor  of  the  New  Statesman. 
Perhaps  the  most  interesting  of  all  are  the  letters 
he  wrote  after  his  enlistment  in  the  war,  in  which 
he  was  killed  at  the  age  of  thirty. 

Lamartinc,  Alphonse  W.  Whitehouse,  H. 
Remsen.  The  life  of  Lamartine.  1918. 
2  v.  C  22848 

The  first  full  life  of  Lamartine  published  in  any 
language. — New  York  Times  Book  Review. 

Lincoln,  Abraham.  Grierson,  Francis.  Abra- 
ham Lincoln;  the  practical  mystic.  1918. 

C  23042 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Wright-Davis,  Mrs.    Mary,    comp.     The 

book  of  Lincoln.     1919.  C  23044 

Convenient  compilation  containing  several  of  Lin- 
coln's speeches  and  many  well-chosen  poems  writ- 
ten about  him. 

Pennypacker,    Samuel    W.     The    autobiog- 
raphy of  a  Pennsylvanian.     1918.     C  23724 

Tennant,  Christopher.  Lodge,  Sir  Oliver  J. 
Christopher;  a  study  in  human  person- 
ality. 1919.  C  24501 
Life  and  letters  of  Christopher  Tennant,  an  Eng- 
lish boy  who  died  in  the  war.  Unlike  the  author's 
Raymond,  the  book  contains  no  record  of  spirit 
communications. 

Wilson,    Woodrow.     Archer,   William.     The 
peace-president,  a  brief  appreciation.    1919. 

C  24910 

An  appreciation  of  President  Wilson  from  the 
English  point  of  view,  which  in  a  brief  sketch  con- 
siders him  as  a  man,  a  writer,  a  historian,  a  states- 
man, and  the  great  leader  at  the  peace  conference. 


HISTORY 

European  War 

Abbott,  Lyman.   The  twentieth  century  cru- 
sade.    1918.  A  6902 
Letters  in  which  the  war  is  regarded  as  a  spiritual 
crusade. 

Anderson,  Mrs.  Isabel.     Zigzagging.     1918. 

A  6908 

Story  of  the  author's  experience  as  a   Red  Cross 
canteen  worker   and   nurse. 

Barton,  Clarence  W.  War  finance,  as  viewed 
from  the  roof  of  the  world  in  Switzer- 
land. 1919.  A  6918 
Finance  is  but  one  of  many  miscellaneous  topics 
discussed  in  this  series  of  war-time  letters  written 
from  Switzerland,  France  and  England. 

Cowing,  Kemper  F.,  comp.     "Dear  folks  at 

home ";     the    glorious     story    of    the 

United  States  marines  in   France  as  told 
by  their  letters  from  the  battlefield.     1919. 

A  6891 

Glimpses  of  the  war  as  depicted  in  letters  written 
home  by   numerous  U.   S.   marines. 

Dawson,    Coningsby.      Living    bayonets;    a 

record  of  the  last  push.     1919.          A  6916 

Letters    that    take   up    the    thread    of   the    English 

soldier's   war    narrative    at    the   point   where    it   was 

broken  at  the  end  of  Carry  on,  A  6387. 

Dawson,  Eric.  P.     Pushing  water.     1918. 

A  6911 

The  life  of  those  who  serve  in  that  department  of 
the  British  navy  known  as  the  Mosquito  fleet. 

Doty,  Madeleine  Z.     Behind  the  battle  line. 
1918.  A  6901 

In  1918  the  writer  made  a  journey  around  the 
world  and  here  records  her  impressions  of  Japan, 
China,  revolutionary  Russia,  Sweden,  Norway, 
France  and  England.  She  is  interested  especially  in 
the  position  of  women  in  the  various  countries 
visited. 


Frost,  Wesley.  German  submarine  war- 
fare; a  study  of  its  methods  and  spirit, 
including  the  crime  of  the  "Lusitania,"  a 
record  of  observations  and  evidence.  1918. 

A  6900 

As  American  consul  at  Queenstown  during  the 
period  that  preceded  our  entrance  into  the  war,  the 
author  collected  evidence  of  no  slight  importance 
concerning  German  methods  of  waging  submarine 
warfare.  These  he  recounts  in  popular  style  in  the 
present  volume.  Probably  nowhere  will  be  found  a 
more  authoritative  account  of  the  torpedoing  of  the 
Lusitania. 


Lake,    Harold, 
kans.     1918.

A6905 


The  lie  of  the  3rd  of  August,  1914. 


1917. 
A  6914 


Loyson,  Paul  Hyacinthe. 
battle.     1917. 


The  gods  in  the 
A  6898 


Translation  of  the  author's:  Etes-vous  neutres 
devant  le  crime? 

Open  letters  in  which  a  well-known  French  play- 
wright passionately  upholds  the  cause  of  France. 

The  latter  half  of  the  book  is  devoted  to  a 
criticism  of  the  attitude  maintained  toward  the  war 
by  Remain  Rolland. 

McMaster,  John  B.  The  United  States  in 
the  world  war.  1918.  A  6910 

Presentation  by  an  eminent  historian  of  a  mass 
of  facts  and  details  concerning  the  events  that  lead 
to  the  entry  of  the  United  States  into  the  war. 

Manwaring,  G.  B.  If  we  return;  letters  of 
a  soldier  of  Kitchener's  army.  1918. 

A  6904 

Impressions  of  the  war  by  a  young  English  officer 
in  France. 

Merrill,  Wainwright.  A  college  man  in 
khaki.  1918.  A  6899 

Letters  of  a  nineteen-year-old  American  who 
joined  the  British  artillery  and  was  killed  at  Ypres. 
Most  of  them  were  written  while  the  young  author 
was  training  in  England  and  are  interesting  chiefly 
because  of  his  literary  enthusiasms,  especially  that 
for  Kipling  and  the  district  of  which  he  wrote. 

Newbolt,  Sir  Henry.     Submarine  and  anti- 
submarine.    1919.  A  6915 
Brief   sketch    of    the    evolution    of    the   submarine 
and    a    graphic    account    of    the    adventures    of    the 
British  undersea  craft   during  the  war. 

Price,  Julius  M.  Six  months  on  the  Italian 
front,  from  the  Stelvio  to  the  Adriatic, 
1915-1916.  1917.  A  6913 

Personal  narrative  of  the  war-artist  correspondent 
of  the  Illustrated  London  News,  illustrated  with  his 
sketches. 

Price,  M.  Philips.  War  &  revolution  in 
Asiatic  Russia.  1918.  A  6895 

As  correspondent  for  the  Manchester  Guardian  the 
author  witnessed  the  campaigns  of  1915  and  1916 
in  the  Caucasus  making  journeys  into  Persia, 
Armenia,  and  the  Black  Sea  coast,  and  was  engaged 
in  relief  work  among  the  refugees  in  the  Trans- 
Caucasus  and  neighboring  regions  of  Turkey.  His 
report  of  these  journeys  and  later  observations  of 
the  effect  of  the  Russian  revolution  on  the  Asiatic 
provinces  form  an  interesting  contribution  to  a  sub- 
ject concerning  which  little  has  been  written.

Phillipson,  Coleman.    Alsace-Lorraine;  past,

Russia 

Gourko,  Basil.     War  and  revolution  in  Rus- 
sia, 1914-1917.    1919.  A  6055 

First  hand  narrative  of  Russia  at  war  by  one  of 
the  most  successful  leaders  of  the  Russian  army. 
Operations  in  East  Prussia,  Poland,  and  Galicia 
where  the  author  rose  to  the  position  of  Commander- 
in-chief  of  the  Western  Armies  are  described  in  de- 
tail and  followed  by  an  account  of  his  experiences 
with  the  Russian  revolution  toward  which  his  atti- 
tude is  frankly  unsympathetic.

A  5049

A6056

Mexico 

Trowbridge,  Edward  D.     Mexico  to-day  and 
to-morrow.     1919.  B  4831 

Description  of  political,  social,  and  economic  con- 
ditions in  Mexico  during  the  period  that  has  fol- 
lowed the  rise  of  Diaz  to  power.  Chapters  deal 
with  Mexico  and  the  world  war  and  the  question 
of  Mexico  and  foreign  capital.  The  whole  is  intro- 
duced by  a  rapid  sketch  of  Mexican  history.

Hendrick,  Burton  J.  The  age  of  big  busi-

dustry. 1919.  B  5086,  39 
The  chronicles  of  America  series. 
Tracing  the  development  of  the  Standard  Oil 
Company,  the  steel  industry,  the  invention  and  spread 
of  the  telephone,  the  automobile,  and  agricultural 
machinery,  and  the  growth  of  public  utilities,  the 
author  pictures  the  transformation  of  the  United 
States  during  the  past  fifty  years  from  "a  nation  of 
farmers  and  small-scale  manufacturer's  to  a  highly 
organized  industrial  state."

Ogg,  Frederic  Austin.  The  Old  Northwest; 
a  chronicle  of  the  Ohio  Valley  and  be- 
yond. 1919.  B  5086, 19 

The  chronicles  of  America  series.

Northwest."  The  newer  book  is  more  readable  and 
concise  and  reflects  more  vividly  the  life  and  spirit 
of  the  times.

F.    Britten. 


According    to    orders. 
F  10739 


Short  stories  presenting  the  German  mind  under 
the  stress  of  war.  The  scenes  are  laid  behind  the 
German  lines. 

Contents:  Zu  Befehl. — In  the  Hindenburg  line. — 
The  terror  in  the  sky. — Panzerkraftwagen. — The 
spy. — Nach  Verdun. — The  conquerors. — The  sea 
devil. — The  iron  cross. — "And  the  earth  opened  her 
month." — Peace. 

Blasco  Ibafiez,  Vicente.  The  shadow  of  the 
cathedral.  1919.  F 11246 

Translation  of  the  author's:  La  catedral. 

A  novel  written  fifteen  years  ago  and  a  discourse 
on  the  misrule  of  the  church  in  Spain.  The  author 
sets  forth  his  opinions  on  Christianity,  evolution  and 
socialism. 

Brand,  Max.     The  untamed.     1919.     F 11283 
An   exciting  western  story. 

Clouston,  J.  Storer.  The  man  from  the 
clouds.  1919.  F 12044 

Spy  story  in  which  two  men  in  an  observation 
balloon  drop  onto  the  Island  of  Ransay  in  the 
North  Sea  when  a  wild  naval  conspiracy  is  in 
progress. 

Connor,  Ralph,  pseud,  of  Charles  W.  Gordon. 
The  sky  pilot  in  No  man's  land.  1919. 

F 13235 

A  story  of  the  Canadians  who  held  the  Ypres 
salient  in  those  terrible  months  in  1915  and  of 
the  Sky  Pilot  as  a  chaplain  in  the  trenches. 

Marmol,  Jose.  Amalia:  a  romance  of  the 
Argentine.  1919.  F  15094 


A  tale  of  Argentine  under  the  dictator  Rosas. 
An  interesting  picture  of  life  in  South  America  dur- 
ing this  period. 

Maxwell,  William   B.     The  mirror  and  the 
lamp.     1918.  F 15190 

A  long  slow-going  English  novel,  but  good  in 
character  study.  The  clergyman  who  loses  his 
faith,  but  is  restored  to  the  church,  is  handled 
with  searching  and  sympathetic  analysis. 

Merrick,    Leonard.      While    Paris    laughed; 

being    pranks    and    passions    of   the    poet 

Tricotrin.      1918.  F  15254 

Short  and  amusing  sketches   of  life  in  the  Latin 

quarter. 

Ostrander,    Isabel.      The    twenty-six    clues. 
1919.  F  15859 

An  unusually  good  mystery  story. 

Parker,  Sir   Gilbert.     Wild   youth,   and  an- 
other.    1919.  F 15998 

Contents:     Wild   youth. — Jordan  is   a   hard   road. 
Two  short  stories,   somewhat   connected.     Not  as 
good  as  his   other  stories. 

Patricia  Brent,  spinster,  by  the  author  of  ?. 
1918.  F  16066 

Light,  but  clever  story.  Patricia  invents  a  fiancS, 
who  promptly  "plays  up." 


Reynolds,  Katharine. 


Green  Valley.     1919. 
F 17113 


A  charming,  quaint  little  story  of  Lombard,  111., 
"Where  life  is  sweet  and  roomy,  and  old-fashioned, 
where  the  days  are  full  of  sunshine  and  work, 
where  neighbors,  really  neighbor,  and  men  and 
women  are  life  size." 

Streeter,    Edward.      "That's    me    all    over, 
Mable."     1919.  F 18411 

Continuation  of  the  author's:  Dere  Mable. 
F  18410. 

Van  Loan,   Charles    E.     Score  by   innings. 
1919.  F  18819 

Contents:  The  National  Commission  decides. — 
Piute  vs.  Piute. — Chivalry  in  Carbon  county. — The 
Squirrel. — I  O  U. — The  bone  doctor. — His  own 
stuff. — Excess  baggage. — Nine  assists  and  two  errors. 
— Mister  Conly. 

Lively  and  interesting  baseball  stories.

Rules  and  Information  for  Patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person 
residing  in  Cook  County  having  permanent 
employment  in  the  city,  may  enjoy  the  priv- 
ileges of  the  Library  by  having  a  resident 
of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear  in  the 
city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon 
application  and  compliance  with  the  rules 
and  regulations  of  the  Board  of  Directors 
a  card  good  for  three  years  will  be  issued, 
which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
five  books  for  home  use. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges 
upon  application. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and 
may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period. 
Books  may  be  renewed  but  once.  Books 
of  recent  purchase  labeled  Seven-day  books 
cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed.  The  card 
must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or 
Delivery  Stations  may  be  returned  at  the 
Main  Library,  but  books  cannot  be  applied 
for  at  the  Main  Library  and  delivered  to 
Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  hab- 
its may  use  the  various  Reading  and  Refer- 
ence Rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is 


required  to  use  the  books  on  the  open 
shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which 
must  be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported 
to  the  Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books 
drawn  on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  pre- 
sented, and  for  all  fines  incurred.  If  a  card 
is  lost,  immediate  notice  must  be  given  to 
the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be  issued 
thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  one  cent  a  day  on  each  work  of 
non-fiction,  and  three  cents  a  day  on  each 
work  of  fiction,  is  imposed  whether  bound 
in  one  or  more  volumes,  which  is  not  re- 
turned within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents 
is  made  for  each  notice  sent  for  the  return 
of  a  book,  and  no  other  books  will  be  de- 
livered to  the  person  incurring  the  fine  until 
it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the 
call  number  is  crossed  off  the  order  slip 
whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited, 
and  all  injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable 
wear  and  all  losses  must  be  promptly  ad- 
justed to  the  satisfaction  of  the  Librarian. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  De- 
partments, third  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30 
p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Cata- 
logue, fourth  floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m., 
Sundays  and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People, 
fourth  floor,  open  9:30  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed 
on  Sunday. 


Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and 
newspapers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street 
entrance;  open  9.  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays 
and  holidays,  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m: 

Music  Room,  fifth  floor,  Washington 
street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulle- 
tin are  issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and 
August,  and  are  distributed  free  of  charge 
in  all  departments  of  the  Main  Library  and 
in  all  branches  Copies  will  be  mailed  to 
any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published 
in  January  of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten 
cents  per  copy.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

RELIGION 

Philosophy  of  Religion 

Hegel,  G.  W.  F.  Lectures  on  the  philosophy 
of  religion,  together  with  a  work  on  the 
proofs  of  the  existence  of  God.  1895. 

M9166 


Orchard,  William   E. 
ligion.     1917. 


The  outlook  for  re- 
M9697 


A  fearless  diagnosis  of  the  condition  of  the  pres- 
ent-day church  by  a  prominent  English  non-con- 
formist minister.  His  hope  for  the  future  lies  in 
a  "social  and  international  application  of  Christi- 
anity in  a  new  Catholicism." 

Yale  University.  School  of  Religion.  Religion 
and  the  war.     1918.  A  6925 

Essays  in  which  members  of  the  School  of  Reli- 
gion at  Yale  discuss  the  question  of  non-resistance; 
the  effect  of  the  war  upon  the  church,  upon  reli- 
gious education,  foreign  missions,  and  social  work; 
and  the  part  the  church  may  play  in  the  reorgani- 
zation of  the  world. 


Future  Life 

Maeterlinck,    Maurice.      The    light    beyond. 
1917.  L 11301 

Essays  taken  from  Our  eternity,  The  unknown 
guest,  and  The  wrack  of  the  storm. 

Contents:  Our  injustice  to  death. — Annihilation. — 
Communications  with  the  dead. — The  fate  of  our 
consciousness. — Two  aspects  of  infinity. — Our  fate 
in  those  infinities. — Conclusions. — The  knowledge  of 
the  future. — Heroism. — On  rereading  Thucydides. — 


The  dead  do  not  die. — In  memoriam. — The  life  of 
the  dead. — The  war  and  the  prophets. — The  will  of 
earth. — When  the  war  is  over. 

Walpole,  George  H.  S.,  bp.   Life  in  the  world 

to  come.     1917.  M  4415 

For  the  stricken  and  desolate  the  Bishop  of  Edin- 

burg  here   interprets  the  promise   of  immortality  as 

preached  by  Christ  and  his  apostles. 


Bible 

Bible.  N.  T.  The  New  Testament  in  mod- 
ern speech:  an  idiomatic  translation  into 
everyday  English,  by  R.  F.  Weymouth. 
1909.  M  6772 


Religious  Education 

Cope,    Henry   Frederick.      Religious    educa- 
tion  in   the  church.     1918.  M  8806 
Discussion    of    the    agencies    through    which    the 
church    may   fulfil   its   function    of   imparting   moral 
and   religious  education  to   the  community. 

Lawrance,      William      Irvin.        The      social 
emphasis  in  religious  education.     1918. 

L  19912 

A  study  of  the  work  of  the  Sunday  School,  bent 
upon  establishing  sympathy  between  the  child  and 
all  nature,  animate  and  inanimate.  The  author 
discusses  the  psychology  of  children  of  different 
ages  and  outlines  a  curriculum  which  includes  more 
than  Bible  study. — The  Booklist. 


Roman  Catholic  Church 

Sullivan,  John  F.  The  externals  of  the 
Catholic  church;  her  government,  ceremo- 
nies, festivals,  sacranrentals,  and  devo- 
tions. 1918.  M7712 


SOCIOLOGY 

Reconstruction 

American  Academy  of  Political  and  Social 
Science.  Phila.  Rehabilitation  of  the 
wounded.  1918.  K  31078 

Annals,   vol.   80,   no.    169. 

Friedman,  Elisha  M.  Labor  and  reconstruc- 
tion in  Europe.  1919.  L  16735 

An  account  of  the  commissions  appointed  in 
eighteen  foreign  countries  to  consider  plans  for 
reconstruction;  discussion  of  the  general  problems 
facing  those  commissions  in  Germany,  England,  and 
France;  and  a  detailed  examination  of  the  labor 
problem  in  Germany  and  England.  The  author  has 
gathered  and  condensed  in  one  volume  a  vast  quan- 
tity of  useful  and  timely  information. 

Howe,  Frederic  C.  The  land  and  the  soldier. 
1919.  L 17539 

Advocates  farm  colonies  planned,  financed,  and 
developed  by  the  government,  not  only  as  a  partial 
solution  of  the  problem  of  the  returning  soldier,  but 
to  relieve  the  food  shortage  and  improve  the  gen- 
eral condition  of  the  laboring  class. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


101 


Wood,  Charles  W.  The  great  change;  new 
America  as  seen  by  leaders  in  American 
government,  industry  and  education  who 
are  remaking  our  civilization.  1918. 

L 16665 

As  reporter  for  the  New  York  World  the  author 
has  interviewed  about  twenty  leaders  of  American 
industry  and  thought  as  to  the  changes  the  war  has 
introduced  into  the  relations  between  capital  and 
labor.  From  these  interviews  he  concludes  that  the 
eventual  ownership  and  control  of  industry  by  the 
workers  is  inevitable. 


Economics 
Co-operation 

Woolf,   Leonard    S.      Co-operation   and   the 

future   of  industry.     1919.  L 17550 

Traces  briefly  the  development  of  the  co-operative 

movement   in   England,   but   is   interested   rather   in 

its  possibilities  than  in  its  past  achievements.

U.  S.  Government  and  Politics 

Munro,  William  Bennett.  The  government 
of  the  United  States,  national,  state,  and 
local.  1919.  L 18384 

General  survey  of  the  American  government, 
national,  state,  and  local,  which  not  only  explains 
the  form  and  function  of  political  institutions  but 
traces  their  development  and  compares  them  with 
those  of  other  countries.

L  17605 


Russell,  Bertrand. 
dom.      1919. 


Proposed  roads  to  free- 
L 17621 


A  clearly  written,  most  readable  brief  survey — 
historical  and  critical — of  socialism,  anarchism,  and 
syndicalism,  each  of  which  the  author  considers 
sympathetically  in  turn,  to  conclude  by  favoring  a 
form  of  guild  socialism  leaning  toward  anarchism.

Political  Science 

Ogg,  Frederick  A.  and  Beard,  Charles  A. 
National  governments  and  the  world  war. 
1919.  L 18066 

Deals  mainly  with  comparative  government  and 
undertakes  to  show  what  1(ie  heritage  and  genius 
of  the  principal  peoples  lately  engaged  in  the  world 
war  have  meant  in  shaping  contemporary  political 
institutions  and  ideas.  It  seeks  also  to  describe 
the  changes  wrought  in  governmental  organization 
and  procedure  during  the  war,  and_  to  point  out  the 
major  political  problems  that  remain  for  settlement 
during  the  early  years  of  peace. — Preface.

History  circle,  comp.  British-American  dis-

A  small  monograph  summarizing  the  relations  of

L  16296 

An  essay  reprinted  from  the  New  Republic,  which 
considers  some  of  the  problems  before  the  peace 
conference  and  advocates  American  support  of  the 
league  of  nations,  provided  the  peace  terms  succeed 
in  embodying  Wilson's  fourteen  points.  The  text 
of  the  proposed  league  is  printed  in  full.

Sanitary  Engineering 

Race,  Joseph.     Chlorination  of  water.     1918. 

K  23772 

The  first  extended  treatment  of  the  subject  in 
book  form.  The  author  traces  the  history  of  the 
process,  discusses  chlorine  production,  the  method 
of  using  it,  its  advantages  and  disadvantages,  bleach 
treatment,  liquid  chlorine,  chloramine,  and  the  re- 
sults obtained  in  cities  which  have  experimented 
with  this  method  of  water  purification.

"The    engineer"    series. 

The  authors  show  how  chemical  science  has  ad-

Poetry 

Hagedorn,    Hermann.     Hymn   of   free   peo- 
ples triumphant.     1918.  E 11036 
A  poem  of  exalted  tons  celebrating  the  victory  of 
the  Allies. 

Helton,  Roy  A.     Outcasts  in  Beulah  Land, 
and  other  poems.     1918.  E  11030 

With  a  talent  for  the  rhymed  narrative  the  poet 
takes  an  incompetent  clerk  or  a  tired  mill-girl,  the 
tinsel  glitter  of  Market  Street,  a  city  boy  and  a 
turn  of  twine,  a  corner  crossing  or  a  chop  suey 
interior  and  makes  ballads  out  of  them  as  romantic 
as  they  are  real. — iLouis  Untermeyer  in  The  new  era 
in  American  poetry. 

Kipling,  Rudyard.    The  years  between.    1919. 

E7706 

Poems  written  for  the  most  part  since  the  out- 
break of  the  war,  many  of  them  characterized  by  the 
old  Kipling  swing  and  vigor. 

Lawrence,   D.    H.     Look!     We   have   come 
through!      1918.  E 11183 

A  poem  sequence. 

McCrae,  John.    In  Flanders  fields,  and  other 
poems.      1919.  E  11327 

The  poem  which  gives  its  name  to  this  collection 
has  doubtless  been  more  universally  popular  than 
any  other  produced  by  the  war.  It  was  written  by 
a  Canadian  doctor  who  died  with  the  army  in 
France.  A  brief  biography  is  appended  to  his  poems 
in  the  present  volume.

1919.

familiar  things.

A  new  venture  in  anthology  making  which  prom-

Baker,  George  Pierce.     Dramatic  technique. 
1919.  E  13486 

A  detailed  study  of  the  art  of  play-making  based 
upon  analysis  of  the  technical  methods  of  a  vast 
range  of  dramatists  and  playwrights.  The  book 
embodies  the  result  of  Professor  Baker's  experience 
in  his  famous  class  in  play-writing  at  Harvard.

Maeterlinck,  Maurice.     The  burgomaster  of 
Stilemonde;  a  play  in  three  acts.     1918. 

E  14053 

A  most  effective  drama  centering  about  the  vol- 
untary sacrifice  of  a  Belgian  burgomaster  to  save 
his  head  gardener  from  the  vengeance  of  the  Ger- 
mans, and  depicting  the  ever-recurring  conflict 
between  the  ideals  of  the  destroying  nation  and  the 
people  they  ruined.

American.     1918. 


Essays,     Irish     and 
E6329

HISTORY 

Levine,  Isaac  Don.  The  resurrected  nations; 
short  histories  of  the  peoples  freed  by 
the  great  -war  and  statements  of  their 
national  claims.  1919.  A  5942 

Brief  popular  histories  of  the  small  nations  of 
Europe  and  Asia  which  have  come  into  prominence 
during  the  war  —  Jugoslavia,  Albania,  Ukraine, 
Poland,  Lithuania,  Lettonia,  Esthonia,  Finland, 
Arabia,  Palestine,  Syria,  Mesopotamia,  Assyria, 
Kurdistan,  Armenia,  Georgia,  and  Azerbaijan.

Letters  indicative  of  a  fine  and  sensitive  nature,

Fairlie,  John  A.  British  war  administration.

Carnegie  Endowment  for  International  Peace. 
Division  of  Economics  and  History.

War  experiences  of  a  Red  Cross  nurse. 

Fribourg,  Andre.    The  flaming  crucible;  the 
faith  of  the  fighting  men.     1918.        A  6926 
Translation  of  a  French  soldier's  diary. 

Jellicoe,  John  R.  Admiral.  The  grand  fleet, 
1914-1916;  its  creation,  development  and 
work.  1919.  A  6921 

The  full,  authoritative  record  of  the  work  of  the 
grand  fleet  from  the  beginning  of  the  war  to 
November,  1916,  when  Jelhco  ceased  to  be  its  com- 
mander. Invaluable  for  its  historical  and  technical 
information  and  a  narrative  not  without,  interest  to 
the  general  reader. 

Maurice,  Sir  Frederick  B.  Forty  days  in 
1914.  1919.  A  6927 

A  detailed  examination  of  the  military  operations 
of  the  first  forty  days  of  the  war,  from  the  days  of 
mobilization  to  the  defeat  at  the  Marne.  The 
author,  the  commander  of  a  division  during  the 
retreat  from  Mons,  writes  with  the  purpose  of 
making  clearer  the  part  played  by  the  British  ex- 
peditionary force  in  inflicting  the  great  German 
defeat. 


Meriwether,    Lee. 
diplomat.    1919. 


The    war    diary    of    a 
A  6928 


As  special  assistant  of  the  American  ambassador 
to  France  the  author  assisted  before  the  entrance 
of_  the  United  States  in  the  war  in  investigating 
prison  camps  in  France  and  Corsica.  His  journal 
is  interesting  for  the  account  it  gives  of  this  work 
and  for  descriptions  of  the  country  through  which 
he  passed.

The  Work  of  the  Library

Daily   average   circula 
Total  increase    . 

It  is  significant  that  by  far  the  largest  gain  is  found  in  the  use  of  the 
branch  libraries.  Although  but  two  new  branches  were  opened  the  increase 
in  branch  circulation  was  11.3%.  That  there  was  a  slight  drop  in  the  circula- 
tion from  the  central  building  simply  confirms  the  theory  that  its  patronage  is 
largely  from  the  younger  business  people — the  first  to  have  been  called  to  serv- 
ice and  tl>€  ones  we  are  most  pleased  to  number  among  our  heaviest  users. 


114  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

Our  work  with  the  schools  has  increased  heavily.  There  are  now  1336 
classroom  and  30  special  school  deposits.  The  high  school  libraries  continue 
to  be  among  our  most  successful  activities.  Their  number  was  not  increased 
during  the  year  because  of  difficulty  in  securing  suitable  help,  but  an  additional 
one  is  to  be  opened  on  or  before  October  1  in  the  new  Robert  Lindblom  High 
School  with  an  initial  collection  of  3500  volumes. 

The  Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People  shows  a  very  remarkable 
gain  in  the  number  of  books  circulated.  The  picture  collection  is  constantly 
growing  and  is  capable  of  unlimited  development  and  widely  extended  use. 

Reference  use  of  the  Library  naturally  suffered  most  from  the  extraordi- 
nary distractions  of  the  public  mind.  The  reduction  in  the  number  of  stu- 
dents in  the  university  and  professional  schools,  the  added  duties  imposed  upon 
teachers  as  well  as  students  in  all  institutions,  and  the  prevailing  unrest  and 
tension  contributed  to  the  neglect  of  studious  pursuits. 

A  new  subdivision  of  the '  Reference  Department  called  the  Short  Loan 
Desk  has  been  organized  to  supply  reference  service  to  branches  and  deposit 
stations.  Through  this  bureau  our  numerous  and  widely  scattered  extra-mu- 
ral agencies  are  enabled  to  offer  their  patrons  the  vast  reference  facilities  of 
the  main  library.  Problems  of  research  are  promptly  covered,  and  the  nec- 
essary books  are  despatched  as  "short  loans"  to  outlying  depositories  through 
the  Delivery  Stations. 

The  use  of  public  documents  has  greatly  multiplied  during  the  past  two 
years.  This  may  be  ascribed  in  part  to  the  tremendous  activities  of  the  gov- 
ernment in  calling  attention  to  the  importance  of  its  publications. 

Four  hundred  and  twenty-six  volumes  for  blind  readers  were  added  during 
the  year  and  8,487  volumes  were  circulated  for  home  use. 

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

PHILOSOPHY 

Cobb,  Stanwood.     The  essential  mysticism. 


1918. 


L  13810 


A  study  of  man's  fundamentally  spiritual  nature 
which  takes  into  account  Oriental  character  as  well 
as  Christian  mysticism. 

De  Laurence,  Lauron  William.  The  old 
book  of  magic;  a  precise  history  of  magic, 
its  procedure,  rites  and  mysteries  as  con- 
tained in  ancient  manuscripts,  embellished 
with  engravings  of  wonderful  charms  and 
talismans.  1918.  L 13365 

Kelway-Bamber,  Mrs.  L.,  ed.  Claude's  book, 
with  an  introductory  letter  from  Sir  Oli- 
ver Lodge.  1919.  L 13494 

Communications  received  by  the  author  through  a 
medium  from  her  son  Claude,  a  member  of  the 
Royal  Flying  Corps,  who  was  killed  in  action  in 
Flanders,  November,  1915. 

Leaf,  Horace.  What  is  this  spiritualism? 
1919.  L  13495 

The  author  is  a  believer,  convinced  like  many 
others  against  his  will  and  most  interesting  when 
recounting  the  experiences  that  effected  his  conver- 
sion. Without  entering  into  arguments,  he  asserts 
that  the  spiritualistic  claims  have  positively  proved 
the  fact  of  personal  survival. 

Lodge,  Rupert  C.  The  meaning  and  func- 
tion of  simple  modes  in  the  philosophy 
of  John  Locke.  1918.  L  15422, 12 

Mackenzie,  John  S.  Elements  of  construc- 
tive philosophy.  1917.  L 12974 

Rignano,  Eugenio.  Essays  in  scientific  syn- 
thesis. 1918.  L 12978 

This  book  by  the  editor  of  the  international  maga- 
zine, Scientia,  consists  of  seven  essays  translated 
into  excellent  English.  They  treat  of  the  latest 
hypotheses  as  to  the  origin  of  animal  and  vegetable 
life,  the  nature  of  consciousness,  the  modern  aspect 
of  religious  phenomena,  historical  materialism,  and 
the  defects  of  all  forms  of  property  expropriation. 

Vance,  John  G.  Reality  and  truth;  a  crit- 
ical and  constructive  essay  concerning 
knowledge,  certainty,  and  truth.  1917. 

L  12900 

Psychology  and  Ethics 

American  Library  Association.  War  Serv- 
ice. Your  job  back  home:  a  book  for 
men  leaving  the  service.  1919.  L  14363 

Illustrations,  articles,  and  good  lists  of  books  in- 
tended to  aid  the  returning  men  to  get  back  into 
their  places,  but  equally  useful  for  any  young  man 
starting  out  in  life.

gell  and  of  the  Union  of  democratic  con- 
trol. 1916.  L  14282

•alcohol.     1919.  L 14531 

A  defence  of  liquor  which  the  late  lamented  Dr. 

Abraham  Jacobi  int^duces  as  a  "protest  against  the

Goddard,  Harold  C.    Morale.    1918.    L  13918 
A  convincing  and  well-sustained  discussion  of  war 
psychology,    briefer   and   less   scholarly    than    Hock- 
ing's. 

Gulick,  Luther  H.  Morals  and  morale, 
with  an  introduction  by  Raymond  B.  Fos- 
dick.  1919.  L  14468 

Dr.  Gulick  has  given  in  this  book  an  enlightening 
account  of  General  Pershing's  repression  of  vice 
and  the  cure  and  prevention  of  disease.  How  our 
men  lived  over  there,  how  their  energies  were 
dircted  into  healthy  channels  under  moral  condi- 
tions, how  the  morale  was  improved  and  preserved 
and  the  team  spirit  promoted  are  matters  told  in 
detail. — Boston  Transcript. 

Hobson,  Richmond  Pearson.  Alcohol  and 
the  human  race.  1919.  L  14533 

A  vigorous  sermon  on  prohibition. 

Hocking,  William  Ernest.  Morale  and  its 
enemies.  1918.  L 13919 

A  thoughtful,  well-rounded  study  designed  to 
help  the  civilian  as  well  as  the  soldier  in  the  self- 
understanding  and  mental  reconstruction  made 
necessary  by  the  war. — Booklist. 

Kellogg,  Walter  Guest.  The  conscientious 
objector;  introduction  by  Newton  D. 
Baker.  1919.  K  31081 

Major  Kellogg  represented  the  army  on  the  com- 
mission that  examined  the  cases  of  conscientious  ob- 
jectors and  so  came  in  personal  contact  with  hun- 
dreds of  them.  The  account  of  how  the  objectors 
bore  themselves  before  the  commission  is  preceded 
by  some  historical  notes  on  the  relation  of  this  class 
to  other  wars  and  other  governments. 

Mateer,  Florence.  Child  behavior;  a  critical 
and  experimental  study  of  young  children 
by  the  method  of  conditioned  reflexes. 
1918.  L 13702 

The  report  of  a  series  of  experiments  intelligently 
performed  by  the  author,  who  is  psychologist  at  the 
Massachusetts  School  for  the  Feeble-minded,  for  the 
purpose  of  comparing  the  reactions  of  normal  and 
sub-normal  children  to  a  conditioned  stimulus. 

Seton,  Julia  (Mrs.  J.  S.  Sears).  The  key 
to  health,  wealth  and  love.  1917.  L  13229 

The  science  of  success.    1914.    L  14411 

Spillman,  Harry  Collins.  Personality;  stud- 
ies in  personal  development.  1919. 

L  14147 

This  is  a  book  which  may  encourage  the  very 
young,  serious-minded  adventurer  into  the  strange 
world  of  business  or  social  relationships.  It  may 
be  used  as  inspirational  material  by  teachers  direct- 
ing vocational  courses. — Booklist. 

Ward,  James.  Psychological  principles. 
1918.  "  L  13988 

Cambridge  psychological  library. 

Essentially  a  philosopher's  psychology.  .  .  .  Who- 
ever the  reader  be,  he  will  find  this  work  written 
with  directness,  with  all  the  simplicity  that>  seems 
possible,  and  to  be  happy  in  its  use  of  literary 
similes  and  to  reflect  a  catholic  learning. — Spectator. 

The  book  is  an  expansion  of  the  author's  Brit- 
annica  article  on  psychology.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


RELIGION 

Browne,  Edward  G.,  comp.  Materials  for 
the  study  of  the  Babi  religion.  1918. 

M7976 

The  Babi  religion  is  a  living  example  of  the  rise 
and  rapid  spread  of  a  religion  growing  up  around 
a  single  personality  within  our  own  century.  Mr. 
Browne  here  records  and  preserves  documents  of 
rare  value  for  students  of  comparative  religion,  to- 
gether with  the  correspondence  which  places  them 
in  the  proper  perspective. 

Davis,  Ozora  S.  The  gospel  in  the  light  of 
the  great  war.  1919.  M  5077 

Dr.    Davis   here   offers   sketches   for   sermons   and 
references  to  literature  that  will   be  useful  to  min-  . 
isters  in  meeting  the  needs  of  congregations  suffer- 
ing  from    recent    contact   with    the    horrors    of   war 
and  its  attendant  spiritual  problems.  . 

Garesche,  Edward  F.  Your  neighbor  and 
you;  our  dealings  with  those  about  us. 
1919.  M9010 

Essays  making  an  eloquent  appeal  for  the  apostle- 
ship  of  the  common  man.  While  the  book  is  ad- 
dressed particularly  to  the  laity  of  the  Catholic 
church,  the  specific  method  of  the  author  in  his 
advice  should  be  found  equally  suggestive  to  men 
and  women  of  other  faiths.  The  book  is  of  value 
as  a  manual  for  the  application  of  religion  to  daily 
life. — Survey. 

Gladden,  Washington.  The  interpreter. 
1918.  M  4892 

Fifteen  uplifting  sermons. 

Hopkins,  E.  Washburn.  The  history  of  re- 
ligions. 1918.  M  7514 

A  comprehensive  study  of  comparative  religion 
beginning  with  the  earliest  animistic  nature  worship 
of  savage  races.  The  author  is  a  recognized,  au- 
thority, especially  on  the  native  religions  of  India. 

Jastrow,  Morris,  jr.  A  gentle  cynic;  being 
a  translation  of  the  book  of  Koheleth 
commonly  known  as  Ecclesiastes,  stripped 
of  later  additions;  also,  its  origin,  growth 
and  interpretation.  1919.  M  6600 

Dr.  Jastrow  sums  up  the  results  of  critical  study 
with  reference  to  the  original  text,  and  in  his  in- 
terpretation gives  an  added  human  quality  and  sense 
of  personality  to  the  Preacher.  He  compares  the 
poem  to  Omar's  quatrains  and  Hesiod's  verses. 

Lyman,  Eugene  W.  The  experience  of  God 
in  modern  life.  1918.  M  9498 

Three  studious  but  clearly  written  lectures  deal- 
ing with  the  function  of  God  in  the  development  of 
personality  and  in  social  progress.  The  author  takes 
issue  with  H.  G.  Wells'  "finite,  personal  god."  Not 
iconoclastic,  but  interesting  to  the  thoughtful. — 
Booklist. 

Murray,  Andrew.  The  prayer-life;  the  in- 
ner chamber  and  the  deepest  secret  of 
Pentecost.  1918.  M  9623 

Library  of  standard  religious  authors. 

Orchard,  W.  E.  The  temple;  a  book  of 
prayers.  1918.  M  9698 


Phelps,  William  Lyon. 
1919. 


Reading  the  Bible. 
M6385 


This  little  volume  gives  a  most  popular  and 
attractive  presentation  of  the  Bible,  comparing  its 
art  with  secular  literature  and  its  ideals  with  other 
ethical  teachings. 

Rauschenbusch,  Walter.    Dare  we  be  Chris- 
tians.    1914.  M  9827

The  University  of  Chicago  publications-  in  re- 
ligious education. 

A  popular  biography  dealing  with  Paul  as  "a 
man  of  action  with  an  international  purpose  to 
which  he  bent  every  energy  of.  'his  'powerful  per- 
sonality." It  is  intended  as  a  textbook  for  junior 
college  classes. 

Walker,  Williston.  A  history  of  the  Chris- 
tian church.  1918.  M  7478 

A  reliable  history  covering  the  entire  life  of  the 
church.  It  is  well  written  in  a  clear  and  lucid 
style,  making  a  very  readable  volume  for  the  stu- 
dent and  general  reader  as  well  as  a  valuable  refer- 
ence book  for  busy  workers.

The  book  gives  a  very  lucid  account  _  of  the

ployment, health  measures,  bonuses  and  depressions. 
.  .  .  It  will  not  satisfy  the  radical,  but  will  com-

.  A  Catholic  priest's  discussion  of  social  and  in-

Kellogg,  Paul  U.  and  Gleason,  Arthur. 
British  labor  and  the  war;  reconstructors 
for  a  new  world.  1919.  L  16739 

Gives  in  detail  the  attitude  of  various  British 
labor  groups  toward  the  problems  raised  by  the  war 
and  reconstruction.  It  is  a  guidebook  to  the  new 
social  order.  Over  100  pa?es  of  appendices  make 
the  volume  invaluable  for  documentary  reference. — 
Dial. 

Leverhulme,  Lord.  The  six-hour  day  and 
other  industrial  questions.  1919.  L  16944 

Co-partnership,  housing  betterment,  education,  and 
a  drastic  reduction  in  hours  of  labor  are  advo- 
cated to  produce  better  workmen  mentally  and 
physically  and  effect  greater  production  in  industry. 
The  author  claims  that  more  leisure  is  absolutely 
essential  for  good  citizenship,  and  this  can  be  had 
with  a  six-hour  working  day. 

Reckitt,  Maurice  B.  and  Bechhofer,  C.  E. 
The  meaning  of  national  guilds.  1918. 

L 17885 

A  comprehensive  exposition  of  the  English  move- 
ment known  as  guild  socialism,  which  advocates 
the  abolition  of  the  wage  system  and  Mate  owner- 
ship of  industries  under  the  control  of  national 
guilds  of  the  workers. 

Slichter,  Sumner  H.  The  turnover  of  fac- 
tory labor.  1919.  L  20384 
Based  on  statistical  records  and  personal  investi- 
gation, this  book  discusses  the  high  rates  of  turn- 
over and  suggests  remedies.  It  will  be  useful  for 
employment  managers,  executives,  industrial  engi- 
neers, and  students  of  labor  problems. 


Stoddard,  William  Leavitt.  The  shop  com- 
mittee; a  handbook  for  employer  and  em- 
ployee. 1919.  L  16752 

It  tells  in  detail  the  introduction,  operation,  and 
success  of  the  shop-committee  system,  showing  how 
for  both  workman  and  employer -it  is  the  ideal  intra- 
factory  machinery  for  eliminating  friction  and  bring- 
ing about  good  relations.  The  author  was  an  ad- 
ministrator of  the  National  War  Labor  Board.

1919. 


Money  and  prices. 
L  17858 


Contents:  A  theory  of  prices. — Gold  and  prices

mingo.— The  refunding  bill  of  1881. — Government

A  semi-technical  presentation  by  a  conservative

Government,  General  and  Local 

Cooper,   E.   H.     How   to    prepare    for   civil 


service.     1918. 


L 18164 


A  discussion  of  the  departments  under  civil 
service,  the  nature  of  the  examinations,  and  desir- 
able preparation  for  them.  Mention  is  made  of 
the  opportunities  in  Washington  for  university 
work  while  in  the  service  and  of  professions  be- 
yond to  which  the  training  and  experience  may  lead. 

Douglass,  Harlan  P.  The  little  town,  espe- 
cially in  its  rural  relationships.  1919. 

L 18215 

The  town_  is  here  regarded  as  the  natural  center 
of  the  farming  community,  and  the  author  presents 
a  plea  for  co-operation  between  the  farms  and  the 
town  in  matters  relating  to  home  and  civic  improve- 
ment 

Fitzpatrick,  Edward  A.,  ed.  Experts  in  city 
government.  1919.  L 18214 

National  municipal  league  series. 

Twenty-three  articles  by  authorities  on  municipal 
government.  Various  plans  for  training  city  em- 
ployees in  order  to  provide  experts  are  suggested. 

Foulke,  William  Dudley.  Fighting  the 
spoilsmen;  reminiscences  of  the  civil  serv- 
ice reform  movement.  1919.  L 18166 
From  the  passage  of  the  Pendleton  law  in  1883 
to  the  present,  with  the  record  of  each  president 
from  Cleveland  to  Wilson. 

Jones,  Henry  Ware.  Safe  and  unsafe  de- 
mocracy; a  commentary  on  political  ad- 
ministration in  the  American  common- 
wealths. 1918.  L  15903 

A  serious  exposition  of  the  relative  failure  of  the 
electoral  and  administrative  machinery  of  the  United 
States.  The  system  of  party  or  partisan  government 
is  strongly  criticised  and  the  necessity  for  full 
recognition  of  its  own  responsibility  by  the  elec- 
torate is  insisted  on.  The  proposed  method  of 
amendment  is  based  on  political  education  of  the 
people  and  the  development  of  a  logical  body  of 
administrative  law. — Athenaeum. 

Lowell,  A.  Lawrence.  Greater  European 
governments.  1918.  L 18023 

An  abridgment  of  the  author's  well-known  Gov- 
ernment of  England  L  6444,  and  Governments  and 
parties  in  continental  Europe,  L  6430.  England, 
France,  Italy,  Germany,  and  Austria-Hungary  are 
the  governments  treated. 


Means,    Philip    Ainsworth. 
in   democracy.     1918. 


Racial    factors 
L  15321 


laborious  citation  of  illustrative  cases.  He  presents 
some  pertinent  questions  with  regard  to  the  short- 
comings of  our  own  political  organization. 

Willoughby,  Westel  W.  Prussian  political 
philosophy;  its  principles  and  implica- 
tions. 1918.  L  18032 

Contains  a  chapter  by  Walter  J.  Shepard,  entitled 
Tendencies  towards  responsible  government  in  Ger- 
many. 

A  clearly  outlined  picture  of  the  underlying  po- 
litical philosophy  of  Prussia:  the  principles  of  mili- 
tarism, absolutism,  and  nationalism.  This  is  pre- 
ceded by  a  survey  of  the  American  ideals  we  daily 
take  for  granted,  and  the  contrast  is  conspicuously 
evident  at  every  point.

Stull,  William.  The  food  crisis  and  Amer- 
icanism. 1919.  L 17863 

The  author,  who  has  worked  at  farming  or  with 
farmers  during  his  whole  life,  here  accuses  America 
of  neglecting  the  farming  industry  and  farming 
education,  of  promoting  unfortunate  betterment 
schemes  and  so  being  responsible  for  an  inexcusably 
large  number  of  crop  failures  and  for  the  growth  of 
an  indigent  tenant  class. — Booklist.

Oppenheim,  L.  The  league  of  nations  and 
its  problems.  1919.  L  18011 

Contributions  to  international  law  and  diplomacy. 

Three  lectures  showing  the  development  of  the 
idea  of  a  league  of  nations  from  the  middle  ages 
to  the  second  Hague  conference.  The  author  thinks 
that  regular  meetings  of  the  Hague  conferences  at 
stated  periods  to  codify  international  law  and  to 
give  hearing  to  international  disputes  will  preserve 
the  "national  state"  from  further  wars.

Stallybrass,  W.  T.  S.  A  society  of  states; 
sovereignty,  independence  and  equality  in 
a  league  of  nations.  1919.  L  18009 

The  author  admits  that  a  league  of  nations  implies 
a  diminution  of  sovereignty,  but  only  in  the  same 
way  and  to  the  same  extent  that  this  is  involved  in 
international  treaties.

BOOK  BULLETIN 


119 


The  most  complete  and  usable  collection  hitherto 
compiled,  extending  to  April,  1917.  The  book  is 
edited  by  Mr.  Albert  Shaw,  who  prefaces  each 
address  with  notes  connecting  it  with  the  preceding 
one,  the  whole  forming  a  continuous  account  of  our 
domestic  and  foreign  relations  for  that  period. 

Reconstruction  and  Rehabilitation 

Cameron,  James  R.  Instruction  of  disabled 
men  in  motion  picture  projection;  an  ele- 
mentary text-book.  1919.  K  29542 

Harris,  Garrard.  The  redemption  of  the 
disabled;  a  study  of  programmes  of  re- 
habilitation for  the  disabled  of  war  and 
of  industry,  with  an  introductory  chapter 
by  Frank  Billings.  1919.  L 19709 

Problems  of  war  and  of  reconstruction. 
A  review  by  a  member  of  the  Federal  Board  for 
Vocational  Education  of  what  is  being  done  by  our 
government  and  those  of  other  countries  to  prepare 
the  disabled  fighting  men  for  the  new  conditions 
to  be  met. 

Paeuw,  Leon  de.  The  vocational  re-educa- 
tion of  maimed  soldiers,  with  a  preface  by 
Madame  Carton  de  Wiart.  1918.  L  19708 
This  book  describes  in  detail  the  excellent  work 
done  by  the  Belgian  government  at  the  two  schools 
of  Port  Villez  and  Ste.^  Adresse  in  France  to  re- 
educate the  crippled  soldiers. 

Simons,  A.  M.  The  vision  for  which  we 
fought;  a  study  in  reconstruction.  1919. 

L 16659 

The  citizen's  library  of  economics,  politics  and 
sociology. 

The  theme  of  this  volume  is  that  in  mobilizing 
society  for  war,  social  forces,  inspired  by  _  the 
vision  of  democracy,  were  given  a  common  direc- 
tion. It  is  the  belief  of  the  author  that  this  social 
solidarity  "should  now  be  mobilized  to  fight  ignor- 
ance, disease,  and  social  injustice." 

Smith-Gordon,  Lionel  and  Staples,  L.  C. 
Rural  reconstruction  in  Ireland;  a  record 
of  co-operative  organization.  1919. 

L 17546 

History  of  the  movement  inaugurated  by  Sir 
Horace  Plunkett  in  1880  and  its  results  in  the 
present  agricultural  and  industrial  co-operation,  with 
its  co-operative  creameries,  credit  societies,  and 
societies  for  the  purchase  of  farmers'  supplies. 
Summary  of  beneficial  results,  social  and  educational 
as  well  as  economic. — Booklist.

evoluti9p.  1919.  K  20915 

In  Civilization  and  climate  [K  20916]  the  problem

ered in  its  relation  to  time. — Preface.

ture of  life.  1919.  K  20987 
A  sequel  to  The  adventure  of  death,  L  13051. 
Both  books  are  a  physician's  attempt  to  prove  the 
existence,  of  a  First  Cause  by  the  argument  of  law 
and  wonders  in  the  physical  universe.  The  evolu- 
tionary origin  and  meaning  of  life  are  discussed.

Problems  of  war  and  of  reconstruction. 

After  a  preliminary  chapter  on  the  birth  of

the  rest  of  his  book  to  a  consideration  of  the 
genesis  and  development  of  the  American  aircraft 
program  in  the  European  war.  The  book  contains 
much  technical  information  popularly  presented.

The  first  part  is  devoted  to  a  discussion  of  the

credit,  exchange,  principles  of  accounting,  financial 
statements  and  other  problems. — Booklist.

credit,  transportation,  etc. 

Moore,  James  T.  M.  American  business  in 
world  markets;  our  opportunities  and  ob- 
ligations in  securing  export  trade,  the 
plans  and  purposes  of  other  nations. 
1919.  L 20119 

Thorne,  William  V.  S.,  comp.  Hospital  ac- 
counting and  statistics.  1918.  L  20491 
The  fact  that  this  book  has  rapidly  reached  its 
fourth  edition  shows  that  it  is  a  valuable  compila- 
tion. 

Vedder,  George  C.  American  methods  in 
foreign  trade;  a  guide  to  export  selling 
policy.  1919.  L  201 18 

The  author  seeks  to  familiarize  American  busi- 
ness men  with  the  existing  American  methods  of 
building  up  foreign  trade  as  practiced  by  our  most 
successful  exporters,  which  are  based  consciously 
or  unconsciously,  he  asserts,  on  the  golden  rule. 
Discourages  imitation  of  foreign  practices. — Book- 
list. 

Manufactures,  Trades, 
Chemical  Technology 

Abraham,  Herbert.  Asphalts  and  allied 
substances;  their  occurrence,  modes  of 
production,  uses  in  the  arts  and  methods 
of  testing.  1918.  K  25253 

This  reference  book  is  designed  for  the  works- 
chemist  engaged  in  testing  and  analyzing  raw  and 
manufactured  products;  to  assist  the  refinery  or 
factory  superintendent  in  blending  and  compound- 
ing mixtures;  to  enlarge  the  knowledge  of  the 
salesman;  and  to  expound  the  principles  underlying 
the  practical  application  of  bituminous  products  for 
structural  purposes. — N.  Y.  Times. 


Allen,  Charles  R.  The  instructor,  the  man 
and  the  job;  a  hand  book  for  instructors 
of  industrial  and  vocational  subjects. 
1919.  K  22272 

Mr.  Allen's  thesis  is  that  training  should  cut  out 
all  merely  scientific  material  not  of  immediate  use 
in  the  factory  .  .  .  This  text  is  not  alone  useful 
as  a  handbook  for  those  who  intend  to  establish 
factory  training,  but  it  is  also  bound  to  be  useful  in 
persuading  practical  managers  of  the  value  of  such 
training. — Survey. 

Bigwood,  George.     Cotton.     1919.       K  25839 

Staple   trades    and   industries. 

This  well-illustrated  book  is  a  non-technical 
treatise  tracing  the  cotton  industry  from  its  begin- 
nings to  the  present  time. 

Giles,  Frederic  M.  and  Giles,  Imogene  K. 
Vocational  civics;  a  study  of  occupations 
as  a  background  for  the  consideration  of 
a  life-career.  1919.  L 14389

tice, including  hand  and  machine  mold-

1918.  K 25708
