The  Haytol  series  of  textbooks  for  industrial 
education. 

Mcjunkin,    Paul.      Tungsten    and    molybde- 
num.    1918.  K 25620 
Ormerod,  Frank.     Wool.     1919.         K  25861 

Stanle    trades   and    industries. 

Tells  the  general  reader  how  the  industry  devel- 
oped in  England,  and  describes  the  technical 
processes  from  the  shearing  of  the  sheep  to  the 
finished  product.  Well  prepared  statistical  informa- 
tion concerning  the  world's  supply  adds  to  the  value 
of  the  book. — New  York  Public  Library,  New  Tech- 
nical Books.

LITERATURE 

Jiowen,  Ray  P.  The  novels  of  Ferdinand 
Fabre,  including  an  account  of  his  life  and 
a  discussion  of  his  position  in  literature. 
1918.  J  7228 

Mr.  Bowen's  painstaking  study  forms  the  best 
companion  in  English  for  the  reader  of  Fabre. — 
Nation. 

Burton,    Richard.      Charles    Dickens.      1919. 

J7216 

Largely  composed  of  selections  from  Dickens' 
novels. 

Cunliffe,  J.  W.  English  literature  during 
the  last  half  century.  1919.  J  6332 

Contents: — 'Introductory.  —  George  Meredith.  — 
Thomas  Hardy. — Samuel  Butler. — Robert  Louis 
Stevenson. — George  Gissing. — George  Bernard  Shaw. 
— Rudyard  Kipling. — Joseph  Conrad. — H.  G.  Wells. 
— John  Galsworthy. — Arnold  Bennett. — The  Irish 
movement. — The  new  poets. — The  new  novelists. 

He  professes  to  have  written  "to  provide  guid- 
ance for  firsthand  study — assistance  in  reading  the 
authors  themselves,  not  a  substitute  for  it."  As  a 
first  aid  to  the  explorer,  the  book  presents  just  what 
is  needed:  biographical  sketches,  summaries  of  nov- 
els and  plays,  critical  excerpts,  bibliographies,  and  a 
temper  generally  hospitable  and  temperately  appre- 
ciative.— The  Review. 

Neal,  Robert  Wilson.  Today's  short  stones 
analyzed;  an  informal  encyclopedia  of 
short  story  art  as  exemplified  in  contem- 
porary magazine  fiction  for  writers  and 
students.  1918.  J  7217 

Each  of  twenty-two  stories  from  recent  maga- 
zines is  preceded  by  a  study  of  its  technique  along 
lines  laid  down  in  the  author's  Short  stories  in  the 
making,  J  3239.  Particular  sentences  and  para- 
graphs are  analyzed  in  foot  notes. 

Pater,  Walter.    Sketches  and  reviews.    1919. 

J  4812 

Contents:  Aesthetic  poetry.  —  M.  LeMaitre's 
Serenus  and  other  tales. — The  life  and  letters  of 
Gustave  Flaubert. — Coleridge  as  a  theologian. — 
Wordsworth. — A  novel  by  Mr.  Oscar  Wilde. — A 
poet  with  something  to  say  (Arthur  Symons). — 
George  Moore  as  an  art  critic. 

Pierce,  Frederick  E.  Currents  and  eddies 
in  the  English  romantic  generation.  1918. 

J  6347 

A  picture  of  the  brilliant  transitional  period  of 
forty  years  in  English  letters  from  the  French  rev- 
olution to  Tennyson,  including,  besides  the  masters, 
minor  figures  who  influenced  thought.  Written 
with  animation  and  with  amusing  and  informing 
details  about  authors  and  their  work. — Booklist. 

Rankin,  T.  E.  American  authorship  of  the 
present-day  (since  1890).  1918.  J6431 

Schevill,  Rudolph.  Cervantes.  1919.  J  5484 
The  results  of  Professor  Schevill's  brilliant  schol- 
arship and  penetrating  criticism  are  interesting  to 
the  general  reader  and  indispensable  to  students 
of  the  Spanish  Renaissance  and  of  its  greatest  fig- 
ure.— Dial. 

Sedgwick,  Henry  Dwight.  Dante;  an  ele- 
mentary book  for  those  who  seek  in  the 
great  poet  the  teacher  of  spiritual  life. 
1918.  E7130 

Mr.    Sedgwick    succeeds    in    giving    a    fresh,    un- 
hackneyed   account    of    the    proem    that    will    induce 
many   a    reader   to   subject    himself   to   the    spell   of 
Dante's  own  language  and  even  to  learn  the  Italian 
tongue  for  the  sake  of  doing  so. — Nation. 


Shanks,  Lewis  Piaget.  Anatole  France. 
1919.  J  5360 

The  best  book  in  English  on  the  world's  greatest 
living  man  of  letters. — Nation. 

Starrett,  Vincent.  Arthur  Machen,  a  novel- 
ist of  ecstasy  and  sin.  1918.  J  7222 

Contains  also  two  poems  by  A.  Machen,  entitled. 
The  remembrance  of  the  bard  and  The  praise  of 
Myfanwy. 

Machen  has  been  writing  in  England  since  1885 
with  occasional  high  praise  from  discriminating 
critics,  but  was  practically  unknown  to  most  read- 
ers until  he  wrote  in  1914  an  account  of  the  re- 
treat at  Mons  describing  the  miraculous  interven- 
tion of  the  English  archers  of  Agincourt. 

Williams,  Blanche  Colton.  How  to  study 
"The  best  short  stories";  an  analysis  of 
Edward  J.  O'Brien's  annual  volumes  of 
the  best  short  stories  of  the  year,  pre- 
pared for  the  use  of  writers  and  other 
students  of  the  short  story.  1919.  J  7227 
Useful  in  connection  with  the  author's  Handbook 
on  story  writing,  J  7225. 

Poetry 

Benet.  Stephen  Vincent.  Young  adventure. 
1918.  E  10649 

"The  hemp,"  a  ballad,  one  of  the  best  of  recent 
years,  I  shall  describe  in  a  word  as  falconlike, — 
keen  of  eye,  strong  in  swoop,  and  not  unworthy  of 
a  ducal  wrist. — Nation. 

The  author  is  a  young  man  of  twenty,  whose 
poems  show  not  a  promise  merely,  but  a  fulfilment 
of  remarkable  maturity  of  thought  and  richness  of 
imagination  and  of  poetic  expression. 

Brody,  Alter.  A  family  album,  and  other 
poems.  1918.  E  10662 

Realistic  free  verse  with  a  definite  personal  note 
placing  it  above  the  average  of  its  type,  which  the 
Dial  says  "is  rapidly  becoming  as  conventional  as 
the  conventions  against  which  it  rebels." 


Chapman,    John    Jay. 
1919. 


Songs    and    poems. 
E  10766 


We  admire  Mr.  Chapman's  poems  for  the  rich  and 
experienced  spirit  out  of  which  they  spring.  He 
gives  us  a  moment  of  pause  in  the  mad  rush  for 
strange  and  exotic  moods  which  beset  and  confuse 
us  wherever  we  turn. — Boston  Transcript. 

Gregory,  Lady.    The  Kiltartan  poetry  book; 
prose   translations   from  the   Irish.     1919. 

E  10984 

A  collection  of  folk  songs,  many  of  them  re- 
printed from  former  collections. 

Grumbine,  Harvey  C.,  comp.     Humanity  or 
hate:  which?     1918.  E  14311 

A  comparison  'between  French  and  German 
ideals  as  expressed  in  thirty-two  of  their  war  songs. 
Introducing  these  are  essays  comparing  the  French 
and  German  God-conceptions  and  the  souls  of  the 
two  nations. 

Hare,  Maud  Cuney,  ed.    The  message  of  the 
trees  |  an  anthology  of  leaves  and  branches. 

1918.  E 14296 
Holland,  Rupert  S.     All  'round  our  house. 

1919.  E 11049 

Poems. 

Jones,  Howard   Mumford.     Gargoyles,   and 
other  poems.     1918.  E 11118 

The  title  is  well  chosen  to  symbolize  the  grotesque 
visages  which  frequently  peer  through  the  thin  veils 
of  rhythmic  fancy.     The  poet  displays  a  vivid  touch 
and  a  facile  dominion  over  words;   his   style  is  in- 
cisive  rather  than  fluid. — Dial.

Kilmer,   Aline.      Candles    that   burn.      1919. 

E  11139 

Poems  by  the  widow  of  Joyce  Kilmer.  _ 
"Using  the  simplest   ways   of   expression   she   yet 
avoids  the   commonplace   by   her  grace,   her   whimsi- 
cality,    her    quiet    sincerity,    her    sensitiveness    to 
beauty." — Dial. 

Lee,  Joseph.     Work-a-day   warriors.      1918. 

E  11199 

Written  in  the  trenches,  all  the  horror  and  exalta- 
tion, the  weariness  and  inspiration,  the  grim  humor 
and  satire  of  trench  warfare  live  in  the  vivid  meas- 
ures, some  lilting  like  old  border  ballads,  some 
•sonorous  and  stately. — Boston  Transcript. 

Middleton,  Scudder.     The  new  day.     1919. 

El  1326 

In  these  poems,  realistic  and  imaginative,  one 
finds  dignity  and  idealism  and  passages  of  beauty 
and  poetic  wisdom. — Booklist. 

Noyes,   Alfred.     The   new   morning.     1918. 

E  11355 

These  poems,  covering  all  that  Noyes  has  pro- 
duced since  the  outbreak  of  the  war  together  with 
some  others  not  hitherto  published  in  this  country, 
seem  to  lack  a  certain  beauty  found  in  his  earlier 
ones,  while  possessing  more  of  moral  earnestness 
and  intellectual  content 

Pierce,  Frederick  E.     Poems  of  New  Eng- 
land and  old  Spain.     1918.  E 11403 
A  collection  of  stories  in  conventional  verse  on 
themes  from   everyday  life  in  rustic  New  England, 
together  with  one  symbolic  poem  on  the  Inquisition. 

Poe,  Edgar  Allan.  The  complete  poems  of

E  11421

T.'s  Line-o'-type  column  in  the  Chicago  Tribune 
and  associate  professor  of  Latin  to  the  students  at 
Northwestern. — Dial.

There  is  so  much  dashing  about  from  one  thing

Essays 

Brooks,    Charles    S.      Chimney-pot    papers. 

1919.  E2123 

Contents:     The  chimney-pots. — The   quest   of  the 

lost   digamma. — On  a   rainy   morning. — "1917." — On 

foing  afoot. — On  livelihoods. — The  tread  of  the 
riendly  giants. — On  spending  a  holiday. — Runaway 
studies. — On  turning  into  forty. — On  the  difference 
between  wit  and  humor. — On  going  to  a  party. — 
On  a  pair  of  leather  suspenders. — Boots  for  run- 
aways.— On  hanging  a  stocking  at  Christmas. 

Carries  on  the  essay  tradition  of  Hazlitt  and 
Lamb  with  that  studied  artlessness  whose  other 
name  is  charm.  It  should  be  popular  with  all  who 
cherish  a  literary  wardrobe  laid  away  in  lavender. — 
Dial. 

Burroughs,  John.     Field  and   study.      1919. 

E  2130 

A  collection  of  random  essays  containing  little 
that  is  new  but  much  that  is  charming  and  refresh- 
ing. Part  1,  "Fiejd,"  deals  with  nature  and  her 
ways,  especially  with  birds,  and  includes  a  good 
characterization  of  the  burdock.  Part  2,  "Study," 
contains  essays  on  literary  criticism,  science,  and 
religion.

Leacock,  Stephen.  The  Hohenzollerns  in

These  satires  attack  the  pride  of  the  "great"  re-

TRAVEL  AND  DESCRIPTION 

Burke,  Thomas.  Out  and  about  London. 
1919.  110118 

A  collection  of  sketches  of  London  in  1917 — Lon- 
don, without  lights — without  color — without  liquor. 
The  author  laments  the  passing  of  the  dock  and 
Limehouse  types  into  a  mold  of  mediocre  respecta- 
bility. "Attaboy"  is  an  appreciation  of  the  every- 
day American  soldier  with  his  baseball  and  slang. 

Desmond,  Shaw.  The  soul  of  Denmark. 
1918.  I  10329 

A  superficial  chatty  account  of  three  or  four  years 
agreeably  spent  in  Denmark.  According  to  the  Lon- 
don Times,  "the  one  thing  which  Mr.  Desmond  tells 
us  nothing  whatever  about  is  the  soul  of  Denmark." 

Edwards,  George  W.  Alsace-Lorraine. 
1918.  17514 

An  anecdotal  and  impressionistic  description  of 
the  "lost  provinces"  hardly  less  illustrative  or  more 
informing  than  the  beautiful  pictures  in  color  and 
black  and  white  that  adorn  it. 

Finley,  John.  A  pilgrim  in  Palestine;  being 
an  account  of  journeys  on  foot  by  the 
first  American  pilgrim  after  General  Al- 
lenby's  recovery  of  the  Holy  Land.  1919. 

1 10907 

Dr.  Finley  was  sent  to  Palestine  by  the  govern- 
ment with  the  Red  Cross  commission.  He  describes 
General  Allenby  about  the  time  of  the  capture  of 
Jerusalem.  Then  follows  an  account  of  the  au- 
thor's journey  from  Jaffa  to  Jericho,  a  night  on  the 
Mount  of  Olives,  a  journey  on  foot  from  Beer- 
sheba  to  Dan,  and  to  other  places  of  hallowed  asso- 
ciations. 

Fisher,  Fred  B.  and  Williams,  Gertrude  M. 
India's   silent   revolution.      1919.       I  10732 
This  volume  endeavors  to  present  from  the  Amer- 
ican viewpoint  the  economic,  social,  political  and  re- 
ligious situation  in  India. — Foreword. 

Frank,  Mary.  Exploring  a  neighborhood; 
our  Jewish  people  from  eastern  Europe 
and  the  orient;  1919.  14917 

Library  work  with  the  foreign  born. 


La  Motte,  Ellen  N.     Peking  dust. 


1919. 
1 10653 


A  series  of  impressions  of  modern  China  in  the 
form  of  letters,  showing  how  unfairly  the  Chinese 
have  been  treated  by  most  of  the  strong  nations  of 
the  world. 

Petrie,  W.   M.   Flinders.     Eastern   explora-

Contents:     Palestine. — Mesopotamia. — The    future. 

Saillens,  E.  Facts  about  France;  brief  an- 
swers to  recurring  questions.  1918. 

16901 

A  French  interpreter  to  the  British  army  has 
prepared  for  the  Allies,  from  the  French  point  of 
view,  a  compact  cyclopedia  which  succeeds  in  being 
at  once  clear,  useful,  and  entertaining. — Booklist.

Alfred  the  Great.  Lees,  Beatrice  A.  Alfred 
the  Great;  the  truth  teller,  maker  of  Eng- 
land, 848-899.  1919.  C  20554 

Heroes  of  the  nations. 

While  following  the  traditional  sources  for 
Alfred's  life  the  author  has  been  able  to  supplement 
them  by  a  reconstruction  of  the  national  life  of  the 
period,  and  the  result  is  a  decidedly  interesting  nar- 
rative. 


Argyll,  Duke  of. 
1907.    2  v. 


Passages  from  the  past. 
C  20635 


Bronte  Society.  Charlotte  Bronte;  1816-1916, 
a  centenary  memorial.  1918.  C  20994 

The  Bronte  Society  was  founded  in  1893.  Mrs. 
Humphry  Ward — the  president  in  1916 — contributes 
a  truly  delightful  paper,  "Some  thoughts  on  Char- 
lotte Bronte."  Other  papers  are  by  Edmund  Gosse, 
G.  K.  Chesterton,  C.  E.  Vaughan,  A.  C.  Benson, 
Sir  Sidney  Lee,  and  Richard  Garnett. 

Clcmenccau,  Georges.  Lecomte,  Georges. 
Georges  Clemenceau,  the  Tiger  of  France. 
1919.  C 21288 

A  Frenchman's  intimate  and  enthusiastic  sketch 
of  the  man  who  has  been  the  inspiration  of  France 
during  the  war.  He  is  pictured  as  strenuous,  fiery, 
lovable,  and  fun-making,  losing  no  opportunity  to 
restore  courage  to  jaded  deputies  or  poilus. — Book- 
list. 

Cobden,  Richard.  Hobson,  J.  A.  Richard 
Cobden,  the  international  man.  1919. 

C  21298 

This  is  not  a  biography  but  consists  largely  of 
hitherto  inaccessible  letters  treating  of  Cobden's 
political  and  economic  philosophy  in  relation  to  the 
world  at  large.  Though  not  a  pacifist  he  was  a  man 
of  peace  and  had  definite  plans  for  international 
disarmament  and  arbitration  of  disputes. 

Edwards,  William  J.  Twenty-five  years  in 
the  Black  belt.  1918.  C  21757 

Autobiographical  narrative  and  history  of  the 
Snow  Hill  Normal  and  Industrial  Institute,  Snow 
Hill,  Ala.  The  last  third  of  the  book  discusses"  the 
author's  solution  of  the  race  problem,  which  he  finds 
in  compulsory  education. 

Ellis,  George  W.  and  Morris,  John  E.  King 
Philip's  war;  based  on  the  archives  and 
records  of  Massachusetts,  Plymouth, 
Rhode  Island  and  Connecticut,  and  con- 
temporary letters  and  accounts.  1906. 

B3777 

The  Grafton  historical  series. 

Isabella,  queen  of  Castile  and  Aragon.  Plunket, 
Irene  L.  Isabel  of  Castile,  and  the  making 
of  the  Spanish  nation,  1451-1504.  1919. 

C  22576 

Heroes  of  the  nations. 

An  impartial  and  vivid  picture  .  .  .  The 
Queen's  relations  with  Columbus  are  told  in  detail 
as  well  as  her  part  in  the  Italian  _  wars  and  her 
services  to  literature  ...  A  picturesque  and 
absorbing  biography  of  a  many-sided  person. — N.  Y. 
Times. 

Meredith,  George.  Barrie,  Sir  J.  M.  George 
Meredith,  1909.  1913  C  23373 

Pershing,  John  J.  Tomlinson,  Everett  T. 
The  story  of  General  Pershing.  1919. 

C  23731 


Smith,  Harry  James.  Letters  of  Harry 
James  Smith.  1919.  C  24292 

These  letters,  covering  a  period  of  twenty  years 
and  abruptly  halted  by  the  fatal  accident  met  in 
Red  Cross  service,  reveal  a  charming  personality 
and  a  buoyant  spirit.  The  writer  was  a  novelist  of 
some  repute. 

Swinburne,  Algernon  Charles.  Gosse,  Ed- 
mund, and  Wise,  Thomas  James,  ed.  The 
letters  of  Algernon  Charles  Swinburne. 
1919.  2  v.  C  24456 

These  letters  cover  the  period  from  1858  to  1909 
and  their  themes_  are  mostly  literary  and  personal 
with  very  little  intrusion  of  public  affairs.  Their 
style  is  as  full  of  hyperbole  as  we  might  expect, 
and  we  learn  of  Swinburne's  enthusiasms  for  his 
fellow  Victorians  and  for  the  Elizabethan  drama. 

Voltaire,  Fransois  Marie  Arouet  de.  Vol- 
taire in  his  letters;  being  a  selection  from 
his  correspondence.  1919.  C  24702 

An  effective  translation  of  letters  of  autobio- 
graphical value,  and  such  as  will  interest  readers 
of  the  present,  notably  those  which  deal  with  Fred- 
erick the  Great,  since  they  present  to  us  a  strong 
likeness  of  the  last  German  emperor. — Booklist. 

HISTORY 

Brown,  Arthur  Judson.  The  mastery  of  the 
far  east;  the  story  of  Korea's  transforma- 
tion and  Japan's  rise  to  supremacy  in  the 
Orient.  1919.  A  4784 

A  discussion  of  the  present  phase  of  the  eastern 
question  presented  with  impartiality  and  common 
sense.  Although  not  blind  to  the  faults  of  Japan, 
the  book  is  on  the  whole  a  justification  of  her  pol- 
icy in  Korea.  Dr.  Brown  feels  that  Japan  rescued 
Korea  from  Russian  oppression  and  also  from  the 
corruption  and  incompetence  of  her  own  official  sys- 
tem. The  subject  or  missions  and  their  influence 
on  the  development  of  the  Orient  is  competently 
handled. 

Cassavetes,  Nicholas  J.  The  question  of 
northern  Epirus  at  the  Peace  conference. 
1919.  ^  A  5232 

Chesterton,  Cecil.  A  history  of  the  United 
States,  with  an  introduction  by  Gilbert  K. 
Chesterton.  1919.  B  5582 

This  history  is  written  for  English  people  by  an 
English  author  after  a  short  stay  in  this  country. 
It  is  necessarily  superficial,  and  the  author  admits 
that  its  interest  to  Americans  will  lie  in  the  fact 
that  it  gives  a  foreigner's  interpretation. 

Dako,  Christo  A.  Albania,  the  master  key 
to  the  Near  East.  1919.  A  5257 

Faris,  John  T.  The  romance  of  old  Phila- 
delphia. 1918.  B8174 
The  author  has  known  how  to  make  us  really 
acquainted  with  the  city's  beginnings,  and  because 
of  this  moving  presentation  of  life  the  book  becomes 
literature,  which  too  few  guidebooks  are. — Dial. 

Hasbrouck,  Louise  S.    Mexico,  from  Cortes 

to  Carranza.     1918.  B  4829 

An  account  of  Mexico's  story  and  her  people  that 

is  not  too  dry  and  heavy  for  school  children  of  the 

upper  grades. 

Hassall,  Arthur.  France,  mediaeval  and 
modern;  a  history.  1918.  A  5724 

A  good  history  suitable  for  reference  use,  with 
maps  and  genealogical  tables  and  full  indexes.

Mathews,  Basil  J.  The  riddle  of  nearer 
Asia,  with  a  preface  by  Viscount  Bryce. 
1919.  A  4833 

A  forceful  and  intelligent  brief  for  Christianity 
as  against  Mohammedanism  and  other  oriental  re- 
ligions in  the  Levant. 

Newell,  Frederick  Haynes.  Asiatic  Tur- 
key; its  problems  and  resources.  1919. 

A  4835 

University    of   Illinois    bulletin. 

Older,  Fremont.     My  own  story.     1919. 

B2873 

It  is  a  picture  n  ^n  epoch,  a  portrait  of  a 
strongly-marked  individuality,  and  a  challenge  to 
the  consideration,  from  a  fresh  angle,  of  old  unan- 
swerable questions  of  human  fate  and  will. — Nation. 

The  author  is  a  well-known  San  Francisco  editor 
who  has  taken  an  active  part  in  some  of  California's 
most  sensational  political  and  judicial  events. 

Vosnjak,  Bogumil.  A  bulwark  against  Ger- 
many; the  fight  of  the  Slovenes,  the  west- 
ern branch  of  the  Jugoslavs,  for  national 
existence.  1919.  A  4324 

Presents  as  does  nothing  else  in  print  in  English 
the  Jugoslav  side  of  the  conditions  leading  to  the 
disagreement  with  Italy  in  the  territory  of  occupa- 
tion in  Austria. — Survey. 

Webster,  Charles  Kingsley.  The  Congress 
of  Vienna,  1814-1815.  1919.  A  5950 

Published  for  the  historical  section  of  the  British 
foreign  office. 

Wells,  Warre  B.  and  Marlowe,  N.  The 
Irish  convention  and  Sinn  Fein.  1919. 

A  5519 

Continuation  to  1918  of  A  history  of  the  Irish 
rebellion  of  1916,  A  1454.  The  authors  defend  the 
movement  for  a  republic  on  the  ground  that  the 
Irish  ideal  of  self-determination  cannot  be  attained 
without  complete  separation.

Benson,  E.  F.  The  white  eagle  of  Poland. 
1919.  A  6093 

This  book  is  intended  as  a  warning  against  the 
encroachments  of  Germany  toward  the  east,  and  so 
is  now  mainly  of  academic  and  historical  value. 

Cantacuzene,  Princess,  nee  Julia  Dent  Grant. 
Revolutionary  days;  recollect:ons  of 
Romanoffs  and  "Bolsheviki,  1914-1917. 
1919.  A  6047 

It  illustrates  how  short  a  time  is  required  to 
bring  one  of  unpretentious  democratic  origin  into 
the  spirit  of  an  aristocratic  caste.  It  is,  however, 
so  natural,  naive  and  unaffected,  that  it  is  not  un- 
pleasing  .  .  .  Princess  C's  account  of  the  Revo- 
lution is  intensely  interesting,  giving,  as  it  does,  the 
impression  of  the  dramatic  events  as  seen  by  those 
on  the  inside  .  .  .  The  volume  contains  several 
excellent  pen-portraits  of  Russian  and  foreign  nota- 
•bilities. — Nathan  Haskell  Dole  in  Boston  Transcript. 

Jones,  Paul  V.  B.     The  new  Poland.     1919. 

A  6095 

University   of  Illinois  bulletin. 


Kerensky,  A.  F.  The  prelude  to  bolshevism; 
the  Kornilov  rising.  1919.  A  6049 

The  title  and  red  flag  on  the  cover  of  this  book 
are  obvious  attempts  to  disguise  from  the  _  reader 
its  point  of  view.  It  is  Kerensky's  deposition  be- 
fore the  Commission  of  Inquiry  with  bracketed  in- 
terpolations written  later.  From  the  questions  of 
the  Commission  and  the  answers  of  the  witness 
emerges  the  picture  of  a  man  fighting  overwhelming 
odds.  It  is  a  valuable  historical  document  but  will 
not  'be  popular  reading. 

Lebedey,  Vladimir  L.  The  Russian  democ- 
racy in  its  struggle  against  the  Bolshevist 
tyranny.  1919.  A  6046 

Published  by  the  Russian  Information  Bureau  in 
the  United  States. 

Rappoport,  Angelo  S.  Pioneers  of  the  Rus- 
sian revolution.  1919.  A  6059 
The  author  compares  the  Russian  with  the  French 
revolution,  emphasizing  the  part  played  by  the  Jews 
in  breaking  down  autocracy.  He  thinks  the  outlook 
for  a  Russian  republic  discouraging  because  of  the 
large  number  of  different  parties  and  races. 

Reed,  John.  Ten  days  that  shook  the  world. 
1919.  A  6057 

A  graphic  account  of  the  Bolshevik  revolution  of 
November  1917  by  a_  participant  in  some  of  the 
most  spectacular  incidents.  The  reproduction  _  of 
manifestoes,  decrees,  and  speeches  add  historical 
value.  The  author's  sympathies  are  with  the  Bol- 
sheviki, but  he  has  "tried  to  see  events  with  the 
eye  of  a  conscientious  reporter  interested  in  set- 
ting down  the  truth." 

Spargo,  John.  Bolshevism,  the  enemy  of 
political  and  industrial  democracy.  1919. 

A  6058 

Valuable  addition  to  our  mounting  literature 
aibout  Bolshevism.  An  overrunning  storehouse  of 
facts  for  which  all  students  will  be  grateful.  Goes 
back  almost  a  century  .  .  .  describes  its  gen- 
esis, follows  its  growth,  considers  every  influence 
that  fostered  it  ...  There  is  no  denunciation 
in  his  book  and  no  conclusion  that  seems  tinged 
with  prejudice.  The  futility  of  the  Bolshevist  con- 
ception of  life  and  society  is  revealed. — C.  E.  Rus- 
sell in  New  York  Times. 

European  War 

Beston,  Henry  B.  Full  speed  ahead;  tales 
from  the  log  of  a  correspondent  with  our 
navy.  1919.  A  6941 

Exciting  stories  and  lively  descriptions. 

Birmingham,  George  A.,  pseud,  of  James 
Owen  Hannay.  A  padre  of  France.  1919. 

A  6948 

Modestly  and  with  many  touches  of  _that  same 
quiet,  deliciously  whimsical  humor  which  makes 
•'Spanish  Gold"  and  the  rest  tales  of  such  rare 
delight,  Canon  Hannay  writes  of  his  experiences  as 
a  chaplain  with  the  British  Expeditionary  Force  in 
France. — New  York  Times. 

Blankenhorn,  Heber.  Adventures  in  propa- 
ganda; letters  from  an  intelligence  officer 
in  France.  1919.  A  6942 

Informal  letters  from  a  member  of  the  intelli- 
gence division  of  the  War  Department  to  his  fam- 
ily in  the  summer  of  1918.  His  mission  was  to  or- 
ganize a  propaganda  to  get  some  knowledge  of  the 
true  state  of  things  over  to  the  enemy  behind  their 
lines. 


1281 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Booth,  Evangeline  and  Hill,  Grace  Living- 
ston. The  war  romance  of  the  Salvation 
Army.  1919.  A  6953 

A  simple  and  direct  narrative,  full  of  incident 
and  anecdote,  giving  an  account  of  the  selection, 
training,  and  activities  of  the  Salvation  workers  in 
the  war  from  the  point  of  view  of  their  own  com- 
mander. 

Brazil.  Brazilian  Ministry  for  Foreign  Af- 
fairs. The  Brazilian  green  book,  consist-, 
ing  of  diplomatic  documents  relating  to 
Brazil's  attitude  with  regard  to  the  Euro- 
pean war,  1914-1917.  1918.  A  6947 

Canby,  Henry  Seidel.  Education  by  vio- 
lence; essays  on  the  war  and  the  future. 
1919.  A  6934 

Essays  by  a  Yale  professor  of  English  who  was 
in  England  and  France  during  the  war.  They  deal 
with  the  effect  of  the  war  on  the  youth  of  the 
world.  Among  the  subjects  treated  are  the  English 
people,  the  Irish  mind,  the  sense  of  race,  morale, 
salvage  and  waste. 

Corcoran,  A.  P.  The  daredevil  of  the  army; 
experiences  as  a  "buzzer"  and  despatch 
rider.  1918.  A  6945 

What  it  is  to  be  a  "glorified  messenger  boy  of 
the  army,"  to  deliver  a  despatch  at  all  costs  or  to 
swallow  it  away  from  the  enemy;  to  win  at  last 
the  mild  commendation  of  "not  so  bad"  from  the 
scornful,  non-bicycling  Tommies;  this  is  the  story 
of  the  telegraph  and  motorcycle  department  of  the 
army. — Booklist. 

Daniels,  Josephus.     The  navy  and  the  na- 
tion; war-time  addresses.     1919.        A  6950 
About    forty    popular  Addresses    and    messages    to 
naval    students,    industrial    societies,    surgeons,    and 
other  groups  during  war  time. 

Gibbons,  Helen  Davenport.  A  little  gray 
home  in  France.  1919.  A  6939 

A  book  of  warm-hearted  hospitality  from  an 
American  woman  who  has  kept  her  little  gray  home 
in  France  open  for  the  doughboys.  It  is  amusing 
and  pleasant  with  the  whole-souled  joy  of  serv- 
ing.— Booklist. 

Gibbs,  Philip  H.    The  way  to  victory.    1919. 

A  6931 

v.    1.     The  menace. — v.   2.     The   repulse. 

Despatches  of  a  war  correspondent  for  the  New 
York  Times  and  the  London  Daily  Chronicle  begin- 
ning November  21,  1917.  These  two  volumes  form 
with  his  Battles  of  the  Somme,  A  6322,  and  From 
Bapaume  to  Passchendaele,  A  6780,  one  of  the  most 
valuable  histories  of  the  war,  notable  for  its 
graphic  descriptive  power  and  intense  human  qual- 
ity. 

Hay,  Ian,  pseud,  of  John  Hay  Beith.  The 
last  million;  how  they  invaded  France — 
and  England.  1919.  A  6946 

The  picture  drawn  of  the  British  soldier  in  The 
first  hundred  thousand  [A  6206]  is  here  compan- 
ioned by  that  of  the  American  doughboy  to  whom 
it  is  dedicated.  A  gay,  cheery,  slangy  account  with 
fictional  characters  who  tell  the  facts  about  the 
ocean  voyage,  the  trenches,  and  the  good  results  of 
two  English  speaking  countries  rubbing  shoulders. 
Will  be  enjoyed  even  though  the  war  interest  is 
past. — Booklist. 

Hayes,  Ralph  A. 
front.     1918. 

Written  by  Secretary  Baker's  private  secretary 
who  accompanied  him  on  his  trip  to  France  and 
Italy. 


Hobbs,  William  Herbert.  :  The  world  w;ar 
and  its  consequences.  1919.-  f  '  A  6943 
Theodore  Roosevelt,  in  his  introduction,  gives  un- 
qualified endorsement  to  the  author's  energetic  ar- 
raignment of  the  conditions,  aims,  and  acts  that 
have  rendered  Germany  a  world  menace;  to  his 
plainly  expressed  disapproval  of  the  president;  to 
his  denunciation  of  pacifists  (frequently  by  name) 
and  to  his  tributes  to  General  Wood  and  others. — 
Booklist. 

Hough,  Emerson.  The  web;  the  authorized 
history  of  the  American  Protective 
League.  1919.  A  6952 

Mr.  Hough  calls  this  the  worst  and  the  best 
book  he  ever  wrote.  It  is  made  up  from  fine  sift- 
ings  of  an  enormous  amount  of  material  from  A.  P. 
L.  centers  in  almost  every  town  in  the  country. 
The  vigorous  opinions  expressed  in  the  summaries 
are  the  inevitable  result  of  contact  with  the  mass 
of  first-hand  evidence  from  this  secret  service  or- 
ganization during  the  war. 

Imbrie,  Robert  Whitney.  Behind  the  wheel 
of  a  war  ambulance.  1918:  A  6929 

A  personal  narrative  of  the  war  beginning  in 
December,  1915.  There  are  gruesome  touches  but 
for  the  most  part  the  tone  is  cheerful.  It  contains 
intimate  glimpses  of  French  peasant  life  and  good 
descriptions  of  the  Verdun  district. 

Isaacs,  Edouard  Victor.  Prisoner  of  the 
U-90.  1919.  A  6930 

"The  personal  narrative  of  the  adventures  of  the 
only  line  officer  of  the  United  States  navy  to  be 
captured  in  the  great  war."  With  an  introduction 
by  Secretary  Daniels. 

Italian  Bureau  of  Public  Information  (New 
York).  The  story-teller's  handbook 
1919.  A  6936 

Short  sketches  of  heroic  acts  performed  by  Ital- 
ians during  the  European  war. 

Lewys,  Georges,  pseud.  The  "charmed  Amer- 
ican," a  story  of  the  Iron  Division  of 
France.  1919.  A  6944 

The  story  of  Francois  Xavier,  a  French  settler  in 
San  Francisco  who  joined  the  French  army  on  the 
day  he  was  to  become  a  naturalized  American.  His 
account  of  his  thirty-odd  months  in  the  war  is 
simple  and  vivid  and  told  with  much  interspersing 
of  poilu  slang. 

Macfarlane,  C.  W.  The  economic  basis  of 
an  enduring  peace.  1918.  A  6954 

Marcosson,  Isaac  F.  S.  O.  S.  America's 
miracle  in  France.  1919.  A  6937 

An  account  of  the  organization  and  operation  of 
the  "service  of  supply"  (S.  O.  S.)  which  equipped 
and  fed  the  A.  E.  F.  It  contains  lessons  of  value 
for  the  later  requirements  of  reconstruction. 

Moore,  Frederick.  The  chaos  in  Europe; 
a  consideration  of  the  political  destruction 
that  has  taken  place  in  Russia  and  else- 
where and  of  the  international  policies  of 
America,  with  an  introduction  by  Charles 
W.  Eliot.  1919.  A  6956 

This  book  was  written  in  December  1918,  and 
offers  three  suggestions  with  reference  mainly  to 
Russia.  One  of  these  is  the  formation  of  a  league 
by  the  A'.hes  for  future  security  and  the  preven- 
tion of  war,  which  the  author  believes  would  be  a 
practical  beginning  for  a  future  permanent  league 
and  in  this  respect  he  is  critical  of  the  position 
of  the  present  administration. 


Rice,  Philip  Sidney, 
at  Verdun.     1918. 


An  American  crusader 
A  6940 


Earlier  edition   published  under  the  title,  An  am- 
bulance driver  in   France.

Rickenbacker,  Co.pt.  Edward  V.  Fighting 
the  flying  circus,  with  maps  and  a  fore- 
word by  Laurence  La  Tourette  Driggs. 
1919.  A  6949 

•  This  gives  a  giaphic  account  of  the  sensations  ex- 
perienced in  flying  and  interesting  anecdotes  con- 
nected with  the  aviation  service  during  the  war. 
Quentin  Roosevelt  is  mentioned. 

Thayer,  William  Roscoe.  Volleys  from  a 
non-combatant.  1919.  A  6932 

Essays  published  in  various  periodicals  during  the 
progress  of  the  war,  many  of  them  illuminating 
and  prophetic. 

Contents:  Preface.— The  collapse  of  superman. — 
America's  international  relations. — Are  the  Hohen- 
zollerns  doomed? — John  Hay's  policy  of  Anglo-Sax- 
onism. — Beware  of  a  Judas  peace. — Despotism  by  the 
dregs. — Italy's  great  service  in  the  war. — John  Hay's 
good  deed  in  a  naughty  world. — Campaigning  for 
dupes:  are  you  one? — Out  of  their  own  mouths. — 
France:  1916. — Let  Foch  decide! — Prussia's  pewter 
Napoleon. — Italy  and  the  Adriatic:  peace  terms. 

Toynbee,  Arnold.  The  new  Europe;  some 
essays  in  reconstruction.  1916.  A  6951 
Contents:  Two  ideals  of  nationality. — Historical 
sentiment. — Politics  and  economics. — Natural  fron- 
tiers and  economic  rights  of  way. — Culture  and  the 
mother  tongue. — Anarchy  and  internationalism. — 
The  Ukraine — a  problem  in  nationality. 

Whitlock,  Brand.  Belgium;  a  personal  nar- 
rative. 1919.  2  y.  A  6955 

The  American  minister  at  Brussels  here  relates 
with  the  vivid  -pen  of  an  experienced  novelist  the 
development  of  Belgium's  tragedy  from  the  peace- 
ful celebration  of  national  ceremonies  in  the  spring 
of  1914  through  the  _  first  approach  and  occupation 
of  the  Germans,  their  military  rule,  intimidations, 
deportations,  etc.,  down  to  the  cessation  of  our  own 
diplomatic  relations  with  Germany. 

Whittemore,  Thomas,  tr.  Ivan  speaks. 
1919.  A  6957 

These  sayings  on  war  and  peace  were  set  down 
by  Madame  Fedorchenko,  a  Russian  nurse,  from 
talks  which  she  overheard  among  Russian  soldiers 
at  the  front  in  1915,  1916,  and  1917. — Preface. 

It  is  at  once  the  most  hopeful  and  disillusioning 
publication^  about  Russia  that  has  come  to  hand. 
Disillusioning  because  the  "mystery  of  Russian 
character"  which  it  "penetrates  and  reveals"  turns 
out  to  be  no  mystery  whatever  in  the  Russian  him- 
self .  .  .  Nothing^  is  held  in  reserve,  nothing  re- 
pressed—and nothing  is  made  ignoble  or  unclean. — 
H.  M.  Kallen  in  The  Dial.

A  gay  tale  of  the  adventures  of  a  brother  and

Extracts  from  the  diary  of  a  talented  and  ambi-

life-long  struggle  -with  disease.

The  story  of  psychic  influences  that  pervaded  a

Bowen,  Marjorie,  pseud,  of  Campbell.  G.  M. 
V.     Kings-at-arms.     1919.  F 11660 

A  carefully  written  and  skilfully  developed  story,

vivid  account  of  a  very  important  and  very  dra-

Duhamel,  Georges.  Civilization,  1914-1917. 
1919.  F 12599 

Stories  about  wounded  French  soldiers  beautifully 
written  and  very  realistic.  The  author,  who  was  a 
surgeon  for  four  years  in  the  war,  tells  his  expe- 
riences. 

France.  Anatole.    The  amethyst  ring.    1919. 

J  154,  20 

The  bishop's  ring  which  is  coveted  by  the  Abbe 
Guitrel  and  how  it  is  obtained  make  a  satirical  com- 
edy of  French  ecclesiastical  life.  It  is  the  third 
volume  in  a  series  of  which  "The  elm  tree  on  the 
Mall,"  and  "The  wicker-work  woman"  are  first  and 
second.

chououk. — Two  Christmases. — The  leading  light. — 
The  Red  Island  shoals. 

Eleven  short  episodes  giving  vivid  pictures  of  life 
in  the  Labrador  wilds  and  describing  the  hardships 
and  tragedies  and  also  the  kindness  and  unselfish- 
ness of  these  simple  people.

Two  gracefully  written  sketches  of  the  war,

Tchernyshevski,  N.  G.    What's  to  be  done? 
a   romance.     1886.  F  6944 

This  Russian  revolutionary  novel  was  written  in 
prison  in  the  years  1862  to  1864  while  the  author 
was  awaiting  his  sentence  to  Siberia.  It  exerted  a 
powerful  influence  on  the  intellectuals  of  Russia 
for  many  years,  not  so  much  because  of  any  great 
literar_v  charm  as  because  it  gave  expression  to  the 
prevailing  ideas  of  reform,  of  liberty,  and  of  per- 
sonal sacrifice. 

Red  Friday.     1919. 
F 18589 

The  story  of  a  Bolshevist  plot  to  secure  dom- 
inance over  the  United  States.  It  is  a  warning  pre- 
sented in  the  form  of  fiction.

F 19066 

The  struggle  between  business  ambition  and  ideal- 
ism is  told  in  this  story  of  a  son  who  learns  of  his 
father's  doubtful  business  methods.

Another  book  setting  forth  the  author's  theoriM

Happy  Jack  is  a  little  gray  squirrel  whose  passion 

in  life  is  thrift. 

"If  you  get   and   spend   a   penny, 
Then  of  course  you  haven't  any. 
Be  like  me — a  Happy  Jack — 
And  put  it  where  you'll   get  it   back," 

is  one  of  his  sayings.

The  Book  Bulletin 


of  the 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume  9  October,  1919  No.  8 


New  Books  for  the  Blind 

THIS  list  includes  books  for  the  blind  which  the  Library  has  received  since 
the  printed  catalogue  was  issued  in  May,  1916.     Over  four  hundred  vol- 
umes were  added  within  the  past  year  and  these  new  titles  contain  some  very 
good  literature  of  a  comparatively  recent  date. 

Books  from  this  collection  are  loaned  to  blind  residents  of  the  State  of 
Illinois,  and  may  be  called  for  at  the  Library  or  may  be  sent  free  through  the 
mails  to  the  nearest  post  office  or  postal  station.  They  are  shelved  in  the  News- 
paper and  Patents  Room,  in  the  main  building,  first  floor,  at  the  right  of  the 
Randolph  Street  entrance,  and  may  be  obtained  on  any  week  day  (not  a  legal 
holiday)  between  the  hours  of  9  a.  m.  and  5  :30  p.  m. 

Information  for  Borrowers  of  Books  for  the  Blind. 

1.  When  ordering  books,   borrowers   should  send  a  list  of  at  least  six  numbers  or 
titles  in  order  of  preference,  and  when  the  book  number  is  not  used  should  indicate  the 
type  desired. 

2.  Whenever    possible    each    book    should  be  returned  as  soon  as  read. 

3.  Books   may   be   kept    one    month,   and  then  renewed  for  one  more. 

4.  Books   sent   by  mail  must  be  well   protected  by  several  thicknesses  of  newspaper 
and  a  strong  outer  wrapper,  and  must  be  wrapped  so  as  to  be   examined   easily. 

On  the  upper  left  corner  of  wrapper  place  the  name  of  the  sender,  and  on  the  upper 
right  corner  write:    "Free.     Reading  matter  for  the  blind." 

A   package  Weighing  more   than  4  pounds  should  contain  only  one  volume. 

5.  The   Library  must  be  notified  if  packages    are    not    received    in    good    condition. 

6.  The   telephone   number   of  the   Library  is  Central  5822. 

7.  Letters  about  books  for  the  blind  should    be    addressed    to:     Chicago    Public    Li- 
brary,   Chicago,   111.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Bibliography,  Library  Economy 

American  Steamship  Association.  Bibliog- 
raphy of  maritime  literature,  comp.  by  W. 
M.  Britain.  1918.  *O  2894 

Brooklyn,  N.  Y.  Public  Library.  After  war — 

what?     1919.  *O989 

List  of  books  prepared  for  returning  soldiers  and 

sailors,   to  aid   them   in   their  choice   of  occupation. 

McCourtie,  William  B.,  comp.  Where  and 
how  to  sell  manuscripts;  a  directory  for 
writers.  1919.  J  2881 

A  remarkably  useful  book  for  writers.  Hun- 
dreds of  periodicals  are  classified  according  to 
the  material  they  publish,  and  each  entry  gives 
editor,  rates,  kinds  and  lengths  of  articjes  desired. 
Information  on  copyright  and  authors'  rights  is  in- 
cluded and  lists  of  book  publishers  here  and  in  the 
British  Isles. 


Sayers,  W.  C.  Berwick.  An  introduction 
to  library  classification;  with  readings, 
questions,  and  examination  papers.  1918.. 

J7309

sion of  bibliography.  List  of  references  on 
the  Monroe  doctrine.  1919.

torical order."

A  companion  volume  to  Present  philosophical 
tendencies,  L  8931,  dealing  with  the  ''moral,  emo- 
tional, political,  and  religious  implications."

Spiritualistic  messages.

RELIGION 
The  Bible 

Lewis,  Frank  Grant.  How  the  Bible  grew, 
the  story  as  told  by  the  Book  and  its 
keepers.  1919.  M  6379 

The  University  of  Chicago  publications  in  re- 
ligious education.  Handboolcs  of  ethics  and  re- 
ligion. 

This  book    traces    in    simple  style    the    develop- 


ment of  the  Bible.  More  than  that,  it  clarifies  the 
study  of  the  book  as  the  literature  of  a  great  spirit- 
ual movement. 

Macllravy,  Cora  Harris.  Christ  and  His 
bride;  an  exposition  of  the  Song  of  Sol- 
omon. 1916.  M  6631 

Missions 

Barton,  James  L.  The  Christian  approach 
to  Islam.  1918.  M  8105 

College  of  missions  lectureship. 

By  presenting  a  history  of  Islam,  together  with 
its  characteristics  and  needs,  the  book  is  designed 
to  aid  missionary  students.  It  suggests  appropriate 
methods  for  making  a  successful  appeal. 

Kelsey,  Rayner  Wickersham.     Friends  and 

the  Indians,  1655-1917.  M  8128 

A   history   of  the   relations   between   the   Quakers 

and  the  Indians  from  before  the  advent  of  Penn  to 

the   present. 

Judaism 

Jacobs,  Joseph.  Jewish  contributions  to 
civilization;  an  estimate.  1919.  A  2854 
A  well-presented  modest  statement  of  the  debt  of 
Western  civilization  to  the  Jew.  One  chapter  is 
devoted  to  answering  Sombart's  The  Jews  and 
modern  capitalism,  A  2930. 

Kirk,  Harris  Elliott.  The  consuming  fire. 
1919.  M  6657 

A  comparison  between  the  Judea  of  the  eighth 
century  B.  C.  and  America  of  the  twentieth  cen- 
tury A.  D.,  on  the  ground  that  "Isaiah  faced  .the 
grave  problems  of  social  and  religious  readjustment 
that  are  at  present  our  supreme  concern." 

Raisin,  Max.  A  history  of  the  Jews  in  mod- 
ern times.  1919.  A  2853 

SOCIOLOGY 
Labor  and  Laboring  Classes 

Angell,  Norman,  pseud,  of  R.  N.  A.  Lane. 
The  British  revolution  and  the  American 
democracy;  an  interpretation  of  British 
labour  programmes.  1919.  -  L 16655 

The  author,  while  denouncing  the  repression  of 
freedom  by  the  dictatorship  of  the  proletariat,  em- 
phasizes that  the  will  of  the  people  is  the  strong- 
est political  reality. 

Carnegie  United  Kingdom  Trust.  Report 
on  public  baths  and  wash-houses  in  the. 
United  Kingdom.  1918.  Civics  Dept. 

Colvjn,  Fred  H.  Labor  turnover,  loyalty  and 
output;  a  consideration  of  the  trend  of 
the  times  as  shown  by  the  results  of  war 
activities  in  the  machine  shops  and  else- 
where. 1919.  L  20315 

Hutton,  J.  E.  WeJfare  and  housing;  a  prac- 
tical record  of  war-time  management. 
1918.  L  17048 

Result  of  the  author's  experience  as  manager 
of  labor  and  the  catering  department  of  a  British 
company  which  operates  several  huge  munitions 
and  shipbuilding  plants.  The  book  deals  with  hous- 
ing, canteens,  hospitals,  and  other  activities  *  in- 
volved in  caring  for  one  hundred  thousand  war 
laborers. 


140 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Veblen,  Thorstein.  The  vested  interests  and 
the  state  of  the  industrial  arts.  1919. 

L  16675 

Professor  Veblen  desires  to  show  the  cause  of 
the  discrepancy  between  the  principles  of  law  and 
custom  underlying  the  business-like  management  of 
industry,  and  the  new  material  conditions  which 
have  grown  up  with  the  invention  and  develop- 
ment of  machinery.  He  discusses  also  the  civil 
and  political  difficulties  to  which  this  discrepancy 
has  given  rise. 

Webster,  W.  L.  Why  be  an  employee?  or, 
Democracy  in  business  after  the  war. 
1918.  L  16753 

Finance,  Insurance 

Gephart,  William  F.  Effects  of  the  war 
upon  insurance,  with  special  reference  to 
the  substitution  of  insurance  for  pensions. 

1918.  L  19246 

Ca_rnegie  endowment  for  international  peace. 
Preliminary  economic  studies  of  the  war. 

McVey,  Frank  L.  The  financial  history  of 
Great  Britain,  1914-1918.  L  17731 

Carnegie  endowment  for  international  peace. 
Preliminary  economic  studies  of  the  war. 

Political  Science 
Democracy 

Cleveland,  Frederick  A.  and  Schafer,  Joseph, 
ed.  Democracy  in  reconstruction.  1919. 

L  15900 

A  compilation  of  _  articles  by  competent  writers 
on  the  ideals  and  institutions  of  democracy  and 
embracing  all  phases  of  after-war  problems.  It 
gives  arguments  for  and  against  the  suggested 
policies  and  will  be  suitable  for  college  classes. 

Laski,  Harold  J.  Authority  in  the  modern 
state.  1919.  L 15789 

Sequel  to  studies  in  the  problem  of  sovereignty, 
L  15788. 

Contents:  Authority  in  the  modern  state. — 
Bonald. — Lamennais. — Koyer. — Col  lard. — Administra- 
tive syndicalism  in  France. 

Leads  through  the  fe_rment  in  which  the  trans- 
formation of  the  state  is  being  worked  out  to  the 
foundation  of  a  new  political  order.  The  book  is 
fair,  and  one  which  no  student  of  politics,  law, 
or  sociology  can  afford  to  leave  unread  and  un- 
mastered . — Nation. 

International  Relations 

Barclay,  Sir  Thomas.  Collapse  and  recon-

Reconstruction 

Kahn,  Otto  H.    The  morning  after.     1919. 

L  16645 

A  Founder's  Day   address  at   Carnegie   Institute.

1919.  L  16658 

Demobilization,  transportation,  and  employment 
for  soldiers,  are  among  the  points  for  which  the

L  16662 

An  appeal  to  moderate  people  to  constitute  a 
mediating  agency  between  the  extremists  of  labor 
and  capital  to  carry  out  reforms  that  will  avoid 
post-beUum  strife.  _  The  author  advocates  freedom 
for  private  enterprise. 

Vanderlip,  Frank  A.  What  happened  to 
Europe.  1919.  L  16664 

A  book  vividly  portraying  industrial  conditions

Carnegie  endowment  for  international  peace. 
Preliminary  economic  studies  of  the  war. 

Pearson,  Sir  Arthur.  Victory  over  blind- 
ness; how  it  was  won  by  the  men  of  St. 
Dunstan's  and  how  others  may  win  it. 
1919.  L  19711 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


141 


Sir  Arthur  Pearson,  owner  and  manager* of  a 
large  chain  of  English  papers,  became  totally  blind 
about  the  time  the  war  broke  out.  H'e  founded 
St.  Dunstan's  Hostel  which  cared  for  practically 
all  the  blinded  British  soldiers.  His  book  is  an 
inspiring  record  invaluable  to  the  worker  with  the 
blind  and  good  reading  for  anyone. 

Practical  Sociology,  Criminology 

Georgia.  University.  Sanitary  conditions 
among  the  negroes  of  Athens,  Georgia. 
1918.  L  16174,4 

Mounts,  Lewis  Hendrix.  Dependents,  de- 
fectives and  delinquents  in  Iowa;  a  study 
of  the  sources  of  social  infection.  1919. 

L 15421,  711 

University  of  Iowa.  Studies  in  the  social  sci- 
ences. 

Mercier,  Charles.  Crime  and  criminals;  be- 
ing the  jurisprudence  of  crime,  medical, 
biological,  and  psychological.  1919. 

L  18895 

Dr.  Mercier  writes  from  the  standpoint  of  com- 
mon sense,  often  protesting  against  absurdities  in 
legal  practice,  especially  as  found  in  English  law. 
His  book  is  an  excellent,  practical,  and  thought- 
provoking  exposition  for  all  those  who  have  at 
heart  the  interests  of  the  derelicts  of  society. 

Rhodes,  D.  P.     Our  immortality.     1919. 

L  15429 

Contents:  The  foundation  of  knowledge. — The 
meaning  of  immortality. — Practical  implications  of 
a  rational  view  of  immortality,  as  regards  war, 
murder,  rape,  eugenics,  alcoholism;  habitual  use 
of  drugs,  marriage  and  feminism,  mother  love,  arts 
and  sciences,  property,  government,  the  first  duty. 

Belief  in  the  future  life  in  its  bearing  on  human 
conduct  with  reference  to  the  present  problems  of 
society.  The  author  attempts  to  define  just  how 
much  may  be  regarded  as  definitely  established, 
and  how  much  is  unknown.

Problems  of  war  and   of  reconstruction. 

Considerable  space  is  devoted  to  various  meas-

universities  to  keep  a  continuous  supply  of  col- 
lege-trained men  in  the  government's  service  dur- 
ing the  war.

Well  illustrated  and  clearly  written  directions

Poetry  by  Individual  Authors 
Aldington,   Richard.     War  and   love,    1915- 
1918.     1919.  E  10624 

There  is  ecstacy  and  exquisite  suffering  in  these 
poems  of  love  in  wartime,  but  not  sentimentality. 
The  war  has  produced  no  more  genuine  poetry. — 
DM. 

Baudelaire,  Charles.  Poems  and  prose 
poems.  1919.  E  10640 

In  an  introductory  essay  which  incidentally 
makes  illuminating  comments  on  Poe,  De  Quincey, 
Hawthorne,  Whistler,  and  other  kindred  personali- 
ties, Mr.  James  Huneker  characterizes  Baudelaire  as 
an  "extraordinary  poet  with  a  bad  conscience." 

Bodenheim,  Maxwell.  Minna  and  myself. 
1918.  E  10674 

Poems    and    two    one-act    poetic    plays. 
Among  the  most  delicately  tinted  and  fantastically 
subtle   of  contemporary  poems  in  free  verse. — Dial. 

Bynner,  Witter.  The  beloved  stranger;  two 
books  of  song  and  a  divertisement  for 
the  unknown  lover.  1919.  E  10729 

This  volume  includes  poems  in  two  rather  dis- 
tinct manners, — some  beautiful  love  poems  like 
those  for  which  Mr.  Bynner  is  best  known,  and 
some  with  an  oddly  futurist  turn  like  those  he  has 
written  under  the  pseudonym  of  Emanuel  Morgan. 

Carmichael,  Waverley  Turner.  From  the 
heart  of  a  folk;  a  book  of  songs.  1918. 

E  10754 

Striking  interpretations  of  negro  life  by  a  South- 
ern negro  of  limited  education  who  has_  the  skill 
to  write  the  feeling  of  his  people  and  his  impres- 
sions of  the  life  about  him. — Boston  Transcript. 

Deutsch,  Babette.    Banners.     1919.      E  10874 
A    collection    of    poems    in    almost    all    grades    of 
form   from   the  stereotyped   sonnet   to    genuine   free 
verse  of  much  delicacy  and  beauty. 

Fletcher,    John    Gould.     The    tree    of    life. 

1918.  E  10911 

A   collection   of  love   poems. 

Hayes,  James  M.    The  grave  of  dreams,  and 

other  verses.    1917.  E  11040 

Housman,   Laurence.     The  heart  of  peace. 

1919.  E  11061 

A  few  verses  of  wartime  are  grouped  among 
these  lyrics  tuned  mostly  to  the  usual  poetical 
themes  of  peace. 

Mac  Cathmhaoil,  Seosamh,  pseud,  of  Joseph 
Campbell.  The  mountainy  singer.  1919. 

E  10752 
Charming   Irish   lyrics. 

Untermeyer,  Jean  Starr.  Growing  pains. 
1918.  E  8769 

Poems  of  self-analysis,  of  visions,  emotions,  and 
experiences,  written  with  much  depth  and  charm 
and  without  sentimentality. 


Wood,  Clement.  The  earth  turns  south. 
1919.  E  11686 

A  volume  of  poems  which  are  unusually  typical  of 
the  best  of  our  current  American  magazine  verse. 
A  few  of  these  are  already  widely  known,  as,  "I 
see  America  marching." 

Essays  and  Miscellanies 

Balfour,  Arthur  James.  The  mind  of  Ar- 
thur James  Balfour;  selections  from  his 
non-political  writings,  speeches,  and  ad- 
dresses, 1879-1917,  including  special  sec- 
tions on  America  and  Germany.  1918. 

E  1889 

A  consideration  of  the  ex-premier  as  a  personal- 
ity and  thinker,  quite  apart  from  his  public  life. 
Something  of  the  philosophic  spirit,  keenness  in 
repartee  and  debate,  and  personal  charm  with  which 
he  delighted  Parliament  may  be  appreciated  even 
from  the  printed  words. 

Hearn,  Lafcadio.     Karma.     1918.         E  3311 
Contents:     Karma. — A  ghost. — The  first  Muezzin, 
Bilal. — China  and  the  western  world. 

Russell,  George  W.  E.  Prime  ministers  and 
some  others;  a  book  of  reminiscences. 
1919.  E  5516 

Genial  essays  on  politics,  education,  religion, 
and  most  delightfully  of  all  on  famous  men,  in- 
cluding nine  prime  ministers  from  Lord  Palmerston 
to  Sir  H'enry  Campbell-Bannerman  and  a  dozen 
others  "in  honor  of  friendship." 

Symonds,  John  Addington.  Last  and  'first; 
two  essays.  1919.  E  5816 

Studies  of  the  function  of  the  Renaissance  in 
the  development  of  human  history,  and  of  Arthur 
Hugh  Clough's  point  of  view  as  poet  and  thinker. 
— Nation. 

Underwood,  Edna  Worthley.  Letters  from 
a  prairie  garden.  1919.  E  6161 

Letters  of  a  woman  who  corresponded  with  an 
unknown  artist.  They  show  a  wide  and  discrimi- 
nating knowledge  of  literature  and  music,  and  a 
vivid  joy  in  color,  nature  and  dream  life. — Book- 
list. 

Wit  and  Humor 

Jourdain,  Philip  E.  B.,  ed.  The  philosophy 
of  Mr.  B*rtr*nd  R*ss*ll,  with  an  appendix 
of  leading  passages  from  certain  other 
works.  1919.  E  3866 

A  sympathetic  reductio  ad  absurdum  of  symbolic 
logic  which  the  Dial  calls  "a  delicious  bit  of  phil- 
osophical spoofing." 

Marquis,  Don.     Prefaces.     1919.  E  4438 

Amusing    prefaces    to     imaginary    books.       These 

include  a  cook  book,  a  medium's  dope  book,  works 

of  Billy  Sunday,  the  novels  of  Harold  Bell  Wright, 

etc. 

BIOGRAPHY 

Parker,  Carleton  H.  Parker,  Cornelia  Strat- 
ton.  An  American  idyll.  1919.  C  23692 
Dr.  Parker  believed  in  applying  the  best  knowl- 
edge of  behavior  psychology  to  the  question  of  mi- 
gratory labor.  He  had  a  winning  personality,  and 
as  mediator  in  thirty-two  strikes  was  trusted  alike 
by_  laborers,  operators,  and  the  government.  His 
wife's  account  of  his  achievements,  and  the  ob- 
stacles overcome  to  make  them  possible,  gives  also 
the  beautiful  love-story  of  their  ten  years  of  mar- 
ried life  richly  lived. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


145 


Pett,  Phineas.     Autobiography.     1918. 

A  3389,51 

Publications   of   the   Navy    Records    Society. 

The  story  of  a  master-builder  and  naval  com- 
missioner in  the  days  of  the  early  Stuarts.  Pett 
was  more  or  less  responsible  for  every  ship  added  to 
the  navy,  and  for  many  merchant  vessels,  during 
the  reigns  of  James  I  and  Charles  I. 

Prominent  Jews  of  America;  a  collection  of 
biographical  sketches  of  Jews  who  have 
distinguished  themselves  in  commercial, 
professional  and  religious  endeavor.  1918. 

C  19898

introduction  by  W.  H.  Taft.  1919. 

C  23993 

The  great  American's  life  as  told  by  a  personal 
friend.  A  biography  that  is  at  the  same  time  in- 
timate and  unbiased.

HISTORY 
Europe 

Ashley,  Roscoe  Lewis.  Modern  European 
civilization;  a  textbook  for  secondary 
schools.  1919.  A  5944 

A  concise  study  of  the  social  and  economic  de- 
velopment of  Europe  upon  a  background  of  political 
history  from  the  beginning  of  the  seventeenth  cen- 
tury. Excellent  for  general  reading  and  for  his- 
torical classes. 

Asquith,  H.  H.  Some  aspects  of  the  Vic- 
torian age.  1918.  A  5378 

The  aspects  dealt  with  comprise  literature,  his- 
tory, philosophy,  and  science,  excluding  the  "con- 
troversial domains  of  politics  and  theology." 

Chekrezi,  Constantine  A.  Albania  past  and 
present.  1919.  A  5258 

"An  interpretation  of  the  Albanian  people,  an 
exposition  of  Albanian  fiistory,  a  presentation  of 
Albanian  claims,  straight  from  the  mind  and  heart 
of  a  native^  of  that  country." — Charles  D.  Hazen 
in  introduction. 


Ferrero,  Guglielmo.  Problems  of  peace, 
from  the  Holy  Alliance  to  the  league  of 
nations.  1919.  A  5948 

A  summary  and  running  comment  on  the  his- 
tory of  Europe  from  1815.  The  only  problem  of 
peace  that  he  sees  in  the  present  is  the  complete 
satisfaction  of  the  claims  of  Italy  to  compensate 
for  her  unparalleled  chivalry  in  rushing  to  the 
aid  of  the  Allies.— Dial. 

European  War 
Deland,    Margaret.      Small    things. 


1919. 
A  6962 


Mrs.  Deland  accompanied  her  two  daughters  when 
they  went  to  France  on  canteen  duty  for  the  Amer- 
ican Author's  Service.  Her  chronicle  reflects  the 
heroic  endurance  of  the  French  people,  the  ideal- 
ism of  American  soldiers,  the  spirit  of  service  of 
our  civilian  relief,  the  frightfulness  of  German 
practice. 

French,  Field-Marshal  J.  D.  P.  1914;  with 
a  preface  by  Marshal  Foch.  1919.  A  6963 

This  book  cannot  fail  to  be  a  valuable  source 
book  for  the  campaigns  of  the  first  year  of  the 
war.  We  get  the  commander's  own  story  of  the 
retreat  on  the  Marne,  the  battles  of  the  Aisne  and 
of  Ypres,  of  his  stand  against  Kitchener's  oppo- 
sition, and  of  Joffre's  achievements. 

Huidekoper,  Frederick  L.  33rd  Division, 
A.  E.  F.,  from  its  arrival  in  France,  until 
the  armistice  with  Germany,  November  11, 
1918.  1919.  f  A  6981 

Nevinson,  Henry  W.  The  Dardanelles  cam- 
paign. 1919.  A  6964 

A  well-balanced  and  clearly  written  treatise  on 
the  failure  at  the  Dardanelles  by  an  authority,  a 
war  correspondent  of  many  years'  standing  with  a 
desire  for  strict  adherence  to  the  facts. 

Reece,  Robert  H.  Night  bombing  with  the 
Bedouins,  by  one  of  the  squadron.  1919. 

A  6959 

History  of  the  aeroplane  in  wartime,  as  well  as 
a  story  of  the  international  unit,  the  Bedouins, 
and  of  the  men  who  composed  it. 

Russell,  Charles  Edward.  After  the  whirl- 
wind; a  book  of  reconstruction  and  profit- 
able thanksgiving.  1919.  A  6960 
Deals  mainly  with  the  causes  and  after-effects  of 
the  war.  It  condemns  the  competitive  system  on 
the  basis  that  the  results  have  been  universally 
disastrous,  and  presents  a  plea  for  co-operation  and 
industrial  democracy. 

United  States.  Dept.  of  State.  Diplomatic 
correspondence  between  the  United  States 
and  Germany,  August  .1,  1914-April  6, 
1917;  ed.  by  James  Brown  Scott.  1918. 

A  6958 

Washburn,  Slater.  One  of  the  Yankee  divi- 
sion. 1919.  A  6961 

Trench-life  commonplaces  of  the  101st  field  artil- 
lery, with  descriptions  of  action  at  Chateau  Thierry 
and  the  Marne,  make  an  interesting  book. 

Y.  W.  C.  A.  War  Work  Council  National 
Board.  Young  Women's  Christian  Associa- 
tion overseas.  1919.  A  6965

Hacobian,  A.  P.  Armenia  and  the  war;  an 
Armenian's  point  of  view  with  an  appeal 
to  Britain  and  the  coming  peace  confer- 
ence. With  a  preface  by  Viscount  Bryce. 

1917.  A  4845 
Pasdermadjian,  G.    Why  Armenia  should  be 

free;  Armenia's  role  in  the  present  war. 

1918.  A  6966 

Siberia  and  the  Far  East 

Ackerman,  Carl  W.  Trailing  the  Bolshe- 
viki;  twelve  thousand  miles  with  the  Al- 
lies in  Siberia.  1919.  A  4801 
The  greater  part  of  the  book  assumes  that  Bol- 
shevism is  a  failure  in  Russia  and  that  outside 
of  Russia  it  is  nothing  but  socialism.  A  valu- 
able feature  is  its  analysis  of  the  extent  of  Japanese 
influence  in  Siberia.  Full  of  good  news  stories 
and  reminiscences. 

Millard,  Thomas  F.  Democracy  and  the 
Eastern  question;  the  problem  of  the  Far 
East  as  demonstrated  by  the  great  war, 
and  its  relation  to  the  United  States  of 
America.  1919.  A  4828 

The  author  is  editor  of  Millard's  Review,  a 
magazine  published  in  China,  and  of  vigorously  anti- 
Japanese  sympathies. 

Mexico  and  South  America 

Gibbon,  Thomas  Edward.  Mexico  under 
Carranza;  a  lawyer's  indictment  of  the 
crowning  infamy  of  four  hundred  years  of 
misrule.  1919.  B  4840 

This  volume  is  an  arraignment  of  Carranza  and 
the  entire  class  of  Latin-Mexicans,  placing  the  sole 
hope  of  the  natives  in  United  States  intervention. 
It  dwells  especially  upon  the  blessing  of  foreign 
capital. 

Sweet,  William  Warren.  A  history  of  Latin 
America.  1919.  B  4960 

The  story  of  Latin  America  with  emphasis  laid 
on  its  old-world  foundations  and  carried  down  to 
its  present-day  domestic  conditions  and  foreign  re- 
lations. Useful  for  college  classes  and  of  in- 
terest to  tke  general  reader.

federate   States    of    America,  1862-1865.

Randolph,  John,*a«rf  Nicholas,  Robert  Car-

of  Virginia.  1919.  B  5714,32 

Reprint  of  Virginia  and  the  revolution;  two

Stahl,  John  M.  The  battle  of  Plattsburg;  a 
study  in  and  of  the  war  of  1812.  To  re- 
mind our  troops  of  the  actions  of  their 
brave  countrymen.  1918.  B  2478 

Mr.  Stahl's  account  of  the  battle  of  Plattsburg, 
fought  on  land  by  raw  troops  against  Wellington  s 
veterans;  fought  on  the  waters  of  Lake  Champlain 
by  men  who  had  themselves  hewn  their  ships  from 
the  primeval  forests,  is  worthy  its  subject, — a 
story  of  unsurpassed  effort  and  valor — and  sig- 
nificance.— Boston  Transcript.

Drawing  a  moral  from  the  capture  of  Wash-

Maugham,  W.  Somerset.  The  moon  and

Tompkins,  Juliet  Wilbor.  The  starling.

A  charming  story  of  a  professor's  daughter  who,

The  folly  of  unequal  marriages  is  very  realistically

YOUNG  PEOPLE'S  BOOKS 

Browne,  E.  Gordon.  Little  Dwarf  Nose  and 
The  magic  whistle.  1916.  H  7585 

Darrow,  Floyd  L.  The  boys'  own  book  of 
great  inventions.  1918.  K  22448 

Takes  up  the  story  of  a  few  of  the  greatest  in- 
ventions, tracing  their  influence  upon  world  prog- 
ress and  describing  simple  experiments  for  home 
laboratory  work.  It  will  appeal  decidedly  to  the 
boy  of  a  scientific  turn  of  mind.  Ages  12-16. 

Lucia,    Rose.      Peter    and    Polly    in    winter. 

1914.  H  4803 

This    is    the    story    of    Peter    and    Polly's    daily 


life  in  the  country.  There  is  a  map  showing  the 
hill  where  they  found  the  Christmas  tree,  also 
the  house  where  they  live,  and  many  other  places. 
Seven  and  eight  year  old  children  will  enjoy  the 
conversational  style  in  which  the  story  is  written. 

Smith,  Gertrude.  The  stories  of  Peter  and 
Ellen.  1903.  H  6557 

A  ^record  of  Peter  and  Ellen's  busy,  happy 
days  in  which  the  making  of  mud  pies  was  one  of 
their  greatest  delights.  The  large,  black  type 
and  many  illustrations  make  it  enjoyable  for  little 
people.  Ages  5—7. 

Stein,  Evaleen.  Our  little  Celtic  cousin  of 
long  ago;  being  the  story  of  Ferdiad,  a 
boy  of  Ireland,  in  the  time  of  Brian  Boru. 
1918.  H  6626 

The  little  cousins  of  long  ago  series. 
Brian   Boru    was    an    early    Irish   king   who    lived 
about  the  year   1000. 

In  the  Thomas  Hughes  Room 

Bosschere,  Jean  de.  Folk  tales  of  Flanders. 
An  interesting  collection  of  the  popular  fables 
and  legends  of  Flanders.  Some  of  the  tales  are 
very  primitive  and  crude,  but  they  have  the  fresh- 
ness and  strength  of  new  things.  The  illustrations 
in  vivid  colors  and  black  and  white  are  most  at- 
tractive, Ages  8-12. 


Garnett,  Louise  Ayres. 
1918. 


The  merrymakers. 


Jolly  rhymes,  in  which  well-known  Mother  Goose 
characters  appear  at  most  unexpected  places.  The 
illustrations,  suggesting  joy  of  childhood  and  full 
of  action,  will  hold  the  attention  of  the  little  tot 
too  young  to  read  the  rhymes.  Ages  4—6.

The  Book  Bulletin 


of  the 


Volume  9  November,  1919  No.  9 

Children's  Book  Week 

November  10  to  15 

AMERICAN  librarians  have  spent  much  time  and  effort  in  developing  and 
enforcing  the  theory  that  one  of  the  most  potent  influences  in  the  for- 
mation of  character  is  found  in  a  child's  reading.  They  have  insisted 
upon  the  importance  of  such  commonplace  ingredients  as  sanity,  probability, 
honest  and  realizable  ideals.  They  enforced  the  truth  of  their  contention  by 
pointing  to  the  wonderful  and  enduring  success  of  such  books  as  those  of 
Louisa  May  Alcott,  who  broke  through  her  own  straight-laced  environment  and 
performed  the  very  divine  feat  of  creating  the  first  human  family  in  American 
juvenile  literature — a  most  ordinary  but  a  most  human  family!  They  denied 
that  books  had  either  to  be  written  down  or  "spiced  up"  for  youthful  compre- 
hension and  dwelt  upon  the  fact  that  the  greatest  children's  books  of  all  time 
were  not  written  for  children  at  all  but  for  their  elders,  viz. :  Arabian  Nights, 
King  Arthur,  Pilgrim's  Progress,  Robinson  Crusoe,  Gulliver.  The  study  of  chil- 
dren's literature  and  the  rights  of  children  in  public  libraries  became  one  of  the 
major  specialties  in  American  library  administration.  Theorizing  presently  gave 
place  to  experiment,  observation,  and  practical  demonstration.  Teachers 
were  easily  led  to  investigate  the  new  doctrine  and  as  easily  convinced  of  its 
essential  truth.  Parents,  contrasting  the  memories  of  the  literary  pabulum  of 
their  own  childhood  with  some  of  the  products  of  the  new  era,  saw  and  ap- 


ISO  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

proved  the  difference.  And  now  the  makers  and  sellers  of  books  have  not  only 
fallen  in  with  the  movement,  but  have  taken  their  proper  place  at  its  head  by 
instituting  the  Children's  Book  Week,  during  which  they,  in  common  with  li- 
brarians, teachers,  and  parents,  will  take  thought  of  Good  Books  for  Children. 
Every  bookshop  will  make  special  displays  of  children's  books.  Conferences 
and  lectures  will  be  organized;  descriptive  and  selected  lists  adapted  to  every 
age  will  be  compiled.  All  persons  charged  with  responsibilities  for  the  reading 
tastes  and  habits  of  a  child  or  group  of  children  will  welcome  the  opportun- 
ity to  survey  the  results  of  the  campaign  as  they  appear  on  the  tables  of  the 
bookseller  or  the  shelves  of  the  library.  And  not  the  least  among  those  inter- 
ested will  be  the  child  of  the  eighties  and  nineties,  who  will  behold  with  won- 
der the  great  advances  that  have  come  in  the  methods  and  ideals  of  those  who 
make  books  for  children. 

The  recognition  of  the  human  child  in  literature  is  a  matter  of  relatively 
recent  accomplishment.  Both  as  hero,  or  heroine,  in  current  juvenile  stories,  and 
as  the  reader  thereof,  he  or  she  has  been  long  in  arriving.  The  present  gener- 
ation of  adults  must  still  recall  the  hopelessly  perfect  little  prigs  whose  perform- 
ances, either  in  sheer  impossible  goodness  or  in  overcoming  all  foes,  animate 
and  inanimate,  or  in  achieving  fame  and  fortune  with  incredible  speed,  were  the 
staple  of  every  childish  tale.  Aside  from  these  there  were  for  the  boy  only 
the  forbidden  atrocities  of  the  dime  novel,  and  for  the  girl  the  pale  romances 
diluted  from  the  formulas  of  the  "family  story  paper." 

The  turning  point  came  when  writers  of  American  juvenile  stories  began 
to  produce  Good  Books  for  and  about  human  children  instead  of  merely  books 
for  and  about  Good  Children,  and  with  the  hastening  of  that  day  the  public  li- 
braries of  America  had  a  good  deal  to  do. 

The  Public  Library,  therefore,  joins  with  great  pleasure  in  the  plans  for 
the  proper  observance  of  Children's  Book  Week.  In  every  branch  library,  as 
well  as  in  the  main  building,  displays  of  suggestive  lists  and  of  recommended 
books  have  been  organized.  The  Central  Library  display  is  installed  in  the  cor- 
ridor on  the  fourth  floor  adjoining  the  Thomas  Hughes  room,  which  is  the  head- 
quarters of  our  children's  department.  Visitors  are  invited,  and  opportunity 
will  be  given  for  the  discussion  of  problems  or  the  inspection  of  groups  of  books 
suited  to  various  ages,  tastes,  or  conditions.  Conferences  between  parents, 
teachers,  and  librarians  will  be  organized  if  desired.  While  the  library  is,  of 
course,  at  all  times  prepared  to  offer  advice  and  expert  assistance  in  matters  of 
book  selection,  the  opportunity  offered  by  Children's  Book  Week  will  be  made 
the  occasion  for  particular  emphasis  upon  this  phase  of  our  service. 

Attention  is  also  called  to  the  lists  printed  on  the  succeeding  pages.  They 
are  offered  merely  as  specimens  and  are  neither  complete  nor  exhaustive.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.   *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

...     PHILOSOPHY 

Psychology 

Blackford,  Katherine  M.  H.,  and  Newcomb, 
Arthur,  ed.  Character  analysis  by  the 
observational  method.  1919.  lOv. 

L 13762 

v.  1.  Introduction. — Color. 

2.  Form. — Size  and  structure. 

3.  Texture,    consistency    and    flexibility. — Pro- 
portion. 

4.  Proportion   (con.). 

5.  Expression. 

6.  Condition. — The  features. 

7.  The  hand  and  the  foot. — Interpreting  com- 
binations of  variations. 

8.  Sample    analysis. — Disease,   crime,    insanity, 
and  ether  deficiencies. 

9.  Marital     and     family     relations.— Character 
analysis  in  salesmanship. — Vocational   guidance. 

10.     Scientific  employment. 


Nicoll,  Maurice. 


Dream  psychology.     1917. 
L  13650 


Oxford   medical   publications. 

An  English  army  surgeon's  explanation  of  dreams 
from  the  non-Freudian  point  of  view  of  the  Swiss 
psychologists. 


Spiritualism 

Barker,  Elsa.  Last  letters  from  the  living 
dead  man,  with  an  introduction.  1919. 

L  10091 

The  author  states  that  these  spiritualistic  mes- 
sages are  from  Judge  David  P.  Hatch,  of  Los 
Angeles,  Feb.,  1917-Feb.,  1918.  The  book  is  a  com- 
panion volume  to  the  author's  Letters  from  a  living 
dead  man  (1914),  L  10102,  and  War  letters  from 
the  living  dead  man  (1915),  L  10093. 

Hubbell,  Walter.  The  great  Amherst  mys- 
tery; a  true  narrative  of  the  supernatural. 
1916.  L 13394 

A  minute  description  of  spirit  manifestations  con- 
nected with  a  haunted  house  in  Amlierst,  Nova 
Scotia,  in  1878  and  1879. 

Hyslop,  James  H.  Contact  with  the  other 
world;  the  latest  evidence  as  to  communi- 
cation with  the  dead.  1919.  L  13538 
A  history  of  the  growth  of  modern  spiritualism. 
The  problems  which  face  the  scientist  are  clearly 
and  fairly  stated,  and  Dr.  Hyslop  has  left  the 
reader  with  an  appreciation  of  the  efforts  being 
made  to  explain  psychic  phenomena  and  also  with 
an  interest  in  the  subject  as  a  field  for  further 
exploration. 

Watson,  Albert  Durrant.  The  twentieth 
plane;  a  psychic  revelation.  1919.  L  13504 
The  author  is  president  of  the  Canadian  Psychical 
Research  Association,  and  vouches  for  the  integrity 
of  his  medium.  The  "20th  plane  publication  com- 
mittee" responsible  for  the  book  consists  of  Lincoln, 
Emerson,  Whitman,  and  Ingersoll.  Coleridge  dedi- 
cates the  book  to  the  heroes  of  the  war  and  names 
some  of  the  synthetic  foods  enjoyed  by  dwellers 
on  the  plane,  while  Alfred_Russel  Wallace  describes 
the  process  of  communication. 


RELIGION 

Bousset,  W.    Jesus.     1911.  M  7123 

Crown  theological  library. 

A  discussion  of  the  historicity  problem. 

Carroll,  Robert  S.  The  soul  in  suffering;  a 
practical  application  of  spiritual  truths. 
1919.  L 14099 

It  stands  for  medical  ideals  reaching  out  to  Chris- 
tian ideals,  that  one  of  the  greatest  problems  of 
human  existence — the  problem  of  suffering,  which 
through  the  ages  has  been  so  pitiably  solved  by  the 
many — may  be  a  bit  more  worthily  met  by  all  helpers 
of  mankind. — Introduction. 

Hunter,  Robert.  Why  we  fail  as  Christians. 
1919.  M  9216 

A  searching  attempt  to  find  and  to  proclaim  a 
truly  Christian  rule  of  ideal  and  of  practice  in  the 
field  of  economic  adjustment  .  .  .  The  strik- 
ing feature  is  its  analysis  of  Tolstoy's  attempt  to 
live  the  truly  Christian  life  and  his  failure. — 
Survey. 

McGarrah,  Albert  F.  Modern  church  man- 
agement; a  study  in  efficiency.  1917. 

M9514 

Petre,  M.  D.  Modernism;  its  failures  and 
its  fruits.  1918.  M  7711 

The  London  Times  calls  this  book,  whose  author 
is  a  friend  of  Tyrrell,  "the  obituary  notice  of  a 
movement  which  has  been  extinguished  by  au- 
thority." 

Salter,  William.  Sixty  years,  and  other  dis- 
courses, with  reminiscences.  1907. 

M4837 


History   of   Religion 

Bartlet,  J.  Vernon,  and  Carlyle,  A.  J.  Chris- 
tianity in  history;  a  study  of  religious  de- 
velopment. 1917.  M  7466 

An  attempt  to  set  forth  the  genesis  and  growth 
of  some  typical  forms  which  Christianity  has 
assumed  under  the  various  secular  influences  to 
which  it  has  been  exposed.  Accurate  and  scholarly. 
— London  Times. 


Moore,  George  Foot. 
1913-19.    2v. 


History  of  religions. 
M  3723,  16 


Professor  Moore  of  Harvard  is  one  of  the  most 
scholarly  authorities  in  this  country  on  comparative 
religion  and  the  history  of  religions.  The  present 
work  (whose  second  volume  was  delayed  by  the 
war)  includes  the  study  of  (vol.  1)  China,  Japan, 
Egypt,  Babylonia,  Assyria,  India,  Persia,  Greece, 
and  Rome,  and  (vol.  2)  of  Judaism,  Christianity, 
an>d  Mohammedanism. 

Non-Christian    Religions 

Hess,  Moses.  Rome  and  Jerusalem;  a  study 
in  Jewish  nationalism.  1918.  A  2852 

King,  Leonard  W.  Legends  of  Babylon  and 
Egypt  in  relation  to  Hebrew  tradition. 
1918.  M  7964 

Contents:  Egypt,  Babylon,  and  Palestine,  and 
some  traditional  origins  of  civilization. — Deluge 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


157 


stories  and  the  new  Sumerian  version. — Creation 
and  the  dragon  myth ;  and  the  problem  of  Babylo- 
nian parallels  in  Hebrew  tradition. 

Koran.  The  Holy  Qur-an;  containing  the 
Arabic  text  with  English  translation  and 
commentary,  by  Maulvi  Muhammad  Ali. 
1917.  M8112 

SOCIOLOGY 

Bevan,  Edwyn.  German  social  democracy 
during  the  war.  1918.  L  18487 

An  English  historian's  account  of  the  position  of 
the  German  Social  Democratic  party  and  its  leaders 
in  August,  1914,  and  how  this  was  affected  by  the 
fortunes  of  war  during  the  first  three  years. 

Gretton,  R.  H.  The  English  middle  class. 
1917.  L  15921 

With  judgment  and  a  due  sense  of  proportion  the 
middle  class  is  defined  as  "that  portion  of  the  com- 
munity to  which  money  is  the  primary  condition 
and  primary  instrument  of  life.  Its  history  is 
followed  to  the  present  from  the  time  of  the  mer- 
chant guilds  in  the  llth  and  12th  centuries. 

Higginson,  John  Hedley.  Tariffs  at  work; 
an  outline  of  practical  tariff  administra- 
tion, with  special  reference  to  the  United 
States  and  Canada.  1913.  L  17790 

Reconstruction 

Canadian  National  Reconstruction  Groups. 

The  problems  of  national  reconstruction; 

a  summary.     1918.  L  16646 

Lindsay,   Samuel   McCune,   ed.     War  labor 

policies    and    reconstruction.      1919. 

L  7286,  8,  II 

Proceedings  of  the  Academy  of  political  science  in 
the  city  of  New  York. 

A  series  of  addresses  and  papers  presented  at  the 
Labor  Reconstruction  Conference  held  under  the 
auspices  of  the  Academy  of  political  science  in  the 
city  of  New  York,  December  6-7,  1918. 

Lippincott,  Isaac.  Problems  of  reconstruc- 
tion. 1919.  L 16669 
The  study  is  thorough  and  comprehensive  and  a 
valuable  addition  to  reconstruction  literature.  The 
title,  however,  is  not  a  true  index  of  the  contents 
of  the  book,  for  fully  two-thirds  of  it  is  devoted  to 
an  outline  of  war  control. — American  Economic 
Review. 

Mprman,  James  B.  The  place  of  agriculture 
in  reconstruction;  a  study  of  national 
programs  of  land  settlement.  1919. 

L 17498 

The  author  thinks  that  only  those  soldiers  should 
be  settled  at  once  upon  the  land  who  have  had 
previous  agricultural  experience  or  show  special 
aptitude  for  and  interest  in  farm  life.  His  careful 
study  ef  settlement  programs  is  as  pertinent  for 
normal  periods  of  transition  as  for  the  readjust- 
ment following  upon  the  war.

L  16643 

Mr.  Frank  presents  the  point  of  view  of  the 
modern  business  man  and  his  attempt  not  only  to 
solve  the  present  unrest,  but  to  introduce  a  con- 
structive policy  that  will  save  industry  from  radical 
bolshevistic  schemes.  He  does  not  advocate  govern- 
ment control  but  "the  organization  of  industry  and 
business  on  a  more  representative  and  democratic 
basis."
