Mallock,  W.  H.  Capital,  war,  and  wages; 
three  questions  in  outline.  1918.  L  16744 

The  first  of  these  two  papers  relates  to  the  con- 
scription of  capital  for  war  purposes;  the  second 
concludes  that  the  future  welfare  of  both  capital  and 
labor  will  result  from  an  increased  production 
brought  about  by  the  continuous  use  and  improve- 
ment of  machine-capital  and  by  the  development 
of  alertness  and  energy  in  wage-earners  by  the  in- 
ducement of  graded  rewards.

Farrington,  Edward  I.  Practical  rabbit  keep-

LITERATURE 

Ford,  J.  D.  M.  Main  currents  of  Spanish 
literature.  1919.  J  5491 

Contents.:  The  heroic  tradition:  the  epic. — The 
heroic  tradition:  the  ballad. — Cervantes:  the  man 
and  his  work. — Tie  rise  of  the  drama  and  its 
triumph  in  the  golden  age:  Lope  de  Vega. — The 
culmination  of  the  dramatic  movement:  Calderon. — 
Lyric  poetry. — The  novel. — High  points  of  Spanish- 
American  literature. 

For  the  general  reader,  who  does  not  desire  ex- 
haustive treatment,  thjs  will  furnish  an  adequate 
survey.  For  more  comprehensive  discussions  see 
Fitzmaurice-Kelly's  History  of  Spanish  literature, 
J  5496,  and  Chapters  on  Spanish  literature,  J  5497, 
and  Coester's  Literary  History  of  Spanish  America, 
J  5483. 

Fowler,  W.  Warde.  The  death  of  Turnus; 
observations  on  the  twelfth  book  of  the 
-/Eneid.  1919.  L  2859 

Contains  Latin  text. 
Lynd,  Robert.     Old  and  new  masters.     1919. 

J4809 

Contents:  Dostqevsky  the  sensationalist. — Jane 
Austen,  natural  historian. — Mr.  G.  K.  Chesterton 
and  Mr.  Hilaire  Belloc. — Wordsworth. — Keats. — 
Henry  James. — Browning,  the  poet  of  love. — The 
fame  of  J.  M.  Synge. — Villon,  the  genius  of  the 
tavern. — Pope. — James  Elroy  Flecker. — Turgenev. — 
The  madness  of  Strindberg. — "The  prince  of  French 
poets." — Rossetti  and  ritual. — Mr.  Bernard  Shaw. — 
Mr.  Masefield's  secret. — Mr.  W.  B.  Yeats — Tchek- 
hov,  the  perfect  story-teller. — Lady  Gregory. — Mr. 
Cunninghame  Graham. — Swinburne. — The  work  of 
T.  M.  Kettle.— Mr.  J.  C.  Squire.— Mr.  Joseph  Con- 
rad.— Mr.  Rudyard  Kipling. — Mr.  Thomas  Hardy. 

He  sketches  a  swift  portrait  in  a  few  trenchant 
phrases,  epigrammatic  and  biting.  This  method 
has  its  obvious  faults.  One  of  them  is  that  it 
scarcely  allows  time  or  space  for  a  variety  of  im- 
pressions. The  virtue  of  the  method  is  that  it 
leaves  a  lasting  picture  on  the  brain. — Carl  van 
Vechten  in  Chicago  Daily  News. 

Machen,  Arthur.  Hieroglyphics;  a  note  upon 
ecstacy  in  literature.  1912.  J4S11 

Comments  on  literature  and  the  standards  by 
which  it  is  to  be  judged.  The  author  finds  in 
ecstasy  "the  touchstone  which  will  infallibly  sepa- 
rate the  higher  and  lower  in  literature." 

Magnus,  Laurie.  A  general  sketch  of  Euro- 
pean literature  in  the  centuries  of 
romance.  1918.  J  4810 

From  the  twelfth  century  to  the  year  1637. 
The  mass  of  knowledge  of  which  he_  disposes, 
if  nowhere  amounting  to  specialism,  is  in  the  ag- 
gregate extraordinarily  copious  and  varied ;  and 
he  handles  it  with  an  agility  of  mind,  an  open- 
ness to  impressions  and  a  deftness  in  seizing  salient 
points  which  make  his  book  constantly  fresh  and 
informing. — London  Times. 

Moore,  George.  Avowals.  1919.  E  4649 
A  book  for  the  literary  reader  written  in  beau- 
tiful studied  prose,  much  of  it  in  the  form  of 
imaginary  conversations.  Subjects  of  discussion 
are  the  achievements  of  English  prose  narrative, 
the  censorship  of  literature,  Tolstoy,  Kipling,  Pater, 
Shakespeare  _  and  Balzac,  and  Moore's  experiences 
and  associations  with  literary  Paris. 

Williams,  Harold.  Modern  English  writers: 
being  a  study  of  imaginative  literature, 
1890-1914.  1918.  .  J6375 

A  very  comprehensive  work  covering  almost  two 
hundre_d  recent  names  and  thus  in  many  instances 
supplying  reference  material  inaccessible  elsewhere. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


161 


Poetry 

Bridges,  Robert.  The  necessity  of  poetry. 
1918.  E  10663 

Poetry  is  necessary  not  as  a  substitute  for  religion 
in  cultivated  minds,  but  as  the  only  possible  ex- 
pression of  a  certain  order  of  ideas  which  are  essen- 
tial to  the  integrity  of  our  mental  life.  It  is, 
therefore,  literally  necessary  to  every  man,  and  not 
least  to  the  intelligent  workingman,  whom  Mr. 
Bridges  is  here  addressing. — Nation. 

Davies,    William    H.      Forty    new    poems. 

1918.  E  10875 
Seven    out    of    his    forty    new    poems    touch    upon 

the  war,  and  these  seven  only  make  more  evident 
his  abstraction  from  it.  As  a  poet,  he  is  still  on 
the  tramp,  remote,  .unfriended,  solitary,  taking  his 
own  thoughts  down  his  own  road,  glad  in  the  chil- 
dren, the  birds  and  the  flowers. — London  Times. 

Knish,  Anne,  and  Morgan,  Emanuel,  pseuds, 
of  A.  D.  Ficke  and  IV.  Bynner.  Spectra ;  a 
book  of  poetic  experiments.  1916. 

E  10914 

One  of  the  most  successful  hoaxes  that  ever 
startled  the  literary  world.  Even  now,  three  years 
after  its  publication,  the  critics  are  not  agreed  as  to 
just  which  poems,  if  any,  are  intended  seriously. 

Locchi,  Vittorio.    The  feast  of  Saint  Gorizia. 

1919.  El  1209 
The  jewels  of  the  eroica. 

English  and  Italian  text  on  opposite  pages. 

Lowes,  John  Livingston.  Convention  and 
revolt  in  poetry.  1919.  E  11230 

A  delightful  and  penetrating  study  of  the  fun- 
damentals of  poetry.  The  author  holds  no  brief 
either  for  established  or  radical  poetical  forms. 
He  feels  that  while  the  history  of  poetry  has  been 
but  a  succession  of  revolts,  the  greatest  poetry  has 
been  written  by  revolters  who  have  infused  new 
life  into  forms  endeared  by  tradition. 

Smith,  C.  Fox.  Sailor  town;  sea  songs  and 
ballads.  1919.  E 11545 

Small  craft;  sailor  ballads  and  chantys. 

1919.  E  11546 

Stirring  and  salty  chanteys.  Miss  Smith'  catches 
and  keeps  the  tone  of  sailors'  personality  with  ex- 
traordinarily masculine  vigor,  and  her  verse  is 
ready  either  to  move  with  a  steady  breeze  or  to 
tack  and  shift  effectively  as  occasion  .  requires. — 
Nation. 

Stevenson,  Robert  Louis.  New  poems  and 
variant  readings.  1918.  E  11550 

The  surprising  additions  are  many  love-poems  and 
other  self-revealing  pieces  that  we  might,  by  a 
process  _of  internal  criticism,  find  appropriate  dates 
for  in  his  biography.  It  would  have  been  a  calamity' 
to  lose  this  unexpected  salvage. — Athenaum. 

Thomas,   Edward.     Last  poems.      1918. 

E8733 

No  other  English  poet  has  expressed  in  a  quietude 
more  profound  the  longing  for  home,  the  passion  for 
solitude.  Ncne  has  left  pictured  more  truly  that 
remoter,  changeless  England  which  for  those  who 
have  fought  and  suffered  should  be  the  inheritance 
of  their  peace. — London  Times. 

Trench,  Herbert.  Poems,  with  fables  in 
prose.  1918.  E  8696 

_His  distinction  is  drawn  from  the  quality  in  which 
Pindar  and  Virgil  are  preeminent,  from  style  as 
Matthew  Arnold  used  the  word,  a  peculiar  height- 
ening, a  happy  excitement,  an  intoxication  derived 


from  the  beauty  of  one's  own  voice,  which  all 
musical  poets  share  with  thrushes  and  nightingales. 
— London  Times. 

Turner,  W.  J.    The  dark  fire.     1918.    E  8719 

The  author  of  these  poems  possesses  a  remark- 
able and  distinctive  talent.  There  is  an  almost  un- 
canny strength  in  his  work.  He  expresses  the 
strangeness  of  strange  places  as  no  poet  since  Cole- 
ridge has  done. 

Wilkinson,  Marguerite.  New  voices;  an  in- 
troduction to  contemporary  poetry.  1919. 

E  11705 

A  well  arranged  anthology  which  groups  its  il- 
lustrative poems  and  comments  under  the  head- 
ings of  technique  and  spirit  of  contemporary  poetry. 
It  is  critically  generous  and  most  useful  to  the 
average  reader,  perhaps,  for  its  inclusiveness. — 
Booklist. 

Drama 

Bennett,  Arnold.  Judith;  a  play  in  three 
acts,  founded  on  the  apocryphal  book  of 
Judith.  1919.  E 13490 

Mr.  Bennett  brightens  up  an  ancient  theme  with 
a  few  references  to  the  war  and  some  simple  fun. 
Such  words  as  "handmaid"  and  "bondman  attest 
the  Biblical  origin  of  the  play,  and  the  second 
person  singular  is  used  throughout — sometimes  with 
painful  results. — Athenaum. 

Colby,  Elbridge.  Early  American  comedy. 
1919.  E  11940 

Drinkwater,  John.  Abraham  Lincoln;  a  play, 
with  an  introduction  by  Arnold  Bennett. 
1919.  E 12018 

This  is  a  one-act  play  written  by  an  Englishman 
and  has  had  a  phenomenally  long  and  successful 
run  in  London.  It  may  not  seem  convincing  to 
Americans  as  there  has  been  no  attempt  to  repro- 
duee  American  setting  or  idiom,  but  the  character 
of  Lincoln  stands  out  in  heroic  relief.  The  form 
is  dignified  prose  relieved  by  lyric  choruses  in  the 
style  of  Greek  tragedy. 

O'Neill,  Eugene  G.  The  moon  of  the  Carib- 
bees,  and  six  other  plays  of  the  sea.  1919. 

E  14168 

Contents:  The  moon  of  the  Caribbees. — Bound 
east  for  Cardiff. — The  long  voyage  home. — In  the 
zone. — He. — Where  the  cross  is  made. — The  rope. 

One-act  plays  of  rough  brutalized  seamen  and 
of  lower  phases  of  the  life  of  those  who  follow 
the  sea.  The  characters  are  well  worked  out,  the 
dialogue  racy  and  arising  out  of  character,  and  the 
author  has  a  remarkable  quality  for  creating 
•  dramatic  tension  and  atmosphere. — Open  Shelf. 

Pearse,  Padraic.  The  singer,  and  other 
plays.  1918.  E 14188 

Contents:  The  singer. — The  king. — The  master. 
— losagan  (tr.  by  Joseph  Campbell). 

Symons,  Arthur.  Studies  in  the  Eliza- 
bethan drama.  1919.  E  12874 
Ten  of  these  essays,  written  by  the  well-known 
critic  in  the  last  years  of  the  nineteenth  and  the 
early  years  of  the  twentieth  century,  are  on  Shake- 
speare, and  three  others  are  on  Massinger,  Day,  and 
Middleton  and  Rowley. 

Trask,  Katrina.  Without  the  walls;  a  read- 
ing play.  1919.  E  12933 
A  drama  of  the  time  of  Christ,  showing  His  in- 
fluence on  various  typical  characters.  The  action 
centers  in  a  Jewish  girl's  struggle  between  her  duty 
to  her  Phansee  father  and  her  love  for  a  young 
Roman  noble.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Essays 

Clemens,  Samuel  L.  (Mark  Twain').  The 
curious  republic  of  Gondour,  and  other 
whimsical  sketches.  1919.  ^  E2239 

Contents:  The  curious  republic  of  Gondour. — 
A  memory. — Introductory  to  Memoranda. — About 
smells. — A  couple  of  sad  experiences. — Dan  Murphy. 
— The  tournament  in  A.  D.  1870. — Curious  relics  for 
sale. — A  reminiscence  of  the  back  settlements. — 
A  royal  compliment. — The  approaching  epidemic. — 
The  tone-imparting  committee. — Goldsmith's  friend 
abroad  again. — Our  precious  lunatic. — The  European 
war. — The  wild  man  interviewed. — Last  words  of 
great  men. 

Dicey,  A.  V.  The  statesmanship  of  Words- 
worth; an  essay.  1917.  E  11685 

Galsworthy,  John.  Addresses  in  America, 
1919.  E  3073 

Seven  addresses  delivered  by  the  well-known 
novelist  and  dramatist  in  his  recent  visit  to  the 
United  States.  The  underlying  theme  in  all  of 
them  is  the  future  unity  of  England  and  America 
and  the  part  this  union  is  to  play  in  reconstruction. 

Lucas,  E.  V.     'Twixt  eagle  and  dove.     1918. 

E4361 

The  first  of  these  delig'htful  essays  recalls  the 
peaceful  state  of  the  world  in  1859  when  the  Ger- 
man emporer  was  born.  The  others  include  sketches 
of  town  and  country  in  England,  and  a  few  are 
on  the  themes  suggested  by  the  war. 

BIOGRAPHY 
Collected  Biography 

Smith,  David  Nichol,  ed.  Characters  from 
the  histories  and  memoirs  of  the  seven- 
teenth century,  with  an  essay  on  the  char- 
acter and  historical  notes.  1918.  C  19989 
Calls  attention  to  the  interest  of  the  seventeenth 
century  in  personality  and  studies  of  human  life 
as  reflected  in  biography  and  history. — Dial. 

The  word  character  is  used  throughout  in  the 
sense  of  character  sketch  or  literary  portrait. 

Individual  Biography 

Dickens.  Charles.  Ley,  J.  W.  T.  The  Dick- 
ens circle,  a  narrative  of  the  novelist's 
friendships.  1919.  C  21607 

This  circle  of  Dickens'  correspondents  consisted 
mainly  of  authors,  artists,  actors  and  lawyers. 
There  is  no  suggestion  of  a  political  atmosphere  to 
mar  the  essentially  literary  quality  of  the  book. 

Foch,  Ferdinand.  Puaux,  Rene.  Marshal 
Foch,  his  life,  his  work,  his  faith.  1918. 

C  21945 

Foster,  George  Burman.  A  memorial  service 
at  the  University  of  Chicago.  1919. 

C  21962 

Geikie,   James.      Newbigin,    Marion    I.,    and 

Flett,  J.   S.     James   Geikie;   the  man   and 

the  geologist.     1917.  C  22071 

Marshall,  John.     Corwin,  Edward   S.     John 

Marshall  and  the  Constitution;  a  chronicle 

of  the  Supreme  court.     1919.       B  5086,  16 

The   chronicles   of  America   series. 


Morris,  William.  Vallance,  Aymer.  Wil- 
liam Morris;  his  art,  his  writings,  and  his 
public  life.  1909.  C  23489 

Redmond,  John.     Wells,  Warre  B.     The  life 

of  John  Redmond.     1919.  C  23901 

An    excellent    character    study    of    a    great    man, 

£  resenting    in    sympathetic    terms    the     history     of 
reland  during  Redmond's  leadership  in  Parliament. 

Menzies,  Amy  Charlotte  (Mrs.  Stuart  Men- 

zies).     Further  indiscretions  by  a  woman 

of  no  importance.     1918.  C  23365 

Continuation    of    the    author's    Memories    discreet 

and  indiscreet,  C  23364. 

Roosevelt,  Theodore.  Bishop,  Joseph  Buck- 
lin,  cd.  Theodore  Roosevelt's  letters  to 
his  children.  19.19.  C  23995 

These  charming  letters  portray  the  delightful  com- 
radeship and  sympathy  that  existed  between  Roose- 
velt and  his  children,  and  cover  the  ten  years  from 
the  Spanish  war  through  his  residence  in  the  White 
House.  The  letters  are  illustrated  with  crude 
sketches  possessing  quite  the  vividness  and  action 
of  Thackeray's,  though  less  of  artistic  merit. 

Hard,    William.      Theodore    Roosevelt, 

a  tribute.     1919.  C  23996 

First  printed  in  the  New  Republic  for  January 
25,  1919,  under  the  title,  Roosevelt  now. 

Lodge,       Henry       Cabot.  Theodore 

Roosevelt.     1919.  *C  23994 

Senator  Lodge's  memorial  address  delivered  be 
fore  Congress,  Feb.  9,  1919. 

FICTION 

Bartlett,  Frederick  Orin.    Joan  &  Co.     1919. 

F 10820 

An  heiress  plays  fairy  godmother  19  a  young  in- 
ventor who  promptly  falls  in  Icve  with  her.  She 
marries  a  man  of  her  own  class  and  is,  we  are  led 
to  suppose,  happy  ever  after. 

Bell,  J.  J.    Just  Jemima.     1919.  F  11019 

The  amusing  experience  of  a  maid  in  a  boarding 
house  at  a  summer  resort.  She  describes  the  pecu- 
liarities of  her  guests  and  their  love  affairs. 

Belloc,  Hilaire.  Emmanuel  Burden,  mer- 
chant of  Thames  St.,  in  the  city  of  Lon- 
don, exporter  of  hardware;  a  record  of 
his  lineage,  speculations,  last  days  and 
death.  1917.  F 10981 

Cannan,  Gilbert.  Mummery,  a  tale  of  three 
idealists.  1919.  F  11670 

A  futurist  scene  painter,  a  dramatist,  and  a  young 
actress,  are  the  three  idealists.  The  story  at- 
tempts to  show  the  conflict  between  idealism  and  our 
commercial  and  social  conventions. 

Deland,  Margaret.  The  promises  of  Alice; 
the  romance  of  a  New  England  parsonage. 
1919.  F 12469 

A  quaint  little  story  about  a  girl  whose  mother 
wanted  her  to  be  a  missionary.  It  shows  the  in- 
domitable power  of  the  New  England  conscience. 

Dell,  Ethel  M.  The  lamp  in  the  desert.   1919. 

F 12461 

A  rather  melodramatic  story  of  India.  It  tells 
how  a  woman  is  punished  for  marrying  a  man  whom 
she  does  not  love. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


163 


De  Morgan,   William.     The   old   madhouse. 
1919.  F 12409 

A  good  mystery  story,  the  last  chapter  of  which 
was  finished  from  the  author's  notes  by  his  wife 
after  his  death.  It  tells  of  the  unaccountable  dis- 
appearance of  the  headmaster  of  a  boys'  school  and 
the  effect  upon  his  family. 

Hay,  James.     The  winning  clue.     1919. 

F 13592 

A  detective  story  centering  about  a  mysterious 
murder  in  which  an  amateur  and  a  professional  de- 
tective attempt  to  run  down  a  clue.  It  has  an 
intricate  plot  which  will  baffle  the  efforts  of  the 
most  resourceful  reader  to  solve  it  in  advance. 


Hergesheimer,    Joseph. 
1919. 


The    happy    end. 
F 13707 


Contents:  Lonely  valleys. — The  Egyptian  chariot. 
— The  flower  of  Spain. — Tol'able  David. — Bread. — 
Rosemary  Roselle. — The  thrush  in  the  hedge. 

Short  stories  which  originally  appeared  in  maga- 
zines. They  are  interesting  in  plot  and  of  fine 
workmanship. 

Jacobs,  W.  W.    Deep  waters.     1919.    F  14317 
Contents:      Shareholders. — Paying    off. — Made    to 

measure. — Sam's  ghost. — Bedridden. — The  convert. — 

Husbandry. — Family    cares. — The    winter    offensive. 

— The    substitute. — Striking   hard. — Dirty   work. 
Stories  of  English  sea-faring  men  told  by  an  old 

sailor    who    relates    his    adventures    and    mishaps. 

Leverage,  Henry.     The  white  cipher.     1919. 

F  14730 

An  interesting  story  of  an  attempt  to  gain  the 
secrets  of  German  dye  formulas  which  are  in  cipher 
form. 

Lynde,  Francis.     David  Vallory.     1919. 

F 14823 

A  young  engineer  has  one  standard  for  business 
and  another  for  private  life.  This  story  tells  of 
his  regeneration  through  the  failure  of  a  great  en- 
gineering enterprise. 

Richardson,  Dorothy  M.     Backwater.     1919. 

F  17151, 2 

The  second  book  in  the  series  of  her  realistic 
novels  entitled  Pilgrimage.  The  first  is  Pointed 
Roofs,  F  17151,  and  the  third,  Honeycomb, 
F  17151,3. 

Sinclair,  May.     Mary  Olivier:  a  life.     1919. 

F 18161 

An  unusual  and  realistic^  biography  of  a  child 
of  abnormal  parents  whose  life  was  a  pitiful  reach- 
ing out  for  everything  that  she  could  not  attain. 

Street,  Julian.  After  thirty.  1919.  F  18415 
The  adventures  of  a  married  man  who,  finding 
life  a  little  stale,  enlivens  things  by  his  friend- 
ships with  different  women  of  his  acquaintance.  He 
gets  into  various  difficulties  from  which  his  patient 
wife  has  to  extricate  him. 

Williamson,  C.  N.,  and  Williamson,  A.  M. 
The  lion's  mouse.  1919.  F  19546 

A  sensational  mystery  story. 

Wodehouse,  Pelham  Grenville.  Their  mu- 
tual child.  1919.  F 19700 

A  satire  on  eugenics  and  the  follies  of  social  life. 
Full  of  humor  and  pleasant  to  read.

adventures.     1919.

For  the  children's  hour  series. 


of  great 
H7380 


Stories  from  the  classics  simplified  for  children. 
The  author  has  imparted  a  certain  charm  in  re- 
telling them  and  they  promise  to  be  as  popular  as 
her  other  stories. 

Chaffee,  Allen.  The  adventures  of  Twinkly 
Eyes,  the  little  black  bear.  1919.  H  7670 
These  tales  are  especially  suitable  for  the  chil- 
dren's bedtime  reading  although  they  may  be  ap- 
preciated by  older  people  for  their  fidelity  to 
natural  science. 

Kelly,  Mrs.  M.  A.  B.     Short  stories  of  our 

shy  neighbors.     1896.  H  4577 

The    wonder    world    of    nature    explained    to    the 

very   young.      Especially    useful   to   teachers   in    the 

primary  grades. 

Knipe,  Emilie  B.,  and  Knipe,  Alden  A.  Vive 
la  France;  a  narrative  founded  on  the  diary 
of  Jeannette  de  Martigny.  1919.  H  4578 
The  story  of  a  young  French  girl's  experiences 
during  the  first  year  of  the  war  in  Reims.  It 
promises  to  be  as  popular  as  the  other  war  stories 
by  the  same  authors. 

Lindsay,  Maud,  and  Poulsson,  Emilie.  The 
joyous  travelers.  1919.  H  4827 

A  group  of  merry  verses  and  fairy  tales  told  in 
an  interesting  way  for  children. 

Mathiews,  Franklin  K.,  ed.  The  boy  scouts 
book  of  stories.  1919.  H  5225 

Contents:  The  great  big  man,  by  O.  Johnson. — 
A  twilight  adventure,  by  M.  D.  Post. — Tad  Sheldon, 
second  class  scout,  by  J.  F.  Wilson. — The  red- 
headed league,  by  A.  C.  Doyle. — The  ransom  of

by  S.  E.  White.— The  devil-fish,  by  N.  Duncan.— 
The  jumping  frog,  by  Mark  Twain. — Bingism,  by 
B.  Tarkington. — Concho  Curly  at  the  op'ra,  by  E. 
B.  Bronson. — The  lie,  by  H.  Hagedorn. — Story  of 
the  bandbox,  by  R.  L.  Stevenson. — The  hero  and 
the  cowboy,  by  J.  C.  Lincoln. — The  dollar,  by  M. 
Robertson. — The  mascot  of  "Troop  I,"  by  S. 
Chalmers. — The  lion's  smile,  by  T.  W.  Hanshew. — 
The  roll-call  of  the  reef,  by  A.  T.  Quiller-Couch. — 
The  house  and  the  brain,  by  E.  Bulwer-Lytton. 

A  collection  of  short  stories  from  the  master 
story-tellers  of  America  and  England.  Although 
written  originally  for  grown-ups  these  stories  are 
interesting  to  boys  in  the  teens.

Georgia.

CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


GOVERNMENT     PUBLICATIONS 
OF  SPECIAL  INTEREST 

Circulating  copies  may  be  issued  on  the  regular 
library  card  in  the  Civics  and  Documents  Depart- 
ment, first  floor,  Randolph  Street  entrance. 

Ayers,  Leonard  P.  The  war  with  Germany; 
a  statistical  summary.  1919.  W2.2  Eu7w 

(U.   S.   War   Dept.) 

A  much  needed  digest  of  American  participation 
with  a  supplement  of  comparisons  with  other  coun- 
tries. Fully  illustrated  by  charts.  Good  for 
schools. 

Bauer,  Stephan.  International  labor  legis- 
lation and'  the  society  of  nations.  1919. 

L2.3254 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Labor  Statistics.  Bulletin 
254.) 

A  translation  of  more  than  timely  interest  on 
the  subject  of  the  "regulation  of  labor  contracts 
through  international  agreements,"  which  indicates 
in  general  the  subjects  that  must  be  dealt  with  by 
the  International  Labor  Conference. 

Bent,   Arthur    C.      Life    histories    of    North 
American  diving  birds.     1919.          SIS. 3  107 
(U.    S.    National    Museum.      Bulletin    107.) 
Interestingly     written     description     of     the     lives, 
habits,  plumage,  nests,  and  behavior  of  these  birds, 
with   many   illustrations   and   colored   plates. 

Brock,  Herman  G.  Boots  and  shoes,  leather, 
and  supplies  in  Argentina,  Uruguay,  and 
Paraguay.  1919.  C18.11  177 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Foreign  and  Domestic  Com- 
merce. Special  agents  ser.  177.) 

Gives  details  of  shoe  business  in  these  coun- 
tries, both  foreign  and  domestic,  emphasizing  op- 
portunities for  American  exporters. 

Brundage,  Frances  F..  Music  in  the  camps. 
1919.  W852M97 

(U.  S.  Commission  on  Training  Camp  Activities. 
War  Dept.) 

The  development  of  music  in  the  army  is  out- 
lined, and  to  it  is  given  great  credit  for  the  splendid 
courage  of  our  men. 

Howard,  Alice  S.    Social  service  in  the  U.  S. 

merchant  marine.  1919.  SB1.2  M35s 

(U.  S.  Shipping  Board.  Recruiting  service.) 
A  brief,  interesting,  and  illustrated  account  of 

the  social  and  educational  work  done  by  the  Social 

Service    Bureau    during   the    war   showing   also   the 

need  for  its  continuance. 

Illick,  Joseph  S.  Pennsylvania  trees.  Rev. 
1919. 

(Penn.    Dept.    of    Forestry.      Bulletin    11.) 
Not  only  interesting  to  lovers  of  trees  but  splen- 
did   for    school    use.      Simple    and    clear    in    style, 
finely  illustrated.     Pt.  2  is  a  dictionary  of  trees. 

Jardine,  Jas.  T.,  and  Anderson,  Mark.  Range 
management  on  the  national  forests.  1919. 

A1.3  790 

(U.    S.    Dept.    of    Agriculture.      Bulletin    790.) 
Covers  the  entire  subject  of  national   forest  graz- 
ing;   its    possibilities,    dangers,    and    regulations    for 
protection.     Illustrated  and  good  for  school  use. 

Logan,  Ann  Stuart.    Citizens'  almanac.  1919. 

Y3.C83-62  C49c 

(U.  S.  Council  of  National  Defense.  Woman's 
Committee.  Illinois  Division.) 

A    really   valuable    compilation    of    pertinent    facts 


regarding  the  civil  side  of  the  nation,  state,  county, 
and  city,  with  especial  reference  to  Illinois  and  Cook 
County;  of  value  alike  to  civic  workers,  voterg, 
teachers,  and  persons  preparing  for  civil  service 
examinations. 

Rosenthal,  S.  W.  Jewelry  and  silverware  in 
Cuba.  1919.  C18.ll  181 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Foreign  and  Domestic  Com- 
merce. Special  agents  ser.  181.) 

A  detailed  report  of  jewelry  and  silverware  used 
in  Cuba,  with  trade  suggestions  for  exporters.  Il- 
lustrated. 

Scott,  W.  M.,  and  others.  Preparation  of 
barreled  apples  for  market.  1919. 

A  1.9  1080 

(Farmers'   bulletin   1080.) 

Modern  methods  of  picking,  packing  and  loading 
of  apples,  with  plans  for  packing-houses. 

Snider,  Guy  E.  Selling  in  foreign  markets. 
1919.  C18.15  81 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Foreign  and  Domestic  Com- 
merce. Misc.  ser.  81.) 

A  popular  study  of  foreign  commerce  valuable 
to  the  exporter  and  student  and  intended  for  use 
in  vocational  training  classes.  It  contains  pub- 
lished statements  of  business  men  and  reports  of 
experts  on  methods  employed  in  export  trade. 

Stuart,  William.  Production  of  late  or  main- 
crop  potatoes.     1919.  A1.9  1064 
(Farmers'    bulletin.) 

A  discussion  of  the  importance  of  the  crops  for 
winter  food,  methods  by  which  the  yield  can  be  in- 
creased, and  storage  houses. 

United  States.  Bureau  of  Education.  Train- 
ing little  children;  suggestions  for  parents. 
1919.  116.3 1919-39 

(Bulletin,   1919.  No.   39.) 

Brief,  popular  articles,  nearly  all  prepared  by 
mothers  who  were  formerly  kindergartners.  First 
issued  in  separate  form  by  the  National  Kinder- 
garten Association.  An  excellent  and  helpful  com- 
pilation, with  one  article  on  story  telling  to  in- 
spire patriotism. 

United  States.  Bureau  of  Industrial  Hous- 
ing and  Transportation.  Housing  war 
workers:  houses,  site-planning,  utilities. 
1919.  v.  2.  L8.1  1919-2 

(Report  of  the  U.  S.  Housing  Corporation:  War 
emergency  construction,  v.  2.) 

A  most  interesting  and  attractive  report  fully  il- 
lustrated, with  plans  valuable  alike  to  those  in- 
terested in  industrial  housing,  or  in  the  building  of 
homes. 

United  States.  Forest  Service.  Vacation 
land;  the  national  forests  in  Oregon,  rev. 
1919.  Al.14/2  4 

(Dept.  circular  4.) 

Contains  directory  of  the  16  national  forests  in 
Oregon,  with  valuable  suggestions  for  campers,  hik- 
ers, and  sportsmen.  Describes  outfits,  food  sup- 
plies, and  equipment,  and  contains  maps  and  illustra- 
tions. 

United  States.  Tariff  Commission.'  Census 
of  dyes  and  coal-tar  chemicals:  1918.  1919. 

TC1.511 

(Tariff   information    ser.    11.) 

The  progress  made  in  the  dye  industry  during 
the  war  indicates  that  the  U.  S.  will  never  again  be 
dependent  on  foreign  countries  for  its  supplies. 


The  Book  Bulletin 


of  the 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume  9  December,  1919  NO-  10 


Opera  Scores,  Librettos,  and  Plots 

IN  the  Music  Room  on  the  fifth  floor  of  the  Main  Library  building  are  to  be 
found  librettos  and  from  ten  to  twenty  copies  of  the  vocal  scores  of  all  the 
favorite  operas  and  a  smaller  number  of  the  less  familiar  ones.     They  may  be 
borrowed  on  special  music  cards  issuable  to  all  registered  book  borrowers  on 
application. 

Much  difficulty  has  been  experienced  in  trying  to  secure  scores  of  the  new 
operas  announced  for  production  in  the  Chicago  Opera  repertory  for  this 
season,  and  not  all  have  proved  to  be  obtainable.  Below  is  a  list  of  references 
to  books  containing  plots  of  the  operas  in  this  winter's  program.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate. 


GENERAL  WORKS 
Library  Science,  Incunabula 

Mpnumenta  Germanize  et  Italiae  typograph- 
ica;  deutsche  und  italienische  Inkunabeln 
in  getreuen  Nachbildungen;  hrsg.  von  der 
Direktion  der  Reichsdruckerei,  begriindet 
von  K.  Burger,  fortgefiihrt  von  Ernst 
Voullieme.  1913-16.  3v.  *P2537 

Wilson,  Martha.  School  library  manage- 
ment. 1919.  J  7316 

PHILOSOPHY 


Adams,  George  Plimpton, 
modern  age.     1919. 


Idealism  and  the 
L  13802 


An  interpretation  of  the  aggressive  spirit  of  the 
modern  economic,  scientific,  and  democratic  age  in 
terms  of  idealism. 

Psychology 

Franz,  Shepherd  Ivory.     Handbook  of  men- 
tal examination  methods.     1919.      L  13902 
A    manual    for    students,    psychiatrists,    and    neu- 
rologists; not  an  exhaustive  treatment. 

Terman,  Lewis  M.  The  intelligence  of 
school  children;  how  children  differ  in 
ability,  the  use  of  mental  tests  in  school 
grading,  and  the  proper  education  of  ex- 
ceptional children.  1919.  L 13717 
Riverside  textbooks  in  education. 

Psychical  Research 

Carrington,  Hereward.  Modern  psychical 
phenomena;  recent  researches  and  specu- 
lations. 1919.  L 13529 

Mr.  Carrington's  object  is  to  describe  the  methods 
which  psychical  research  is  using  today  and  the 
very  latest  knowledge  attained  through  it;  and  also 
to  explain  the  value  of  the  phenomena,  if  proved 
true.  The  first  portion  treats  of  the  relation  of 
these  phenomena  to  the  biological  and  psychological 
sciences,  and  the  rest  is  devoted  to  recent  experi- 
ments and  theories. 


Crawford,  W.  J.  Experiments  in  psychical 
science,  levitation,  contact,  and  the  direct 
voice.  1919.  L 13531 

The  author  has  described  very  interesting  me- 
chanical tests  in  levitation  and  table  rapping  in 
his  Reality  of  psychic  phenomena,  L  13533.  In 
this  book  he  enters  more  into  detail  and  records 
tests  in  "contact"  phenomena  and  the  "direct 
voice." 

RELIGION 

Lawson,  J.  Gilchrist,  comp.  Greatest 
thoughts  about  Jesus  Christ,  compiled 
from  many  sources.  1919.  M  7138 

Steele,  David  M.  Addresses  and  sermons 
to  students;  being  a  series  of  commence- 
ment orations  and  baccalaureate  sermons. 
1919.  M  4964 

The  author  is  an  Episcopalian  rector  of  Philadel- 
phia. 

Thomas,  W.  H.  Griffith.     The  Christian  life 

and  how  to  live  it.     1919.  M  4210 

A     reprint     of    the     sermons     contained     in     the 

volume    entitled    Royal    and    loyal,    including    two 

additional    chapters. 

Thompson,  Silvanus  P.  A  not  impossible 
religion.  1918.  M  4221 

A  scientist's  conviction  that  though  orthodox 
religion  has  been  made  impossible  by  "the  un- 
reality, the  uselessness,  of  the  theological  ecjuip- 
ment  that  has  done  duty  for  religion,"  religion 
itself  and  the  value  of  the  example  of  Christ  nave 
not  been  destroyed. 

The  Bible 

Fullerton,  Kemper.  Prophecy  and  authority; 
a  study  in  the  history  of  the  doctrine  and 
interpretation  of  Scripture.  1919.  M  6658 
An  Oberlin  professor's  historical  investigation  of 
the  theories  of  prophecy,  inspiration,  infallibility, 
and  of  the  millennial  hope  which  has  lately  been 
revived  by  the  war  among  many  Christian  people. 

Lawson,  J.  Gilchrist,  comp.  Greatest 
thoughts  about  the  Bible  gleaned  from 
many  sources.  1918.  M  6375 

Selections  in  prose  and  poetry  from  well-known 
preachers  and  laymen  emphasizing  the  spiritual 
power  and  beauty  of  the  Bible. 


174 


Penniman,   Josiah    H.     A   book   about    the 
English  Bible.     1919.  M  6384 

Religious    science    and    literature    series. 

Perhaps  the  real  contribution  of  the  volume  is 
its  emphasis  of  the  fact  that  Israel  made  her 
heritage  a  new  creation.  It  was  not  only  a  literary 
deposit,  but  something  unique  in  itself. — Boston 
Transcript. 

Church  Work 

Brunner,  Edmund  de  S.    The  country  church 
in  the  new  world  order.    1919.  M  8683 

Briefly  considers  the  country  church  war  activities, 
rural  community  organization,  and  the  obligation  of 
the  church  to  furnish  inspired  leadership  in  initiat- 
ing the  rural  community  into  the  new  world  order. 
— Booklist. 

Earp,  Edwin  L.     The   rural  church  serving 
the  community.     1918.  M  8883 

A  concise  and  practical  treatment  of  the  country 
church  problem,  givjng  definite  suggestions  for 
procedure  in  making  it  a  rural  social  center  of  the 
most  helpful  kind.  The  psychological  and  economic 
features  are  not  neglected. 


Religious   Education 

Bower,  William  Clayton.  A  survey  of  re- 
ligious education  in  the  local  church. 
1919.  L 19908 

The  University  of  Chicago.  Publications  in  re- 
ligious education. 

Instructions  for  making  surveys  in  church  work 
on  as  scientific  principles  as  those  employed  in 
other  lines  of  social  service.  Frequent  reference 
is  made  to  the  best  books  on  both  religious  and 
secular  teaching.

Labor 

Leitch,  John.  Man  to  man;  the  story  of 
industrial  democracy.  1919.  L  16746 

A  plan  for  a  representative  form  of  government 
in  industrial  plants  on  the  principle  of  the  federal 
political  organization.  It  has  been  successfully  in- 
troduced into  more  than  a  dozen  factories  in  the 
last  few  years. 

Political  Science 

Ferrara,  Orestes.  Lessons  of  the  war  and 
the  peace  conference.  1919.  ' L 17948 

Contents:  German  policy. — Dismemberment  of 
Austria-Hungary. — Tie  remains  of  the  Turkish

lern.— The  Russian  revolution. — Pacifism,  past  and 
future. — The  league  of  nations. — The  fourteen 
points. 

A  general  discussion  by  a  professor  of  public 
law  at  the  National  University  of  Havana,  emphasiz- 
ing throughout  the  political  rather  than  the  social 
or  economic  situation. 

Geiser,  Karl  Frederick.  Democracy  versus 
autocracy;  a  comparative  study  of  govern- 
ments in  the  world  war.  1918.  L  18061 

A  brief  and  simple  study  of  political  ideals  and 
institutions.  After  a  consideration  of  comparative 
government,  the  specific  governments  of  Germany, 
Austria-Hungary,  Belgium,  and  Brazil  are  treated 
in  detail.

Thayer,  William  Roscoe.  Democracy:  dis- 
cipline: peace.  1919.  L  15918 

Brown   University.     The   Colver   lectures,   1919. 


Discusses  arguments  for  and  against  democracy, 
showing  that  a  practical  democracy  is  worth  while; 
contrasts  American  educational  ideals  with  German, 
emphasizing  our  need  of  discipline  to  fit  us  to  the 
tasks  of  democracy;  and  urges  interest  in  the  bat- 
tles of  peace  time  against  disease,  social  evils  and 
poverty. — Booklist. 

Law 
Shealey,    R.    Preston.     The  law  of   govern- 


ment contracts.     1919. 


N5490 


A  book  for  lawyers  or  business  men,  interpreting 
contracts  with  the  government  and  the  statutes  and 
policies  governing  them.  It  quotes  the  laws,  cites 
references  to  illustrative  cases,  and  gives  much 
clear  explanatory  matter  necessary  to  the  average 
person  engaged  in  contract  with  the  government. 
—  Booklist. 

International  Relations 

Brown,  Louise  Fargo.  The  freedom  of  the 
seas.  1919.  L  17945 

This  includes  a  historical  account  of  the  cen- 
turies-old controversy  and  recommendations  for  its 
solution,  involving  the  abolition  of  trade  barriers 
and  an  international  law  of  the  sea  determined  by 
an  international  court  and  administered  by  in- 
ternational machinery. 

Chung,  Henry.  The  oriental  policy  of  the 
United  States.  1919.  L  16289 

A  Korean's  appeal  to  the  United  States  on  behalf 
of  China  whose  national  existence  (together  with 
the  welfare  of  the  world)  he  considers  imperiled 
by  Japanese  aggression. 

Elliot,  Arthur  D.  Traditions  of  British 
statesmanship;  some  comments  on  passing 
events.  1919.  L 16291 

The  author  maintains  that  England's  foreign 
policy  has  always  been  as  peaceful  as  was  con- 
sistent with  her  own  security,  and  enters  in  de- 
tail into  her  pre-war  relations  with  Germany. 
Among  other  subjects  he  takes  up  her  preparedness, 
methods  of  warfare,  reconstruction  programs,  and 
the  Irish  question.

Lippincott,  Horace  Mather.  The  University 
of  Pennsylvania,  Franklin's  college;  being

This  book  has  a  broader  interest  than  its  at- 
traction for  the  children  of  Alma  Mater  in  that  it 
serves  to  perpetuate  the  memory  of  college  _tradi-

Moore,  Ernest  Carroll.  What  the  war 
teaches  about  education,  and  other  papers 
and  addresses.  1919.  L  19398 

The  entire  book  is  centered  around  the  theme 
of  the  unlimited  power  of  education,  which  in  its 
utilitarian  aspects  the  author  thinks  must  bear  the 
burden  of  responsibility  for  the  new  world-order 
that  is  to  follow  upon  the  war. 

Parker,  Samuel  Chester.  General  methods 
of  teaching  in  elementary  schools,  includ- 
ing the  kindergarten  and  grades  I  to  VI. 
1919.  L 19545 

A  simple  and  elementary  consideration  of  the 
subject  intended  primarily  for  normal  schools  and 
training  classes. 

West,  Andrew  F.  The  war  and  education; 
together  with  a  translation  of  the  war  ad- 
dress of  M.  Lafferre.  1919.  L  19433 
Considers  that  the  most  important  contribution 
of  the  war  to  education  lies  in  the  increased  em- 
phasis placed  upon  discipline  and  the  idea  of  duty, 
as  opposed  to  mercenary  or  sentimental  theories.

McFee,  Inez  N.     The  tree  book.     1919. 

K 21455 

A  delightful  book  of  tree  lore  including  the  year's 
history  of  tree  life,  an  account  of  the  most  im- 
portant tree  families  together  with  the  legends  and

Morse,  Mary  E.  First  lessons  in  bacteriology 
for  nurses.     1919.  K  21538 

The  main  facts  of  bacteriology  are  here  presented 
by  the  pathologist  of  the  State  Hospital  at  Boston 
in  non-technical  language  that  rloes  not  presuppose 
a  scientific  training. 

Porter,  Gene  Stratton.  Homing  with  the 
birds;  the  history  of  a  lifetime  of  personal 
experience  with  the  birds.  1919.  K  22066 
Mrs.  Porter  describes  quite  simply  her  early  in- 
terest in  birds  and  how  she  came  to  write  on 
natural  history.  She  has  much  to  relate  of  her  ob- 
servation of  the  habits  of  birds,  their  song,  court- 
ship, and  nest-building.  The  illustrations  are  from 
photographs  of  her  own  making.

K  20421 

A  clear  presentation  of  fundamental  principles 
with  which  the  inexperienced  investor  in  oil  prop- 
erty or  securities  ought  to  familiarize  himself.

la  monografia  dell'  artista  scritta  da 
Ernesto  Basile.  1911.  *P  1309 

Sixty-five  plates  of  reproductions  of  the  sculp- 
ture and  stucco  work  of  Serpotta. 


Drawing,    Design 


Mijer,    Pieter. 
them.     1919. 


Batiks,    and    how    to    make 
K 28683 


Batiks  are  fabrics  in  which  the  decorative  orna- 
mental effect  is  produced  by  artificial  methods  of 
distributing  the  dyes.  The  process  comes  to  us 
from  Java,  and  this  book  describes  the  history  and 
evolution  of  the  art  and  the  modern  methods  em- 
ployed by  contemporary  craftsmen. 

Sparrow,  Walter  Shaw.  Prints  and  draw- 
ings by  Frank  Brangwyn,  with  some 
other  phases  of  his  art.  1919.  *P3196 
Though  his  etchings  take  up  the  larger  part  of  the 
book  in  both  text  and  illustrations,  Mr.  Brang- 
wyn's  versatility  is  amply  brought  out  in  relation  to 
architecture,  decoration,  and  different  methods  of 
painting. — Athenaeum. 

Storey,  G.  A.  The  theory  and  practice  of 
perspective.  1910.  K  28593 

Painting 

Brown,  Percy.     Indian  painting.     1919. 

K  29320 
The  heritage  of   India. 

Parrish,  Maxfield,  and  Guerin,  Jules.  Water 
colour  rendering-suggestions.  1917. 

*P  3211 

Sinclair,  W.  A.     Painting.     1918.         K  29028 
A  very  brief  essay,   the   second  in  a  series  deal- 
ing with  the   "seven   arts,"   of   which   the   first   was 
Lord   Dunsany's  paper  on  poetry   called   Nowadays. 

Out- Door  Sports 

Carroll,  Dixie,  pseud,  of  Carroll  Elaine  Cook. 
Fishing,  tackle  and  kits;  practical  infor- 
mation on  game  fish:  how  to  land  them; 
the  correct  tackle  and  how  to  use  it. 
1919.  K  30818 

Leaves  nothing  unsaid  that  pertains  to  the  noble 
sport  of  fishing. — Book  Review  Digest. 

Coale,  Anna  Worthington.  Summer  in  the 
girls'  camp.  1919.  K  30907 

A  description  of  life  in  girls'  camps  which  will 
prove  suggestive  to  those  in  charge  of  such  activi- 
ties. 

LITERATURE 

Johnson,  R.  Brimley.  The  women  novel- 
ists. 1918.  J6345 

A  critical  and  historical  study  of  "The  great 
four" — Fanny  Burney,  Jane  Austen,  Charlotte 
Bronte,  and  George  Eliot — by  means  of  whose 
work  the  author  develops  the  theory  that  women 
have  contributed  to  the  writing  of  fiction  a  certain 
quantity  of  femininity  that  has  ever  escaped  the 
masculine  writer. — New  York  Times. 

Pattee,  Fred  Lewis,  ed.  Century  readings 
for  a  course  in  American  literature.  1919. 

J6434 

Our  popular  poetry — political  and  sentimental — 
is  excellently  represented,  and  some  of  the  greater 
hymns;  there  are  thirty  short  stories  so  well  chosen 
to  illustrate  the  development  of  their  type  that  the 
American  short  story  may  be  better  studied  here 
than  anywhere  else. — Book  Review  Digest.

179 


Whiteford,     Robert     Naylor.       Motives     in 
English  fiction.     1918.  J  7224 

Curious  jumbles  of  ideas  and  impressions  deal- 
ing with  novelists  and  their  imaginative  creations 
from  Malory  to  De  Morgan. — Boston  Transcript. 

Journalism 

Lee,   James    Melvin.      Opportunities   in    the 
newspaper    business.      1919.  J  7253 

The  opportunity  books. 

This  handy  little  book  describes  the  workings  of 
every  kind  of  newspaper  irom  the  country  weekly 
to  the  large  metropolitan  daily.  It  also  discusses 
the  methods  of  the  successful  reporter  and  the  quali- 
fications for  success  in  journalistic  work  generally. 

Poetry 

DeMoney,    May    Hartley.      Victory    verse. 
1918.    .  E6546 

A  small  volume  of  newspaper  verses  by  a  Chicago 
poet. 

Garesche,   Edward    F.     The  world   and  the 
waters.     1918.  E 10950 

A  book  of  poems  by  a  Jesuit  priest,  which  the 
Bookman  says  "contains  some  very  superior  sing- 
ing." 

Lowell,    Amy.       Pictures     of     the     floating 
world.     1919.  E  7753 

A  book  of  impressions,  fleeting  and  delicate,  yet 
keenly  and  vividly  defined  .  .  .  There  are 
few  poems  in  it  that  lack  distinguished  beauty,  a 
beauty  expressed  simply  and  eloquently. — New  York 
Times. 

Oppenheim,  James.     The  solitary.     1919. 

E  11381 

Contents:  The  sea. — Songs  out  of  solitude. — 
Songs  out  of  multitude. — Night,  a  drama. 

Poems  notably  expressive  of  the  social  and  in- 
tellectual confusion,  the  blend  of  half-formed, 
worthily  felt  indignations,  hopes,  and  despair  of 
America  at  the  present  moment. — Nation. 

Spicer,  Anne  Higginson.     The  last  crusade. 
1918.  E  11549 

Poems. 

Squire,  J.  C.     Poems;  first  series.     1919. 

El  1543 

His  poems  are  as  compact  of  detail  as  a  patch 
of  turf,  and  like  the  turf  they  have  a  deceptive 
air  of  plainness.  As  a  result  the  book  is  packed 
with  interest. — Athenaum. 

Tietjens,  Eunice.     Body  and  raiment.     1919. 

E8736 

Many  forms  of  verse  to  suit  many  environments 
and  moods  and  the  poet's  own  complexity  which  is 
attractively  analyzed  in  the  first  poem.  Renderings 
and  translations  of  a  few  Japanese  and  other  poems. 
— Booklist. 

Wheelock,  John  Hall.    Dust  and  light.   1919. 

El  1708 

For  a  tender  virility,  a  charm  of  unsullied  youth, 
a  searching  ardor,  a  human  poignancy  and  tragic 
sense  of  reality — for  these  rare  things  expressed  in 
verse  unwavering  and  untrammeled  by  any  snare  of 
mere  literariness,  his  first  volume  is  a  permanent 
boon  to  our  native  literature. — Percy  Mackaye. 


Wilbur,  Russell  J.  Theodore  Roosevelt;  a 
verse  sequence  in  sonnets  and  quatorzains. 
1919.  E 11709 

A  series  of  verses  which,  as  William  Hard  says 
in  the -preface,  "do  not  give  us  the  Roosevelt  of 
a  faction  or  of  a  moment,  but  rather  Roosevelt's 
assemblage  of  conflicts,  of  human  eternal  conflicts, 
demanding  to  be  solved." 

Wilson,  Eugene  E.,  comp.  Comrades  of  the 
mist,  and  other  rhymes  of  the  grand 
fleet.  1919.  El  1689 

A  memento  of  one  phase  of  our  participation  in 
the  war  that  is  well  worth  having. — New  York 
Times.

CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Morraca;  a  play  in  one  act.     1918. 

E  12466 
In  The  Drama,   November,   1918. 

Holland,  Romain.  The  wolves;  a  play  in 
three  acts.  E  12466 

In  The   Drama,   November,   1918. 

Shaw,  George  Bernard.  Heartbreak  house, 
Great  Catherine,  and  playlets  of  the  war. 
1919.  E  12847 

"Heartbreak  House"  represents  cultured,  leisured 
England  before  the  war — its  sentimentality,  ro- 
mance, and  snobbishness.  At  the  end  the  war  itself 
is  symbolized  by  a  bomb  whose  sole  effect  is  to 
bring  disaster  to  the  business  men  of  the  party 
through  their  excessive  caution.  There  are  the 
usual  Shavian  prefaces. 

Besides  this  "fantasia  in  the  Russian  manner  on 
English  themes"  the  volume  contains:  Great  Cath- 
erine (whom  Glory  still  adores) ;  O'Flaherty,  V.  C., 
a  recruiting  pamphlet;  The  inca  of  Perusalem,  an 
almost  historical  comedietta;  Augustine  does  his  bit, 
a  true-to-life  farce;  Annajanska  the  bolshevik 
empress,  a  revolutionary  romancelet. 

Theis,  Grover.  Numbers,  and  other  one-act 
plays.  1919.  E 12894 

Contents:  Numbers. — Between  fires. — The  crack 
in  the  bell. — There's  a  difference. — Like  a  book. 

The  first  gives  a  picture  of  trench  madness;  the 
scene  of  the  second  is  laid  in  Italy;  the  third 
thrashes  out  the  riddle  of  existence  at  night  on  a 
park  bench;  and  the  last  two  -are  comedies.  Ac- 
cording to  the  Nati9n  they  are  "neither  very  firm 
in  structure  nor  quite  clarified  in  their  motivation 
and  tendency,  but  give  evidence  of  an  intelligent 
mind  and  a  talent  for  the  drama." 

Turrell,  Charles  A.  ed.  and  tr.  Contemporary 
Spanish  dramatists.  1919.  E  12947 

Contents:     Introduction.  —  Electra.       (Galdos.)  — 
The    claws     (Rivas).  —  TV>e    women's    town     (Quin- 
tero). — When  the  roses  bloom  again   (Marquina). — 
The    passing    of    the    magi    (Zamacois). — Juan   Jose 
(Dicenta). 

This  is  the  only  representative  collection  of 
modern  Spanish  plays  in  Englis_h,  and  the  intro- 
duction serves  to  give  them  their  proper  place  in 
contemporary  Spanish  literature. 

Wellman,  Rita.  Dawn;  a  one-act  play. 
1919.  E  13497 

In   The    Drama,    February,    1919. 

The  Gentile   wife;  a  play  in   four  acts. 

1919.  E  13016 

The  theme  is  the  disastrous  consequence  of 
marriage  between  a  Jew  and  a  Gentile.  Better 
adapted  for  acting  than  for  reading. 

Wilcox,  Constance.    Told  in  a  Chinese  gar- 
den; a  story  in  one  act.     1919.          E  11897 
In  The   Drama,   May,    1919.

Essays  and  Miscellanies 

Eason,  J.  Lawrence,  and  Weseen,  Maurice 
H.,  ed.  English,  science,  and  engineering; 
a  collection  of  expository  essays  for  stu- 
dents of  science  and  engineering.  1918. 

E2655 

These  essays  are  collected  from  the  writings  of 
such  well-known  scientists  as  H'uxley,  Darwin,  Tyn- 
dall  and  Newcomb,  and  are  grouped  around  the 
following  subjects:  thinking  and  writing,  natural 
science,  science,  scope  and  limits,  engineering,  and 
engineering  education. 

Hendrick,  Ellwood.  Percolator  papers. 
1919.  E  3487 

A  collection  of  scholarly  essays,  for  the  most  part 
reprinted  from  the  Chemists'  Club  organ,  The 
Percolator.  According  to  the  Dial,  "Mr.  Hendrick 
sees  life  amiably,  tranquilly;  his  pen  betokens  a 
mind  as  neat — and  as  untrammeled — as  a  clipped 
hedge." 

Middleton,  Arthur,  pseud,  of  E.  J.  H.  O'Brien. 

The  forgotten  threshold;  a  journal.  1919. 

E4991 
Maurice,  Arthur  Bartlett.  The  Paris  of  the 

novelists.     1919.  1 6828 

Includes  one  hundred  pages  of  storied  rural 
France  as  well. 

O'Brien,  Frederick.  White  shadows  in  the 
South  Seas.  1919.  I  1637 

This  excellent  book  of  travels  records  the  ex- 
periences of  some  years  spent  in  the  Marquesas 
islands,_  which  once  had  a  large  cannibalistic 
population,  now  almost  extinct.  It  is  a  fascinating 
story  of  a  strange  people  in  a  strange  place. 

Wharton,  Edith.  French  ways  and  their 
meaning.  1919.  1 6915 

An  intimate  and  interesting  commentary  with  wis- 
dom enough  in  some  of  its  single  sentences  to 
furnish  the  raison  d'etre  of  a  more  pretentious  vol- 
ume.— Dial. 

TRAVEL  AND  DESCRIPTION 
North  America 

Burr,   Agnes    Rush.      Alaska,    our   beautiful 
northland  of  opportunity.     1919.         I  4439 
"See  America  first"  series. 

_  A  beautiful  book,  with  much  of  the  charm  in 
pictures,  some  colored,  and  much  in  picturesque  in- 
cidents and  romantic  descriptions. — Booklist. 

Chamberlain,  Allen.  Vacation  tramps  in 
New  England  highlands.  1919.  14803 

An  inviting  _"fingerboard"  of  the  New  England 
wilderness,  which  is  pleasant  reading  whether  one 
expects  to  go  vacationing  or  not.  It  discusses  the 


BOOK   BULLETIN 


181 


hiker's  equipment,  the  spots  of  beauty  to  visit,  and 
gives  outlines  and  maps  for  several  journeys. — 
Booklist. 

Stephenson,  William  B.,  jr.  The  land  of 
tomorrow.  1919.  1 4440 

A  rather  general  description  of  nature  and  life 
in  Alaska. 

Yard,  Robert  Sterling.  The  book  of  the 
national  parks.  1919.  14314 

A  particularly  fresh  and  stimulating  description 
from  a  popular  scientific  point  of  view.  Mr.  Yard 
thinks  the  enjoyment  of  the  mountains  and  can- 
yons will  be  the  greater  if  the  intellectual  pleasure 
of  understanding  is  added  to  the  emotional  appeal 
of  the  scenery. 

BIOGRAPHY 

Ayscough,  John,  pseud,  of  Mgr.  John  Bicker- 
staff  e  Drew.  Letters  to  his  mother  during 
1914,  1915  and  1916.  1919.  A  7024 

The  author  served  as  chaplain  to  a  British  field 
ambulance  for  the  first  eighteen  months  of  the 
war.  The  charm  of  his  letters  lies  not  so  much  in 
the  description  of  life  at  the  front  as  in  the  filial 
devotion  that  breathes  through  their  pages. 

Dodd,  Walter  James.  Macy,  John  Albert. 
Walter  James  Dodd;  a  biographical 
sketch.  1918.  C  21614 

An  intimate  friend's  tribute  to  the  lovable  per- 
sonality and  valuable  services  of  a  well-known 
physician  and  X-ray  expert  of  international  repu- 
tation, who  died  from  injuries  received  through  his 
experiments. 

Hamilton,  Emma,  Lady.  Danby,  Frank, 
pseud,  of  Mrs.  Julia  Frankau.  Nelson's 
legacy.  Lady  Hamilton;  her  story  and 
tragedy.  1915.  C  22289 

Howe,  Julia  Ward.  The  walk  with  God; 
extracts  from  Mrs.  Howe's  private  jour- 
nals, together  with  some  verses  hitherto 
unpublished;  and  an  essay  on  immortality 
entitled  "Beyond  the  veil."  Ed.  by  her 
daughter,  Laura  E.  Richards.  1919. 

C  22496 

McCormack,  John.  Key,  Pierre  V.  R.  John 
McCormack,  his  own  life  story.  1918. 

C  23125 

Written  by  a  newspaper  reporter  from  intimate 
interviews.  Simply  told  and  with  many  personal 
details. 

Osborn,   Chase  S.     The  iron  hunter.     1919. 

C  23655 

An  interesting  and  essentially  American  auto- 
biography of  one  of  Michigan's  former  governors, 
furnishing  at  the  same  time  a  chapter  in  the  his- 
tory of  the  development  of  the  Great  Lakes  region. 

Parker,  General  Ely  S.  Parker,  Arthur  C. 
The  life  of  General  Ely  S.  Parker,  last 
Grand  Sachem  of  the  Iroquois,  and  Gen- 
eral Grant's  military  secretary.  1919. 

B  4004,  23 
Mainly   a   narrative    of   Indian   life. 


Roosevelt,  Theodore.  Abbott,  Lawrence  F. 
Impressions  of  Theodore  Roosevelt.  1919. 

C  23986 

The  publisher  of  the  Outlook  disclaims  any 
biographical  intention  and  simply  sketches  the  im- 
pressions received  in  personal  contact  with  Roose- 
velt during  the  last  twenty  years.  His  book  is 
particularly  valuable  for  its  intimate  account  of  the 
European  trip. 

Thayer,     William     Roscoe.       Theodore 

Roosevelt;   an  intimate   biography.      1919. 

C  23985 

One  of  the  best  books  on  Roosevelt  by  the  suc- 
cessful biographer  of  Hay  and  Cavour.  Mr.  Thayer 
was  his  classmate  at  Harvard  and  a  lifelong  friend, 
and  writes  the  more  impartially  as  he  was  not 
of  the  same  political  faith. 

Tynan,  Katherine,  (Mrs.  H.  A.  Hinkson). 
The  years  of  the  shadow.  1919.  C  22442 

Continues  from  1912  to  1918  the  entertaining  and 
gossipy  reminiscences  begun  in  Twenty-five  years 
(C  22439),  and  The  middle  years  (C  22440). 
Many  interesting  people  drift  through  the  pages — 
Lord  and  Lady  Dunsany,  Ledwidge,  H.  G.  Wells, 
etc. 

HISTORY 
Europe 

Dawson,  William  Harbutt.  The  German 
empire,  1867-1914,  and  the  unity  move- 
ment. 1919.  2v.  A  4222 

A  dispassionate  political  history  of  the  origin 
and  progress  of  the  German  empire  treated  through 
the  effect  of  personalities  on  the  course  of  history. 

Glover,  T.  R.  From  Pericles  to  Philip. 
1918.  A  2567 

A  survey  of  many-sided  Greek  life  during  the  in- 
teresting and  significant  period  of  which  Herodotus, 
Thucydides,  Xenophon,  Plato,  and  Aristotle  wrote 
their  contemporary  accounts  retold  and  interpreted 
in  a  delightful  manner. — Open  Shelf. 

Hazen,  Charles  Downer.  Fifty  years  of 
Europe,  1870-1919.  A  5951 

This  new  history  of  recent  Europe,  dealing  espe- 
cially with  the  rise  and  fall  of  the  German  empire, 
reprints  the  later  chapters  of  Mr.  Hazen's  Modern 
European  history  (A  5928)  with  certain  modifica- 
tions and  adds  new  material  covering  the  war. 

Jusaitis,  Kunigas  Antanas.  The  history  of 
the  Lithuanian  nation  and  its  present  na- 
tional aspirations.  1918.  A  6096 

Besides  furnishing  *  information  with  regard  to 
Lithuania's  history  and  institutions,  the  book  con- 
tains interesting  facts  on  Lithuanian  organizations 
in  America. 

Lavell,  Cecil  Fairfield.  Reconstruction  and 
national  life.  1919.  A  5955 

Professor  Lavell  traces  the  history  of  idealism  in 
modern  times  in  four  principal  countries — England, 
France,  Germany,  and  Russia — and  makes  some 
appraisal  of  the  part  which  liberal  thought  is  pre- 
pared to  take  in  these  lands  in  shaping  the  political 
and  economic  policies  of  the  coming  age.  His 
book  is  a  useful  summary. — Nation. 


182 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Marvin,  F.  S.  The  century  of  hope;  a 
sketch  of  western  progress  from  1815  to 
the  great  war.  1919.  A  5958 

A  philosophical  history  of  the  nineteenth  cen- 
tury, in  which  one  of  the  most  distinguished  Ox- 
ford humanists  "endeavors  to  exhibit  the  growth  of 
humanity  in  the  wdrld,"  taking  as  a  leading  theme 
"the  development  of  science  and  its  reactions  on 
other  sides  of  national  and  international  life." — 
Dial. 

Phillipson,  Coleman,  and  Buxton,  Noel.  The 
question  of  the  Bosphorus  and  Darda- 
nelles. 1917.  A  5166 

European  War 

Andre,  C.  H.  A.     Above  the  battles.     1919. 

A  6972 

An  unusually  well  translated  account  of  a  French 
aviator's  experiences  in  the  war,  full  of  humor 
and  picturesque  incident. 

Brooke,  Tucker,  and  Canby,  Henry  Seidel, 
ed.  War  aims  and  peace  ideals;  selections 
in  prose  and  verse,  illustrating  the  aspira- 
tions of  the  modern  world.  1919.  A  6975 
Representative  selections  from  the  literature  of 
the  nations  engaged  in  the  great  war  chosen  to 
characterize  the  souls  of  the  peoples.  Quotations 
are  made  from  Cardinal  Mercier,  Daudet,  Tolstoy, 
Arthur  Henderson,  Joyce  Kilmer  and  many  others. 

Cable,  Boyd,  pseud.    Air  men  o'  war.     1919. 

A  6976 

A  collection  of  short  stories,  full  of  the  signifi- 
cant daily  happenings  of  the  air  "pieced  together 
from  facts."  The  thrilling  adventures  are  told  in 
a  vivid  style  especially  suited  to  hold  the  interest 
of  the  older  boys. 

Dresser,  Horatio  W.  On  the  threshold  of 
the  spiritual  world;  a  study  of  life  and 
death  over  there.  1919.  A  7025 

From  experiences  at  the  front  the  author  pro- 
nounces the  war  "something  altogether  splendid 
in  human  history."  Man  emerges  as  a  spirit  with 
perception  of  things  beyond  the  flesh;  death  is 
simply  "a  step  in  life"  and  there  are  comforting 
answers  to  some  of  the  human  questionings  about 
our  soldier  dead. — Booklist. 

Gaines,  Ruth.  Helping  France;  the  Red 
Cross  in  the  devastated  area.  1919. 

A  7022 

Though  more  statistical  and  less  picturesque  than 
the  Village  in  Picardy  (A  6834)  by  the  same 
author,  this  book  is  nevertheless  an  interesting 
record  of  obstacles  overcome. 

Hainsselin,  Montague  Thomas.  The  curtain 
of  steel,  by  the  author  of  In  the  northern 
mists,  Grand  fleet  days,  Naval  intelligence. 
1918.  A  6978 

Hamon,  Augustin.  Lessons  of  the  world- 
war.  1919.  A  6974 

A  translation  of  a  Franco-Belgian's  deeply  anti- 
militaristic lectures  on  the  world  problems  of  the 
present  and  future.  Regarding  war  as  criminal,  it 
advocates  complete  disarmament,  death  to  autocracy, 
and  liberty  through  the  small  government  unit  in 
which  each  man's  personality  is  still  retained.  It 
ardently  supports  President  Wilson. — Booklist. 


Hurd,  Archibald.  Italian  sea-power  and  the 
great  war.  1918.  A  6980 

Husband,  Joseph.  On  the  coast  of  France; 
the  story  of  the  United  States  naval  forces 
in  French  waters.  1919.  A  6971 

This  is  not  a  systematic  history  but  offers  some 
interesting  sidelights  on  a  phase  of  our  navy 
service.  The  descriptions  are  good  and  the  cuts 
illuminating. 


-A  year  in  the  navy.     1919. 


A  6973 

Interesting  account  of  the  author's  .  experiences 
at  the  Great  Lakes  training  station  and  in  overseas 
service. 

Man,  Henry  de.  The  remaking  of  a  mind; 
a  soldier's  thoughts  on  war  and  recon- 
struction. 1919.  A  7026 
Treats  of  the  author's  reactions  to  the  successive 
phases  of  the  war.  A  Belgian  intellectual  who  had 
entered  earnestly  into  the  internationals'  attempt 
to  oppose  the  war,  he  enlisted  in  the  Belgian  army 
when  that  opposition  proved  unavailing  and  fol- 
lowed the  fortunes  of  the  war  on  four  fronts. 

Milan,  Rene,  pseud,  of  Maurice  Larrouy. 
Vagabonds  of  the  sea;  the  campaign  of  a 
French  cruiser.  1919.  A  6979 

A  romantic  narrative  of  a  French  naval  officer's 
service  in  a  part  of  the  war  zone  which,  though 
not  the  scene  of  decisive  battles,  was  nevertheless 
important.  His  accounts  of  the  Mediterranean 
and  its  pirates  contain  many  dramatic  situations. 


Nordhoff,  Charles   Bernard. 
1919. 


The  fledgling. 
A  6977 


Reminiscences  and  scraps  from  the  diary  of  an 
American's  service  as  ambulance  driver  and  aviator 
in  the  Lafayette  escadrille. 

Tunney,  Thomas  J.  Throttled!  The  de- 
tection of  the  German  and  anarchist  bomb 
plotters.  1919.  A  7023 

The  full  story  of  nearly  a  dozen  plots  against 
our  war  industries  told  by  the  head  of  the  bomb 
squad  of  the  New  York  City  police  department. 


Ward,   Mrs.    Humphry. 
1919. 


Fields    of   victory. 
A  7021 


Letters  describing  'the  author's  observations  on 
the  fields  of  France  where  the  British  army  was 
most  engaged  in  1918.  It  follows  Mrs.  Ward's 
Toward  the  goal,  A  6440,  and  England's  effort, 
A  6245. 


Great  Britain  and  Ireland 

Boulger,  Demetrius  Charles.  The  battle  of 
the  Boyne,  together  with  an  account  based 
on  French  and  other  unpublished  records 
of  the  war  in  Ireland  ( 1688-1691')  and  of 
the  formation  of  the  Irish  brigade  in  the 
service  of  France.  1911.  A  5504 

Coulton,  G.  G.,  comp.  Social  life  in  Britain 
from  the  conquest  to  the  reformation. 
1918.  A  5379 

Extracts      from       mediaeval      documents.     .     .     . 
Schoolboys    and    university     students,     monks     and

183 


secular     scribes,     priests     and     friars,     knights     and 
kings,    rich    and    poor,    manor    lords    and    serfs,    pil- 
grims   and   merchants,    master   masons    and   workers, 
ghysicians    and    their    ungrateful    patients — all    pass 
efore    us    in   a   long,    long    line    and   leave    an    im- 
pression that  will  not  fade. — Survey. 

Creel,  George.  Ireland's  fight  for  freedom, 
setting  forth  the  high  lights  of  Irish  his- 
tory. 1919.  A  5502 
This  account  is  in  entire  sympathy  with  Ireland's 
cause  and  does  not  touch  at  all  upon  England's  side 
of  the  question. 

Shaw,    Bernard.      How    to    settle    the    Irish 
question.     1917.  A  5517 

A   pamphlet  consisting   of  articles   written   at   the 
suggestion   of  the  London   Daily   Express.

Adriacus,  pseud.  From  Trieste  to  Valona; 
the  Adriatic  problem  and  Italy's  aspira- 
tions. 1919.  A  5117 

Annunzio,  Gabriele  d',  pseud,  of  Gaetano  Ra-

Contents:  To    the    guard    of    the    Piave. — To    a

1919.  A  5118 

Trevelyan,  George  Macaulay.  Scenes  from 
Italy's  war.  1919.  A  6970 

An  interesting  and  vivid  account  at  close  range 
of  great  events  in  Italy  from  the  author's  per- 
sonal experiences  during  four  years  as  commander 
of  the  first  British  Red  Cross  detachment  in  Italy. 


Russia 

Bolshevik  aims  and  ideals,  and  Russia's  re- 
volt against  Bolshevism.     1919.          A  6041 

Two  articles  reprinted  from  the  British  quarterly 
review,  the  Round  Table.  Their  author  believes 
with  Spargo  that  though  not  deserving  the  ex- 
aggerated abuse  heaped  upon  it,  bolshevism  ,is  a 
menace  to  Russia  and  the  world.  He  thinks  its 
power  is  crumbling  under  the  growing  resistance  of 
the  Russian  people. 


Long,   Robert   Crozier. 
.  aspects.     1919. 


Russian  revolution 
A  6043 


He  refuses  to  despair  of  Russia's  ultimate  fate, 
although  he  makes  no  effort  to  minimize  the  seri- 
ousness of  her  present  situation.  His  book  is 
chiefly  interesting  for  its  pen  pictures  of  Kerensky, 
Korniloff,  Lvoff,  and  other  commanding  figures  of 
the  revolutionary  era. — Review  of  Reviews. 


Masaryk,  Thomas  Garrigue.  The  spirit  of 
Russia;  studies  in  history,  literature  and 
philosophy.  1919.  2v.  A  6042 

A  study  of  the  Russian  spirit  (published  in  Ger- 
man in  1913)  by  the  man  who  was  later  to  become 
the  political  leader  of  Czecho-Slovakia.  The  en- 
tire work  centers  around  Dostoevski.  It  compares 
his  philosophy  of  history  and  religion  with  that 
of  his  predecessors  and  successors,  and  discusses 
his  relationship  to  the  literature  of  Russia  and  of 
western  Europe. 

Ransome,  Arthur.  Russia  in  1919.  A  6044 
This  account  of  present-day  life  in  Russia  by  an 
English  man  of  letters  is  exceedingly  well  written, — 
simple,  clear  and  graphic  with  a  quiet  charm  of 
style.  It  leaves  the  reader  with  a  distinct  picture 
of  the  political,  industrial,  economic  and  intellectual 
life  under  the  Bolshevik  regime  and  seems  to  have 
been  written  with  no  desire  to  mould  opinion. 

Russell,  Charles  Edward.  Bolshevism  and 
the  United  States.  1919.  A  6045 

The  author  is  an  advocate  of  the  soviet  system 
of  representative  government  but  is  not  in  sym- 
pathy with  the  bolshevist  movement  in  America  on 
the  ground  that  it  is  more  autocratic  in  principle 
than  it  is  democratic. 


Asia,  Oceania 


Fletcher,  C.  Brunsdon. 
Pacific.     1919. 


The  problem  of  the 
A  3887 


The  Australian  author  of  The  new  Pacific 
(A  3886)  in  this  new  work  adds  materially  to  his 
former  discussion  of  the  world  questions.  The 
discussion  of  the  relations  of  Australia  to  India 
and  France  is  valuable  in  suggestion  and  exposi- 
tion.— Outlook. 

Hyndman,  H.  M.  The  awakening  of  Asia. 
1919.  A  4827 

Mr.  Hyndman  not  only  believes  that  European 
influence  has  not  been  w:holly  beneficial  to  the 
Asiatic  peoples,  but  even  that  they  would  be  far 
better  able  to  work  out  their  own  salvation  if  that 
influence  were  now  to  be  withdrawn. 
Kalaw,  Maximo  M.  Self-government  in  the 
Philippines.  1919.  B  7619 

"A  record  of  what  the  Filipino  people  have  done 
since  the  establishment  of  Philippine  autonomy,  and 
the  reasons  which  impel  them  to  demand  their 
separation  from  the  United  States,"  by  the  author 
of  The  case  for  the  Filipinos,  B  7621. 

Weale,  B.  L.  Putnam,  pseud,  of  B.  L.  Simp- 
son. The  truth  about  China  and  Japan. 
1919.  A  4953

Miller,  Alice  Duer. 


The  charm  school.  1919. 
F 15366 

A  delightful  young  man  undertakes  to  manage 
a  girls'  school  which  has  been  bequeathed  him  by 
his  aunt.  He  decides  that  his  pupils  should  be 
taught  charm  of  manner  rather  than  dull  subjects 
like  mathematics,  and  proceeds  to  carry  out  his 
ideas.  The  result  is  an  amusing  story. 

Norris,  Kathleen.     Sisters.     1919."        F 15692

the  Russian  mind.  Savinkov  was  minister  of  war 
in  the  cabinet  of  Kerensky.

Notes 

The  U.  S.  Bureau  of  Education  in  co- 
operation with  the  American  Library  Asso- 
ciation has  continued  the  courses  for  home 
reading  by  issuing  "After  war  reading 
course,  no.  11,  France  and  her  history." 
There  are  14  books  listed,  and  the  Bureau 
of  Education  will  give  a  certificate  to  any 
person  who  reads  the  books  in  the  course. 
Clubs  will  find  this  list  of  special  interest. 
The  call  number  is  116.12  11. 

An  interesting  series  of  brief  pamphlets 
is  being  issued  jointly  by  the  Department  of 
Agriculture  and  the  Treasury  Department  as 
part  of  our  government's  thrift  campaign. 
Housewives  will  find  this  series  of  practical 
seryice,  as  it  covers  family  budgets,  labor- 
saving  methods  in  home  management,  re- 
moval of  stains,  laundry  suggestions,  mar- 
keting and  fuel.  The  call  number  is  A1.29. 

The  U.  S.  Training  Service  of  the  Depart- 
ment of  Labor  is  issuing  a  timely  and  help- 
ful series  for  various  trades  and  industries. 
A  description  is  given  of  the  trade  with  its 
requirements  and  methods  of  training  for  it. 
The  series  is  of  value  to  persons  choosing 
an  occupation  and  to  employers. 
U.  S.  Training  Service.  Training  bulletins

production.

of  the 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume  10  January,  1920  No.  1 


The  Year  in  the  Library 

DURING  the  current  year  the  Public  Library  passed  the  million  mark  in  the 
number  of  volumes  on  its  shelves,  the  millionth  volume  being  acquired 
some  time  in  September,  though  its  identity  cannot  be  exactly  determined.  At 
the  close  of  the  year  the  total  number  of  volumes  was  1,008,394.  Circulation 
of  books  to  home  readers  took  a  great  upward  leap  as  soon  as  the  war  stopped 
and  shows  a  total  of  7,509,257  volumes  circulated  from  the  main  library  and 
1,584  outlying  stations,  branches,  deposits,  etc.,  as  against  7,061,180  volumes  is- 
sued in  1918  through  1,260  agencies.  Our  work  with  the  public  schools  has  in- 
creased very  heavily,  thanks  to  the  hearty  cooperation  of  the  teachers.  There 
are  now  1,336  classroom  deposits  in  use  and  new  applications  are  received 
more  rapidly  than  they  can  be  filled.  The  amount  expended  for  books  during 
the  year  was  $110,000;  for  periodicals,  $11,000;  for  binding  and  repair  of  books, 
$45,000.  There  are  now  570  persons  on  the  staff,  with  an  annual  payroll  of 
over  $500,000. 

Chief  among  OUP-  projects  for  extension  is  the  new  Regional  Branch,  to  be 
named  after  the  late  Henry  E.  Legler,  librarian  from  1909  to  1917.  Work  on 
this  handsome  building,  located  at  West  Monroe  and  40th  Sts.,  was  begun  in 
March ;  the  corner  stone  was  laid  on  Memorial  Day,  and  in  spite  of  strikes  and 
unforeseen  delays,  the  building  will  be  finished  early  next  spring.  The  West 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Side  will  then  have  a  center  of  book  distribution  which  in  size,  scope,  and  extent 
of  service  will  be  second  only  to  the  Central  Library,  and  will  bring  the  Public 
Library  closer  to  the  citizens  of  that  section  than  was  ever  before  possible. 

Similar  Regional  Branches  on  the  North  and  South  Sides  are  included  in 
our  plans  for  the  future.  Indeed  our  plans  for  increasing  and  developing  the 
activities  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library  are  limited  only  by  the  size  of  our  bud- 
get. We  fully  realize  the  overwhelming  need  and  possibilities  of  such  develop- 
ment and  are  constantly  inspired  to  greater  efforts  by  the  many  evidences  of 
appreciation  that  come  to  us  from  our  large  and  ever  growing  host  of  patrons. 


Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.
