BOOK  BULLETIN 


PHILOSOPHY 

Baylor,  Courtenay.  Remaking  a  man;  one 
successful  method  of  mental  refitting. 
1919.  L 13204 

Relaxation  of  mental  tenseness  must  precede  the 
re-education  of  mental  processes.  Mr.  Baylor  shows 
how  the  diagnosis  is  arrived  at  and  gives  sugges- 
tions for  improving  the  thinking  process  until  nor- 
mality is  recovered  along  with  health.  Illustrative 
cases  are  cited  in  differing  types  of  neurosis. — 
Springfield  Republican. 

Dresser,  Horatio  W.  A  history  of  the  new 
thought  movement.  1919.  L  13214 

An  analysis  and  spiritual  interpretation  of  the 
modern  movement  as  one  of  many  liberalizing  tend- 
encies. Quimby's  healing,  the  beginnings  of  Chris- 
tian Science,  the  "mental  science  '  period,  the  de- 
velopment of  the  New  Thought  movement  (without 
definition)  here  and  abroad,  are  followed  in  chrono- 
logical order. 

King,  Basil.     The  abolishing  of  death.    1919. 

L  13498 

The  author  believes  he  has  received,  through 
automatic  writing,  messages  from  spirits  in  the  other 
world.  The  philosophy  communicated  by  the  leader 
of  these  spirits  is  given  in  detail.  It  is  very  much 
like  that  of  Margaret  Cameron's  Seven  purposes, 
L  13488. 

Radhakrishnan,  Sarvcpalli.  The  philosophy 
of  Rabindranath  Tagore.  1918.  L  14870 

Psychology,  Ethics 

Burger,  W.  H.   Boy  behavior.   1919.     L  13686 

A  small  psychology  of  boy  life.  The  conditions 
of  the  physical,  emotional  and  instinctive  life  of 
the  boy  are  simply  stated,  showing  the  importance 
of  muscular  development,  of  confidence  from  older 
people,  and"  of  preventing  repression.  Freud  and 
Adler  are  quoted  especially  for  their  suggestions  of 
treatment  of  the  abnormal  child. — Booklist. 

Chamberlain,  Arthur  Henry,  and  James  F. 
Thrift  and  conservation;  how  to  teach  it. 
1919.  L 14376 

(Lippincott's  educational   guides.) 

Essays  to  provide  the  teacher  with  suggestions  and 
data  on  the  subject  of  national  and  personal  econ- 
omy.— Survey. 

James,  William.     Habit.     1890.  L  13920 

Miinsterberg,  Hugo.     Business  psychology. 

1918.  L 13933 

(La  Salle  Extension  University,  Business  ad- 
ministration.) 

Ross,  J.  Elliot.  Christian  ethics;  a  textbook 
of  right  living.  1919.  L  14140 

Intended  for  use  in  Catholic  schools. 

White,  William  A.  The  mental  hygiene  of 
childhood.  1919.  L 13722 

(Mind  and  health  series.) 

A  rather  technical  discussion  on  Freudian  lines 
emphasizing  the  sexual  elements  in  the  child's  im- 
pulses and  the  normal  antagonisms  between  parents 
and  children. 

RELIGION 

Jastrow,  Morris,  jr.  Zionism  and  the  future 
of  Palestine;  the  fallacies  and  dangers  of 
political  Zionism.  1919.  A  2851 


Professor  Jastrow  sympathizes  with  the  effort  to 
establish  Jewish  colonies  in  Palestine,  but  does  not 
favor  its  reorganization  as  a  Jewish  state  with  a 
complete  control  involving  the  oppression  of  native 
nationalities. 


Kelman,    John. 
1919. 


The    war    and    preaching. 
M9338 


(Lyman  Beecher  lectureship  on  preaching  in  Yale 
university.) 

The  author,  who  spent  much  time  with  the  Y.  M. 
C.  A.  at  the  front,  urges  the  necessity  of  a  new 
and  vital  interpretation  of  Christianity  to  meet  the 
challenge  that  has  come  through  the  individual  ex- 
perience of  the  war.  The  ideal  preacher  should  be 
statesman,  priest,  and  prophet. 


Tiplady,  Thomas. 
1919. 


new  era. 


Social  Christianity  in  the 
M  4238 

Mr.  Tiplady  is  well  known  as  the  author  of  The 
cross  at  the  front  (A  6574),  and  The  soul  9f  the 
soldier  (A  6771),  written  while  a  chaplain  in  the 
war.  This  book  is  a  plea  for  a  league  of  churches 
which  he  considers  as  great  a  need  as  a  league  of 
nations.  He  points  out  many  fields  where  reform  is 
needed. 

The  Bible 

Bryant,  Sophie.  How  to  read  the  Bible  in 
the  twentieth  century.  1918.  M  6365 

Outlines  for  Bible  reading  and  suggestive  talks 
on  teaching  methods  and  interpretation,  with  a  dis- 
cussion of  the  development  of  Hebrew  genius  and 
of  the  historic  and  eternal  Christ  from  the  orthodox 
point  of  view. 

Soden,  Baron  Hermann  von.  Books  of  the 
New  Testament;  contributions  to  early 
Christian  literature.  1907.  M  6903 

Crown   theological   library,    v.    13. 

Stix,  Henry  S.  The  three  men  of  Judea: 
John,  Jesus,  and  Paul.  1917.  M  6901 

Readable  and  interesting  chapters  whose  substance 
reflects  the  viewpoint  of  the  Jew.  Written  with 
the  hope  of  mitigating  the  antagonism  between  Jew 
and  Christian,  which  the  author  believes  to  be 
largely  the  product  of  general  ignorance  of  religious 
history. — Booklist. 

Doctrinal  Theology 

Holmes,  John  M.  Jesus  and  the  young  man 
of  to-day.  1919.  M  7135 

Studies  on  the  life  of  Christ  intended  to  confirm 
the  faith  of  young  people  who  have  become  con- 
fused by  their  secular  education.  The  book  is 
clearly  written  and  contains  many  quotations. 

Macintosh,  Douglas  Clyde.  Theology  as  an 
empirical  science.  1919,  M  9518 

An  attempt  to  relate  theological  theory  to  the 
knowledge  of  the  divine  acquired  through  individual 
religious  experience,  and  to  show  how  this  knowl- 
edge may  be  formulated  and  inductively .  developed. 

Webb,  Clement  C.  J.     God  and  personality; 

being  the  Gifford  lectures  delivered  in  the 

University  of  Aberdeen  in  the  years  1918 

and  1919.  M  4743,  1918-19 

(Library  of  philosophy.) 

Willey,  John  Heston.  Betwen  two  worlds; 
the  new  day  and  the  old  questions.  1919. 

M4493 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


A  cheerful,  sensible  little  book  for  those  trou- 
bled with  doubts  about  immortality,  the  nature  of 
the  next  world  if  there  is  one,  the  horror  of  death. 
He  shows  that  pure  materialism  robs  life  of  its 
meaning  and  by  quotations  from  many  well-known 
writers  establishes  a  wholesome  faith  in  the  future. 
— Booklist. 

SOCIOLOGY 

Cannan,  Gilbert.  The  anatomy  of  society. 
1919.  L 15409 

Contents:  Definitions.  —  Humanity.  —  The  social 
contract.  —  Patriarchalism.  —  Marriage.  —  Women  as 
citizens. — Science  and  art. — Social  structure. — East 
and  west. — Democracy. 

Society's  first  step  toward  protecting  itself  against 
a  repetition  of  the  war  calamity  should  be  a  careful 
analysis  of  existing  human  institutions.  These 
should  then  be  so  reshaped  as  to  give  themselves 
to  the  needs  of  the  spirit  rather  than  to  the  de- 
mands of  a  sordid  and  mechanistic  age,  to  the  mak- 
ing of  men  rather  than  of  things. 

Phelan,  John  J.  Pool,  billiards  and  bowling 
alleys  as  a  phase  of  commercialized  amuse- 
ments in  Toledo,  Ohio.  1919.  L  15574 

(Social  survey  series  1.) 

A  survey  designed  to  lead  to  the  improvement  of 
bad  local  conditions.  The  summary  of  Ohio  laws 
and  of  various  city  ordinances  relating  to  the  sub- 
ject, together  with  outlines  and  questions  for 
surveys,  bibliographies,  etc.,  give  it  general  value 
as  a  social  service  textbook. 

Sumner,  William  Graham.  The  forgotten 
man,  and  other  essays.  1918.  L  15440 

Two  dozen  essays  for  the  most  part  on  national 
currency,  tariff,  and  industrial  organization,  written 
between  the  years  1876  and  1896,  with  the  exception 
of  one  which  appeared  ten  years  later. 

Statistics 

Dublin,  Louis  I.,  and  others.  Mortality  sta- 
tistics of  insured  wage-earners  and  their 
families;  experience  of  the  Metropolitan 
Life  Insurance  Company,  industrial  de- 
partment, 1911  to  1916,  in  the  United 
States  and  Canada.  1919.  L 15605 

West,  Carl  J.  Introduction  to  mathematical 
statistics.  1-918.  L 15612 

A  clear  exposition  of  the  best  methods  of  plotting 
data  and  smoothing  curves,  with  applications  to 
fluctuations  of  prices,  rainfall,  yield  of  crops,  etc., 
also  containing  a  welcome  chapter  on  the  method 
of  moments  and  its  application  to  curve  fitting. — 
American  Economic  Review. 

Economics 

American  Academy  of  Political  and  Social 
Science,  Philadelphia.  Industries  in  read- 
justment. 1919.  L  16604 

(Annals.) 

Contents:  Pt.  1.  Industries  in  readjustment.— 
2.  Capital  and  labor  in  readjustment. — 3.  Economic 
utilization  of  industrial  equipment. — -4.  Standardiza- 
tion of  industrial  equipment. — 5.  The  industrial  and 
financial  outlook. 

Goldstein,  Dr.  Joseph  M.     Russia,  her  eco- 
nomic past  and  future.     1919.  L  16642 
A  review  of  economic  conditions  put  out  by  the 
Russian   Information   Bureau  in   the   United   States. 

Neilson,  Francis.     The  old  freedom.     1919. 

L  16667 


Mr.  Neilson  discusses  socialism,  communism,  and 
syndicalism  as  attempts  to  repair  the  damage  that 
has  been  done  in  departing  from  the  old  freedom 
when  democracy  was  based  on  the  man  with  land 
to  till.  He  advocates  the  taxation  for  the  benefit 
of  the  community  of  values  created  by  it,  though 
he  does  not  use  the  term  "single  tax. ' 

Robertson,  J.  M.  The  economics  of  prog- 
ress. 1918.  L 16640 

Robinson,  Cyril  E.  New  fallacies  of  Midas; 
a  survey  of  industrial  and  economic  prob- 
lems. 1919.  L 16666 

One  of  the  sanest,  and  at  the  same  time  most 
attractive,  books  on  political  economy  that  we  have 
ever  read.  The  author  keeps  his  mind  firmly  fixed 
on  the  real  man  behind  the  abstractions  of  produc- 
tion, distribution,  and  consumption,  but  he  is  not 
led  astray  by  amiable  sentimentalities.  He  thinks 
strongly  and  writes  crisply. — Spectator. 

Shelby,  Gertrude  Mathews.  How  to  face 
peace;  a  handbook  of  community  pro- 
grams. 1919.  L 16672 

An  attempt  to  outline  general  principles  and 
specific  suggestions  for  co-operative  effort  toward 
post-bellum  readjustment.

encyclopedia,  reference  book,  prepared

Cohen,  Julius  Henry.  An  American  labor 
policy.  1919.  L 16755 

Mr.  Cohen,  formerly  counsel  for  the  Employers' 
Protective  Association,  advocates  in  the  present 
crisis  the  freedom  to  organize  for  both  employed  and 
employer,  the  registration  of  all  agreements  between 
them  with  a  national  labor  board,  and  their  enforce- 
ment by  a  tribunal  created  for  the  purpose. 

Stowell,  Charles  Jacob.  The  Journeymen 
Tailors'  Union  of  America;  a  study  in 
trade  union  policy.  1918.  L4079.  7IV 

(University  of  Illinois  studies  in  the  social  sci- 
ences.) 

Wood,  Edith  Elmer.  The  housing  of  the 
unskilled  wage  earner,  America's  next 
problem.  1919.  L 17059 

(American  social  progress  series.) 

Mrs.  Wood  investigates  housing  conditions  and 
legislation  here  and  abroad  and  outlines  a  housing 
policy  for  the  United  States  which  should  interest 
those  concerned  in  big  industrial  organizations.

Democracy 

Herron,  George  D.    The  greater  war.    1919. 

L  16323 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


On  the  ground  that  the  Peace  of  Paris  is  no 
peace  at  all,  Mr.  H'erron,  a  socialist,  asserts  that 
if  the  Allies  do  not  undertake  to  remake  it-  in 
the  interest  of  democracy,  Germany  will  do  so  in 
her  own  interest,  working  insidiously  through  in- 
ternational socialism. 

Mackinder,  Halford  J.  Democratic  ideals 
and  reality;  a  study  in  the  politics  of  re- 
construction. 1919.  L 15792 
Mr.  Mackinder  interprets  history  from  the  geo- 
graphical point  of  view.  He  asserts  that  the 
grouping  of  lands  and  seas,  and  of  fertility  and 
natural  pathways,  is  such  as  to  lend  itself  to  the 
growth  of  empires  and,  in  the  end,  of  a  single 
world  empire."

The  principles  of  our  government  are  simply  and 
clearly  presented  for  the  benefit  of  children  of

tacts of  their  daily  life.

League  of  Nations 

Duggan,  Stephen  Pierce,  and  others,  ed.  The 
league  of  nations,  the  principle  and  the 
practice.  1919.  L 17946 

Sixteen  _  chapters  by  experts  in  the  various  phases 
of  law,  history,  economics,  government,  and  other 
branches  of  knowledge  involved  in  international  co- 
operation. It  is  not  edited  to  meet  the  require- 
ments of  any  point  of  view  and  makes  a  good 
reference  work. 

Hill,  David  Jayne.  Present  problems  in  for- 
eign policy.  1919.  L 17944 

A  vigorous  criticism  of  the  league  of  nations 
on  the  ground  that  it  is  un-American,  prpvides  for 
war  rather  than  justice,  and  does  not  guarantee 
international  law.  The  volume  contains  copies  of 
the  "fourteen  points,"  the  covenant  of  February, 
1919,  the  senate  round-robin,  and  the  revised  cove- 
nant.

Education 

Alexander,  Hartley  Burr.  Letters  to  teach- 
ers, and  other  papers  of  the  hour.  1919. 

L 19362 

Professor  Alexander  of  the  University  of  Ne- 
braska is  seeing  to  it  that  educational  tradition 
there  shall  be  founded  on  a  spirit  of  liberalism  as 
far  as  he  can  influence  it.  His  letters  are  designed 
to  combat  the_  dangers  that  lurk  in  servility  to  texts 
and  methods,  in  a  standardized  curriculum,  and  gen- 
erally in  the  domination  of  the  letter  that  killeth.

Rowley,- Hannah  Teresa,  and  Farrell,  Helen

A  chemistry  text  and  laboratory  manual  in  one.

(The   University   of   Chicago    science   series.) 
An   untechnical   account    of   these    fernlike    plants

Downing,  Elliot  R.  A  field  and  laboratory 
guide  in  biological  nature-study.  1918. 

K 20682 

(The  University  of   Chicago   nature-study   series.) 
Companion    volume    entitled,    A    source    book    of 
biological   nature-study,   K  20681. 

Farwell,  Ellen  Drummond.  Bird  observa- 
tions near  Chicago.  1919.  K  22046 

Hudson,  W.  H.  The  book  of  a  naturalist. 
1919.  K  21595 

Delightful  informal  excursions  into  the  by-ways 
of  natural  history  in  which  few  of  the  small  denizens 
of  wood  and  field  appear  to  escape  the  affectionate 
eye  of  the  writer.  The  serpent  is  accorded  the  dis- 
tinction of  five  chapters. — Booklist. 

Long,  William  J.  How  animals  talk,  and 
other  pleasant  studies  of  birds  and  beasts. 
1919.  K  21605 

Pseudo-scientific  theories  clothed  with  poetic  senti- 
ment and  romance,  which  make  engaging  reading. 
The  writer  is  confident  that  animals  possess  the 
power  of  direct  communication  and  speculates  upon 
the  nature  of  that  power. — Booklist. 

Thomson,  J.  Arthur.  Secrets  of  animal  life. 
1919.  K  21616 

Lectures  to  senior  students  of  natural  history  in 
the  University  of  Aberdeen. 

Groups  of  essays  on  individual  adaptation,  inter- 
relations of  organisms,  development  and  behaviour, 
and  evolution  and  heredity  by  a  follower  of  Weis- 
mann  who  makes  the  most  occult  mysteries  of 
animal  life  a  fascinating  study  to  the  layman  and 
young  student. 

USEFUL  ARTS 
Inventions 

Bond,  A.  Russell.  Inventions  of  the  great 
war.  1919.  K  22447 

Accurate  information  given  in  popular  style  by 
the  editor  of  the  Scientific  American.  The  in- 
ventions include  the  new  methods  of  attack  and 
defense  brought  about  by  the  use  of  trench  war- 
fare, the  increased  effectiveness  of  firearms  both 
small  and  large,  poisonous  gases,  tanks,  air-planes 
and  submarines.

Remington,  Joseph  P.,  and  Cook,  E.  Fuller- 
ton.  The  practice  of  pharmacy;  a  treatise 
on  the  modes  of  making  and  dispensing 
official,  unofficial,  and  extemporaneous 
preparations,  with  descriptions  of  medici- 
nal substances,  their  properties,  uses,  and 
doses,  intended  as  a  hand-book  for  phar- 
macists and  physicians  and  a  text-book  for 
students.  1917.  N  1708 

The  sixth  edition  of  this  invaluable  handbook. 

Vedder,  Edward  B.  Syphilis  and  public 
health,  published  by  permission  of  the 
surgeon-general,  U.  S.  A.  1918.  N  1853 

It  gives  statistics  on  the  prevalence  of  syphilis 
in  other  countries  and  in  connection  with  other  dis- 
eases; discussion  of  the  sources  of  infection  and 
methods  of  transmission;  personal  prophylaxis;  pub- 
lic health  measures;  and  an  appendix  of  tests.  — 
Booklist. 

Wright,  Florence  Swift.  Industrial  nursing, 
for  industrial,  public  health,  and  pupil 
nurses,  and  for  employers  of  labor.  1919. 

N2759 

The  book  gives  an  account  of  the  development  of 
industrial  nursing  and  of  its  elementary  principles, 
and  outlines  the  qualifications  and  training  of  the 
industrial  nurse,  her  relations  with  the  community 
and  her  fellow  workers,  the  day's  work,  and  what 
the  profession  offers  to  nurses.  There  are  rules  for 
first  aid  in  the  appendix.

Benedict,  Francis  G.,  and  others.  Human 
vitality  and  efficiency  under  prolonged  re- 
stricted diet.  1919.  K  15961,  280 


(Carnegie    Institution    of    Washington. 
tion.) 


Publica- 


Paget, Stephen.   Adolescence.    1919.     N  1326 

In  a  delightful  informal  lecture  delivered  to  Ox- 
ford University  extension  students,  this  renowned 
English  physiologist  outlines  his  ideas  with  regard 
to  teaching  children  about  sex  and  helping  adoles- 
cents to  face  its  problems. 

Proudfit,  Fairfax  T.  Dietetics  for  nurses. 
1918.  N  2491 

The  main  discussion  is  of  food,  its  sources,  com- 
position, and  nutritive  value;  the  effect  of  food  in 
the  body  under  normal  conditions,  and  in  disease. 
Especially  good  on  typhoid  fever.  —  Booklist. 

Williams,  Jesse  Feiring.  Healthful  living, 
based  on  the  essentials  of  physiology  for 
high  school  pupils.  1919.  N  64 

Beginning  with  a  microscopic  study  of  the  cells 
of  the  body,  the  whole  human  anatomy  is  briefly 
described  and  clearly  illustrated  with  continuous 
emphasis  on  hygiene.  —  Booklist.

Agriculture 


Arnold,  Jacob  Hiram. 
1919. 


Farm  management. 
K  23831 


A  definite  program  of  organization,  financing,  and 
juipment    for    aerial    traffic    based    on    the    convic- 
tion   that    an    air    service    of    rapid    transit    can    be 
made  safe,   reliable,  and  profitable. 


eq 


Describes  the  modern  farm  clearly  and  concisely; 
explains  its  processes,  and  discusses  its  relations  to 
business  and  to  the  state. 

Fredericksen,  Johan  Ditlev.  The  story  of 
milk.  1919.  K  24489 

Deals  with  milk  from  the  milking  of  the  cow  to 
its  use  in  cooking,  covering  among  other  details 
the  action  and  control  of  ferments  and  bacteria, 
pasteurization,  and  the  manufacture  of  dairy  prod- 
ucts. 

Pearce,  Joseph  A.  The  Pearce  new  method 
of  beekeeping.  1918.  K  24532 

Stimson,  Rufus  W.  Vocational  agricultural 
education  by  home  projects.  1919. 

K 23911 

The  Massachusetts  state  supervisor  of  vocational 
agricultural  education  here  describes  fully  and  in 
an  interesting  way  the  system  of  home  projects 
which  is  his  -  state's  special  contribution  to  the 
teaching  of  agriculture. 

Domestic  Economy 

Frederick,  Mrs.  Christine.  Household  engi- 
neering; scientific  management  in  the 
home.  A  correspondence  course  on  the

1919.  K  24585 

Business,  Commerce 

Marcosson,  Isaac  F.  Peace  and  business. 
1919.  L  20126 

The  problem  of  industrial  and  economic  read- 
justment from  the  point  of  view  of  commercial  ex- 
pansion. The  Allies  are  expected  to  divide  among 
themselves  Germany's  former  markets,  and  she  is 
to  be  kept  "economically  curbed"  that  the  war  may 
remain  won. 

Pepper,  Charles  M.  American  foreign  trade; 
the  United  States  as  a  world  power  in  the 
new  era  of  international  commerce.  1919. 

L  20136 

In  the  new  international  struggle  involved  in 
competitive  world  trade  the  author  believes  the 
United  States  should  stand  as  the  great  exponent 
of  economic  individualism. 

Savay,  Norbert.  Principles  of  foreign  trade. 
1919.  L  20140 

Shorthand,    Typewriting 
Business  Writing 

Fritz,  Rosa  L.,  and  others.  Essentials  of 
expert  typewriting;  a  short  course  in  touch 
typewriting.  1919.  K  24905 

Gardner,  Edward  Hall.  Constructive  dicta- 
tion; "plan  your  letter."  1919.  J5928 


The  structure  of  a  good  business  letter  for  the 
purpose  of  developing  shorthand  speed  and  econ- 
omizing time  is  taught  by  illustrations  from  sample 
letters  of  business  houses.  An  appendix  contains 
glossaries  of  terms  and  tables  of  rules,  abbrevia- 
tions, etc. 

Palmer,  A.  N.  The  Palmer  method  of  busi- 
ness writing;  a  series  of  self-teaching  les- 
sons in  rapid,  plain,  unshaded,  coarse-pen, 
muscular-movement  writing.  1913. 

K  24928 

Scientific    Management 
Industrial    Efficiency 

Duncan,  Carson  S.  Commercial  research; 
an  outline  of  working  principles.  1919. 

L 20411 

A  non-technical  treatise  intended  to  assist  the 
average  merchant  to  replace  guesswork  as  a  basis 
for  his  buying  and  selling  by  facts  obtained  by 
scientific  methods  of  research.  Chapters  on  col- 
lecting data  and  how  to  use  them. 

loteyko,  Josefa.  The  science  of  labour  and 
its  organization.  1919.  L  20410 

(Efficiency  books.) 

Link,  Henry  C.  Employment  psychology; 
the  application  of  scientific  methods  to 
the  selection,  training,  and  grading  of  em- 
ployes. 1919.  L 20319 

The  tests  here  presented  were  devised  by  Dr. 
Link  in  industrial  plants  under  actual  working  con- 
ditions. The  truly  scientific  basis  of  his  work  is 
not  to  be  confused  with  the  pseudo-psychology  fad  of 
character  reading  followed  by  many  business  men 
of  to-day. 

Meyer,  Charles  A.  Mercantile  credits  and 
collections.  1919.  L  20320 

Explains  the  fundamental  principles  of  credit, 
methods  of  securing  reliable  information,  the  func- 
tion of  credit  grantors,  passing  judgment  on  orders, 
systems,  unique  methods  of  preventing  losses,  the 
operation  of  your  own  collection  agency,  and  the 
bankruptcy  law. 

Western  Efficiency  Society,  Chicago.  The 
human  factor  in  industrial  preparedness; 
report  of  the  proceedings  of  the  national 
conference,  Chicago,  May  23,  24,  25,  1917! 

L  20318

K  25072 

A  good  general  course  including  explanation  of

Chemical  Technology 
Metallurgy 

Calvert,  Albert  F.  Sak  and  the  salt  indus- 
try. 1919.  K  25413 

(Pitman's    common    commodities    and    industries.) 
Presents    some    account    of    the    chemistry    of    salt 
and  the   history   of  the   industry,   followed   by   a   dis- 
cussion of  processes  and  deposits  relating  specifically 
to  the  salt  district  of  Cheshire,  England. 


Fay,  Irving  W.  The  chemistry  of  the  coal- 
tar  dyes.  1919.  K  25533 

Mitchell,  C.  Ainsworth.  Edible  oils  and 
fats.  1918.  K  25466 

(Monographs  on  industrial  chemistry.) 
Treats    of    the    chemical    composition    and    prop- 
erties of  oils  and  fats,  their  extraction,  purification, 
hydrogenation,   and   the   manufacture  of  margarine. 

Oberg,  Erik,  and  Jones,  Franklin  D.  Iron 
and  steel;  a  treatise  on  the  smelting,  re- 
fining, and  mechanical  processes  of  the 
iron  and  steel  industry,  including  the 
chemical  and  physical  characteristics  of 
wrought  iron,  carbon,  high-speed  and 
alloy  steels,  cast  iron,  and  steel  castings, 
and  the  application  of  these  materials  in 
machine  and  tool  construction.  1918. 

K  25656 

Sibley,  Robert,  and  Delany,  Chas.  H.  Ele- 
ments of  fuel  oil  and  steam  engineering; 
a  practical  treatise  dealing  with  fuel  oil, 
for  the  central  station  man,  the  power 
plant  operator,  the  mechanical  engineer, 
and  the  student.  1918.  K  25356 

First  an  exposition  of  the  elementary  laws  of 
steam  engineering;  second,  the  processes  involved 
in  the  utilization  of  fuel  oil  in  the  modern  power 
plant;  and,  third,  the  testing  of  boilers  when  oil 
fired. — Booklist. 

Warnes,  Arthur  R.  Coal  tar  and  some  of 
its  products.  1919.  K  25586 

(Pitman's  common  commodities  and  industries.) 
Simply  and  without  the  use  of  technicaj  formulas 
the  author  follows  an  interesting  historical  intro- 
duction with  an  endeavor  to  explain  to  the  ordinary 
business  man  the  processes  by  which  dyes,  perfumes, 
ammonia,  explosives,  benzol,  carbolic  acid,  naphtha, 
saccharine,  etc.  are  obtained  from  coal  tar. 

Manufactures,  Trades 

Hall,  A.  Neely.  Carpentry  and  mechanics 
for  boys,  up-to-the-minute  handicraft.. 
1918.  K 26149 

Treats  of  work  for  the  home  workshop,  backyard, 
camp,  and  garden,  and  is  illustrated  with  over  700 
drawings,  diagrams,  and'  halftones.  Attention  is 
given  the  mechanics  of  the  air,  sea,  undersea,  and 
garden  as  they  were  developed  by  the  war. 

Johnson,  Emil  A.  Furniture  upholstery  for 
schools.  1919.  K  26073 

A  manual  for  school  shops.  After  a  brief  his- 
torical sketch  and  a  preliminary  chapter  on  •  tools 
and  materials,  the  book  is  divided  into  two  sections 
dealing  respectively  with  upholstery  without  springs 
and  upholstery  with  springs. 

Kozmin,  Peter  A.  Flour  milling;  a  theo- 
retical and  practical  handbook  of  flour 
manufacture  for  millers,  millwrights,  flour- 
milling  engineers,  and  others  engaged  in 
the  flour-milling  industry.  1919.  K  25922 

Levi,  Louis  E.,  and  Orthmann,  Aug.  C. 
Laboratory  manual  of  Pfister  &  Vogel 
leather  company  laboratories.  1918. 

K 25779 

A  guide  for  chemists  in  leather  laboratories,  de- 
scribing methods  of  analyzing  leather  and  the  ma- 
terials used  in  its  manufacture.

FINE  ARTS 

Anderson,  Paul  L.  The  fine  art  of  photog- 
raphy. 1919.  K  29424 
Points  out  the  underlying  principles  of  art  that 
apply  to  photography;  and  gives  helpful  suggestions 
on  position,  values,  mystery,  winter  work,  motion 
pictures,  illustrations,  portraiture,  and  landscape, 
architectural,  and  marine  photography. 

French,  Thomas  E.,  and  Svensen,  Carl  L.

questions  and  answers;  a  ready  reference 
for  those  who  are  building,  remodeling, 
furnishing,  decorating  or  gardening.  1918.

Music 

Cooke,  James  Francis.  Music  masters  old 
and  new;  a  series  of  educational  biog- 
raphies of  the  greatest  musicians  from 
Bach  and  Handel  to  the  present  time,  pre- 
pared for  individual  reading  and  self-study 
as  well  as  for  use  in  clubs  and  history 
classes.  1918.  Music  Room

with  master  violinists  and  teachers.  1919. 

K  29978 

Interviews  with  twenty-four  violinists — including 
Ysaye,  Kreisler,  Elman,  Heifetz  and  Maud  Powell — 
introduced  by  biographical  notes  and  adorned  with 
sixteen  portraits. 

Amusements 

Elsom,  J.  C.,  and  Trilling,  Branche  M.  So- 
cial games  and  group  dances;  a  collection 
of  games  and  dances  suitable  for  com- 
munity and  social  use.  1919.  K  30025 

Contains  music  for  the  dances. 

A  comprehensive  and  clearly  written  book  con- 
taining (1)  chapters  on  acquaintance  games,  games 
of  tag,  games  with  pencil,  and  stunts;  (2)  descrip- 
tion of  group  dances  and  the  music  for  them;  (3) 
description  of  children's  singing  games  and  dances, 
with  music. 

Athletics  and  Outdoor  Sports 

Fedden,  Romilly.  Golden  days,  from  the 
fishing-log  of  a  painter  in  Brittany.  1919. 

K  30821 

Recollections,  written  at  the  front,  of  dallying 
hours  spent  by  little  streams  and  rivers  of  Brittany 
in  the  golden  days  of  peace.  There  are  many  items 
of  angler's  lore  but  a  great  deal  also  of  the  quaint 
folklore  and  artistic  beauties  of  the  country. 

Grey,  Zane.  Tales  of  fishes.  1919.  K  30823 
Stirring  tales  of  battles  of  wit  and  muscle  with 
cunning,  powerful,  and  rare  game  fishes  of  the 
Pacific  coast,  and  of  angling  in  the  Gulf  Stream 
and  along  the  coast  of  Florida,  written  by  the  fish- 
erman-novelist.— Booklist. 

Jacornb,  William  J.  Boxing  for  beginners, 
with  chapter  showing  its  relationship  to 
bayonet  fighting.  1918.  K  30641 

Lessons  consisting  mainly  of  photographs  ac- 
companied by  a  few  explanatory  remarks. 

Practical   self-defence.     1918.       K  30642 


Intended  principally  for  use  against  those  who 
attack  without  regard  to  the  rules  of  boxing. 

Miller,  Warren  H.  Canoeing,  sailing  and 
motor  boating;  practical  boat  building 
and  handling.  1919.  K  30741 

A  wise  and  racily  written  treatise  for  the  not 
too  green  amateur  waterman.  The  author  has 
evidently  had  broad  experience  in  all  three  sports, 
and  what  is  more  unusual  has  reflected  upon  his 
experience. — Dial. 

LITERATURE 

Eagle,  Solomon,  pseud,  of  J.  C.  Squire. 
Books  in  general.  1919.  J  4807 

Snatches  of  entertaining  literary  gossip  sug- 
gested by  such  subjects  as  a  recent  book  of  Mrs. 
Barclay,  a  request  from  the  trenches  for  an  opinion 
on  the  three  most  lasting  books  in  literature,  Mr. 
Archer's  tirade  on  music-hall  songs,  or  the  alleged 
obscurity  of  Henry  James. 

Gordon,  George,  pseud,  of  Charles  C.  Bald- 
win. The  men  who  make  our  novels. 
1919.  J7110 

(Modern  American  writers.) 

Biographical  outlines  and  some  quoted  critical 
comment  on  forty-seven  American  men  novelists. 

Mencken,  H.  L.  Prejudices:  first  series 
1919.  J  6439 

Keen  and  stimulating  essays  combating  many  cur- 
rent prejudices.  Howells  is  condemned,  and  Wells 
in  his'  recent  work.  George  Ade  is  glorified,  Mary 
MacLane  is  pitied;  Bernard  Shaw  is  called  "the 
Ulster  Polonius,"  Hamlin  Garland  "a  stranger  on 
Parnassus."  Henry  Sydnor  Harrison,  "a  merchant 
of  mush,"  etc. 

Mordell,  Albert.  The  erotic  motive  in 
literature.  1919.  J  4808 

A  Freudian  discussion  of  the  significance  of  the 
sex  motive  in  literature,  whether  expressed  or 
omitted. 

Waugh,  Arthur.  Tradition  and  change; 
studies  in  contemporary  literature.  1919. 

J6356 

Discerning  papers  on  literary  topics.  The  style 
is  smooth  a_nd  mellow,  in  keeping  with  the  writer's 
tolerant  spirit.  He  holds  that  youth  must  revolt 
or  there  would  be  no  progress,  but  that  youth  must 
also  accept  something  from  tradition  or  its  literature 
will  perish  with  its  own  generation.  He  has  more 
sympathy  with  the  recent  developments  in  the  novel 
than  with  the  new  poetry.

Kemp,  Harry.     The  passing  god;  songs  for

BOOK  BULLETIN 


11 


The  first  poem  is  based  on  the  classical  theme 
of  Cressid,  and  the  author  acknowledges  his  debt 
to  the  15th  century  tale  of  Robert  Henry  son.  The 
rest,  the  New  York  Times  says,  are  "pleasant,  fre- 
quently felicitous  lyrics  upon  the  single  topic  of 
love." 

McClure,  John.     Airs  and  ballads.     1918. 

E  14858 

Masefield,  John.     Reynard  the  fox;  or,  The 

Ghost  Heath  run.     1919.  E  11240 

One  of  the  most  English  poems  ever  written,  an 

epic   of   the   soil   and   of   those   who    gallop   over   it. 

The   narrative  of  a  day's  hunting. — London 

Times. 

Speaight,  Frank.    My  Dickens  friends.    1916. 

El  1542 

The  Yale  book  of  student  verse,  1910-1919; 
ed.   by  John  Andrews   and  others.     1919. 

E  14314 

The  poems  by  Stephen  Vincent  Benet  are  dis- 
tinctly the  best  in  the  volume.  He  alone  displays 
the  fine  enthusiasm  of  youth. — Dial. 

Drama,  Theater 

Belasco,    David.      The    theatre    through    its 
stage  door.     1919.  E  13489 

The  well-known  manager  and  playwright  here 
tells  how  a  play  is  evolved  and  produced,  how 
actors  are  chosen  and  trained,  and  what  mechanical 
devices  are  used  to  enhance  the  beauty  of  dramatic 
production.  He  also  considers  the  problem  of  the 
child  actor. 


Craig,   Edward    Gordon, 
vancing.     1919. 


The 


theatre — ad- 
E  11957 


A  collection  of  brief  theatrical  criticisms  written 
for  the  most  part  before  the  war.  It  comments 
on  the  use  and  abuse  of  the  properties  of  the  theatre, 
the  possibility  of  a  permanent  theatre,  the  ab- 
surdity of  a  really  perishable  theatre,  etc. 

Harvard  University.  Dramatic  club.  Plays 
of  the  Harvard  dramatic  club.  1918-1919. 
2v.  E 12296 

One  act  plays. 

v.  1.  The  florist  shop,  by  Winifred  Hawkridge. 
The  bank  account  by  H.  Brock.  The  rescue,  by 
Rita  C.  Smith.  America  passes  by,  by  K.  Andrews. 

2.  The  harbor  of  lost  ships,  by  Louise  W.  Bray. 
Garafelia's  husband,  by  E.  W.  Bates.  The  scales 
and  the  sword,  by  F.  Bishop.  The  four-flushers, 
by  C.  Kinkead. 

Matthews,  Brander.  The  principles  of  play- 
making,  and  other  discussions  of  the 
drama.  1919.  E  14059 

Prof.  Matthews  says  that  dramatic  writing  must 
be  guided  by  the  principles  of  the  art  rather  than 
mere  technical  rules  which  are  more  or  less  change- 
able. He  emphasizes  the  importance  of  the  audience 
which  requires  that  a  play  appeal  to  collective  human 
nature-; — a  closet  drama  being  in  his  opinion'  an 
absurdity. 

Plays 

Gordon,  Leon.  The  gentleman  ranker,  and 
other  plays.  1919.  E  12267 

Three  actable  little  plays  with  good  dialogue  by 
an  English  actor  and  playwright.  "The  gentleman 
ranker"  is  a  four-scene  play  of  the  war  laid  in 
German  Southwest  Africa;  "As  a  pal"  is  a  cockney 
comedy;  and  "Leave  the  woman  out,"  in  which  a 
clever  cracksman  matches  wits  with  a  keen  de- 
tective recalls  Gillette's  interpretation  of  Sherlock 
Holmes. 


MacKaye,  Percy,  and  Barnhart,  Harry.  The 
will  of  song;  a  dramatic  service  of  com- 
munity singing.  1919.  E  14055 
For  use  as  a  two  days'  song  festival,  in  two 
parts;  part  i:  Soul  of  earth;  part  n:  Soul  of  light. 
A  two-part  dramatic  ritual  of  community  singing 
designed  to  arouse  the  desire  for  group  expres- 
sion and  activity  in  the  American  people.  It  is 
"the  first  work  in  its  field  to  utilize  the  untrained 
singing  of  the  people  as  a  structural  part  of  its 
artistry." 

Mygatt,  Tracy  D.     Good  Friday;  a  passion 
play  of  now.     1919.  E  140-71 


Porter,  A.  Kingsley. 
1919. 


The  seven  who  slept. 
E  14195 


A  drama  centering  about  the  return  to  Ephesus 
of  one  of  the  seven  sleepers  who  finds  Christianity 
established  in  the  world  but  the  world  no  nearer 
perfection  than  in  the  days  when  he  and  his  Chris- 
tian companions  had  fled  to  escape  persecution. 

Tarkington,   Booth,   and  Wilson,   Harry    L. 
The  Gibson  upright.     1919.  E  12890 

In  this  play  a  capitalist  surrenders  his  piano  fac- 
tory to  his  workmen  and  it  goes  to  pieces  almost 
immediately  through  their  ignorance  and  bad  man- 
agement. Neither  a  serious  problem  play  nor  a 
farce. 

Walker,   Stuart.      More    portmanteau    plays. 
1919.  E  12978 

Contents:  A  lady  of  the  weeping  willow  tree. — 
The  very  naked  boy. — Jonathan  makes  a  wish. 

The  first  is  founded  on  the  old  Japanese  legend 
of  a  human  soul  imprisoned  in  a  tree;  the  second 
is  a  delightful  bit  of  nonsense;  the  third  is  a  sym- 
pathetic and  artistic  record  of  a  dreamy  boy  eager 
to  write  plays  whose  stubborn  and  well-meaning 
uncle  is  determined  to  make  him  an  engineer. 

Essays 

Duhamel,    Georges.      The    heart's    domain. 
1919.  E  2528 

The  author  is  a  French  physician  who  emerges 
from  four  years  of  war  with  this  gospel  of  broad 
and  gentle  humanity  in  which  happiness  is  to  be 
found  in  contact  with  man  and  with  nature.  He 
has  also  written  Civilization,  F  12599,  and  The  new 
book  of  martyrs,  A  6732. 

Gardiner,  Alfred  G.     Leaves  in  the  wind,  by 
Alpha  of  the  plough.     1919.  E  3089 

Informal  sketches  of  commonplace  subjects  full 
of  good  sense  and  good  humor.  Appearing  weekly 
in  the  dailies  throughout  the  last  two  years  of  the 
war,  they  cannot  but  reflect  incidentally  some  of  its 
emotional  aspects. 

1919. 
E4271 

A  miscellany  from  the  author's  urbanely  gossipy 
department  in  the  New  Republic,  a  department  the 
more  delightful  for  being  rather  less  bookish  than 
thingy. — Dial. 

Maeterlinck,     Maurice.       Mountain     paths. 
1919.  E  4467 

Smooth  and  well-written  essays,  like  all  of  Maeter- 
linck's, covering  a  wide  variety  of  subjects  in- 
cluding life  after  death,  German  atrocities,  and 
Fabre's  work  in  natural  history. 


Littell,  Philip.     Books  and  things. 


12 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


TRAVEL  AND  DESCRIPTION 

Clark,  Francis  E.  Our  Italian  fellow  citi- 
zens in  their  old  homes  and  their  new. 
1919.  1 10444 

The  author's  desire  is  to  give  Americans  an  in- 
telligent and  sympathetic  acquaintance  with  the  char- 
acter and  traditions  of  the  many  Italians  who  have 
come  and  will  continue  to  come  to  our  shores  from 
every  province  of  Italy. 

Collins,  Joseph.  My  Italian  year;  observa- 
tions and  reflections  in  Italy  during  the 
last  year  of  the  war.  1919.  I  10443 

The  author  has  had  excellent  opportunities  for 
studying  the  social  life  and  customs,  national  char- 
acteristics, and  political  institutions  of  Italy,  and 
he  gives  much  useful  information. 

Fitch,  R.   Louise.     Madame   France.     1919. 

16867 

An  interesting  record  of  woman's  public  and 
private  war  work  in  France  founded  on  the  author's 
personal  survey  of  conditions. 

Forbes,  Rosita.  Unconducted  wanderers. 
1919.  I  11391 

A  dramatic  account  of  the  travels  of  two  English 
women  in  America  and  eastern  Asia  which  the 
Booklist  characterizes  as  "amusing,  astonishing,  and 
flippant." 

Hershey,  Amos  S.,  and  Susanne  W.  Modern 
Japan,  social — industrial — political.  1919. 

110693 

(Problems  of  the  nations.) 

Every  aspect  of  Japanese  life  is  treated  in  this 
book,  which  is  based  on  years  of  study  of  Japan 
and  is  intended  to  help  toward  a  just  estimate  of 
the  land  and  people  as  they  really  are. 

Sedgwick,  Anne  Douglas.  A  childhood  in 
Brittany  eighty  years  ago.  1919.  I  6877 
A  charming  mosaic  of  childhood  recollections 
of  life  in  an  out-of-the-way  corner  of  old  France, 
rich  in  legends  and  tenacious  of  its  inherited  customs 
and  loyalties. — Dial. 

United  States 

Abdy,    H.    Bennett.      On    the    Ohio.      1919. 

14354 


Paris,  John  T.     Seeing  Pennsylvania.     1919. 

14888 

The  state  is  divided  so  as  to  make  eight  delight- 
ful routes  for  travelers.  The  book  contains  appro- 
priate historical  reminiscences,  and  the  descriptions 
of  scenic  beauty  are  accompanied  by  good  photo- 
graphs. 

Henderson,  Helen  W.  A  loiterer  in  New 
York;  discoveries  made  by  a  rambler 
through  obvious  yet  unsought  highways 
and  byways.  1917.  14920 

Moore,  William  Henry.  The  clash!  a  study 
in  nationalities.  1919.  14182 

A  careful  presentation  of  the  conflict  between  the 
Protestant  English-speaking  and  Catholic  French- 
speaking  Canadians,  which  is  not  without  applica- 
tion to  Ireland,  Poland,  and  possibly  to  the  United 
States.  Mr.  Moore  advocates  the  recognition  of 
large  national  groups  instead  of  attempted  assimila- 
tion. 


Ross,  Edward  Alsworth.  What  is  America? 
1919.  1 4315 

A  short,  extremely  condensed  exhibit  of  the 
United  States,  its  racial  make-up,  social  and  politi- 
cal institutions,  rural  and  urban  conditions,  its 
labor,  and  its  cultural  standards. — Survey. 

Young,  S.  Hall.  Adventures  in  Alaska. 
1919.  1 4438 

The  missionary  labors  of  an  "Alaska  sky  pilot" 
in  the  gold-rush  days.  His  ministry  was  among 
Eskimos,  Indians,  trappers,  gamblers,  gold  diggers 
of  every  degree,  and  professional  men.  Remark- 
able adventures  and  hardships  are  related  which 
betray  in  spirit  and  incident  a  strong  and  winning 
personality. 

BIOGRAPHY 

Fales,   De    Coursey.     The   Fales   family    of 
Bristol,   Rhode   Island;  ancestry  of   Hali- 
burton  Fales  of  New  York.    1919.    C  20254 
Contains  also  fifty  pages  of  Haliburton  genealogy. 
Fiske,    Bradley    A.      From    midshipman    to 
rear-admiral.     1919.  C21910 

Autobiography  of  a  naval  officer  who  has  con- 
tributed largely  to  the  invention  of  naval  and  mili- 
tary appliances,  as  well  as  to  the  literature  of  his 
profession.  Relates  his  efforts  to  further  pre- 
paredness for  the  war. 

Grenfell,  Wilfred  Thomason.  A  Labrador 
doctor;  an  autobiography.  1919.  C  22179 
Permits  a  figure  of  wide  and  admirable  notoriety 
to  tell  the  story  of  his  life  from  the  first  youth- 
ful roamings  across  the  sands  of  Dee  to  his  ad- 
venturous ministrations,  as  medical  missionary, 
along  the  bleaker  coast  of  Labrador. — Dial. 

Henschel,  Sir  George.  Musings  and 
memories  of  a  musician.  1918.  C  22413 
Delightful  memoirs  of  one  of  the  most  versatile 
of  contemporary  musicians,  including  reminiscences 
of  many  interesting  people,  Burne-Jones,  Whistler, 
Browning,  Paderewski,  Patti,  Jenny  Lind,  Liszt  and 
others. 

Repington,  Lieut.  Col.  Charles  a  Court. 
Vestigia;  reminiscences  of  peace  and  war. 
1919.  C  23911 

The  author  was  a  British  officer  through  the 
second  Afghan  war,  the  Burmese  war,  the  Atbara 
and  Omdurman  campaigns,  and  the  South  African 
war,  and  has  served  also  in  the  British  intelligence 
department  and  as  military  correspondent.  His 
dramatic  narrative  ends  in  19-14  at  the  outbreak 
of  the  greatest  war  of  all. 

Speer,  Robert  W.  Robert  W.  Speer,  a  city 
builder;  published  by  the  authority  of  the 
Council  of  the  city  and  county  of  Denver. 
1919.  C  24327 

HISTORY 

Europe 

Stephanove,  Constantine.  The  Bulgarians 
and  Anglo-Saxondom.  1919.  A  5239 

Turner,  Edward  Raymond.  Ireland  and 
England  in  the  past  and  at  present.  1919. 

A  5518 

A  thoroughly  informed  history  of  Ireland's  politi- 
cal and  economic  struggle  with  England  from  its 
beginning  to  the  end  of  the  European  war.  There 
are  also  chapters  on  Irish  language  and  literature 
and  •  the  modern  revival  of  Celtic  culture. 


BOOK   BULLETIN 


13 


European  War 

Benwell,  Harry  A.  History  of  the  Yankee 
division,  with  appreciations  by  General 
John  J.  Pershing,  Major-General  Clarence 
R.  Edwards  and  Secretary  of  war  Newton 
D.  Baker.  1919.  A  6985 

A  record  of  official  orders  given  and  movements 
made  day  by  day,  producing  a  fine  sense  of  actual 
operations. 


Biddle,   Charles  J. 
1919. 


The  way  of  the  eagle. 
A  7033 


Letters  from  an  American  ace  in  the  Lafayette 
escadrille,  giving  mechanical  and  tactical  details 
of  air  fights  and  flights. 

Carlo,   Camillo   de.     The  flying  spy.     1919. 

A  7032 

The  diary  of  an  Italian  aviator  who  flew  into  the 
Austrian  lines  in  search  of  information  with  regard 
to  the  enemy's  movements.  A  spirited  narrative 
of  adventures. 

Clover,  Greayer.  A  stop  at  Suzanne's,  and 
lower  flights.  1919.  A  7037 

A  memorial  volume  of  lively  sketches  and  letters 
written  by  a  young  American  who  served  in  the 
French  camion  service  and  later  with  the  American 
aviators,  together  with  tributes  to  the  author  in 
prose  and  verse. 

Dennett,  Carl  P.  Prisoners  of  the  great 
war;  authoritative  statement  of  conditions 
in  the  prison  camps  of  Germany.  1919. 

A  7031 

Statement  by  the  American  Red  Cross  deputy 
commissioner  to  Switzerland  in  charge  of  finding, 
feeding,  clothing  and  otherwise  caring  for  American 
prisoners  in  German  prison  camps.  Describes  the 
organization  of  the  service,  the  agreements  with  the 
enemy,  and  gives  exciting  details  of  many  prison 
escapes. — Booklist. 


Ely,    Dinsmore. 
served.     1919. 


Dinsmore    Ely,    one   who 
A  7041 


Diary  letters  edited  by  his  father,  Dr.  James  O. 
Ely. 

Gilliland,  Horace  Gray.  My  German 
prisons;  the  story  of  my  two  and  a  half 
years  of  captivity  in  Germany  and  my 
final  escape,  November  14,  1914-April  8, 
1917.  1919.  A  7040 

In  his  introduction,  Mr.  James  W.  Gerard,  for- 
mer ambassador  to  Germany,  commends  the  book  to 
the  perusal  of  "those  who  are  inclined  to  forgive 
an  unrepentant  Germany." 

Harden,  Elmer  Stetson.  An  American  poilu. 
1919.  A  7029 

Letters  from  an  American  who  early  enlisted  in 
the  French  army.  The  pen  pictures  are  vivid 
and  artistic  and  the  author  retains  something  of 
the  civilian's  cynicism  toward  the  paradoxes  of 
war. 

Hurd,  Archibald,  and  Bashfprd,  H.  H.  The 
heroic  record  of  the  British  navy;  a  short" 
history  of  the  naval  war,  1914-1918.  1919. 

A  7034 

A  popular  history  in  narrative  form.  It  is  much 
less  technical  and  statistical  than  Kurd's  The 
British  fleet  in  the  great  war,  A  6890,  or  Pollen's 
British  navy  in  battle,  A6920. 


Kelly,  Florence  Finch.  What  America  did; 
a  record  of  achievement  in  the  prosecution 
of  the  war.  1919.  A  7028 

A  series  of  chapters  each  dealing  concisely  and 
comprehensively  with  a  separate  war  activity.  It 
'is  written  in  a  delightful  style  giving  it  a  true  nar- 
rative interest. 

Kummer,   Frederic  Arnold.     The  battle   of 
the  nations,  1914-1918;  a  young  folks'  his- 
tory of  the  great  war.     1919.  A  7038 
Will  also  serve  as  a  clear  and  simple  review  of 
the  war  for  busy  adults. 

McKinley,  Albert  E.,  and  others.  A  school 
history  of  the  great  war.  1918.  A  7035 

Red  Cross.  American  National  Red  .Cross. 
The  work  in  Europe  of  the  American  Red 
Cross;  a  report  to  the  American  people 
by  the  Red  Cross  War  Council.  1917. 

A  7027 

With  this  is  bound  the  Report  by  the  War  Coun- 
cil of  appropriations  and  activities  from  outbreak 
of  war  to  Nov.  1,  1917;  and  the  Financial  state- 
ment of  Red  Cross  War  fund,  Mar.  1,  1918,  with 
details  of  the  various  activities  through  which  this 
fund  is  distributed. 

Sanborn,  Joseph.  The  131st  U.  S.  Infantry 
(First  Infantry  Illinois  National  Guard)  in 
the  world  war.  1919.  *A  6982 

"Narrative — operations — statistics,  general  narra- 
tive by  Colonel  Sanborn;  special  narratives  by  offi- 
cers commanding  various  units  and  detachments; 
operations  comp.  by  Capt.  George  N.  Malstroni; 
statistics  by  the  Personnel  Adjutant's  office,  with 
maps,  photographs  taken  on  various  battle  fields, 
photographs  of  officers,  rosters,  etc." 

Sibley,  Frank  P.  With  the  Yankee  division 
in  France.  1919.  A  6984 

A  careful  description  of  the  operations  of  the 
twenty-sixth  division  at  Chemin  des  Dames,  Chateau- 
Thierry,  Belleau  Wood,  St.  Mihiel,  etc.,  at  the 
same  time  retaining  the  perspective  of  the  larger 
movements  of  the  armies. 

Tompkins,  Raymond  S.     The  story  of  the 

Rainbow  division.     1919.  A  6983 

The   story    has    been    told    accurately,    fully,    and 

absorbingly. — Maj.   Gen.  Menoher  in  the  Foreword. 

Vignes  Rouges,  Jean  des,  pseud,  of  Capt. 
Taboureau.  Bourru,  soldier  of  France. 
1919.  A  7039 

A  historical  .and  psychological  study  outlined  with 
characteristic  Gallic  keenness  and  delicacy  of  touch. 
Its  glimpses  of  the  war  have  not  been  surpassed 
in  the  horrors  of  their  realism,  or  the  tenderness  of 
their  sympathetic  feeling. 

Waugh,  Alec.  The  prisoners  of  Mainz. 
1919.  A  7036 

A  spirited  account  by  a  young  British  lieutenant 
of  how  the  tedious  hours  of  eight  months  in  a  Ger- 
man prison  were  whiled  away.  Good  natured  and 
entertaining,  illustrated  with  pen  sketches  as  well 
as  photographs. 

Woollcott,  Alexander.  The  command  is 
forward;  tales  of  the  A.  E.  F.  battlefields 
as  they  appeared  in  the  Stars  and  Stripes. 
1919.  A  7030 

Dramatic,  pointed,  slangy,  touching  and  funny, 
as  the  war  picture  happens  to  appear  at  the  moment. 
Illustrated  with  pen  and  ink  sketches. — Booklist.

CHICAGO    PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


FICTION 

Bassett,  Sara  Ware.    The  harbor  road.    1919. 

F 10858 

A  story  of  life  in  a  New  England  seaport  town 
told  in  a  quiet  and  leisurely  way. 

Begbie,  Harold.     The  convictions  of  Chris- 
topher Sterling.     1919.  F 10984 
A   pacifist   novel   which   is   rather   unconvincing. 

Black,  Alexander.     The  great  desire.     1919. 

F 11202 

A  story  dealing  with  the  every-day  happenings  in 
the  life  of  a  brother  and  sister  who  go  from  a 
New  England  town  to  New  York  in  search  of  ad- 
venture. It  has  the  charm  of  literary  novelty  and 
very  uncommon  power. 

Blythe,  Samuel  G.    Hunkins.    1919.      F  11273 
Hunkins  is  an  old  time  political  boss  and  he  un- 
dertakes   to    organize    the    returned    soldiers    of    his 
city   to   turn   out  the  corrupt  bosses.     How   he  suc- 
ceeds makes  a  good   political  story. 

Cummins,  Geraldine  D.  The  land  they 
loved.  1919.  F  12218 

A  picture  of  Irish  life  which  emphasizes  the  in- 
fluence of  love  of  country  upon  the  characters.  It 
is  pleasantly  told. 

Dawson,  Coningsby.  The  test  of  scarlet;  a 
romance  of  reality.  1919.  F  12353 

Vivid  pictures  of  the  endless  marches,  the  cold, 
the  thirst,  the  despairing  courage,  the  universal 
.presence  of  death.  They  are  half-connected  episodes 
leading  up  to  the  time  just  before  the  signing  of 
the  armistice. 

Day,  Holman.  The  rider  of  the  king  log;  a 
romance  of  the  northeast  border.  1919. 

F 12295 

A  logging  story  in  the  Maine  woods  somewhat  like 
King  Spruce  (F  12365).  Full  of  incident  and  good 
description. 

Dunbar,  Ruth.  The  Swallow;  a  novel  based 
upon  the  actual  experiences  of  one  of  the 
survivors  of  the  famous  Lafayette  esca- 
drille.  1919.  .  F  12534 

The  thrilling  experiences  of  a  young  aviator  in 
the  war  told  in  his  own  language. 


Harry,  Myriam,  pseud. 
of  Jerusalem.     1919.

The  liberal-minded  and  loving  but  meddlesome

Hergesheimer,  Joseph.    Linda  Condon.  1919. 

F 13700 

A  book  whose  workmanship  and  artistry  are 
supreme.  Linda  Condon  is  an  odd  child  who  lives 
apart  from  life,  yet  inspires  others  to  great  idealism. 
It  is  by  far  the  best  book  of  this  author. 

Hueston,  Ethel.     Leave  it   to  Doris.      1919. 

F 14153 

A  story  that  will  appeal  to  girls. 

Kenyon,  Camilla.    Spanish  doubloons.    1919. 

F 14465 

Treasure  hunting  on  a  desert  island  is  an  old 
theme  but  this  quest  for  treasure  has  a  modern 
twist  and  makes  pleasant  reading. 

Lardner,   Ring  W.     Own  your  own   home. 
1919.  F  14635 

Fred  Gross  had  been  reading  the  signboards  and 
he  decided  to  see  how  it  worked.  He  tells  in  a 
very  funny  way  the  difficulties  of  building  a  home 
in  the  suburbs. 

Oemler,  Marie  Conway.     A  woman  named 
Smith.     1919.  F 15738 

One  of  the  most  delightful  stories  of  the  year,  its 
vitality  is  so  invigorating,  its  plot  so  interesting, 
and  its  characters  so  charming.  The  woman  named 
Smith  falls  heir  to  an  old  Carolina  mansion  with  all 
its  traditions,  ghosts,  and  mysteries. 


Roe,    Vingie 
1919. 


E. 


Tharon    of    Lost    Valley. 
F  17418 


A  well  written  story  of  a  small  town  in  the  heart 
of  the  desert  country  of  New  Mexico  and  its  de- 
liverance from  a  band  of  outlaws. 


A  story  of  the  Mexican  border  in  which  a  girl 
undertakes  the  management  of  her  father's  ranch 
after  his  death,  and  the  protection  of  her  fellow 
ranchers  from  the  maraudings  of  an  outlaw. 

Rohmer,    Sax,    pseud,    of   Arthur   S.    Ward. 
Dope.     1919.  F 18954 

An  expose  of  the  opium  traffic  in  London. 

Rolland,   Remain.      Colas    Breugnon.      1919. 

F 17414 

Colas  Breugnon  is  a  jolly  Burgundian  peasant 
who  tells  of  the  joys  of  his  daily  life. 

Ruck,    Berta,    (Mrs.    Oliver    Onions).      The 

disturbing  charm.     1919.  F  15769 

A    pleasant    story    of    a    Welsh    girl    who    uses    a 

love    charm    on    some    of    her    friends.      The    charm 

works,   but  not  altogether,  as   she   had  expected. 

Walpole,  Hugh.     Jeremv.     1919.          F  18948 

By  some  magic  Hugh  Walpole  in  this  delightful 
story  of  a  little  boy  has  made  to  live  again  the 
childhood  that  we  all  knew  and  to  which  we  all 
turn  back  with  longing. 

Williams,  Ben  Ames.     The  sea  bride.     1919. 

F 19516 

A  tale  of  the  cruise  of  a  whaler  in  the  South 
Sea  hunting  grounds,  of  the  mutiny  of  the  crew, 
and  how  the  captain's  wife  brings  the  vessel  safely 
home  after  his  death. 

Wodehouse,   Pelham    Granville.      A    damsel 
in  distress.     1919.  F  19701 

A  book  for  the  tired  business  man.  It  is  full 
of  gaiety  and  delightfully  impossible.  As  usual  a 
vigorous  young  American  stirs  up  the  atmosphere 
in  a  quiet  conventional  English  hamlet.

Bdtsford,  Charles  A. 
S.  army.     1919. 

(Victory   series,   v.    2.) 

Second  book  in  a  series  of  which  "Joining  the 
colors"  is  the  first.  Two  boys  join  the  A.  E.  F. 
after  having  served  with  Canadian  forces.  Methods

Burton,  Charles   Pierce. 
1919. 


The  trail  makers. 
H7635 


Two  boy  scouts  are  spending  their  summer  vaca- 
tion in  a  railroad  construction  camp  at  the  time  that 
war  is  declared.  The  book  is  full  of  exciting  ad- 
ventures including  the  detection  of  a  huge  bomb 
plot.  It  promises  to  be  as  popular  as  the  "Bobs 
Hill"  series. 


Colver,    Alice    Ross. 
1919. 


Babs    at    Birchwood. 
H7715 


The  second  book  of  the  "Babs"  series.  When 
Babs  finds  herself  in  her  cousin's  spacious  home  in 
Birchwood,  she  wonders  how  she  is  going  to  like 
her  year  in  civilization.  She  enters  whole-heartedly 
into  club  and  other  activities  and  makes  the  most 
of  a  happy  year  in  the  city. 

Curtis,  Alice  Turner.  A  little  maid  of  old 
Philadelphia.  1919.  H  7745 

Another  "Little  Maid"  book,  describing  the  life 
in  Philadelphia  at  the  time  when  the  city  was  in 
the  hands  of  the  British  army.  Two  patriotic 
girls  prove  of  great  service  to  the  American  army. 

Gray,  Violet  Gordon.  Margery  Morris, 
mascot.  1919.  H  8281 

The  second  of  the  Margery  Morris  series. 
Margery's  attempt  to  bring  happiness  into  the  lives 
of  others  turns  out  to  be  not  so  simple  as  it  seems. 
Her  final  resolution  to  stick  it  out  brings  her  suc- 
cess and  happiness  once  more. 

Normand,  Charles.  The  emerald  of  the 
Incas;  a  story  of  the  Peruvian  Sierras. 
1917.  H  5303 

An  intensely  interesting  story  of  South  Amer- 
ica with  a  number  of  Oriental  characters  whose  ex- 
periences will  interest  boys  and  girls  between  the 
ages  of  twelve  and  fourteen. 

Pier,  Arthur  Stanwood.  The  Hilltop  troop. 
1919.  H  5688 

A  boy  scout  troop  plays  a  vital  part  in  the  uniting 
of  two  factions  in  a  village. 

Price,  Edith  Ballinger.     Blue  magic.     1919. 

H  5806 

Fen  is  a  little  crippled  child  whose  parents  have 
taken  him  to  Egypt  by  the  order  of  his  physician. 
His  utter  lonesomeness  is  remedied  by  a  friend  in 
the  guise  of  a  Djinn,  whose  magic  amuses  and  de- 
lights little  Fen  and  makes  life  very  much  worth 
living. 

Sabin,  Edwin  L.  Lost  with  Lieutenant  Pike. 
1919.  H  6328 

(The  trail  blazers  series.) 

"How  from  the  Pawnee  village  the  boy  named 
Scar  Head  marched  with  the  young  American  chief 
clear  into  the  snowy  mountains,  how  in  the  dead  of 
winter  they  searched  for  the  lost  river  and  thought 
that  they  had  found  it,  and  how  the  Spanish  soldiery 
came  upon  them  and  took  them  down  to  Santa  Fe 
of  New  Mexico,  where  another  surprise  awaited 
them." 

Saunders,  Marshall.  Golden  Dicky;  the  story 
of  a  canary  and  his  friends.  1919.  H  6386 

An  entertaining  story  of  bird  life  and  bird  ways 
told  by  Dicky-Dick,  a  canary.  In  this  story,  Miss 
Saunders  has  enlarged  her  range  of  humane  in- 
terests in  the  animal  world  to  include  the  feathered 
world  as  well.

The  Book  Bulletin 


of  the 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume   10  February,  1920  No.  2 


Books  of  1919 

THE  list  of  books  following  these  paragraphs    represents,    not    the    "Best 
Books"  of  the  year,  but  merely  those  which,  out  of  all  that  swam  within 
our  ken  during  the  twelve  months,  have   proven  most  useful  and  important, 
judged    by    such    standards    of   selection    and    experience   as   we   have   at   our 
command. 

The  process  of  book  selection  in  a  large  public  library  is  perhaps  a  more 
serious  and  careful  one  than  it  is  commonly  understood  to  be.  It  generally 
begins  with  the  actual  reading  or  scanning  of  the  book,  supplemented  by  an 
examination  and  checking  of  reviews.  The  factor  of  probable  public  interest 
and  demand  is  approximated  by  means  of  conferences  of  department  heads 
and  branch  librarians  most  competent  by  contact  with  the  library  constituency 
to  form  an  opinion.  In  these  semi-monthly  conferences  a  decision  is  reached 
as  to  whether  (a)  a  given  book  shall  be  bought;  (&)  how  many  copies  shall 
constitute  the  initial  purchase;  and  (c)  to  what  departments,  branches,  or 
other  agencies  these  copies  shall  be  assigned.  An  order  for  sixty  to  a  hun- 
dred copies  of  a  ne\v  book  is  rather  more  frequent  than  an  order  for  one 
copy,  and,  in  general,  purchases  range  widely  between  these  two  figures.  The 
whole  number  of  new  books  acquired  during  the  year,  therefore,  represents 
the  result  of  critical  judgments,  carefully  compiled  and  digested,  plus  an  esti- 


18 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


mate  as  to  probable  public  demand  formulated  as  above  described.  There 
remains  the  final  verdict  rendered  by  the  public  itself  as  to  the  validity  of 
the  choice  thus  arrived  at. 

The  monthly  lists  in  these  Book  Bulletins  show  the  whole  number  of 
books  added.  The  list  below  comprises  those  which  have  received  the  public 
approval  of  most  frequent  and.  persistent  use.  It  is  thus  a  list  of  "Best  Books" 
only  in  the  sense  that  it  presents  the  most  useful  books,  or  the  most  used 
books  at  any  rate,  as  determined  through  the  spontaneous  suffrage  of  a  large 
army  of  library  patrons  whose  judgment  we  have  every  reason  to  receive 
with  respect.  As  such  it  should  be  of  interest  to  the  patrons  of  the  library 
at  large.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.   *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

narratives.

PHILOSOPHY 

Jung,  Carl  G.,  ed.  Studies  in  word-associa-

matical philosophy.  1919  K  18788 
(Library  of  philosophy,  ed.  by  J.  H.  Muirhead.) 
A  discussion  of  the  main  results  of  symbolic  logic 
in  its  bearing  upon  the  more  scientific  parts  of 
the  principles  of  mathematics. 

Spiritualism 

Bond,  Frederick  Bligh.  The  hill  of  vision; 
a  forecast  of  the  great  war  and  of  social 
revolution  with  the  coming  of  the  new 
race,  gathered  from  automatic  writings 
obtained  betwen  1909  and  1912,  and  also, 
in  1918,  through  the  hand  of  John  Al- 
leyne,  under  the  supervision  of  the  author. 
1919.  A  7045 

Cumberland,  Stuart.  That  other  world; 
personal  experiences  of  mystics  and  their 
mysticism.  1918.  L  13482 

Animism 

Gilmore,  George  William.  Animism;  or 
Thought  currents  of  primitive  peoples. 
1919.  L  13047 

An  interesting  study  of  the  logic  of  animistic  con- 
ceptions, showing  the  correctness  of  the  mental 
processes  of  primitive  man  when  once  his  premise 
is  granted. 

Character  Analysis 
Conduct  of  Life

alyzing character.     1919.  L  13761 
Principles  formulated  by  the  author  on  the  basis 
of  many  years  of  experience  as  employment  adviser. 

Parlette,   Ralph  Albert.     The  university  of 

hard   knocks;    the   school   that   completes 

our  education.     1917.  L  14126 

The    fourth    edition    of    a    popular    book    on    the 

lessons  of  adversity  in  the  laboratory  of  experience. 

RELIGION

ligion. 1919.  L  19913 

(The  Abingdon  religious  education  texts.  Teacher 
training  series.) 

By  far  the  best  book  we  have  as  yet  on  how  to 
teach  the  child  religion.  .  .  .  How  not  to 
teach  religion  in  the  Sunday  school  and  the  home 
as  well  is  here  set  forth  in  many  impressive  con- 
trasts.— Outlook.

21 


Hough,  Lynn  Harold.  The  productive  be- 
liefs. 1919.  M  4736,  1919 

(The  Cole  lectures  for  1919,  delivered  before 
Vanderbilt  University.) 

Lectures  by  the  presid_ent  of  Northwestern  Uni- 
versity intended  to  outline  on  an  orthodox  basis 
an  expression  of  Christian  faith  which  shall  meet 
the  insistent  demand  of  the  present  age  for  produc- 
tive results. 

Kelly,  Caleb  Guver.  French  Protestantism, 
1559-1562.  1918.  *L  3201,  36 

(Johns  Hopkins  University  studies  in  history  and 
political  science.) 

Contents:  Social  and  economic  forces. — The  re- 
sources of  the  Huguenots. — The  organization  of  the 
Calvinists. — The  reform  at  its  height. — Friends  and 
foes  at  home  and  abroad. — Guise  or  Valois? — The 
arsenal  of  Protestantism. 

Peck,  W.  G.  The  coming  free  Catholicism. 
1919.  M  7855 

The  projected  program  will  incorporate  into  Chris- 
tianity both  the  element  of  natural  religion  ex- 
pressed in  Catholic  ritual  and  the  intellectual  and 
spiritual  freedom  which  Protestantism  has  exerted. — 
American  Journal  of  Theology. 

SOCIOLOGY 

Chancellor,  William  Estabrook.  Educa- 
tional sociology.  1919.  L  15293 
A  popular  treatment  which  covers  the  subject 
quite  fully  and  more  readably  than  many  others  of 
its  kind.  Views  social  movements  from  an  edu- 
cational standpoint,  in  a  general  introductory  sur- 
vey.— Booklist. 

Cram,  Ralph  Adams.    Walled  towns.     1919. 

L  15411 
A  plea  for  constructive  reform. 

Economics 

Garvin,  J.  L.  The  economic  foundations  of 
peace;  or,  World-partnership  as  the  truer 
basis  of  the  league  of  nations.  1919. 

L  16623 

Much  of  the  extraordinary  economic  machinery  de- 
vised to  win  the  war  "must  be  retained  during  the 
period  of  reconstruction,  and  some  corresponding 
system  of  economic  cooperation  must  be  made  a 
permanent  and  indispensable  part  of  any  league 
of  nations." 

Gebhard,  Hannes.  Co-operation  in  Fin- 
land. 1916.  L  17533 

Middleton,  P.  Harvey.  Industrial  Mexico; 
1919  facts  and  figures.  L  16668 

Its  accumulated  evidence  of  commercial  possi- 
bilities and  of  the  willingness  of  the  present  gov- 
ernment to  modernize  the  institutions  of  the  re- 
public supports  the  movement  in  this  country  for 
friendlier  relationships  on  the  basis  of  open  and 
honest  dealings  between  private  citizens. — Survey. 

Labor 

Cole,  G.  D.  H.  An  introduction  to  trade 
unionism;  being  a  short  study  of  the 
present  position  of  trade  unionism  in 
Great  Britain,  prepared  for  the  trade 
union  survey  of  the  Labour  research  de- 
partment. 1918.  L  17192 

(Fabian  Society,  London.  Trade  union  series, 
no.  4.) 

Mr.  Cole  suggests  a  plan  for  an  easy  transition 
from  present  industrial  conditions  through  trade 
unionism  to  the  formation  of  national  guilds,  which 
he  regards  as  equally  removed  from  state  socialism 
and  the  pre-war  system  of  wage  exploitation. 


Labour-  in   the   commonwealth;  a  book 

for  the   younger  generation.     1919. 

L  16932 

(The    new    commonwealth    books.) 

A  critical  review  of  present  industrial  and  social 
conditions  from  the  standpoint  of  guild  socialism. 
Mr.  Cole  distinguishes  between  state  and  common- 
wealth, desiring  for  the  latter  an  interrelation  of 
institutions  subordinated  to  the  will  of  the  indi- 
vidual. 

Hunter,  Robert.  Bolshevism  and  the  labour 
movement.  1919.  L  17589 

Maclver,  R.  M.  Labor  in  the  changing 
world.  1919.  L  16948 

A  statement  of  the  liberal  view  of  the  labor  prob- 
lem and  its  solution,  advocating  a  more  cooperative 
rather  than  competitive  order  of  production  in 
which  there  shall  be  neither  "prodigal  waste  nor 
sheer  degrading  poverty." 

Renard,  Georges.  Guilds  in  the  middle 
ages.  1918.  L  17886 

Finance,  Tariff 

Browne,  Scribner,  and  others.  Practical 
points  on  stock  trading.  1918.  L  17449 

Koehler,  George.  Importers'  first  aid  in 
American  tariff  and  customs  procedure. 
1919.  Civics  Dept. 

Selden,  George  C.  The  A  B  C  of  bond 
buying;  how  the  ordinary  investor  may 
judge  bond  values.  1919.  L  17470 

Political  Science 

The  Aga  Khan  (Sultan  Muhammad  Shah). 
India  in  transition;  a  study  in  political 
evolution,  by  His  Highness  the  Aga 
Khan.  1919.  L  11838' 

A  loyal  conservative  Indian  subject  criticizes  the 
weak  points  of  British  rule,  especially  with  regard 
to  education.  He  thinks  there  is  great  need  for 
radical  changes,  which  should  be  brought  about  by 
fitting  the  masses  for  representative  government. 

Hobhouse,  L.  T.  The  metaphysical  theory 
of  the  state;  a  criticism.  1918.  L  15781 

(London  school  of  economics  and  political  science. 
Studies.) 

Mainly  a  criticism  of  the  Hegelian  theory  of  the 
state  and  a  critique  of  Bosanquet's  work,  particu- 
larly of  his  Philosophical  theory  of  the  state,  L  6412. 

Weber,  Gustavus  A.  Organized  efforts  for 
the  improvement  of  methods  of  admin- 
istration in  the  United  States.  1919. 

L  18078 

Published  with  the  purpose  of  making  available 
information  regarding  governmental  and  private 
agencies  for  investigating  extravagant  and  inefficient 
machinery  and  methods  of  administration,  local, 
state,  and  national,  and  of  furnishing  constructive 
suggestions. — Booklist. 

Democracy 
Desmond,  Shaw.     Democracy.     1919. 

F  12415 

A  young  Irish  writer  expounds  his  views  on 
democracy  and  the  present  political  situation  in 
Ireland  and  England. 

Mallock,  W.  H.  The  limits  of  pure 
democracy.  1918.  L  15901 

Careful,  conscientious,  and  sincere — albeit  in- 
tolerant— discussion  of  our  social  and  economic 
world.  To  the  reactionary  it  will  prove  that  all  is 
well;  to  the  progressive,  an  excellent  mental  exercise, 
but  it  offers  no  peril  to  liberal  views. — Survey.

CHICAGO    PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


White,  Bouck.  The  free  city;  a  book  of 
neighborhood.  1919.  L  15835 

Contrasts  the  advantages  of  municipal  govern- 
ments with  the  evils  of  national  politics  of  today 
and  advocates  a  chain  of  small  republics  as  the 
formula  for  extending  democracy  over  wide  areas. 

Negroes 

Blascoer,  Frances.  Colored  school  children 
in  New  York.  1915.  L  16167 

Immigration  and  Americanization 

Bogardus,  Emory  S.  Essentials  of  Amer- 
icanization. 1919.  L  16046 
The  author  writes  from  his  experience  as  a  settle- 
ment worker  and  believes  that  if  the  immigrant  is 
protected  from  exploitation  and  given  better  stand- 
ards of  living  and  education  he  will  automatically 
become  a  good  American. 

Brooks,  Charles  Alvin.  Christian  American- 
ization; a  task  for  the  churches.  1919. 

L  16047 

The  author  is  a  secre  -ry  of  the  Baptist  home 
mission  society.  He  define  Americanization  as  the 
achievement  of  national  unity  for  service  upon  the 
plane  of  the  highest  ideal-.,  and  recognizes  the  im- 
portance to  this  end  of  a  common  language. 

Foerster,    Rcl>-;rt    F.      The    Italian    emigra- 
tion of  oir  times.     1919.  L  6587,  20 
(Harvard   economic   studies,     v.   20.) 
A  thorough  survey  of  the  greatest  migratory  move- 
ment of  our  times,  studying  the  conditions  in  Italy 
which  gave  it  rise  and  following  the  emigrant  into 
Europe,  Africa,  South  America,  and  especially  with 
much  detail  into  the  United  States. 

International   Relations 

Chamberlain,  Thomas  G.  Why  we  fought. 
1919.  L  17943 

Short  studies  of  the  provisions  of  the  covenant  of 
the  League  of  Nations,  together  with  the  text  of  the 
covenant  and  addresses  in  its  favor.  It  is  prefaced 
by  a  foreword  by  Mr.  Taft. 

Law 

Hall,  Fred.  S.,  and  Brooke,  Elisabeth  W. 
American  marriage  laws  in  their  social 
aspects;  a  digest.  1919.  *N  5488 

This  work  is  divided  into  three  parts  dealing  with 
(1)  suggested  proposals  for  marriage  reforms,  (2) 
state  laws  summarized  by  topics,  and  (3)  a  digest 
of  marriage  laws  by  states.  The  Survey  regards  it 
as  a  "valuable  and  much  needed  contribution  to 
social  science." 

Reed,  John  C.  Conduct  of  lawsuits  out  of 
and  in  court:  practically  teaching,  and 
copiously  illustrating,  the  preparation  "and 
forensic  ma'.ngement  of  litigated  cases  of 
all  kinds;  be  ng  a  new  ed.  of  "Practical 
suggestions,"  rev.  and  rewritten.  1912. 

N  5465 

Smith,  Reginald  Heber.  Justice  and  the 
poor;  a  study  of  the  prescrt  denial  of 
justice  to  the  poor  and  of  the  agencies 
making  more  equal  their  position  before 
the  law,  with  particular  reference  to  legal 
aid  work  in  the  United  States.  1919. 

L  10643,  13 

(The  Carnegie  foundation  for  the  advancement  of 
teaching.  Bulletin.) 


Exhaustive  inquiry  by  a  member  of  the  Boston 
bar  into  the  methods  of  civil  and  criminal  courts 
in  the  different  states  by  which  justice  is  supposedly 
obtained  for  the  poor.  The  author  concludes  that 
the  poor  are  at  a  disadvantage  before  the  law, 
analyzes  the  causes,  and  suggests  remedies. 

Practical  Sociology 
Social   Survey 

Bonser,  F.  G.  School  work  and  spare  time. 
1918.  L19370,  28 

(Cleveland  foundation.     Publications.) 
Cleveland   recreation   survey. 

Colcord,  Joanna  C.  Broken  homes;  a  study 
of  family  desertion  and  its  social  treat- 
ment. 1919.  L  20771 

(Social   work  series.) 

A  study  of  exceptional  penetration  and  thorough- 
ness, in  which  a  clear  insight  into  human  personality 
and  relationships  and  a  warm  appreciation  of  human 
values  combine  with  an  accurate  knowledge  of  the 
various  implications  of  the  problem  of  family  de- 
sertion and  the  skilled  practice  of  a  highly  de- 
veloped social  case-worker. — Survey. 

Gillin,  John  L.  Wholesome  citizens  and 
spare  time.  1918.  L  19370,29 

(Cleveland  foundation.     Publications.) 

Grant,  Robert.     Law  and  the  family.     1919. 

L  20971 

These  essays  were  written  by  a  judge  of  the 
probate  court  in  Boston  and  refer  particularly  to 
the  legal  status  of  woman  in  Massachusetts,  though 
showing  also  the  general  tendency  of  the  age  toward 
a  broadening  interpretation  of  the  law. 

Education 

Blanton,  Margaret  Gray,  and  Smiley,  Blan- 
ton.  Speech  training  for  children;  the 
hygiene  of  speech.  1919.  L  19614 

Practical  helps  for  the  correct  development  of 
the  speech  organs  and  processes.  The  suggestions 
are  excellent  for  early  home  training  but  helpful 
also  for  the  teacher  of  children  who  have  missed 
its  advantages. 

Dell,  Floyd.    Were  you  ever  a  child?     1919. 

L  19373 

Mr.  Dell  thinks  that  the  present  system  of  edu- 
cation has  failed  to  fit  the  child  to  meet  the 
realities  of  life,  and  he  contends  in  a  clear-cut, 
humorous  way  for  a  new  system  which  will  con- 
sider the  facts  of  the  child's  future. 

Dooley,  William  H.  Principles  and  methods 
of  industrial  education  for  use  in  teacher 
training  classes.  1919.  L  19568 

Contains  chapters  on  the  value  of  material  edu- 
cation, the  educational  needs  of  industries,  how  men

of  industrial  schools  and  courses;  also  methods  of 
teaching  shop-work,  shop  science,  English,  etc.

century.  1918.  '  J  6351 

A  brief  survey  of  the  century  as  divided  into

earlier  Victorian,  1830-1872;  the  later  Victorian, 
1872-1900.  Most  valuable  for  its  notes  on  minor 
writers  to  whom  the  author  gives  special  attention.

Payne,  E.  Ursula.  Plays  and  pageants  of 
democracy.  1919.  E  14185 

Contents:  The  vision  of  Columbus. — At  the  gate
