Patriotic  plays  for  the  upper  grades.  They  are 
especially  suited  for  school  use  since  a  large  num- 
ber of  characters  is  desirable  for  the  production  of 
four  of  them. 

Essays 

Bergengren,  Ralph.  The  perfect  gentleman. 
1919.  E  1905 

Turns  a  humorous  plow  into  familiar  furrows, 
shedding  ephemeral  freshness  on  such  eternal 
themes  as  lying  in  bed,  shaving,  dressing,  and  vis- 
iting the  barber.  In  the  fashioning  of  essays,  Mr. 
Bergengren  strives  for  the  droll  rather  than  the 
durable. — Dial. 

Holliday,  Robert  Cortes.  Broome  Street 
straws.  1919.  E  3469 

Charmingly  written  sketches  in  the  vein  of  the 
Walking-stick  papers  (E3467),  but  of  somewhat  un- 
even merit.  The  title  of  the  collection  is  remi- 
niscent of  the  author's  early  boarding-house  days. 

Howe,  E.  W.  Ventures  in  common  sense. 
1919.  E  3465 

(The  free  lance  books,  n.) 

Apothegms  collected  from  E.  W.  Howe's  Monthly 
and  prefaced  by  H'.  L.  Mencken.  They  deal  cleverly 
and  succinctly  with  such  subjects  as  women,  poli- 
tics, the  poor,  business,  literature,  fools,  war,  critics, 
and  advertising.  Something  on  the  order  of 
Nathan's  Book  without  a  title  (E4691)  and  Menck- 
en's Damn!  (E4475). 

Jenkins,  MacGregor.  Literature  with  a 
large  L  and  Fellow  travelers.  1919. 

E  3840 

Two  essays  in  a  gently  satirical  vein.  The  first 
deplores  the  sacredness  of  the  literature  cult,  and 
the  second  sums  up  the  essential  task  of  educa- 
tion as  the  imparting  of  optimism^  helpfulness,  faith, 
and  enthusiasm  in  the  child  mind. 

Overton,  Grant  M.  Why  authors  go  wrong 
and  other  explanations.  1919.  J  7385 

A  justification  of  literary  critics — especially  when 
connected  with  the  daily  press — in  catering  to  the 
cheaper  elements  of  popular  taste. 

BIOGRAPHY 

Barnctt,  Samuel  A.  Barnett,  Mrs.  Henrietta 
O.  Canon  Barnett,  warden  of  the  first 
University  Settlement,  Toynbee  Hall, 
Whitechapel,  London;  his  life,  work,  and 
friends,  by  his  wife.  1919.  2  v.  C  20750 
The  record  of  an  energetic  human  life  lived  in 

simplicity    and    greatness    where    it    counted    most. 

All   the   powers   of   Barnett's  life   are  united  in   his 

endeavor  to  bring  to  the  east  end  of   London   the 

highest  values  of  life. — Nation. 

Browne,  Charles  Farrar.  Seitz,  Don  C. 
Artemus  Ward  (Charles  Farrar  Browne); 
a  biography  and  bibliography.  1919. 

C  21023 

An  excellent  biography  long  meditated  and  amply 
documented.  It  has  been  a  Tabor  of  love  and  Mr. 
Seitz  has  spared  no  pains  to  dig  the  facts  out 
of  the  files  of  newspapers  and  magazines,  to  set 
them  in  order  and  to  arrange  them  in  a  seemly 
narrative. — Brander  Matthews  in  New  York  Times. 

Clarke,  Sir  Edward.     The  story  of  my  life. 

1919.  C  21269 

Comiskev,  Charles  A.  Axelson,  G.  W.   "Com- 

my."  ~  1919.  C  21328 


"The  life  story  of  Charles  A.  Comiskey,  the 
'Grand  Old  Roman'  of  baseball  and  for  nineteen 
years  president  and  owner  of  the  White  Sox." 

Dante  Alighieri.  Dinsmore,  Charles  Allen. 
Life  of  Dante  Alighieri.  1919.  C  21503 

A  more  comprehensively  biographical  and  his- 
torical treatment  than  Grandgent  s  Power  of  Dante 
(E  10824),  against  the  background  of  Florence  and 
thirteenth  century  Europe. 

Dundee,  John  Graham  of  Claverhouse,  1st 
Viscount.  Harrington,  Michael.  Grahame 
of  Claverhouse,  Viscount  Dundee.  1911. 

C  21660 

A  handsome  volume  dealing  with  exciting  events 
in  the  history  of  Scotland  during  the  latter  half  of 
the  seventeenth  century. 

Forsyth,  Christina.  Livingstone,  W.  P. 
Christina  Forsyth  of  Fingoland;  the  story 
of  the  loneliest  woman  in  Africa.  1919. 

C  21954 

A  remarkable  missionary  biography  showing  what 
one  woman  accomplished  in  thirty  years  in  "a 
wild  region  of  unbroken  heathenism"  in  South 
Africa. 

Frederick  the  Great.  Young,  Norwood.  The 
life  of  Frederick  the  Great.  1919.  C  21997 
A  work  based  on  a  large  volume  of  literature 
second  in  quantity  and  interest  only  to  the  Napoleon 
literature.  It  points  out  that  Frederick  has  become 
the  center  of  much  legendary  material  that  is  not 
warranted  by  the  facts. 

Marks,     Murray.     Williamson,     George     C. 

Murray  Marks  and  his  friends;  a  tribute 

of  regard.     1919.  C  23256 

Delightful    reminiscences    of    the    pre-Raphaelite 

Soup,   centering  about  the  charming  personality   of 
urray    Marks,    the    famous    connoisseur    and    col- 
lector. 

Massenet,  Jules.  My  recollections  (184&- 
1912).  1919.  C  23291 

An  authorized  translation  of  the  popularly  in- 
teresting memoirs  of  the  French  composer  who  is 
most  known  here  through  his  operas  Thais  and 
Manon. 

Roosevelt,  Theodore.  Henderson,  Daniel. 
Great-heart;  the  life  story  of  Theodore 
Roosevelt.  1919.  C  23984 

This  is  not  a  complete  biography  but  a  series 
of  anecdotes  and  incidents  reflecting  the  romance 
and  inspiration  of  Roosevelt's  life  and  justifying 
Kipling  in  looking  upon  him  as  the  incarnation  of 
Bunyan's  allegorical  character. 

Sewall,  William  Wingate.    Bill  Sewall's 

story  of  T.   R.,  with  an  introduction,  by 
Hermann   Hagedorn.     1919.  C  23983 

An  intimate  account  of  Roosevelt's  early  days  in 
Maine  and  the  happenings  on  his  Dakota  ranch, 
written  by  the  friend  who  was  his  guide  in  the 
eastern  woods  and  the  manager  of  his  ranch  in  the 
west. 

Saint-Saens,  Camille.  Musical  memories. 
1919.  C  24062 

An  informal  and  untechnical  book  of  reminis- 
cences of  contemporary  musicians  by  one  of  the 
most  eminent  of  them. 

Wilson,  Woodrow.  Nelson,  Lars  P.  Presi- 
dent Wilson;  the  world's  peace-maker. 
1919.  C  24841 

Wood,  Leonard.  Sears,  Joseph  Hamblen. 
The  career  of  Leonard  Wood.  1919. 

C  24926 

An  enthusiastic  account  of  General  Wood  as 
Indian  fighter,  official,  soldier,  organizer,  adminis- 
trator, statesman,  and  patriot

Briggs,  Martin  S.  Through  Egypt  in  war- 
time. 1919.  I  9045 
A  lively  and  entertaining  account  of  Egypt  and 
Palestine,  their  history,  art,  and  archeology,  by 
a  soldier-architect  who  served  in  the  British  army 
as  captain  and  sanitary  officer. 

Burnett-Smith,  A.  As  others  see  her;  an 
Englishwoman's  impressions  of  the 
American  woman  in  war  time.  1919. 

I  43!6 

A  tribute  to  the  beauty  and  generosity  of  Ameri- 
can women  while  deploring  the  restlessness  which 
formed  the  author's  strongest  impression  of  the 
club  movement. 

Chase,  J.  Smeaton.  California  desert  trails; 
and  an  appendix  of  plants,  also  hints  on 
desert  travelling.  1919.  I  4435 

With  whole-souled  interest  in  the  desert  as  a 
desert,  in  its  solitude  and  thirst,  its  plant,  animal 
and  human  life,  the  author  describes  the  beauty 
of  the  wastes  along  the  Colorado  river  valley  in 
California.  Suggestive  to  travelers,  both  as  to 
routes  and  equipment. — Booklist. 

Frank,  Waldo.  Our  America.  1919.  I  4317 
Contents:  The  land  of  the  pioneer. — New  Eng- 
land.— The  chosen  people. — The  land  of  buried  cul- 
tures.— Chicago. — The  Puritan  says  "yea." — New 
York. — The  multitudes  in  Whitman. — The  turning 
of  the  soil. 

Writing  primarily  to  interpret  America  to  the 
young  French  generation.  Mr.  Frank  succeeds  in 
giving  a  fairly  convjncing  picture  of  the  continuity 
of  motives  and  powers  which  runs  through  American 
history  and  is  expressed  so  differently  in  east  and 
west. 

Hallays,  Andre.    The  spell  of  Alsace.     1919. 

I  7515 

A  sympathetic  account  of  the  land  and  people 
of  Alsace  prefaced  by  a  rapid  historical  survey  from 
the  time  of  Caesar  to  the  present.

Bigelow,  Poultney.  Prussianism  and  paci- 
fism; the  two  Wilhelms  between  the  revo-

A  sharp  criticism  of  Prussianism  by  a  boyhoo/i 
acquaintance  of  the  Kaiser  who  believes  that  Prussia 
must  not  only  be  made  harmless  but  must  be  com- 
pelled to  keep  so.  Mr.  Bigelow  has  no  oatie'nce 
with  the  consideration  of  a  league  of  nations  as 
a  practical  solution. 

Brown,  Carroll  N.,  and  Ion,  T.  P.,  tr.  Perse- 
cutions of  the  Greeks  in  Turkey  since  the

official^  Greek  documents.  1918.  A  6146 

(American-Hellenic    Society.      Publications.) 
Bullard,   Arthur.     The   Russian   pendulum; 
autocracy — democracy — bolshevism.    1919. 

A  6039 

The  Russian  pendulum,  the  author  says,  has 
swung  its  full  length  from  the  autocracy  of  the 
czar  to  the  autocracy  of  the  Bolsheviki.  He  writes 
in  July  1918  and  defends  armed  intervention  by 
the  Allies  as  necessary  to  save  Russia  for  democracy.

Younghusband,  Sir  George.    The  Tower  of 

London  from  within.     1919.  A  5410 

The  keeper  of  the  "jewel  house"  in  the  Tower 
of  London  here  seems,  as  the  Spectator  says,  "not 
only  to  dwell  within  the  precincts  of  the  Tower 
but  to  have  entered,  as  it  were,  into  its  memory." 

European  War 

Addison,  James  Thayer.  The  story  of  the 
First  Gas  Regiment.  1919.  A  6986 

The  chaplain's  record  of  the  organization  and 
training  of  the  30th  Engineers — the  first  U.  S. 
regiment  to  use  gas  as  its  only  offensive  weapon — 
and  of  their  activities  all  along  the  western  front 
from  Ypres  to  Switzerland. 

Gibbs,  A.  Hamilton.  Gun  fodder;  the  diary 
of  four  years  of  war.  1919.  A  7046 

The  author  is  a  younger  brother  of  the  war  cor- 
respondent Philip  Gibbs  who  introduces  the  diary. 
He  saw  service  in  Salonica,  in  Egypt,  and  on  the 
Marne,  and  describes  it  with  a  graphic  pen. 

Greece.  Ministry  of  Foreign  Affairs.  The 
Greek  white  book;  diplomatic  documents, 
1913-1917,  prefaced  by  the  treaties  deal- 
ing with  the  establishment  of  the  Hellenic 
state  and  the  incorporation  with  it  of  the 
Ionian  islands.  1918.  A  7054 

(American-Hellenic  Society.  Publications.) 
Contains  also  supplementary  diplomatic  documents, 
1913-1917,  issued  by  the  Ministry  of  Foreign  Af- 
fairs of  the  Greek  government,  dealing  with  the 
Greco-Serbian  treaty  and  the  Germano-Bulgarian 
invasion  of  Macedonia,  and  containing  telegrams 
exchanged  by  the  royal  courts  of  Athens  and  Berlin. 

Hart,  W.  Lee,  comp.  History  of  Base  Hos- 
pital Number  Fifty-three,  Advance  Sec- 
tion, Service  of  Supply.  1919.  A  7052 

logolevitch,  Paul.  The  young  Russian  corr 
poral;  the  story  of  the  youngest  veteran 
of  the  war.  1919.  A  6987 

An  interesting  personal  record  of  the  war  esca- 
pades of  a  Russian-Jewish  lad  of  twelve  who  served 
in  the  Russian  army,  was  decorated  for  bravery, 
and  after  the  Russian  collapse  did  war  propaganda 
work  in  the  United  States. — Booklist. 
Jesse,  F.  Tennyson.  The  sword  of  Deborah; 
first-hand  impressions  of  the  British 
women's  army  in  France.  1919.  A  7050 

Both  informative  and  interesting.  Here  one 
makes  friends  with  war  organizations  and  thrills 
with  respect  at  the  really  serious,  useful  work  they 
do.  The  author  approached  them  with  much  of 
the  skepticism  that  an  ordinary  stay-at-home  woman 
does,  but  this  adds  to  the  reader's  confidence  in 
the  descriptions. — Booklist. 

Kellogg,  Vernon.  Germany  in  the  war  and 
after.  1919.  A  7044 

Contents:  A  post-mortem:  the  German  army. — 
German  control  of  Germans. — What  the  blockade 
did  to  food. — Other  inside  difficulties  during  the 
war. — How  the  people  were  deceived. — What  the 


28 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Germans    thought   during   the   war   and    armistice. — 
Germany    now   and   to-morrow. 

'Mr.  Kellogg  was  with  the  Commission  for  Belgian 
Relief  at  German  headquarters  until  the  United 
States  entered  the  war,  and  he  gives  an  authentic 
report  of  German  conditions  and  policies. 

Magnes,  Judah  L.  Russia  and  Germany  at 
Brest-Litovsk.  1919.  A  7042 

A  compilation  of  documentary  evidence  bearing 
on  the  situation  at  Brest-Litovsk  and  after.  The 
sources  have  of  course  been  secondary  since  original 
documents  are  not  available. — Book  Review  Digest. 

The  Palestine  News.  A  brief  record  of  the

A  7051 

Consists  of  maps  accompanied  by  descriptive  text. 

Peixotto,     Ernest.     The     American     front. 

1919.  A  7049 

An  attractive  book  by  an  official  artist  of  the 
A.  E.  F.  who  was  an  eye-witness  at  all  the  im- 
portant offensives  in  which  America  took  part.  The 
plates,  mostly  architectural,  are  made  from  U.  S. 
official  photographs  of  the  author's  drawings. 

Pepys,  Sam'l,  jun'r.,  pseud.  A  last  diary 
of  the  great  warr.  1919.  A  6989 

Sequel  to  A  diary  of  the  great  warr,  A  6297;  A 
second  diary  of  the  great  warr,  A  6604. 

Powell,  E.  Alexander.  The  army  behind  the 
army.  1919.  K  31086 

This  describes  the  work  of  all  the  different  de- 
partments in  which  the  two  million  soldiers  who 
saw  service  in  this  country  were  engaged,  including 
the  signal  corps,  the  gas  makers,  the  quartermaster 
corps,  the  ordnance,  the  motor  transport  corps,  and 
the  medical  department. 

Punch.     Mr.    Punch's   history   of   the   great 

war.     1919.  A  7048 

An    extraordinarily    interesting    and    entertaining 

volume    of    pictures    and    comment    made    up    from 

the  pages  of  Punch  during  the  war. — Bookman.

Roosevelt,  Kermit.  War  in  the  garden  of 
Eden.  1919.  A  6990 

There  is  little  of  war  and  much  of  the  Garden 
of  Eden  in  this  book  by  Theodore  Roosevelt's  son. 
The  picturesque  country  is  interestingly  described 
and  the  photographs  taken  by  the  author  are  really 
very  fine. 

Roosevelt,  Theodore.  Average  Americans. 
1919.  A  6988 

Lieut.-Coj.  Roosevelt's  narrative  of  the  war,  aside 
from  the  identity  and  family  connections  of  the 
author,  is  little  different  from  numerous  others. 
It  is  interesting  descriptive  reading. — Springfield 
Republican. 

Saint-Rene  Taillandier,  Mme.  The  soul  of 
the  C.  R.  B.;  a  French  view  of  the  Hoover 
relief  work.  1919.  A  7047 

A  sentimental  expression  of  French  appreciation

Stoddard,  F.  R.,  jr.  War  time  France;  the 
story  of  an  American  commission  abroad. 
1918.  A  7043 

The  author  was  chief  of  the  commission  sent  to 
study  anti-aircraft  artillery,  and  he  gives  a  spirited 
report  of  their  many  adventures  at  the  front  and 
the  valuable  work  accomplished. 


The  Jews 

Ravage,  M.  E.  The  Jew  pays;  a  narrative 
of  the  consequences  of  the  war  to  the 
Jews  of  eastern  Europe,  and  of  the  man- 
ner in  which  Americans  have  attempted 
to  meet  them.  1919.  A  2686 

Peace  finds  the  Eastern  Jew  with  no  more  hope- 
ful outlook  than  at  the  beginning  of  the  war.  It 
is  the  task  of  American  Jews  to  persuade  the 
civilized  world  to  demand  that  the  Jew  everywhere 
be  allowed  to  live  his  life  in  the  freedom  and  use- 
fulness that  is  accorded  to  him  here. 

Sokolow,  Nahum.  History  of  Zionism,  1600- 
1918;  with  an  introduction  by  the  Rt. 
Hon.  A.  J.  Balfour,  M.P.  1919.  2v. 

A  2688 

Scholarly  m  its  inception  and  clear  and  readable 
in  style,  this  book  gives  to  all  who  are  interested 
in  the  many-sided  aspects  of  the  economic,  social 
and  political  questions  not  only  of  Europe  but  of 
America,  an  insight  into  the  predominating  Jewish 
point  of  view. — Boston  Transcript. 

Asia 

Coleman,  Frederic.  The  Far  East  unveiled; 
an  inner  history  of  events  in  Japan  and 
China  in  the  year  1916.  1919.  '  A  4826 
A  spicy  interesting  record  of  what  came  to  the 
attention  of  an  experienced  newspaper  correspondent 
in  China  and  Japan,  who  had  had  a  long  early  ac- 
quaintance with  those  countries.  It  is  full  of  in- 
formation on  financial,  industrial,  and  political  con- 
ditions. 

Gibbons,  Herbert  Adams.  The  new  map  of 
Asia  (1900-1919).  1919.  A  4825 

This  book  presents  a  record  of  the  encroachments 
of  each  of  the  European  powers  upon  Asia.  The 
author  believes  that  in  Japan  the  increasing  demo- 
cratic sentiment  is  a  hopeful  sign  and  that  Japan 
is  not  to  be  blamed  for  refusing  to  permit  her- 
self to  be  exploited  like  China,  for  instance. 

Kendall,  Carleton  Waldo.  The  truth  about 
Korea.  1919.  A  4791 

North  America 

Mexico 

Inman,  Samuel  Guy.  Intervention  in  Mex- 
ico. 1919.  B  4839 

An  American  educator  discusses  various  aspects 
of  the  Mexican  problem  as  much  as  possible  from 
the  Mexican's  point  of  view:  the  present  disturb- 
ances, the  character  of  Carranza,  the  present  and

States,  etc.

Illinois 

Cole,  Arthur  Charles.  The  era  of  the  civil 
war,  1848-1870.  1919.  B  7675, 3 

(The  centennial  history  of  Illinois,  v.  3.) 
For  the  period  the  history  of  no  one  of  the  North- 
ern states  is  more  important  nor  interesting.  It 
includes  the  rise  of  the  great  Free  Soil  movement, 
the  origin  of  the  Republican  party,  the  Lincoln- 
Douglas  debates,  the  election  of  1860,  and  finally 
the  profound  disturbance  of  society  wrought  by 
the  war  itself. — Review  of  Reviews. 

Kirkland,  Caroline,  comp.  Chicago  yester- 
days; a  sheaf  of  reminiscences.  1919. 

B  7853 

Contents:  The  twenties  and  thirties  by  C.  Kirk- 
land. — A  pioneer  couple,  letters  of  Mr.  and  Mrs. 
Augustus  Burley. — A  new  home,  letter  of  Mrs.  Le- 
ander  McCormick. — The  first  church  wedding  in 
Chicago,  by  Mrs.  A.  B.  Meeker. — Gleanings  from 
a  family  memoir,  by  J.  T.  Ryerson. — Out  of  the 
past,  by  Mrs.  W.  Blair. — As  I  remember  it,  by  Mrs. 
J.  F.  Ward. — Long  ago,  by  M.  Drummond. — For- 
gotten Chicago,  by  Mme.  C.  Bigot. — A  Kentucky 
colony,  by  C.  H.  Harrison. — Old  Hyde  Park,  by 
(Mrs.  B.  F.  Aver. — Before  the  fire,  by  Mrs.  F. 
Greeley. — The  Chicago  Club,  by  E.  Blair. — Through 
a  child's  eyes,  by  Mrs.  F.  T.  West. — Early  Lake 
Forest,  by  Mrs.  R.  G.  McGann. — From  fire  to  fair, 
by  various  people. — The  yesterday  of  the  horse,  by 
H.  C.  Chatfield-Taylor.— The  World's  Fair,  by  Mrs. 
W.  J.  Calhoun. 

Frank,  illuminating,  historically  interesting  ac- 
counts of  Chicago's  infancy  and  development. 
.  A  gay  and  informative  book. — Chicago 
Tribune. 

McLean,  John.  One  hundred  years  in  Illi- 
nois (1818-1918);  an  account  of  the  de- 
velopment of  Illinois  in  the  first  century 
of  her  statehood.  1919.  B  7693 

Includes    several    chapters    on    Pullman,    Illinois. 

Pease,  Theodore  Calvin.  The  frontier  state, 
1818-1848.  1918.  B  7675,  2 

(The  centennial  history  of  Illinois,  v.  3.) 
In  the  drawing  of  the  outlines  the  perspective 
remains  admirable  throughout.  Although  some 
tediousness  of  detail  could  not  always  be  avoided 
the  author  nowhere  loses  his  perception  of  the  vital 
relation  between  state  politics  and  the  larger  aspects 
of  national  affairs. — Amer.  History  Review.

Andrews,  Mary  Raymond  Shipman.  Joy 
in  the  morning.  1919.  F  10572 

Contents:  The  ditch. — Her  country  too. — The 
swallow. — Only  one  of  them. — The  V.  C. — He  that 
loseth  his  life  shall  find  it. — The  silver  stirrup. — 
The  Russian. — Robina's  doll. — Dundonald's  de- 
stroyer. 

Stories  of  the  war  republished  from  different 
magazines. 

Bacon,  Josephine  Daskam.  Square  Peggy. 
1919.  F  10793 

Contents:  Square  Peggy. — Comrades  in  arms. — 
The  ghost  of  Rosy  Taylor. — Alice  of  the  red  tape. 
— The  fruits  of  the  earth. — The  film  of  fate. — 
Stepping  stones. — Merry-go-round. — Quartermastery. 
— Ru  of  the  reserves. 

Ten  romantic  stories,  with  a  Fifth  Avenue  at- 
mosphere, about  some  charming  New  York  girls 
who  helped  in  the  war  and  so  found  themselves. 

Barbusse,   Henri.     Light.     1919. 

F  10864 

The  story  of  a  French  soldier  who  has  been 
through  the  war,  and  as  a  result  sees  the  big  reali- 
ties of  life.  He  decries  war  and  all  social  in- 
justices. The  descriptions  are  most  graphic. 

Benson,    E.   F.     Up   and  down.      1918. 

F  11123 

More  a  series  of  essays  and  bits  of  travel  than 
a  novel,  but  charmingly  written  in  Benson's  best 
style.  He  tells  of  his  travels  from  day  to  day  in 
Italy,  and  intermingles  the  narrative  with  spiritual 
musings. 

Bertrand,  Adrien.  The  call  of  the  soil 
(L'appel  du  sol).  1919.  F  11102 

The  translation  of  a  French  novel  written  by 
a  young  soldier  who  fell  in  the  war.  It  is  the 
history  of  a  company  and  shows  the  intense 
patriotism  and  exaltation  of  the  men  who  died  that 
France  might  live. 

Bindloss,  Harold.  Partners  of  the  out- 
trail.  1919.  F  11135 

A  tale  of  an  adventurous  young  Canadian  wood- 
man in  British  Columbia  who  falls  heir  to  an 
estate  in  Northern  England. 

Birmingham,  G.  A.,  pseud,  of  James  Owen 
Hannay.  Our  casualty,  and  other  stories. 
1918.  "  F  11192 

Contents:  Our  casualty. — Getting  even. — A  mat- 
ter of  discipline. — The  second  bass. — Her  right. — 
Journey's  end. — His  girl. — Sir  Galahad. — A  gun- 
running  episode. — Ireland  for  ever. — Sir  Timothy's 
dinner-party. — United  Ireland. — Old  Biddy  and  the 
rebels. — Civilized  war.— The  mermaid. — An  upright 
judge. 

Humorous  stories  of  the  war  with  a  touch  of  sad- 
ness here  and  there. 

Bower,  B.  M.,  pseud,  of  Mrs.  B.  M.  Sinclair. 

Rim  o'  the  world.     1919.  F  18149 

A    typical    Bower    story    dealing    with    ranch    life 

in  the  Idaho  desert  and  a  feud  to  be  settled.     Full 

of  action  and   gun-play. 

Brown,  Alice.     The  black  drop.      1919. 

F  11298 

The  story,  vividly  told,  of  a  patriotic  American 
family  whose  son  has  the  black  drop  of  blood  and 
becomes  a  German  spy. 

Cannan,  Gilbert.    Pink  roses.    1919.    F  11671 

A  little  group  of  young  men  in  London  is  broken 

up  by  the  war  and  only  one_  is  left  at  home.  _  The 

story    deals    with    his    re-adjustment    to    conditions 

and  is  a  satire  upon  life. 

Couperus,  Louis.     Ecstasy;  a  study  of  hap- 
piness.     1919.  F  12209 
One    of    the    author's    first    novels    but    only    just 
translated.      It   is    the    story    of   an   ideal    love    told 
in  a  very  poetic  and  mystical  way.

Fletcher,  Joseph  S.  The  Middle  Temple 
murder.  1919.  F  12853 

Frank  Spargo  is  a  keen-brained  reporter  who 
with  no  clue  solves  the  mysterious  murder  of 
Middle  Temple  Lane.  The  plot  is  clever  and  skil- 
fully worked  out 

Fuller,  Henry  B.  Bertram  Cope's  year. 
1919.  F  13072 

An  Evanston  professor's  adventures  in  love  and 
learning. 

Gale,  Zona.  Peace  in  Friendship  Village. 
1919.  F  13107 

More  Friendship  Village  stories  which  make  en- 
joyable reading  because  they  are  so  human  and 
portray  life  in  a  little  town  so  well. 

Heriry,  O.,  pseud,  of  W.  S.  Porter.  Waifs 
and"  strays;  twelve  stories,  together  with 
a  representative  selection  of  critical  and 
biographical  comment.  1919.  F  16586 

Contents:  Pt.  1.  Twelve  stories:  The  red  roses 
of  Tonia. — Round  the  circle. — The  rubber  plant's 
story. — Out  of  Nazareth. — Confessions  of  a  humor- 
ist.— The  sparrows  in  Madison  Square. — Hearts  and 
hands. — The  cactus. — The  detective  detector. — The 
dog  and  the  playlet. — A  little  talk  about  mobs. — 
The  snow  man. — Pt.  2.  Critical  and  biographical 
comment. 

Stories  taken  from  old  magazines  and  newspapers 
and  not  among  his  best  work. 

Hergesheimer,  Joseph.  The  lay  Anthony; 
a  romance.  1919.  F  13701 

Written  in  1914  and  recently  re-written.  It  is  a 
character  study  of  a  boy  who  was  elemental  in 
his  love  of  nature  and  in  his  outlook  on  life.  A 
good  piece  of  writing.  • 

Hichens,   Robert.     Mrs.    Marden.      1919 

F  13828 

A  bereaved  mother  goes  to  a  medium  in  search 
of  comfort  and  then  in  her  hour  of  disillusion 
turns  back  to  find  in  answer  to  her  questions  "the 
divine  adventure." 

Irwin,  Inez  Haynes.  The  happy  years. 
1919.  F  14270 

A  sequel  to  the  author's  Phoebe  and  Ernest, 
F 13257,  and  Phoebe,  Ernest,  and  Cupid,  F  13268. 

The  grandparents  decide  that  "there  is  no  such 
thing  as  old  age"  and  keep  young  by  always  having 
new  plans  and  interests. 

Lee,   Jennette.      The   rain-coat    girl.      1919. 

F 14673 

A  wholesome  story  for  older  girls. 

Lefevre,  Edwin.     Simonetta.    1919.    F.14691 

A  charming  Florentine  mystery  story  in  which 
the  heroine  is  the  living  counterpart  of  Botticelli's 
Simonetta.  How  her  artist  father  takes  advantage 
of  this  and  how  he  is  discovered  make  a  delightful 
tale. 

Lewis,  Sinclair.     Free  air.     1919.        F 14743 

A  breezy  tale  of  an  automobile  trip  from  Minne- 
apolis to  the  coast. 

London,  Jack.  On  the  Makaloa  mat.  1919. 

F  14768 

Contents:  On  the  Makaloa  mat. — The  bones  of 
Kahelili. — When  Alice  told  her  soul. — Shin-bones. 
— The  water  baby. — The  tears  of  Ah  Kim. — The 
Kanaka  surf. 

The  setting  of  these  seven  adventure  tales  is 
Hawaii.  They  give  a  vivid  picture  of  life  on  the 
islands  but  would  not  be  ranked  among  London's 
best  work. 


MacGrath,  Harold.  The  yellow  typhoon. 
1919.  F 15128 

A  swift  moving  tale  with  plenty  of  incident,  ad- 
venture and  mystery,  and  a  beautiful  woman  who 
is  a  German  spy. 

McKenna,   Stephen.     Sonia   married.     1919. 

F  14929 

A  sequel  to  Sonia,  (F  14925)  and  rather  disap- 
pointing. 

Malet,  Lucas,  pseud,  of  Mrs.  M.  St.  L.  K. 
Harrison.  Deadham  Hard;  a  romance. 
1919.  F  13534 

A  sequel  to  "Damaris"  (F  13555).  Victorian  in 
plot  and  treatment,  it  relates  the  inner  life  of 
Damaris  Verity  from  the  age  of  eighteen. 

Morley,  Christopher,  and  Haley,  Bart.     In 
the  sweet  dry  and  dry.     1919.         F  15503 
Humorous  sketches  on  prohibition. 

Newton,  W.  Douglas.     Green  ladies.     1919. 

F  15665 

"Green  Ladies"  is  a  delightful  country  place 
which  Peter  John  leases.  How  he  unravels  the 
mystery  that  clings  about  it  and  becomes  owner 
ot  the  estate  makes  a  clever  story. 

Norton,    Roy.     Drowned    gold;    being    the 

story  of  a  sailor's  life.     1919.         F  15707 

A  tale  of  Spanish  waters  and  a  sunken  treasure 

which    is    found    by    a    submarine    equipped    with   a 

light  so  powerful  that  it  can  penetrate  any  depths. 

Ogden,  George  W.  The  land  of  last  chance. 
1919.  F  15747 

A  tale  of  Oklahoma  in  the  days  of  the  home- 
steading  rush.  There  are  fights  between  settlers  and 
squatters  and  there  is  plenty  of  action  throughout. 

O'Higgins,  Harvey.  From  the  life.  1919. 

F 15743 

Contents:  Owen  Carey. — Jane  Shore. — Thomas 
Wales  Warren.  —  Benjamin  McNeil  Murdock.  — 
Conrad  Norman. — W.  T. — H'on.  Benjamin  P.  Divins. 
— Sir  Watson  Tyler. — District-Attorney  Wickson. 

Imaginary  portraits  of  men  and  women  taken 
at  the  psychological  moment  in  their  lives  when  a 
trifling  event  starts  them  on  toward  success.  They 
are  very  original  and  skillfully  done. 

Oppenheim,  E.  Phillips.  The  box  with 
broken  seals.  1919.  F  15837 

This  tale  deals  with  the  hiding  and  discovery  of 
certain  papers  which  are  vitally  important  to  one 
of  the  neutral  governments.  It  was  written  to  ex- 
plain  how  the  documents  missing  from  the  chest 
belonging  to  Ambassador  von  Bernstorff  when  he 
sailed  from  America  were  conveyed  to  Europe. 

Parrish,  Randall.     Comrades  of  peril.     1919. 

F  15990 

A  spirited  tale  of  Indian  uprisings,  kidnapping, 
and  other  thrilling  events. 

Pertwee,   Roland.     The   old   card.      1919. 

F 16139 

A  book  of  incidents  and  episodes  in  the  life  of 
an  old  English  provincial  actor  who  is  a  very  human 
and  lovable  character. 

Philip  Dru:  administrator;  a  story  of  to- 
morrow, 1920-1935.  1912.  F  16243 

A  novel  in  which  Woodrow  Wilson  is  supposed 
to  be  the  leading  character. 

The  rain-girl;  a  romance  of  today,  by  the 
author  of  Patricia  Brent,  spinster.  1919. 

F 16067 

A  readable  romance  which  shows  that  very  in- 
teresting things  can  happen  on  rainy  days.

Reynolds,    Mrs.    Baillie.      "Open    sesame!"

^Contents:    "Open  sesame." — It  began  with  a  wed- 
ding.— Her  final  choice. — The  haunted  well. 

Four  spirited  tales  of  adventure. 

Richardson,   Norval.     The  world   shut   out. 

1919.  F 17167 

A  charming  tale  with  an  Italian  villa  for  a  back- 
ground, the  splendor  of  old  palaces,  and  the  at- 
mosphere of  all  that  is  most  appealing  in  Italian 
life,  interwoven  with  a  mystery. 

Robins,  Elizabeth,  (Mrs.  G.  R.  Parkes).    The 
messenger.     1919.  F 17386 

Story  of  a  most  intricate  web  of  intrigue  spun 
by  a  German  woman  spy,  who  has  found  her  way 
into  the  household  of  an  English  official  as  gov- 
erness. 

Sinclair,     Bertrand     W.       Burned     bridges. 
1919.  F 18153 

Wesley  Thompson  makes  a  failure  as  a  mis- 
sionary in  the  Hudson  Bay  country,  so  he  sets  out 
for  the  Pacific  coast.  The  story  of  his  struggles 
and  ultimate  success  makes  an  interesting  book. 

Smith,  Henry  Justin.    The  other  side  of  the 
wall.    1919.  F  18243 

f  A  story  portraying  life  in  Chicago  at  the  present 
time  and  showing  how  the  war  made  a  man  out  of 
Lance,  the  hero. 

Stacpoole,    H.    De    Vere.      The    breach    of 

dreams;  a  romance.    1919.  F  18360 

A    really    new    castaway    story    with    an    unusual 

heroine.    The  book  is  full  of  impressive  descriptions 

of  the  sea  and  the  South  Sea  islands. 

Terhune,  Albert  Payson.    Lad;  a  dog.     1919. 

F 18536 

An  interesting  account  of  the  main  incidents 
in  the  life  of  a  super  dog"  who  is  almost  too  won- 
derful and  intelligent  to  be  true. 

Van  Dyke,  Henry.     The  broken  soldier  and 
the  Maid  of  France.     1919.  F  18822 

A  French  veteran  about  to  desert  because  of 
his  shell-shock  fears,  visits  Jeanne  d'  Arc's  home, 
and  in  a  dream  receives  the  inspiration  from  her 
which  leads  to  his  sacrifice  for  France. 

Van  Slyke,  Lucille.    Little  Miss  By-the-day. 
1919.  F  18823 

Felicia's  efforts  to  provide  a  home  for  poor 
artists  in  New  York  City  is  well  told  and  we  have 
a  rather  improbable  but  delightfully  refreshing  story. 

Ward,   Mrs.    Humphry.      Helena.      1919. 

F  18982 

A  lighter  novel  than  Mrs.  Ward  usually  writes 
without  any  background  of  English  politics  or  so- 
ciety. The  love  affairs  of  Helena  are  told  in  an 
interesting  way. 

Wiggin,    Kate    Douglas,     (Mrs.    K.    D.    W. 
Riggs).     Ladies  in  waiting.     1919. 

F  17217 

Contents:  Miss  Thomasina  Tucker. — The  turning- 
point. — Hulda  the  prophetess. — Two  on  a  tour. — 
Philippa's  nervous  prostration. 

Five  pleasant  little  stories  for  an  hour's  reading. 

Willsie,  Honore.    The  forbidden  trail.   1919. 

F  19582 

Another  novel  of  the  Arizona  country,  dealing 
with  a  young  investor  who  goes  to  the  desert  to 
perfect  a  solar  engine  which  will  promote  irriga- 
tion.

Brown,  Nelson   C.     The  lumber   market  in 

Italy    and     reconstruction     requirements. 

1919.  C18.11     182 

(U.     S.    Eept.    of    Commerce.       Special    agents 

series   182.) 

Contains  much  valuable  material  on  the  normal 
lumber  trade,  production,  and  use  in  Italy,  the 
changes  caused  by  the  war,  and  the  probable 
trend  of  Italy's  import  trade  in  lumber,  with  spe- 
cial reference  to  the  opportunity  for  American 
woods.  Illustrated. 

Chicago.  Sanitary  District.  History  of  its 
growth  and  development  as  shown  by  de- 
cisions of  the  courts  and  work  of  its  law 
department.  1919. 

"Designed  as  a  convenient  and  ready  reference 
to  court  decisions  and  matters  of  record." 

We  si  Chicago  Park  Commissioners.  50th 

annual  report:  1918.     1919. 

This  annual  report  is  particularly  interesting,  as 
it  contains  Jens  Jensen  s  report  of  the  Greater 
West  Park  System  with  plans  and  map_s,  also  the 
statistics  for  the  year  of  the  social  activities  carried 
on  in  the  small  parks. 

Crittenden,  T.  H.  The  bean  ladybird  and 
its  control.  1919.  Al.9  1074 

(Farmers'  bulletin   1074.) 

Summarizes  control  measures  for  this  serious 
enemy  to  the  bean  crop  in  the  southwest. 

Edmunds,  Charles  K.  Modern  education  in 
China.  1919.  116.3  1919-44 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Education.  Bulletin  1919. 
no.  44.) 

An  interesting  report,  well  illustrated,  on  the 
changes  of  educational  methods  and  tV<e  advance- 
ment made. 

Ferrin,  A.  W.  Chinese  currency  and  finance. 
1919.  "CIS.  11  186 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Foreign  and  Domestic  Com- 
merce. Special  agents  ser.  186.) 

_  An  endeavor  is  made  to  make  clear  the   intrica- 
cies   and  _  complications    of    the    Chinese    currency 
situation    in   a    simple    manner   for   the    lay   reader,

currency  expert.

Chicago  Public  Library 

Volume  10  March,  1920  No.  3 

Music  for  Easter  and  Lent 

THE  Music  Collection  in  the  Chicago  Public  Library  now  comprises  more 
than  4,000  bound  volumes  and  20,000  pieces  of  sheet  music,  all  of  which 
may  be  borrowed  for  home  use.  The  Music  Room  on  the  fifth  floor  of 
the  Library  building  enjoys  a  large  and  steadily  growing  patronage  of  appre- 
ciative music  lovers,  students,  amateurs  and  professional  musicians,  who  have 
found  pleasure  and  profit  in  the  use  of  these  varied  collections  and  have  fre- 
quently taken  occasion  to  express  their  gratitude  and  approval. 

The  season  of  Lent  and  the  approach  of  Easter  afford  an  Opportunity  for 
calling  attention  to  our  musical  resources  by  means  of  the  following  brief 
selected  lists  of  compositions  appropriate  to  their  religious  observance.  Com- 
plete and  exhaustive  card  indexes  kept  in  the  Music  Room  will  reveal  the 
wealth  and  variety  of  its  resources,  of  which  the  present  list  is  merely  a  sug- 
gestion. Compositions  for  all  instruments  as  well  as  for  the  voice,  and  in  all 
combinations,  in  great  variety,  and  for  every  degree  of  skill  will  be  found  in 
abundance. 

Music  cards  are  issued  in  the  Music  Room  to  all  registered  holders  of  book 
cards  on  presentation  of  the  latter.  One  bound  volume  and  five  pieces  of  sheet 
music  will  be  issued  as  a  single  loan,  the  former  for  a  period  of  four  weeks,  the 
latter  for  two  weeks,  without  renewal. 

All  compositions  in  the  list  below  are  in  vocal  score  with  piano  accom- 
paniment unless  otherwise  described. 


li

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.   *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

PHILOSOPHY 

Psychology 

Goddard,  Henry  Herbert.  Psychology  of 
the  normal  and  subnormal.  1919. 

L 13925 

The  author  is  an  expert  psychologist  and  writes 
from  his  experience  as  formerly  director  of  the 
Vineland  Laboratory  and  now  of  the  Ohio  Bu- 
reau of  Juvenile  Research. 

Spiritualism 

Doyle,  Arthur  Conan.  The  vital  message. 
1919.  L 13503 

Here,  as  in  The  new  revelation  (L  13487),  the 
fauthor  treats  spiritualism  as  a  religion.  The 
book  has  little  new  to  offer  by  way  of  argument, 
but  much  as  to  the  ethical  and  religious  import 
of  spiritualism. 

Herbine,  Charlotte  G.     The  meeting  of  the 

spheres;  or,  Letters  from  Doctor  Coulter. 

1919.  L 13500 

The   letters    purport    to    be   spirit   communications 

and    contain    remarkable    messages. 

Raupert,  J.  Godfrey.  The  new  black  magic 
and  the  truth  about  the  ouija-board. 
1919.  L 13501 

The  author  expresses  the  attitude  of  the  Cath- 
olic church  toward  spirit  communications,  main- 
taining their  reality  but  discouraging  as  danger- 
ous all  efforts  to  seek  after  them. 

Temperance 

Carver,  Thomas  Nixon.  Government  con- 
trol of  the  liquor  business  in  Great  Bri- 
tain and  the  United  States.  1919. 

L 17921 

(Carnegie  Endowment  for  International  Peace. 
Preliminary  economic  studies  of  the  war.) 

Bound  with   the  author's  War  thrift. 

Discusses  the  repression  of  drunkenness  and 
conservation  of  food  materials  in  both  countries; 
also  why  America  has  tended  toward  prohibition 
rather  than  control  of  liquor  traffic. 

Smith,  Randolph  Wellford.  The  sober 
world.  1919.  L  14543 

An  altogether  timely,  quite  readable,  and  un- 
deniably entertaining  recital  of  the  march  toward 
prohibition.  The  indictment  is  couched  in  such 
lurid  terms  that  the  Rev.  Billy  Sunday  could  use 
it  verbatim. — Dial. 

RELIGION 

Bible.  The  Holy  Bible,  containing  the  Old 
and  New  Testaments  (King  James  ver- 
sion). 1897.  *R3417 

Oxford    self-pronouncing    Bible. 

Buckham,  John  Wright.  Progressive  reli- 
gious thought  in  America;  a  survey  of 
the  enlarging  Pilgrim  faith.  1919. 

M8680 

Contents:  Introduction:  Horace  Bushnell  and 
the  liberators. — Theodore  T.  Munger:  the  new 
theology  defined  and  related. — George  A.  Gordon: 
the  new  theology  universalized. — William  T.  Tuck- 
er: the  new  theology  in  action. — Egbert  C.  Smyth 
and  the  Andpver  theology. — Washington  Gladden 
and  the  social  _  theology. — Newman  Smyth  and 
later  representatives  of  theological  progress. — Con- 
clusion: the  future  of  theology  in  America. 


An  optimistic  view  of  the  development  of  theol- 
ogical thinking  written  with  reference  to  the  Pil- 
grim tercentenary. 

English,  John  Mahan.  For  pulpit  and  plat- 
form; a  handbook  on  preparation.  1919. 

M8904 

A  handbook  for  theological  students  and  pul- 
pit craters. 

Singer,  Ignatius.  The  rival  philosophies  of 
Jesus  and  of  Paul;  being  an  explanation 
of  the  failures  of  organized  Christianity, 
and  a  vindication  of  the  teachings  of 
Jesus,  which  are  shown  to  contain  a  re- 
ligion for  all  men  and  for  all  times.  1919. 

M9951 

The  author  thinks  that  the  Christology  of  the 
churches  has  been  founded  on  a  Christ-myth  orig- 
inated by  Paul,  and  that  the  present  falling  off 
of  attendance  at  public  worshjp  is  due  to  a  re- 
vival of  religion  rather  than  its  decline. 

SOCIOLOGY 

Economics 

Carter,  Hun tly,  ed.  The  limits  of  state  in- 
dustrial control;  a  symposium  on  the 
present  situation  and  how  to  meet  it. 
1919.  L  9178 

Views  of  such  representative  men  as  Lloyd 
George,  Bonar  Law,  Lord  Bryce,  Sir  Edward 
Carson,  and  Bernard  Shaw  are  cited. 

Keynes,  John  Maynard.  The  economic  con- 
sequences of  the  peace.  1920.  L  16626 

An  arraignment  of  the  peace  terms  as  econom- 
ically dangerous  by  a  representative  of  the  Brit- 
ish treasury _  who  resigned  from  the  Conference 
and  Economic  Council  because  of  his  objections  to 
their  entire  economic  policy. 

Labor  and  Laboring  Classes 

Hobson,  S.  G.  National  guilds;  an  inquiry 
into  the  wage  system  and  the  way  out. 
1919.  L 17883 

This  is  the  third  edition  of  the  first  book  to  be 
devoted  entirely  to  the  new  philosophy  of  guild 
socialism.  Mr.  Hobson  portrays  the  evils  of  the 
wage-system,  pleads  for  a  new  conception  of  la- 
bor, and  analyzes  his  idea  of  the  guild  system. 

Maclver,    R.    M.      Labor    in    the    changing 

world.     1919.  L 16947 

This    book    was    listed    in    the    February    Book 

Bulletin   on  page   21,  where   by  a   clerical   error   it 

was  given  the  wrong  call  number. 

National  Civic  Federation.  Commission  on 
foreign  inquiry.  The  labor  situation  in 
Great  Britain  and  France.  1919.  L  16949 
Offers  comparisons  between  America  and  Euro- 
pean trade  unions  and  discusses  valuable  attempts- 
thai  have  been  made  toward  the  solution  of  hous- 
ing and  kindred  problems. 

Odencrantz,  Louise  C.  Italian  women  in 
industry;  a  study  of  conditions  in  New 
York  city.  1919.  Civics  Dept. 

Southern  Pine  Association,  New  Orleans. 
Homes  for  workmen;  a  presentation  of 
leading  examples  of  industrial  commu- 
nity development.  1919.  L 17053 

It  advertises  some  very  good  work,  with  illus- 
trations and  special  articles  on  financial  and  other 
details  by  competent  authorities.

German  pcctry.  The  translation  is  upon  the  whoie

Individual  Biography 

Butler,  Samuel.  Jones,  Henry  Festing. 
Samuel  Butler,  author  of  Erewhon  (1835- 
1902);  a  memoir.  1919.  2v.  C  21059 

The  book  is  a  chronicle,  all  narrative  and  little 
description;  but  the  narrative  itself  describes,  and 
with  tach  chapter  we  know  more  of  Butler  as  a 
man  living  and  changing  and  growing  before  us.— 
Times  (London). 

Granville,  2d  Earl.  Fitzmaurice,  Lord  Ed- 
mond.  The  life  of  Granville  George  Lev- 
eson  Gower,  second  earl  Granville,  K.  G. 
1815-1891.  1906.2v.  C  22169 

Meredith,  George.  Hammerton,  J.  A.  George 
Meredith;  his  life  and  art  in  anecdote 
and  criticism.  1911.  C  18517 

Riley,  James  Whit  comb.  Dickey,  Marcus. 
The  youth  of  James  Whitcomb  Riley;  for- 
tune's way  with  the  poet  from  infancy  to 
manhood.  1919.  C  23951 

Anecdotes  and  quotations  presenting  a  sympa- 
thetic picture  of  Riley's  childhood,  schooldays  and 
early  attempts  at  making  a  living,  successful  and 
otherwise. 

Lauder,  Sir  Harry.  Between  you  and  me. 
1919.  C 22879 

As  Lauder  himself  says,  "in  this  book  there's 
no  great  plan,  it's  just  as  if  we  were  speerin'  to- 
gether." It  is  little  more  than  a  collection  of 
rambling  personal  incidents  and  anecdotes  in 
Scotch  dialect,  like  those  he  is  famous  for  on  the 
stage. 

Taylor,  James  Monroe.  Haight,  Elizabeth 
Hazelton.  The  life  and  letters  of  James 
Monroe  Taylor;  the  biography  of  an  edu- 
cator. 1919.  C  24496 
Dr.  Taylor  was  president  of  Vassar  from_  1886 
to  1914.  Mrs.  Haight  collaborated  with  him  in 
his  history  of  the  college  (L  10551),  to  which  this 
account  of  his  life  and  tribute  to  his  devotion  to 
ideals  furnishes  a  fitting  supplement. 

Tucker,  William  Jewett.  My  generation; 
an  autobiographical  interpretation.  1919. 

C  24602 

Reminiscences  as  pastor,  instructor  at  Andover 
and  president  of  Dartmouth  during  the  latter  half 
of  the  nineteenth  century. 

White,  J  William.  Repplier,  Agnes.  J.  Wil- 
liam White,  M.  D.;  a  biography.  1919. 

C  24812 


Dr.  White  is  a  distinguished  surgeon  of  Phila- 
delphia who  was  one  of  the  most  outspoken  advo- 
cates of  the  Allied  cause  before  we  entered  the 
war  and  did  valuable  work  in  organizing  hospital 
service  in  France. 

Williams,  Albert  Rhys.  Lenin,  the  man, 
and  his  work;  and  the  impressions  of 
Col.  Raymond  Robins  and  Arthur  Ran- 
some.  1919.  C  22943 

Mr.  Williams'  contribution  is  the  least  impres- 
sive of  the  three.  Colonel  Robins,  with  William 
Hard  for  emanuensis,  does  much  better  and  in 
fact  shares  honors  with  the  chapters  reprinted 

•     from    Ransome's    Russia    in    1919    (A  6044).— Dial. 

HISTORY 

Adams,  Henry.  The  degradation  of  the 
democratic  dogma.  1919.  A  2335 

Contents:  Introduction. — The  heritage  of 
Henry  Adarrs. — The  tendency  of  history. — A  let- 
ter to  American  teachers  of  history. — The  rule  of 
phase  applied  to  history. 

Three  essays  on  history  and  Its  teaching  which 
express  the  author's  creed  as  summed  up  by  his 
brother  Brooks  in  the  introduction:  "The  perfect 
plebiscite,  the  democratic  ideal,  is  the  synonym 
not  of  perfect  truth  but  of  disaster  and  confu- 
sion." 

European  War 

Aldrich,     Mildred.      When    Johnny    comes 

marching  home.     1919.  A  6997 

Continuation     of    the     author's    /Hilltop     on     the 

Marne,    A  5805;    On    the    edge    of    the    war    zone, 

A  6450;   The   peak   of  the  load,   A  6886. 

Letters  from  August  1918  to  June  1919  relating 
the  suspense  in  France  during  the  last  months  of 
the  war,  the  sense  of  joy  and  relief  occasioned 
by  the  armistice,  and  the  welcome  accorded  the- 
returning  soldiers. 

Barton,  George.  Celebrated  spies  and 
famous  mysteries  of  the  great  war.  1919. 

A  6995 

Contents:  The  curious  disappearance  of  Lord 
Kitchener. — Miss  Edith  Cayell,  first  martyr  of  the 
great  war. — The  fate  of  Nicholas  II.,  the  greatest 
mystery  of  the  war. — Consul-General  Gottschalk 
and  the  mystery  of  the  Cyclops. — The  judicial 
murder  of  Captain  Charles  A.  Fryatt. — Eugene 
Van  Doren  and  the  secret  press  of  Belgium. — The 
mad  adventure  of  Sir  Roger  Casement. — The  mys- 
tery of  the  Turkish  beauty. — The  romantic  life  of 
the  Dutch-Javanese  dancer  who  was  shot  as  a 
spy.  —  Amazing  adventures  and  tragic  death  of 
Bolo  Pasha. — The  story  of  Lieutenant  Robert  Fay 
and  the  ship  bomb  plots. — Ram  Chandra  and  the- 
German-Hindu  plots  in  the  United  States. — The 
soldier  of  fortune  who  became  a  German  spy. — The 
artless  German  who  dynamited  the  Vanceboro 
bridge. — The  unsolved  mystery  of  the  master 
German  sny. — The  dark  mystery  surrounding  the- 
murder  of  the  Archduke  Ferdinand. 

Companion  volume  to  The  world's  greatest  mili- 
tary spies  and  secret  service  agents,  K  31018. 

Bogart,  Ernest  L.  Direct  and  indirect  costs 
of  the  great  world  war.  1919.  A  6993 

(Carnegie  Endowment  for  International  Peace. 
Preliminary  economic  studies  of  the  war.) 

A  greatly  enlarged  and  improved  edition  of  Di- 
rect costs  of  the  present  war,  A  6730.  This  book^ 
which  is  virtually  a  new  work,  compares  the  war 
expenditures  for  the  different  nations  and  prov- 
inces with  the  corresponding  expenses  in  peace 
time. 

Duffy,  Francis  P.  Father  Duffy's  story;  a 
tale  of  humor  and  heroism,  of  life  and 
death  with  the  Fighting  Sixty-ninth,  with 
a  historical  appendix  by  Joyce  Kilmer. 
1919.  A  7000 


BOOK   BULLETIN 


45 


A  detailed  account  of  the  war  record  of  Joyce 
Kilmer's  division  faithfully  told  by  its  genial, 
warm-hearted  chaplain.  Kilmer  was  to  have  writ- 
ten it  and  his  brief  beginning,  interrupted  by 
death,  is  added  in  the  appendix. 

Dunsay,  Lord.  Unhappy  far-off  things. 
1919.  A  6998 

In  these  sketches  the  utter  desolation  of  war- 
ridden  France  is  vividly  portrayed  in  what  the 
New  York  Times  calJs  Dunsany's  "singularly  lucid 
and  beautiful  style,  in  which  the  simplest  descrip- 
tion takes  on  a  dramatic  value." 

Freeman,  Lewis  R.  To  Kiel  in  the  "Her- 
cules." 1919.  A  6996 
An  account  of  the  tour  of  inspection  made  by 
the  Allied  Naval  Armistice  Commission  reporting 
conditions  in  the  ports  of  northern"  Germany  and 
incidentally  giving  a  German  view  of  the  battle  of 
Jutland. 

The  Greek  armv  and  the  recent  Balkan 
offensive."  1919.  A  6968 

Contents:  Summaries  of  the  campaign. — Offi- 
cial communiques  and  press  dispatches. — Congrat- 
ulatory telegrams  and  orders  of  the  day. — Official 
statements. — Editorials  and  articles. 

Knapp,   Grace   H.      The   tragedy   of    Bitlis; 
being    mainly    the    narratives    of    Grisell  . 
M.  McLaren  and  Myrtle  O.  Shane.    1919. 

A  4814 

An  American  who  was  once  a  teacher  in  this 
little  Armenian  town  relates  the  story  as  she  had 
it  from  two  missionary  witnesses  of  the  terrible 
suffering  and  brave  resistance  of  the  village 
when  it  was  besieged  by  the  Turks,  captured  by 
the  Germans,  and  deserted  by  the  Russians. 

A  li$t  of  neutral  ships  sunk  by  the  Germans, 
from  August  8,  1914,  to  April  26,  1917. 
1917.  A  6994 

Maurice,  Sir  F.  The  last  four  months;  how 
the  war  was  won.  1919.  A  6999 

A  comprehensive  picture  of  Foch's  great  cam- 
paign by  a  British  major  general.  He  attributes 
victory  chiefly  to  three  causes:  the  genius  of 
Foch,  the  surprising  development  of  America's 
fighting  power,  and  the  British  army's  recovery 
from  the  spring  reverses. 


A  complete  survey  of  French  history  written 
for  purposes  of  instruction  in  the  American  army. 
Emphasis  is  naturally  placed  upon  the  period  since 
the  Revolution.  The  world  war  is  treated  briefly. 


Russia 

Sayler,    Oliver    M.     Russia,    white    or    red. 
1919.  A  6038 

An  American  newspaper  correspondent  reports 
conditions  in  1917  and  1918  during  the  early 
months  of  the  Bolshevik  regime.  He  writes  dis- 
passionately from  a  somewhat  liberal  point  of 
view. 

Trotsky,   Leon,  pseud.    The   history   of  the 
Russian  revolution  to  Brest-Litovsk.  1919. 

A6040 

The  revolution  is  treated  as  a  people's  movement 
during  which,  the  changing  popular  consciousness 
has  caused  the  Bolshevik  party  to  replace  other 
political  parties.  The  book  contains  a  vigorous 
indictment  of  the  Central  Powers.

Baker,  Ray  Stannard. 
Paris.    1919. 


What  Wilson  did  at 
A  6991 


Being  director  of  the  press  bureau  of  the  con- 
ference Mr.  Baker  speaks  as  the  official  press  agent 
of  the  administration.  His  report  is  clear  and 
concise  and  emphasizes  President  Wilson's  devo- 
tion and  energy  and  explains  his  decisions. 

Hansen,  Harry.  The  adventures  of  the 
fourteen  points;  vivid  and  dramatic 
episodes  of  the  peace  conference  from  its 
opening  at  Paris  to  the  signing  of  the 
treaty  of  Versailles.  1919.  A  6992 

An  entertaining,  informing  and  dramatic  ac- 
count of  the  peace  conference  written  by  a  jour- 
nalist who  is  also  a  student  of  European  diplo- 
macy and  has  a  gift  for  presenting  salient  facts  in 
a  fair-minded  way.

Asia 

The  encyclopedia  sinica. 
*R84 


An  encyclopedic  dictionary  of  China  by  a

there  for  many  years.  It  is  probably  the  most 
useful  reference  work  in  one  volume  on  the  sub- 
ject for  the  general  reader.

The  Book  Bulletin 


of  the 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume   10  April,  1920  No.  4 


Our  Annual  Catalog 

OUR  yearly  list  of  accessions,  the  "Books  of  1919,"  is  now  off  the  press  and 
on  sale  at  the  nominal  price  of  ten  cents  per  copy.  This  is  the  ninth  an- 
nual issue  of  the  list  which  has  established  itself  as  a  convenient  and  use- 
ful library  guide.  It  is  printed  from  the  type  used  in  the  monthly  Book  Bulletin, 
stored  and  re-arranged  for  this  purpose  at  the  end  of  the  year.  Cost  of  new 
composition  is  thus  eliminated  and  publication  expense  reduced,  making  the 
low  selling  price  possible.  Copies  of  the  lists  covering  former  years  are  still 
available  at  the  same  price. 

It  is  interesting  to  follow  the  literary  fashions  and  predilections  of  the 
year  as  exhibited  by  the  new  publications  appearing  in  various  classes.  The 
number  of  books  here  recorded  as  added  to  the  Library  in  1919  is  2,502,  of 
which  286  are  novels  and  other  forms  of  fiction  for  adult  readers,  106  are 
juvenile  stories,  and  101  are  juvenile  books  on  various  subjects.  All  classes  of 
adult  literature  other  than  fiction  are  represented  by  2,009  titles,  their  distri- 
bution being  conditioned  by  the  output  of  the  publishers,  expert  in  anticipating 
the  desires  of  the  reading  public  and  enterprising  in  the  business  of  respond- 
ing thereto. 

It  is  not  surprising,  therefore,  to  find  the  overwhelming  public  interest  in 
psychic  phenomena,  spiritualistic  manifestations  and  kindred  topics  represented 


50  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

by  sixteen  new  books,  general  psychology  by  eight,  and  the  absorbing  topic  of 
personal  efficiency  and  the  development  of  personality  as  an  aid  to  success  by 
six,  not  to  mention  a  ten- volume  treatise  on  character  analysis  and  three  new 
books  on  New  Thought.  Prohibition,  as  a  phase  of  personal  ethics,  is  repre- 
sented by  two  new  books  evenly  balanced  for  and  against.  Eighty-five  titles, 
nearly  all  new,  on  various  topics  in  Religion  were  added  to  the  Library;  four- 
teen of  these  being  on  Bible  study,  four  on  future  life,  seven  volumes  of  ser- 
mons, and  four  on  missions. 

In  the  wide  field  of  Sociology  the  most  fruitful  divisions  were  Economics, 
economic  post-war  readjustment  (thirty-five  titles),  Labor,  including  syndical- 
ism and  socialism,  Democracy  (ten  titles),  books  on  citizenship  and  American- 
ization, government  in  Europe,  and  Foreign  Relations.  No  less  than  fourteen 
books  appeared  on  the  League  of  Nations.  In  Practical  Sociology  the  prob- 
lem of  the  disabled  soldier  is  represented  by  the  same  number  of  new  publi- 
cations, special  efforts  having  been  made  by  the  Library  to  cover  this  subject 
as  completely  as  possible. 

Americanization  is  again  met  with  in  the  field  of  Languages,  where  books 
on  learning  English  and  teaching  English  to  foreigners  are  listed  in  great  va- 
riety. Interest  in  the  Sciences,  pure  and  applied,  together  with  technology  and 
the  Useful  Arts  in  general,  appears,  on  the  other  hand,  to  have  been  tempora- 
rily eclipsed  by  the  preoccupation  of  the  world  with  'political  and  economic  prob- 
lems. Exceptions  were  the  ever-popular  subject  of  automobile  construction 
and  operation,  military  science,  and  the  more  peaceful  pursuits  of  husbandry 
and  housewifery.  Thirty-four  new  books  on  different  branches  of  farming,  gar- 
dening, and  animal  culture  found  their  way  to  our  shelves  and  twenty  new 
cook  books  brought  up  the  rear. 

The  perennial  and  highly  significant  interest  in  books  on  business  subjects 
again  brought  forth  a  large  number  of  admirable  works.  Business  manage- 
ment, efficiency  and  methods ;  office  pr  ictice,  stenography  and  typistry ;  adver- 
tising, accountancy  and  salesmanship,  are  some  of  the  phases  of  commercial 
life  upon  which  a  steadily  growing  number  of  excellent  manuals  is  published, 
evidently  in  response  to  a  keen  and  wholesome  demand  from  young  business 
folk  seeking  help  in  getting  ahead.  These  books  are  usually  costly  and  the 
number  of  copies  required  to  meet  the  persistent  call  involves  large  expendi- 
tures from  the  book  fund.  But  their  usefulness  and  importance  is  so  obvious 
that  there  can  be  no  question  as  to  the  propriety  of  making  the  most  generous 
provisions  possible  in  this  one  of  the  most  practical  and  least  literary  fields  of 
library  service. 

The  Fine  Arts,  covering  altogether  but  four  scant  pages  in  this  catalog, 
hardly  received  their  due  meed  of  attention  from  the  makers  of  books,  but  Lit- 
erature fills  fifteen  pages  with  three  hundred  and  thirty-eight  book  titles,  re- 


BOOK   BULLETIN  51 


fleeting  again  the  steadily  widening  interest  in  literary  pursuits  among  both 
readers  and  writers.  The  number  of  readable  volumes  of  literary  criticism 
and  ca'userie  is  specially  noteworthy,  while  the  growing  popularity  and  in- 
creasing number  of  clever  essayists,  both  English  and  American,  is  a  phenom- 
enon pleasant  to  note.  Writing  for  the  fun  of  writing  instead  of  for  the  fash- 
ioning of  grindstones  for  axes  is  coming  again  into  its  own,  and  our  new  Amer- 
ican practitioners  of  the  art,  with  their  Walking-stick  papers,  Chimney-pot 
papers,  Percolator  papers,  Broome  street  straws  and  similar  delightful  titles', 
are  making  places  for  themselves  besides  such  true  and  tried  Britons  as  Hilaire 
Belloc  and  E.  V.  Lucas.  But  Poetry  and  Drama  still  take  the  lead  by  sheer 
weight  of  numbers,  and  we  "point  with  pride"  to  the  ten  solid  columns  under 
the  former  head,  in  which,  we  venture  to  hope,  not  a  single  worthy  new  ver- 
sifier, however  free,  will  be  found  missing.  The  drama,  too,  is  worthily  repre- 
sented in  this  selection  from  the  year's  product,  both  in  quality  and  quantity. 

Travel  and  description  scarcely  held  its  own  in  the  number  of  new  works 
published,  perhaps  because  of  world  conditions  which  have  extinguished  the 
tourist  and  rendered  even  arm-chair  travels  uninviting.  The  perennial  interest 
in  the  lives  of  other  human  beings  is  sustained  in  the  large  number  of  biog- 
raphies recorded  in  our  list,  while  History  was  naturally  engrossed  with  the 
story  of  the  War,  which  here  fills  no  less  than  eight  pages.  This  material  is 
properly  added  to  the  Library  for  the  use  of  students  and  future  historians, 
though  the  casual  reader  has  been  glad  to  turn  away  from  the  subject  and 
makes  small  demands  upon  our  vast  and  growing  collection  of  War  literature. 
There  are,  besides,  several  flaming  new  books  about  Ireland,  twenty  on  Russia, 
and  a  whole  page  of  miscellaneous  material  on  American  subjects,  including 
a  detailed  list  of  the  charming  little  volumes  comprising  the  scholarly  and 
fascinating  Chronicles  of  America. 

The  total  number  of  works  of  fiction  published  in  this  country  in  1919  was 
671.  Out  of  this  number  286  were  chosen  for  addition  to  the  Library,  after 
examination  of  practically  the  entire  annual  crop,  and  as  the  result  of  such  crit- 
ical and  selective  methods  as  have  been  evolved  from  our  experience,  combined 
with  knowledge  of  the  average  desires  and  preferences  of  our  patrons.  We 
are  fairly  confident  that  the  selection  is,  on  the  whole,  adequate  and  representa- 
tive. The  total  number  of  American  publications  in  all  fields  during  the  year 
was  7,625,  of  which  our  catalog  comprises  2,502. 

Anatole  France  has  defined  criticism  as  the  "adventures  of  a  soul  among 
masterpieces."  The  "Books  of  1919"  may  be  regarded  as  an  annual  report  of 
our  adventures  among  the  literary  manufactures  of  the  year,  taking  the  master- 
pieces with  the  humbler  products.  As  such  it  is  submitted  to  our  readers  for 
their  use  and  approval.

An  expose  of  the  personal  charlatanism  and  graft

was  for  some  time  the  editor  of  TK'S  books  and 
here  seeks  to  defend  and  perpetuate  the  theories, 
while  denouncing  the  man.

(Brief    course    series    in    education.) 

A  textbook  for  normal  schools.  Chapters  are  de- 
voted to  the  various  mental  processes,  social  and 
non-social  instincts,  play,  moral  and  religious  de- 
velopment, exceptional  children,  etc. 

Conduct  of  Life 

Brown,  Charles  Reynolds.    Yale  talks.    1919. 

L  14101 

Simplicity,  directness,  and  a  very  human  and 
arousing  phraseology  characterize  these  talks  to 
young  men  at  Yale  and  at  other  universities.  The 
author  is  the  dean  of  the  school  of  religion  at  Yale. 
— Booklist. 

Jackson,     Bennett     B.,     and    others,     comp. 
Thrift  and  success.     1919.  L  14392 

Useful  compilation  of  articles,  poems,  maxims, 
plays,  and  biographies  instructing  children  in  the 
value  of  thrift  and  thrift  agencies,  such  as  savings 
accounts,  mortgages,  bonds  and  insurance.  It  dis- 
tinguishes carefully  between  thrift  and  the  meaner 
qualities  sometimes  mistaken  for  it. — Booklist. 

McCall,  Anne  Bryan,  pseud.    The  larger  vi- 
sion;  Tower-room   talks.     1919.       L 14108 

Intimate  talks  with  young  girls  collected  from 
the  Woman's  Home  Companion. 


RELIGION 

Cram,  Ralph  Adams.  Gold,  frankincense 
and  myrrh.  1919.  M  8815 

Contents:  Monasticism  and  the  world  crisis. — 
Sacramentalism  and  the  future. — The  philosophical 
necessity. 

Gold  is  the  pure,  imperishable  quality  of  the 
monastic  ideal,  frankincense  the  supreme  act  of 
worship  through  the  Blessed  Sacrament,  myrrh  the 
saving  quality  of  a  right  philosophy  of  life. — Pref-

53 


An  effort  to  comfort  the  bereaved  by  insisting  on 
a  continuance  of  life  after  death  for  those  who  are 
"in  Christ."  .  The  second  part  contains  quotations 
from  men  of'  letters  testifying  to  their  belief  in  im- 
mortality. 

Speer,  Robert  E.  The  new  opportunity  of

L 14914

The  Bible 

Case,  Shirley  Jackson.  The  Revelation  of 
John;  a  historical  interpretation.  1919. 

M6783 

There  is  a  new  translation  of  Revelations  with 
a  running  commentary  explaining  the  meaning  of  its 
language  in  the  light  of  history  and  conditions  in 
the  Roman  province  of  Asia  at  the  time;  also  some 
account  of  other  contemporary  apocalyptic  literature 
to  explain  the  use  of  that  form. 

Genung,  John  Franklin.  A  guidebook  to 
the  Biblical  literature.  1919.  M  6371 

A  constructive  study  showing  the  reciprocal  ac- 
tion of  Biblical  literature  on  the  mind  and  racial 
culture  of  the  Hebrew  people. 

Missions 

Beard,  Frederica.  Graded  missionary  educa- 
tion in  the  church  school;  progressive 
plans  of  social  service  and  missionary  in- 
struction for  training  pupils  from  four 
to  eighteen  years  of  age.  1917.  M  8124 

Speer,  Robert  E.  The  Gospel  and  the  new 
world.  1919.  Ml  1201 

The  author  is  secretary  of  the  Presbyterian  Board 
of  Foreign  Missions  and  discusses  among  other 
things  whether  under  the  changed  world  conditions 
foreign  missions  must  undergo  a  radical  reorgan- 
ization or  merely  broaden  out  in  their  relation  to 
social  and  political  activities. 

Stowell,  Jay  S.,  and  others.  Making  missions 
real;  demonstrations  and  map  talks  for 
teen-  age  groups.  1919.  M  8123 

SOCIOLOGY 

Dobbs,  A.  E.  Education  and  social  move- 
ments, 1700-1850.  1919.  L 19448 

A  contribution  to  the  history  of  social  science 
from  the  time  when  England  was  mainly  agricul- 
tural down  through  the  Chartist  movement,  whose 
failure  resulted  in  the  beginnings  of  economic  or- 
ganization. 

Ellis,  Havelock.  The  philosophy  of  con- 
flict, and  other  essays  in  war-time.  2d 
ser.  1919.  L  15408 


First   series,    Essays   in    war-time,    L  8805. 

Many  of  thtse  essays  are  concerned  with  the  phi- 
losophy of  war  and  related  subjects;  others  con- 
sider aspects  of  the  sex  problem — eugenics,  birth- 
control,  and  psycho-analysis  in  sex;  a  few  deal  with 
the  problems  of  woman,  and  a  number  are  purely 
literary. 

Economics 

Bureau  of  Applied  Economics,  Washington,

of  budgetary  studies.  1919.  Civics  Dept. 

Carver,  Thomas  Nixon.    Principles  of  politi- 
cal economy.    1919.  L  16615 
A   textbook   popularly   written,    but   somewhat    ad- 
vanced   for    high    schools. 

Labor  and  Laboring  Classes 

Basset,  William  R.  When  the  workmen 
help  you  manage.  1919.  L 16934 

While  opposed  to  profit-sharing  as  a  substitute 
for  the  wage  system,  Mr.  Basset  believes  the  in- 
creased dividends  that  follow  from  the  increasing 
representation  of  workmen  in  the  management  of 
industry  point  the  way  to  the  solution  of  labor  dif- 
ficulties. 

Clark,  Neil  M.  Common  sense  in  labor 
management.  1919.  L  16933 

The  editor  of  System  draws  these  suggestions 
from  the  experience  of  practical  managers.  Appen- 
dixes by  way  of  illustrations  give  the  industrial 
council  plan  of  the  International  Harvester  Co., 
and  the  wage  -policy  of  the  Oneida  community. 

Hammond,  M.  B.  British  labor  conditions 
and  legislation  during  the  war.  1919. 

L  16936

Socialism,  Bolshevism 

Goldstein,  David,  and  Avery,  Martha  Moore. 
Bolshevism:  its  cure.  1919  L  17584 

Myers,  William  Starr.  Socialism  and 
American  ideals.  1919.  L 17608 

The  author's  thesis  is  that  "socialism  is  in  theory 
and  practice  opposed  and  contrary  to  the  principles 
of  Americanism,  of  democracy,  and  even  of  the 
Christian-Jewish  religion  itsglf." 

Spargo,  John.  The  psychology  of  bolshe- 
vism.  1919.  .  L.  17635 

Explains  the  causes  of  the  movement,  the  severe 
measures  of  repression  it  has  brought  out,  and 
the  cure  for  what  the  author  calls  an  anti-social 
evil.  He  shows  that  Lenine's  doctrines  are  old 
Marxian  socialism  popularized,  and  that  unrest  can 
not  be  overcome  by  brute  force. — Booklist.

to  the  American  journal  of  science,  1818- 
1918.  K  18629 

Contents:  The  American  journal  of  science  from

The  progress  of  historical  geology  in  North  Amer- 
ica, by  C.  Schuchert;  Steps  of  progress  in  the  in- 
terpretation of  land  forms,  by  H.  E.  Gregory;  The 
growth  of  knowledge  of  earth  structure,  by  J.  Bar- 
rell. — -A  century  of  government  geological  surveys, 
.by  G.  O.  Smith. — On  the  development  of  vertebrate 
paleontology,  by  R.  S.  'Lull. — The  rise  of  petrology 
as  a  science,  by  L.  V.  Pirsson. — The  growth  of  min- 
eralogy from  1818  \o  1918,  by  W.  E.  Ford. — The 
work  of  the  geophysical  laboratory  of  the  Carnegie 
institution  of  Washington,  by  R.  B.  Sosman. — The 
progress  of  chemistry  during  the  past  one  hundred 
years,  by  H.  L.  Wells  and  H.  W.  Foote. — A  cen- 
tury's progress  in  physics,  by  L.  Page. — A  century 
of  zoology  in  America,  by  W.  R.  Coe. — The  develop- 
ment of  botany  since  1818,  by  G.  L.  Goodale.

Physics 

Ferry,  Ervin  S.,  and  others.  A  handbook  of 
physics  measurements.  1918.  2  v. 

K  19097 

The  book  contains  over  a  hundred  experiments 
and  requires  no  knowledge  of  mathematics  beyond 
that  of  the  college  freshman  year. 

Southall,  James  P.  C.  Mirrors,  prisms  and 
lenses;  a  text-book  of  geometrical  optics. 

1918.  K  19557 

An  introduction  to  the  theory  of  modern  optical 
instruments,  based  on  the  extraordinary  progress 
in  ophthalmology  and  applied  optics  in  recent  years. 
The  fundamental  principles  of  ophthalmic  lenses 
and  prisms  are  explained  and  the  whole  field  of 
spectacle  optics  reviewed. 

Geology 

Miller,  Benjamin  L.,  and  Singewald,  Joseph 
T.,  jr.  The  mineral  deposits  of  South 
America.  1919.  K  20403 

Based  upon  the  authors'  experiences  in  several 
of  the  countries  and  upon  published  data.  The 
location  and  sizes  of  the  deposits,  metallic  and  non- 
metallic,  are  given,  together  with  complete  descrip- 
tions of  the  important  mining  districts. — New  Tech- 
nical Books. 

Biology 

Gruenberg,  Benjamin  C.  Elementary  biol- 
ogy; an  introduction  to  the  science  of 
life.  1919.  K  20578 

The  author  lays  stress  upon  the  dynamic  phase 
of  biological  phenomena  and  is  careful  to  apply  the 
scientific  principles  to  matters  of  everyday  life. 
The  book  is  intended  for  hig-h  schools  and  contains 
an  excellent  elementary  presentation  of  Mendel's 
theory. 

Natural  History 

Mills,  Enos  A.  The  adventures  of  a  nature 
guide.  1920.  K 18570 

Mr.  Mills  brings  down  his  observations  with  a 
keen  pair  of  eyes  rather  than  a  shotgun,  and  pre- 
sents his  experiences  in  modest  narrative. — Dial.

Business 

Allen,  Frederick  J.  Advertising  as  a  voca- 
tion. 1919.  L  20647 

The  nature,  growth,  and  probable  future  of  ad- 
vertising as  a  profession  are  discussed,  the  divisions

devoted  mainly  to  practical  systems  of  cost  account- 
ing in  specific  industries  ranging  in  variety  from  a 
blast  furnace  to  a  bakery  or  textile  mill.

LITERATURE 

Curtis,  Georgina  Pell.  The  interdependence 
of  literature.  1917.  J  4806 

Poetry 

Colson,  Ethel  M.  How  to  write  poetry. 
1919.  J  3381 

Mackenzie,  W.  Roy.  The  quest  of  the  bal- 
lad. 1919.  E  14319 

There  is  still  a  class  of  people  in  Nova  Scotia 
who  up  to  recent  years  have  practiced  ballad-sing- 
ing as  a  form  of  social  enjoyment.  Mr.  Macken- 
zie's quest  'was  undertaken  for  the  purpose  of  res- 
cuing the  relics  of  these  ballads  from  oblivion. 

Untermeyer,  Louis,  ed.  Modern  American 
poetry;  an  introduction.  1919.  E  14320 
This  is  the  most  comprehensive  and  concise  se- 
lection that  we  have  had.  There  are  poems  of  hu- 
mor, pathos,  irony,  sentimentalism,  buffoonery,  of 
social  content,  and  of  pure  beauty,  poems  by  the 
rigid  adherents  to  the  traditional  forms  and  poems 
by  the  freest  of  the  vers  libristes. — Chicago  Tribune. 

Poems 

Presland,  John.  Poems  of  London,  and 
other  verses.  1918.  E  11414 

The  pictures  of  London  suggest  rather  than  de- 
pict the  familiar  scenes  to  those  who  know  London 
well.  Mr.  Presland  often  reminds  the  Spectator 
(London)  of  Stevenson  "in  the  imaginative  effects 
he  secures  by  simplicity  and  directness  of  state- 
ment." 

Robinson,  Corinne  Roosevelt.  Service  and 
sacrifice;  poems.  1919.  E  11474 

The  sonnets  are  extraordinarily  fine.  The  poems 
on  her  brother,  Theodore  Roosevelt,  are  not  mere 
lines  written  on  the  occasion  of  his  death,  but  real 
poetry  gushing  from  a  bereaved  heart.  In  her  na- 
ture poems  Mrs.  Robinson  becomes  the  sympathetic 
interpreter. — Bookman. 

Rostrevor,  George.  Escape  and  fantasy, 
poems.  1919.  .  E  11477 

Excellent  technically,  and  many  of  the  pieces  are 
lifted  from  the  ordinary  by  a  sprightly  fancy  and  a 
lyric  swing.  This  young  man  has  a  pleasing  way  of 
his  own. — Springfield  Republican. 

Drama 

Austin,  Jane  G.    Standish  of  Standish.    1919. 

E11773 

Barney,  Laura  Clifford.  God's  heroes;  a 
drama  in  five  acts.  1910.  E  13485 

A  drama  of  early  Esbism.

Agassis,  Elisabeth  Gary.  Paton,  Lucy  Allen. 
Elizabeth  Gary  Agassiz;  a  biography. 
1919.  •  C 20511 

The  life  of  a  New  England  woman  for  twenty- 
three  years  the  wife,  fellow-traveler,  and  profes- 
sional associate  of  Louis  Agassiz,  and  later  one 
of  the  founders  and  first  president  of  Radcliffe  col- 
lege.— Booklist. 

Cadillac,  A.  de  la  Mothe.  Burton,  C.  M.  A 
sketch  of  the  life  of  Antoine  de  la  Mothe 
Cadillac,  founder  of  Detroit.  1895.  C  21108 

Carnegie,  Andrew.  Memorial  service  in 
honor  of  Andrew  Carnegie,  on  his  birth- 
day, Tuesday,  November  25,  1919,  Car- 
negie Music  Hall,  Pittsburgh,  Pennsyl- 
vania. C  21165 

Carroll,  Charles.  Leonard,  Lewis  A.  Life 
of  Charles  Carroll  of  Carrollton.  1918. 

C  21166 

Cody,  Wm.  F.  Cody,  Louisa  F.,  and  Cooper, 
Courtney  R.  Memories  of  -Buffalo  Bill. 
1919.  C  21303 

Somewhat  rambling  sketches  of  the  life  and  per- 
sonal characteristics  of  Buffalo  Bill  as  a  scout,  as 
a  slayer  of  buffaloes  and  of  Indians,  as  a  showman, 
and  as  a  lover  and  friend. 

Macfarlane,  Alexander.  Lectures  on  ten 
British  physicists  of  the  nineteenth  cen- 
tury. 1919.  C 19772 

(Mathematical  monographs,  no.  20.) 
Contents:  James  Clerk  Maxwell. — William  John 
Macquorn  Rankine. — Peter  Guthrie  Tait. — Sir  Wil- 
liam Thomson,  Lord  Kelvin. — Charles  Babbage. — 
William  Whewell. — Sir  George  Gabriel  Stokes. — Sir 
George  Biddell  Airy. — John  Couch  Adams. — Sir 
John  F.  W.  Herschel. 

Rogers,  Clara  Kathleen  (Clara  Doria). 
Memories  of  a  musical  career.  1919. 

C  23979 

Tirpitz,  Admiral  von.  My  memoirs.  1919. 
2  v.  C 24534 

The  first  volume  tells  the  story  of  a  life  devoted 
to  the  building  of  the  German  navy  as  a  part  of 
Pan-German  policy;  the  second  is  a  history  of  the 
progress  of  Germany's  unrestricted  submarine  war- 
fare during  the  war.  Written  largely  to  justify  the 
author  in  his  countrymen's  eyes. 


TRAVEL  AND  DESCRIPTION 
Gaugu.'n,   Paul.     Noa-Noa.     1920.  I  1638 

The  record  of  two  years  spent  in  Tahiti  by  the 
French  artist  in  whom  Maugham  has  created  a 
widespread  interest  through  his  Moon  and  sixpence 
(F  15183).  It  is  illustrated  with  ten  of  Gauguin's 
paintings  and  is  redolent  with  the  charm  of  life 
on  the  perfumed  island  in  the  South  Seas. 

Loti,  Pierre,  pseud,  of  L.  M.  J.  Viaud. 
Madame  Prune.  1919.  I  10706 

Sequel  to  Madame  Chrysantheme,  F  7325. 

A  succession  of  oictures  of  Japan  fifteen  years 
after  the  autr-.or's  first  visit.  Contact  with  the  Oc- 
cident has  changed  and  commercialized  the  people, 
and  while  they  imitate  the  white  man,  they  seem 
secretly  to  hate  him. 

Shackleton,  Sir  Ernest.  South;  the  story 
of  Shackleton's  last  expedition,  1914-1917. 
1920.  I  11351 

The  simple  account  of  the  loss  of  the  ship  En- 
durance, of  the  months  of  drifting  on  huge  floes, 
of  the  journey  made  by  six  men  in  an  open  boat 
for  800  niles  to  fiad  aid  for  those  left  behind  and 
of  the  four  attempts  at  their  rescue,  make  a  fasci- 
nating tale  of  unparalleled  heroism.

•1918.  A  5501 

Story  of  the  Dublin  insurrection  by  a  participant 
— the  daughter  of  James  Connolly,  the  Irish  labor
