Laughlin,  Clara  E.  The  martyred  towns 
of  France.  1919.  A  5752 

These  twenty  or  more  little  histories  serve  the 
author's  purpose  of  reviving  the  intimate  things 
which  recall  the  France  of  "the  centuries  when  she 
was  working  out  her  salvation."  Historic  person- 
ages walk  among  the  pages  and  speak  realistically 
from  out  of  the  ruins  of  today.  A  tribute. — Book- 
list. 

Nosek,  Vladimir.  Independent  Bohemia;  an 
account  of  the  Czecho-Slovak  struggle  for 
liberty.  1918.  A  4327 

European  War 

Bott,  Alan.  Eastern  nights — and  flights;  a 
record  of  oriental  adventure.  1919.  A  7003 
A  thrilling  tale  by  an  English  flyer  who  was  taken 
prisoner  by  the  Turks  and  lived  in  hospital  and 
prison  camp  until,  after  'hair-breadth  failures  to  es- 
cape, he  finally  succeeded  in  making  his  way  into 
Russia. 

Chambrun,  Colonel  de,  and  Marenches, 
Captain  de.  The  American  army  in  the 
European  conflict.  1919.  A  7007 

Davison,    Henry    P.      The    American    Red 

Cross  in  the  Great  War.    1919.          A  7009 

Mr.    Davison    was    chairman    of    the    war    council 

of   the    Red    Cross   during   our   participation    in   the 

war. 

Out  of  the  ruins.  1919. 
A  7001 

Photographs  and  statistics  showing  the  devastation 
of  France,  the  efforts  at  relief  and  reconstruction 
already  in  progress,  and  suggestions  for  coming  to 
her  further  assistance. 

Graham,  Stephen.  A  private  in  the  guards. 
1919.  A  7011 

Even  the  reader  surfeited  with  war  books  will 
find  much  of  interest  in  this  sincere  and  well-writ- 
ten study  of  the  life,  thoughts,  and  feelings  of  the 
man  in  the  ranks  and  the  effect  of  the  war  upon 
him. 

Harrison,  Henry  Sydnor.  When  I  come 
back.  1919.  A  7002 

In  memory  of  an  American  soldier  who  fell  in 
France.  He  was  a  cultured  lawyer,  physically  frail, 
and  he  had  insisted  upon  volunteering  as  a  private 
because  men  were  most  needed  in  that  service. 

Janis,  Elsie.    The  big  show;  my  six  months 

with   the   American   expeditionary  forces. 

1919.  A  7006 

Miss  Janis  contributed  her  talents  to  the  soldiers' 

entertainment  at  the  front. 

McPherson,  William  L.  The  strategy  of 
the  great  war;  a  study  of  its  campaigns 
and  'battles  in  their  relation  to  Allied  and 
German  military  policy.  1919.  A  7004 


Ford,   George   B. 


The  author  was  military  critic  on  the  New  York 
Tribune  during  the  war.  The  first  part  deals  with 
the  general  principles  underlying  the  strategy  of 
both  sides,  and  the  second  shows  the  application  of 
these  principles  in  the  special  campaigns. 

Usher,  Roland   G.     The  story  of  the  great 
war.    1919.  A7056 

A  brief  and  interesting  narrative  of  the  war,  serv- 
ing as  an  interpretation  of  spiritual  values  rather 
than  presenting  a  categorical  statement  of  the  events. 

Mexico 

The  plot  against  Mexico. 
B8571 


De  Bekker,  L.  J. 

1919. 


An  accusation  directed  against  foreign  Investing 
interests  in  Mexico,  claiming  that  they  are  trying 
to  foment  war  in  order  to  get  the  oil  concessions 
under  American  domination.

Government  Aid  for  Gardeners 

HE  following  lists  offer  a  brief  selection  from  the  hundreds  of.  publications 
issued  by  the  Federal  and  State  governments  to  help  the  amateur  gar- 
dener. The  choice  has  been  made  with  special  reference  to  the  needs  of 
those  city  people  who  are  fond  of  outdoor  work  and  are  at  the  same  time 
anxious  to  do  what  they  can  to  reduce  the  high  cost  of  living  and  yet  are  not 
experienced  in  agricultural  details. 

Comprehensive  lists  of  the  Farmers'  Bulletins  and  other  series  published 
by  the  United  States  Department  of  Agriculture  together  with  the  circulars 
of  the  various  Agricultural  Experiment  Stations  of  the  different  states  may 
be  found  in  the  Civics  and  Documents  Department  (Randolph  Street  entrance) 
and  these  documents  may  be  taken  out  on  the  usual  Library  cards. 

The  bulletin  board  at  the  door  of  the  Department  and  the  last  page  of  the 
monthly  Book  Bulletin  will  give  information  of  additional  material  from 
time  to  time.

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Contents:  The  place  of  science  in  modern  civili-

ture of  capital.— Some  neglected  points  in  the  theory 
of  socialism. — The  socialist  economics  of  Karl  Marx.

Economics 

Economic  History  and  Conditions 
O'Brien,  George.     The  economic  history  of 
Ireland  in    the   eighteenth    century.     1918. 

L 16674 


64 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Turner,     John     Roscoe.       Introduction     to 
economics.     1919.  L 16673 

A  book  for  the  layman  which  deals  thoroughly 
and  convincingly  with  the  development  and  laws 
of  economic  science,  with  the  fluctuation  of  prices, 
the  demands  of  labor,  the  functions  of  money,  etc. 
It  furnishes  a  general  survey  with  which  every  voter 
should  be  acquainted. 

Wade,  Sir  Charles  G.  Australia;  problems 
and  prospects.  1919.  L  16676 

Labor 

Gompers,  Samuel.  Labor  and  the  common 
welfare.  1919.  L 16935 

(Labor  movements  and  labor  problems  in  Amer- 
ica.) 

A  compilation  of  the  most  significant  statements 
made  by  the  president  of  the  A.  F.  of  L.  during 
the  last  thirty  years.  They  are  grouped  according 
to  subject,  and  besides  being  of  current  interest 
will  form  reference  material  for  future  biographical 
and  historical  work. 

Hoagland,  Henry  E.  Collective  bargaining 
in  the  lithographic  industry.  1917. 

L  6589,  74 

(Columbia  University  studies  in  history,  eco- 
nomics and  public  law.) 

Lescohier,  Don  D.     The  labor  market.     1919. 

L  16950 

(Social  science  textbooks,  ed.  by  R.  T.  Ely.) 
A    review    of    labor    conditions    during    the    past 
ten    years.      The    author    advocates    a    central    em- 
ployment   bureau    through    which    the    government 
can  control  the  distribution  of  labor. 

Penty,  Arthur  J.  Guilds  and  the  social 
crisis.  1919.  L 17887 

Mr.  Penty  thinks  that  the  present  tendencies  to- 
ward economic  readjustment  are  leading  to  the 
same  evils  of  capitalism,  congestion  and  over-pro- 
duction that  brought  Europe  to  disaster.  He  advo- 
cates a  return  to  the  guild  system  as  the  remedy. 

Slesser,  Henry  H.  An  introduction  to  trade 
union  law.  1919.  L  16661,  6 

(Reorganisation   of   industry   series.) 

The  trade  unions;  organization  and  action; 
papers  by  J.  T.  Murphy,  John  W.  Muir, 
William  Graham,  M.  P.,  with  a  report  of 
the  discussion  to  which  they  gave  rise. 
1919.  L  16661,  5 

(Reorganisation    of    industry    series.) 


Bolshevism 

Hanson,    Ole.      Americanism     versus     bol- 
shevism.     1920.  L  17587 

.  Mr._  .Hanson  tells  how  he  became  mayor  of  Seat- 
tle, discusses  labor  conditions  before  the  attempted 
"revolution"  of  1918,  describes  the  I.  W.  W.  strike 
and  the  drastic  measures  by  which  it  was  suppressed. 
He  traces  the  history  of  Bolshevism  in  Russia  and 
its  advent  here,  contrasting  its  theories  with  princi- 
ples of  Americanism.

Production  and  Conservation 

Smith,  J.  Russell.  The  world's  food  re- 
sources. 1919.  L 17861 

A  large  book  summarizing  the  cultivation  and 
distribution  of  all  the  important  food  products 
used  for  human  consumption  throughout  the  world. 

Spopner,  Hen-ry  J.  Wealth  from  waste; 
elimination  of  waste  a  world  problem. 

1918.  L  17922 

An  invaluable  reference  work  for  thrift  cam- 
paigns in  which  are  collected  examples  of  avoid- 
able waste  of  an  amazing  variety — waste  of  time 
and  strength;  of  life,  limb,  and  health;  of  food  and 
land;  and  miscellaneous  household  and  industrial 
wastes.  It  also  relates  wonders  accomplished  by 
utilizing  what  was  once  waste  material. 

Finance 

Fisher,  Irving.  Stabilizing  the  dollar;  a 
plan  to  stabilize  the  general  price  level 
without  fixing  individual  prices.  1920. 

L 17291 

A  readable  and  convincing  plea  for  ,  stabilizing 
the  purchasing  power  of  the  dollar  by  abolishing 
gold  coin  and  redeeming  certificates  in  gold  bullion, 
varying  the  weight  of  the  bullion  dollar  to  corre- 
spond with  the  cost  of  commqdites.  Clearly  pre- 
sented, with  all  technical  details  relegated  to  the 
appendix. 

MacGregor,  T.  D.  Bank  advertising  experi- 
ence; practical 'ideas  for  financial  publicity 
as  used  by  hundreds  of  banks  and  trust 
companies  in  the  United  States  and  Can- 
ada. 1919.  L 17364 

Owen,  Robert  L.     The  Federal  Reserve  act. 

1919.  L 17363 


Political  Science 

Pillsbury,    Walter    B.     The    psychology    of 
nationality   and   internationalism.     1919. 

L 15866 

From  a  consideration  of  questions  rising  out  of 
the  war  the  conclusion  is  reached  that  national  dif- 
ferences can  be  so  overcome  by  the  spread  of  liter- 
acy and  intercommunication  that  political  organiza- 
tion may  reach  its  ultimate  unity  in  a  community 
of  states. 

Citizenship,  Americanization 

Aronovici,  Carol.     Americanization.     1919. 

L 16045 

Mr.  Aronovici's  practical  suggestions  hinge  on 
the  idea  of  cross-fertilization,  culturally  speaking. 
The  book  is  independent,  thoughtful,  based  on 
twenty  years'  experience,  and  entirely  free  from 
the  clap-trap  that  so  often  accompanies  the  discus- 
sion of  this  topic. — Survey.

zen of  New  York  city;  a  comparative

tice in  fourteenth  century  England  and 
colonial  New  York  city.  1918.  L  15368,  1

sciences  and  history.)

Negroes 

Du  Bois,  W.  E.  Burghardt.  Darkwater; 
voices  from  within  the  veil.  1920.  L  16168 
Only  an  artist  like  Dr.  Du  Bois  can  possibly 
speak  as  he  does  for  the  negro.  And  the  strength 
of  the  case  is  manifested  by  the  story  of  his  own 
relations  with  people,  his  own  temptations  and 
struggles,  his  own  appreciation  of  beauty  through 
the  landscape  veiled  in  pain.  —  From  Francis  Hackett 
in  the  New  Republic. 

Sandburg,  Carl.  The  Chicago  race  riots, 
July,  1919.  1919.  L  16190 

A  report  of  the  conflict  as  it  occurred  from  day 
to  day,  accompanied  by  suggestions  for  avoiding 
its  recurrence.  These  include  a  federal  investiga- 
tion of  race  relations  and  insistence  on  better  hous- 
ing and  labor  opportunities.

Truette,    Everett    E.      Organ    registration. 

K  29950 

"A  comprehensive  treatise  on  the  distinctive  qual- 
ity of  tone  of  organ  stops,  the  acoustical  and  musical 
effect  of  combining  individual  stops,  and  the  selec- 
tion of  stops  and  combinations  for  the  various 
phrases  of  organ  compositions;  together  with  sug- 
gested registration  for  one  hundred  organ  composi- 
tions, hymns,  and  anthems  intended  to  be  played 
on  specific  organs."

Davies,  Trevor  H.  Spiritual  voices  in  mod- 
ern literature.  1919.  J  4839 
Contents:  Francis  Thompson:  The  hound  of  heaven. 
— Ibsen:  Peer  Gynt. — John  Ruskin:  The  seven  lamps 
of  architecture. — Tennyson:  In  memoriam.— The 
letters  of  James  Smetham. — Wordsworth:  Ode  to 
duty. — Morley:  Life  of  Gladstone. — Robert  Brown- 
ing: Saul. — Nathaniel  Hawthorne:  The  scarlet  let- 
ter.— John  Masefield:  The  everlasting  mercy. 

From  each  of  these  literary  works  Mr.  Davies 
draws  a  parallel  to  some  definite  Christian  truth. 

Dowson,  Ernest.     The  poems  and  prose  of 

Ernest  Dowson.    1919.  E  6549 

(The  modern  library  of  the  world's  best  bocks.)

one  act.

literature  of  the  eighteenth  century.  1918. 

J  4450,58 
(Yale   studies    in    English.) 

Jones,  H.  S.  V.  Spenser's  defense  of  Lord 
Grey.  1919.  J  4854,  5  m 

(University  of  Illinois  studies  in  language  and 
literature.) 

A  monograph  dealing  with  the  political  views  of 
the  great  Elizabethan  poet,  with  special  reference 
to  his  defense  of  Lord  Grey's  harsh  measures  in 
Ireland,  which  in  the  sixteenth  century  was  a  strong- 
hold of  Spanish  imperialism.

ry.      1919.  J4854,  51-11 
(University    of    Illinois    studies    in    language    and 
literature.)

Journalism 

Ramsay,  Robert  E.  Effective  house  organs; 
the  principles  and  practice  of  editing  and 
publishing  successful  house  organs.  1920. 

J  7259 

The  book  is  greatly  needed  because  many  publica- 
tions of  commercial  and  professional  organizations 
are  short  lived  from  _  lack  of  knowledge  of  just 
the  fundamental  technical  details  that  are  here  dis- 
cussed. There  are  many  illustrations  of  house- 
organ  covers  and  inside  pages  for  comparison  and 
criticism. 

Composition  and  Rhetoric 

Curl,  Mervin  James.  Expository  writing. 
1919.  J7175 

A  really  interesting  college  text  on  rhetoric  with 
a  wide  variety  of  suggestive  topics  for  themes  and 
of  illustrations  from  classical  and  contemporary 
writers  that  will  make  it.  a  boon  to  high  school 
teachers. 

Foerster,  Norman,  and  Steadman,  J.  M.,  jr. 
Sentences  and  thinking;  a  practice  book  in 
sentence  making.  1919.  J  7174 

A  review  of  the  chief  rules  of  rhetoric  and  com- 
position from  the  standpoint  «f  the  underlying 
principles  of  right  thinking  on  which  they  have 
been  based.  A  textbook  for  college  freshmen  with 
many  useful  suggestions  for  writers  in  general. 

Poetry 

Braithwaite,  William  Stanley,  ed.  The  book 
of  modern  British  verse.  1919.  E  14323 
By  "modern"  Mr.  Braithwaite  means  the  strictly 
contemporary  period  coming  to  be  known  as 
Georgian,  which  he  defines  as  beginning  with 
Masefield  and  growing  into  the  newer  blossom- 
ing of  Sassoon,  Graves  and  Nichols. 

Bunker,  John.  Shining  fields  and  dark  tow- 
ers. 1919.  E  10702 

A  first  book  by  a  young  poet  who  tries  his  hand 
both  at  the  classical  models  and  the  simplicity  of 
the  most  unrestricted  verse.  Critics  differ  widely 
as  to  his  merits. 

Cromwell,  Gladys.  Poems;  with  an  intro- 
duction by  Padraic  Colum.  1919.  E  10782 

Donaldson,  Robert  A.  Turmoil;  verses 
written  in  France,  1917-1919.  E  6550 

War  pictures  of  which  the  best  poetry,  accord- 
ing to  the  Dial,  is  to  be  found  in  the  imagist 
ve/ses.  ' 

Hay,  Elijah.  Poems.  1919.  E 11525 

Published  with  Marjorie  A.  Seiffert's  A  woman  of 
thirty. 

Heidenstam,  Verner  von.  Sweden's  laure- 
ate; selected  poems  tr.  from  the  Swedish 
with  an  introduction  by  Charles  Wharton 
Stork.  1919.  E  11024 


Among  the  earlier  poems  are  narratives  of  ori- 
ental color  and  some  puerilely  gloomy  subjective 
pieces.  Through  a  middle  period  of  ripening,  the 
poet  passes  into  warmer,  more  vigorous  feeling  and 
greater  understanding.  He  has  an  ironic  humor 
of  a  taking  sort. — Dial. 

Lindsay,  Vachel.  The  golden  whales  of  Cal- 
ifornia and  other  rhymes  in  the  American 
language.  1920.  E  7742 

He  chants  of  .just  those  unmitigated  vulgarities 
which  only  emotion  can  rarify.  He  is  strongly 
imaginative,  gaily  inventive,  hugely  humorous  and 
whole-sculed. — Hackett  in  the  New  Republic. 

Besides  the  title  poem  the  most  notable  are  The 
comet  of  prophecy,  Bryan  and  Kalamazoo,  The 
Statue  of  old  Andrew  Jackson,  Daniel-Jazz,  and 
those  on  Roosevelt  and  Alexander  Campbell. 

Mathers,  Edward  Powys.  Coloured  stars; 
versions  of  fifty  Asiatic  love  poems.  1918. 

E  14321 

A  selection  not  only  from  the  poetry  of  China, 
Japan,  and  India,  but  of  Arabia,  Persia,  Afghan- 
istan and  minor  countries  as  well.  In  their  Eng- 
lish' guise  the  poems  show  that  Oriental  opulence 
of  color  and  passion  which  must  characterize  the 
original. 


Misrow,    Jogesh    Chander. 
songs  of  the  dawn.     1920.

other  poems.     1919.  E  11529 

Poems  distinguished  by  a  cultured  artistry  in 
the  use  of  really  poetic  phrasing.  "The  queen  of 
China"  is  a  dramatic  poem;  many  others  are  IOTC 
poems.  Not  striking  any  new  note  in  imagination 
but  fluent  in  the  use  of  conventional  form.  —  Book- 
list. 


Drama,  Theater 

Sayler,  Oliver  M.     The  Russian  theatre  un- 
der the  revolution,     1920.  E  12846 

The  Russian  theater  went  serenely  on,  conserv- 
ative, occupied  with  much  the  same  problems  and 
movements  as  the  theater  in  America.  He  describes 
its  activities  5r>  Moscow  and  Petrograd,  giving  many 
scenes  from  plays  and  ballets  in  vivid  detail.  — 
Booklist.

Plays 

Alencar,  Jose  de.  The  Jesuit;  tr.  from  the 
Portuguese.  1919.  E 13166 

(Poet  Lore  plays.) 

Bennett,  Arnold.  Sacred  and  profane  love; 
a  play  in  three  acts,  founded  on  the  novel 
of  the  sanie  name.  1920.  E  13491 

The  heroine  renounces  her  "profane"  love  for  a 
noted  musician,  becomes  a  famous  novelist,  later 
returns  to  her  lover  on  learning  that  he  has  lost 
everything  and  has  taken  to  drugs,  and  nurses 
him  back  to  health,  sanity,  and  to  his  art. 

Cunningham,  Leon.  The  dralda  bloom;  a 
plav  in  one  act.  1919.  E  13166 

(Poet    Lore    plays.) 

McKnight,  Robert  Wilson.  The  pigeon. 
1919.  E  13166 

(Poet    Lore    plays.) 

Ross,    Clarendon.      Reconsiderations:    .  The 
murderer,  a  miracle;    The  derelict,  a  fan- 
tasy.    1919.  E  13166 
(Poet    Lore    plays.) 

Tassin,  Algernon.  The  craft  of  the  tortoise; 
a  play  in  four  acts.  1919.  E  12886 

A  highly  humorous  intellectual  comedy  on  the 
Shavian  order  but  not  an  imitation.  It  illustrates 
the  relation  of  man  and  woman  in  four  moments 
of  history,  each  act  ending  with  the  woman  ahead 
on  another  stretch  in  the  unequal  race.

Dreiser,  Theodore.  Hey  rub-a-dub-dub;  a 
book  of  the  mystery  and  wonder  and  ter- 
ror ot  life.  1920.  E2504 
Mr.  De  Cassares  m  the  New  York  Times  calls 
these  observations  on.  the  vanity  of  life  "good, 
solid,  ponderous,  bourgeois  skepticism  with  a  tre- 
mendous piece  of  satiric  buffoonery  at  the  end 
called  The  court  of  progress,  which  is  worth  all 
the  iterations  of  the  obvious  that  precedes  it."

morals.     1920.

E3096 


Stimulating  and  provocative  essays  on  a  variety

Hackett  calls  "New  England  idealism  and  priggish-

resartus.     1899.  J  6361 

(Tufts  College  studies.)

E  4586, 10 

(Shelburne    essays.) 

Contents:  Beaumont  and  Fletcher. — Halifax. — 
A  bluestocking  of  the  restoration  (Aphra  Behn). —

Morley,  Christopher.    Mince  pie;  adventures 
on  the  sunny  side  of  Grub  street.     1919. 

E4648 

A  collection  of  essays  which  the  Booklist  calls 
"a  mixture  of  chuckles  on  any  subject  that  seems 
to  come  to  mind — babies,  alarm  clocks,  dogs, 
Christmas  cards,  Robert  Holliday,  syntax,  Presi- 
dent Wilson,  and  others." 


Tomlinson,  H.  M.  Old  junk.  1920.  E  6032 
Sketches  of  ocean  travel  and  sailing  jaunts  in 
the  most  colorful  of  imaginative  prose,  interspersed 
with  intimate  incidents  of  home  and  streets  that 
betray  as  lovable  a  personality  as  Lamb's  own.  "On 
leave"  shows  the  pathetic  estrangement  between 
those  who  have  suffered  the  war-experience  and 
even  the  most  sympathetic  of  home  folk. 

BIOGRAPHY 

Blake,  William.  Gardner,  Charles  William 
Blake,  the  man.  1919.  C  20883 

As  an  introduction  to  Blake's  whole  attitude  to- 
ward life,  this  book  could  hardly  be  bettered.  The 
author  is  sanely  critical  and  always  sympathetic. — 
Chicago  Evening  Post.

Browne,  gentleman,  of  Plymouth  (and  one

Edwards,  Matilda  Betham.  Mid- Victorian

Sarah  Grand.  1919.  C 19471 

Contents:  Coventry  Patmore. — Frederic  Harri- 
son.— George  Eliot,  Madame  Bodichon,  and  Her- 
bert Spencer. — A  visit  to  Baron  Tauchnitz. — An 
after  loon  with  Lord  John  Russell. — Henry  James. 
— Amelia  Blandford  Edwards. — An  hour  with  Miss 
Braddon. — A  French  talk  with  Lord  Kitchener. — 
Recollections  of  Viscount  Morley. — "Mark  Ruther- 
ford."— A  trio  of  pioneers. — Mudie. — Mr.  John 
Murray. — Postscript. 

A  thoroughly  entertaining  volume  of  literary 
reminiscence.  The  author's  shrewd  and  kindly 
judgments  are  to  be  found  implicit  in  the  selec- 
tion and  point  of  her  many  anecdotes  rather  than 
expressly  stated.

La  Fayette,  Marquis  de.  Morgan,  George. 
The  true  La  Fayette.  1919.  C  22834 

A  biography  which  aims  to  give  the  story  ot 
La  Fayette  in  America,  in  his  home,  and  in  the 
light  of  his  connection  with  French  politics.  His 
power  as  a  personality  and  friend  rather  than  as 
a  statesman  is  made  evident. — Booklist.

Bound    with    the    author's    George    Washington. 

Marcosson,  Isaac  F.  Adventures  in  inter- 
viewing. 1920.  C 19788 
Mr.  Marcosson,  whom  the  Bookman  calls  "an 
incorrigible  and  irrepressible  reporter,"  has  had 
more  or  less  intimate  contact  with  very  many  of  the 
dominating  personalities  of  England  and  our  own 
country  for  twenty-five  years.  Besides  leading  sol- 
diers, statesmen,  and  financiers  we  find  also  de- 
lightful glimpses  of  Barrie,  Bennett,  Wells,  Frank 
Narris,  Eugene  Field,  O.  Henry,  etc.

TRAVEL  AND  DESCRIPTION 

Birmingham,  George  A.,  pseud,  of  James 
Owen  Hannay.  An  Irishman  looks  at  his 
world.  1919.  1 10256 

Cannon  Hannay  describes  Ireland's  religion  and 
culture,  her  political  parties,  educational  methods, 
aristocracy  and  middle  classes,  and  concludes  that 
the  form  of  the  state  is  less  important  than  the  , 
work  of  church  and  school,  and  especially  of  parents 
in  the  home. 

Brady,  Edwin  I.     Australia  unlimited.     1919. 

*V3156 

Chesterton,  G.  K.     Irish  impressions.     1920. 

I  J0249 

Contents:  Two  stones  in  a  square. — The  root  of 
reality. — The  family  and  the  feud. — The  paradox 
of  labour. — The  Englishman  in  Ireland. — The  mis- 
take of  England. — The  mistake  of  Ireland. — The 
example  and  a  question. — Belfast  and  the  religious 
problem. 

He  rrgues  his  case  characteristically.  In  mak- 
ing out  a  case  for  Ireland  he  also  makes  out  a 
case  for  a  rural,  a  Catholic,  and  a  "distributive" 
civilization.  Everywhere  there  are  quotable  say- 
ings.— London  Mercury. 

Irving,  Washington.  The  journals  of  Wash- 
ington Irving  (from  July,  1815,  to  July, 
1842).  1919.  3v.  *I  11392 

v.  1.  The  tour  in  Wales,  1815. — France,  1820. — 
Aix-La-Chapelle,  etc. — The  Rhine  country,  Dres- 
den, etc. 

2.     France,  August  1824  to  February  8,   1826. 
3.      Spain. — Tour   through   the    west. — Esopus 
and   Dutch  tour. 

La  Varre,  William  J.  Up  the  Mazaruni  for 
diamonds.  1919.  I  11145 

A  vivid  first-hand  account,  modestly  told,  of 
adventure  in  British  Guiana  by  a  boy  of  twenty 
who  is  a  product  of  boy  scout  training. 

Moore,  F-rederick  F.     Siberia  to-day.     1919. 

18391 

The  author  sees  Bolshevism  as  a  scheme  of  Ger- 
many to  gain  an  ultimate  foothold  in  Russia.  His 
outlook  is  not  cheerful  for  the  immediate  future 
of  Siberia  which  he  believes  can  be  saved  only  by 
education  of  its  peasantry. — Booklist.

Four  hundred  and  eighty-eight  witty  aphorisms 
set  forth  as  conscious  or  subconscious  beliefs  of 
the  usual  American.  These  are  preceded  by  a 
much  longer  preface  containing  destructive  criticism 
of  things  as  they  are  in  the  trenchant  style  of 
these  stimulating  satirists. 

Shipiey,  Arthur  E.  The  voyage  of  a  vice- 
chancellor.  1919.  14321 
This  member  of  the  British  university  mission 
who  toured  the  United  States  in  1918  had  a  good 
time  along  with  his  educational  studies.  Except 
for  one  chapter  seriously  discussing  American 
university  education  the  book  is  a  diary  running 
smoothly  through  a  maze  of  dinners  and  recep- 
tions.— Booklist.

Observations  of  an  English  liberal  presenting  a

110460 

Gibbons,  Helen  Davenport.  Paris  vistas. 
1919.  1 6829 

A  lively,  intimate  autobiographical  description  of

child,  as  a  young  girl,  as  a  wife  living  in  the  Latin 
Quarter,  and  as  a  mother  during  the  war. — Booklist.

Amonelli,  fitienne.     Bolshevik  Russia.     1920. 

A  6036 

A  report  of  conditions  in  Russia  in  April  1918, 
»nd  of  the  first  Bolshevistic^  decrees.  In  spite  of 
the  later  progress  of  events  it  is  still  a  wholesome 
»nd  helpful  presentation  of  the  subject.  The  author 
accounts  for  Bolshevism  as  the  logical  resultant 
of  Slavic  psychology.

Butler,  Ralph.  The  new  eastern  Europe. 
1919.  A  6035 

Deals  in  a  singularly  broad-minded  way  with  the 
status  of  the  Baltic  territory,  Finland,  Lithuania, 
Ukraine,  and  of  Poland  in  1917,  1918,  and  1919. 
Mr.  Butler  lays  due  stress  on  jfce  importance  of 
nationalism  as  a  factor,  but  does'  not  regard  it  for 
that  reason  as  necessarily  good  or  an  end  in  itself. 

Greece  (modern).  Delegation  to  Peace  Con-

(American    Hellenic    Society.) 

Hard,  William.  Raymond  Robins'  own 
story.  1920.  A  6034 

This  book  shows  clearly  and  vigorously  what  Mr. 
Robins  considers  the  opportunity  of  Allied  diplo- 
macy and  its  failure.  He  strongly  desires  economic 
and  other  intercourse  with  the  present  Russian 
government  and  people. 

Lynd,  Robert.     Ireland  a  nation.     1920. 

A  5511 

•Notes  on  the  history  and  literature  of  Ireland 
are  here  sandwiched  in  between  arguments  for  self- 
determination  as  the  only  way  for  England  to  save 
herself  and  the  world  from  another  destructive  war. 
Mr.  Lynd  is  a  strong  nationalist,  though  a  Protes- 
tant.

Bourne,  Randolph.     Untimely  papers.     1919. 

A  7010 

Contents:  Old  tyrannies. — The  war  and  the  in- 
tellectuals.— Below  the  battle. — The  collapse  of 
Americon  strategy. — A  war  diary. — Twilight  of  the 
idols.— Unfinished  fragment  on  the  state. 

Political  papers  by  one  who  was  never  recon- 
ciled to  America's  participation  in  the  war.  "Be-

Contents:  The  doughboy's  religion. — The  junker

understanding. 

The  publishers'  announcement  calls  this  book 
"salt  for  the  tail"  of  the  dove  of  peace.  "The 
radicals  will  quarrel  with  it  because  it  does  not 
go  far  enough.  The  reactionary  win  be  displeased 
because  it  goes  too  far.  But  the  average  American 
will  be  stimulated  and  helped  because  it  points  the 
road  that  he  will  inevitably  travel."

1919. 


Making    tin    can    toys. 
K  30052 


A  useful  book  on  toy  making  by  the  instructor

Wilson,  E.  N.  The  white  Indian  boy;  the 
story  of  Uncle  Nick  among  the  Shoshones. 
1919.  B  4552 

(Pioneer    life    series.) 

_A  story  of  the  real  wild  west  told  by  an  old 
pioneer  and  edited  by  a  university  professor  from 
Utah.  Its  very  evident  truth,  its  artless  story- 
telling style,  and  its  real  thrills  of  Indian  dav» 
and  frontier  troubles  give  it  precedence  over  the 
usual  second-hand  stories. — Booklist.

Holidays  comprise  New  Year's  Day,  Lincoln's  Birthday,  Washington's  Birthday,  Memorial 
Day,  Independence  Day,  Labor  Day,  Thanksgiving  Day,  Christmas  Day. 
All  departments  are  closed  en  Independence  Day  and  Christmas  Day. 


BRANCHES 

Independent  collections  of  3,000  to  20,000  volumes  in  quarters  owned  or  rented  by  the 
Library,  or  occupied  by  arrangement  with  the  Park  Boards,  and  administered  by  a  librarian 
and  staff. 

Branches  marked  *  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  those  marked  f  are  open  from 
9  a.  m.  to  9  p.  m. ;  those  marked  $  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  5  p.  m. ;  all  others  are  open 
from  1  to  9  p.  m.;  and  all  are  open  holidays  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

The  Book  Bulletin 


of  the 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume  10  June,  1920  No.  6 


"Why,  I  Never  Knew—!" 

THE  secretary  to  the  president  of  a  large  coal  company  was  requested  to 
look  up  data  on  publicity  for  the  coal  business.     She  came  first  to  the 
Public  Library  where  she  was  given  Borsodi's  'Coal  Advertising,'  which 
seems  to  have  been  just  what  the  chief  wanted.  "Why!"  he  exclaimed,  "I  never 
knew    there    were    such    books !"      The  next  time  he  addressed  his  sales  force 
he  took  occasion  to  mention  his  "efficient  secretary  who  knows  how  to  use  the 
Public  Library,"  adding  that  his  hearers  might  find  much  to  profit  them  by 
acquiring  similar  familiarity  with  it. 

The  librarian  of  our  largest  branch  recently  called  upon  some  of  the  busi- 
ness men  in  her  district.  Among  others  she  visited  the  banker,  taking  with 
her  'Advertising  and  Service,'  published  by  the  A.  W.  Shaw  Company.  "Why !" 
said  the  banker,  "that's  a  book  I've  been  wanting  to  read  for  a  long  time  but 
it  is  so  expensive  that  I  hesitated  about  buying  it.  Can  I  borrow  it  for  a.  few 
days?"  "Of  course,"  said  the  librarian,  "that  is  what  I  bought  it  for.  And,  by  the 
way,  we  have  others  along  the  same  lines."  "You  don't  tell  me !"  replied  the 
banker.  '.'Do  you  know,  I  never  knew  you  kept  such  books." 

For  hep  visit  to  the  caterer  the  librarian  armed  herself  with  Kirkland's 
'Modern  Baker,  Confectioner  and  Caterer/  an  English  work  replete  with  elab- 
orate illustrations  of  table  decorations  and  fancy  cookery  and  bakery  in  the  high- 


78  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

est  reaches  of  the  art.  "Say,"  spoke  the  caterer,  "I  have  a  big  wedding  job 
that  I  want  to  do  in  particular  style.  Let  me  take  that  book  for  three  days." 
He  got  the  book  and  came  to  the  branch  on  the  third  day  to  return  it  with 
profuse  expressions  of  gratitude,  to  which  he  added :  "That  book  just  earned 
me  fifty  dollars.  I  got  some  ideas  out  of  it  that  had  never  been  used  before. 
I  never  knew  I  could  get  such  books  in  the  Public  Library." 

One  of  the  largest  of  the  packing  companies  at  the  Stock  Yards  was  slow 
in  accepting  the  suggestions  of  certain  heads  of  departments  who  desired  to  as- 
semble an  office  library  of  the  few  but  expensive  books  that  have  been  pub- 
lished on  the  meat  industry,  together  with  some  collateral  lines.  When  the  idea 
was  finally  approved  and  the  books  bought,  the  iiouse  grudgingly  assigned  a 
young  woman  file  clerk  to  take  charge  of  them  in  her  odd  moments.  A  rep- 
resentative of  our  traveling  library  department,  calling  to  "sell"  a  Public  Li- 
brary deposit  of  books,  found  the  young  woman  responsive  but  uninformed, 
and  deeply  sceptical  about  the  attitude  of  the  firm.  A  beginning  was  made, 
however,  with  a  small  collection  from  the  Library,  comprising  works  on  pub- 
licity, salesmanship,  accounting,  and  other  pertinent  subjects,  which  were  ea- 
gerly welcomed  by  the  staff  in  the  several  departments.  The  enthusiasm  of 
the  young  file  clerk  was  kindled  and  her  latent  ability  brought  into  play,  with 
the  result  that  the  officials  finally  became  interested,  the  deposit  was  increased 
to  include  a  good  collection  of  leisure-time  reading,  the  entire  personnel  was 
put  in  touch  with  the  library,  and,  incidentally,  the  file  clerk  developed  into 
a  real  and  highly  efficient  librarian.  A  few  weeks  ago  she  was  sent  at  Com- 
pany expense  to  the  convention  of  the  Special  Libraries  Association  in  New 
York,  where  she  met,  heard  and  mingled  with  some  hundreds  of  her  own  kind, 
librarians  of  "special  libraries"  in  business  houses,  factories,  banks  and  indus- 
trial plants ;  an  assemblage  of  experts  who,  within  a  few  years,  have  made  a  dis- 
tinct place  for  themselves  in  modern  business  administration.  And  thus  the 
library  idea  was  "sold"  to  the  last  of  the  big  packers.  Our  deposit  depart- 
ment declares  that  it  takes  about  ninety  days  to  sell  the  Library  to  a  new  pros- 
pect in  the  business  world.  Nearly  always  the  first  response,  either  expressed 
or  pointedly  implied,  is  to  the  effect  that  "we  never  knew  there  were  such 
books !" 

It  is  no  longer  good  natural  history  to  refer  to  the  alleged  propensity  of 
the  ostrich  to  stick  his  head  in  the  sand  by  way  of  escaping  trouble.  Yet  the 
outworn  simile  suggests  itself  as  a  fit  illustration  of  the  attitude  still  held  by 
a  large  though  rapidly  diminishing  number  of  business  men  toward  the  Public 
Library.  The  fault,  no  doubt,  rests  partly  with  the  libraries — but  not  alto- 
gether. 


BOOK  BULLETIN  79 


BOOKS  FOR  VACATION 

VACATION  loans  are  made  on  and  after  June  first  to  any 
registered  card-holder.  Ten  books  or  less  may  be  taken  out 
to  be  returned  on  or  before  October  first.  Five  of  the  ten 
may  be  fiction,  other  than  seven-day  books;  the  rest  may  be  drawn 
at  will  from  any  or  all  classes  of  literature,  except  the  most  recent 
books,  or  all  ten  may  be  non-fiction  if  preferred. 

Vacation  loans  are  made  to  juvenile  borrowers  upon  the  writ- 
ten request  of  parent  or  guardian. 

The  loan  must  be  made  in  one  transaction  either  at  the 
Central  Library  or  at  a  branch.  Ten  books  or  less  will  be  issued  at 
one  time  for  the  whole  vacation  period  and  may  be  returned  in 
installments.  The  borrower's  regular  library  card  must  be  sur- 
rendered when  the  vacation  loan  is  made,  and  will  be  returned  to 
him  when  all  obligations  have  been  canceled. 

Borrowers  may  arrange  to  have  their  vacation  books  shipped 
out  of  town  to  their  summer  address  by  leaving  the  amount  of 
postage  plus  a  fee  to  cover  wrapping  and  shipping,  at  the  rate  of 
three  cents  per  volume  or  twenty-five  cents  for  ten.  All  shipping, 
both  ways,  will  be  at  the  borrower's  risk. 

Borrowers  may  obtain  help  in  making  their  selections  by 
inquiring  at  any  branch  or  at  the  Information  Desk  of  the  Central 
Library. 

When  books  urgently  desired  for  vacation  use  are  not  avail- 
able on  the  day  when  the  borrower  calls,  arrangements  may  be 
made  to  have  them  sent  later  by  leaving  a  deposit  to  cover  post- 
age and  shipping  charges.  Any  balance  of  such  deposit  remaining 
at  the  end  of  the  vacation  period  will  be  refunded. 

Parcel-Post  Cards 

We  would  call  attention,  also,  to  the  parcel-post  card  issued 
by  the  Library.  This  is  a  plan  enabling  the  borrower  to  pay  for  a 
dollar's  worth  of  postage  at  a  time,  so  that  he  may  receive  and  send 
back  books  by  mail  when  out  of  the  city.  This  arrangement, 
however,  covers  only  the  regular  loan  period  of  two  weeks  (with 
the  privilege  of  renewal  for  two  more  weeks)  and  does  not  have 
reference  to  vacation  loans.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

PHILOSOPHY 


Nietzsche,   F.    W.      The 


antichrist.      1920. 
L 14787 


(The  free  lance  -books.) 

_H.  L.  Mencken,  who  is  responsible  for  this  re- 
vival of  Nietzsche,  defends  it  on  the  ground  that 
in  his  opinion  Nietzsche  will  prove  to  be  civiliza- 
tion's most  important  ally  against  Bolshevism 
which  he  virtually  described  in  terms  of  one  of  the 
hated  forms  of  democracy. 

Psychology  and  Ethics 

Bradby,  M.  K.  Psycho-analysis,  and  its 
place  in  life.  1920.  L  13891 

(Oxford    medical    publications.) 

A  discussion  of  the  practical  possibilities  of  psy- 
cho-analysis not  merely  confined  to  the  practitioner 
and  neurotic.  The  London  Times  calls  it  a  "good- 
tempered"  book. 

Dumpnt,  Theron  Q.  Practical  memory 
training.  1916.  L 13966 

Peters,  Charles  Clinton.  Human  conduct;  a 
textbook  in  general  philosophy  and  ap- 
plied psychology  for  students  in  high 
schools,  academies,  junior  colleges,  and 
for  the  general  reader.  1918.  L  13937 

A  practical  mixture  of  science,  logic,  psychology 
and  ethics  combined  into  the  introductory  form 
common  to  courses  in  general  science  for  high 
schools.  There  is  much  in  the  book  for  young 
people  to  think  about. — Booklist. 


Straus,  S.  W.  History  of  the  thrift  move- 
ment in  America.  1920.  L 14415 

(Lippincott's  thrift  text  series.) 

The  first  part  deals  with  America's  record  of 
thriftlessness  and  the  need  for  an  organized  move- 
ment here,  while  the  second  offers  suggestions  to 
help  the  individual  in  the  practice  of  thrift.

General  Works,  Social  Theory 
Leacock,   Stephen.     The   unsolved  riddle  of 
social  justice.     1920.  L 15419 

The  riddle  is  the  unaccountable  fact  that  no 
matter  how  infinitely  the  increase  in  the  world's 
production  surpasses  that  of  its  population  the 
supply  fails  more  and  more  to  be  enough  for  all.

tions, industrial  progress,  organization.

Todd,  Arthur  James.  The  scientific  spirit 
and  social  work.  1919.  L  18671 

Mr.  Todd  believes  that  the  social  worker  should 
be  trained  to  apply  all  available  sociological 
knowledge  and  the_  principles  of  scientific  man- 
agement employed  in  the  most  progressive  busi- 
ness houses. 

Ward,  Harry  F.  The  new  social  order; 
principles  and  programs.  1919.  L  15508 

An  optimistic  view  _  of  the  economic,  political, 
and  religious  reorganizations  of  the  present,  in- 
cluding activities  of  the  British  labor  party,  A.  F. 
of  L.,  the  Russian  soviet,  socialistic  leagues,  fed- 
erated churches,  etc. 

Economics 

Sonnichsen,  Albert.  Consumers'  cooperation. 
1919.  L  17547 

This  book  gives  a  full  account  of  the  co-opera- 
tive movement,  including  its  origins  in  Rochdale, 
through  its  development  in  Europe  and  its  his- 
tory in  this  country,  with  a  full  description  of  its 
present  status  and  an  interpretation  of  its  fail- 
ures and  successes.

L  20147 

An  unconventional  treatment  of  the  subject  dis- 
approving strongly  of  government  ownership  on  the 
basis  of  the  undesirable  social  and  political  re- 
actions sure  to  result  from  the  consequent  con- 
fusion of  public  and  private  affairs.

on  every  phase  of  Americanization  from  a  number 
of  such  widely  different  authors  as  Edward  Ever- 
ett, Miss  Addams,  and  Justice  Brandeis.

SCIENCE 

Mills,  John.  The  realities  of  modern  science; 
an  introduction  for  the  general  reader. 
1919.  K 18549 

The  earlier  chapters  develop  the  fundamental 
concepts  of  modern  science  in  chronological  order 
without  abstract  mathematical  formulations. 

Mathematics 

Warne,  Frank  J.  Chartography  in  ten  les- 
sons. 1919.  "  K  18665 

A  guide  for  beginners  in  reducing  statistics  to 
a  clear  and  forcible  presentation  in  the  form  of 
easily  read  charts,  tables,  and  diagrams. 


Physics 

Lorentz,  H.  A.  The  Einstein  theory  of 
relativity;  a  concise  statement.  1920. 

K  19104 

Brief  lectures  on  the  underlying  principles  of 
Einstein's  theory  by  the  Dutch  professor  who  has 
been  most  closely  associated  with  him  in  the  de- 
velopment of  details.  The  anonymous  introduc- 
tion gives  a  clear  idea  of  the  significance  of  this 
contribution  to  natural  science.

Business 

Kelly,  Fred  C.  Human  nature  in  business; 
how  to  capitalize  your  everyday  habits 
and  characteristics.  1920.  L  20430 

Articles  reprinted  from  popular  magazines.  They

conditions,  location  with  reference  to  the  points 
of  the  compass,  people's  moods  as  affected  by  meal 
time,  etc.,  should  be  taken  into  account  in  every- 
day business  transactions.

LITERATURE 
Poetry 

Gudterman,  Arthur.  Ballads  of  old  New 
York.  1920.  E 10994 

Interesting  bits  of  history  and  lore  put  together 
chronologically.  Each  ^incident  is  given  a  prose 
introduction,  then  told  in  swinging  narrative  verse 
and  followed  by  light  lyrics  ...  A  delightfully 
whimsical  book. — Booklist.

by  the  two  ideals  of  art  and  science  he  made  his 
stories,  and  the  complete  philosophy  of  his  prac- 
tice as  an  artist  might  be  woven  together  of  ex- 
tracts from  his  letters  .  .  .  The  translation  is  in 
admirable  English. — The  Times  (London).

"She  seemed  so  essentially  feminine,  low-voiced,

Hutten,  B.  R.,  Baroness  von. 
1920. 


Happy  house. 
F 14237 


Ward,  Mrs.   Humphry.     Harvest. 


A  quiet  little  story  of  the  domestic  type,  deal- 
ing with  English  rural  life.  It  records  the  hap- 
penings of  the  family  of  a  successful  authoress 
who  do  not  appreciate  her  in  spite  of  the  fact 
that  her  writings  have  supplied  all  their  needs 
and  luxuries. 

McCutcheon,  George  Barr.  Anderson  Crow, 
detective;  illustrated  by  John  T.  Mc- 
Cutcheon. 1920.  F 15071 

Sequel  to  The  daughter  of  Anderson  Crow. 
F  14993. 

McKenna,  Stephen.     Sheila  intervenes.  1920. 

F 14951 

Sheila  is  happy  and  she  wants  every  one  else  to 
be  happy,  so  she  tries  to  make  over  four  lives. 
She  gets  them  all  in  a  hopeless  tangle  from  which 
she  eventually  frees  them.  It  is  one  of  the  au- 
thor's earlier  novels  reprinted. 

Marshall,  Archibald.     Many  Junes.     1920. 

F 15087 

An  early  novel  recently  rewritten  by  the  au- 
thor. The  hero's  happy  childhood  is  brought  ab- 
ruptly to  an  end  by  his  father's  death.  After  that 
follow  bitter  sorrow  and  the  loss  of  all  he  loves. 
We  are  led  to  believe  that  he  will  build  on  the 
ashes  of  his  faijure  a  rather  make-the-best-of-it 
philosophy  of  living. 

Morley,  Christopher.     Kathleen.     1920. 

F 15576 

A  slight  and  improbable  story  of  the  college 
prank  of  some  Oxford  students  out  in  search  of 
adventure. 

Mundy,  Talbot.     The  eye  of  Zeitoon.     1920. 

F 15576 

This  book  describes  the  present  conditions 
among  the  Armenians.  The  four  friends  who  fol- 
lowed the  "Ivory  trail"  appear  also  in  this  ex- 
citing tale  of  war  and  adventure  in  the  mountains 
of  Armenia. 

Orczy,  Baroness,  (Mrs.  Montague  Bar  stow). 
His  Majesty's  well-beloved.  1919.  F  15847 

The  story  of  Thomas  Betterton,  an  English  ac- 
tor in  the  seventeenth  century,  and  his  many  ad- 
ventures in  winning  the  love  of  the  lovely  Lady 
Barbara. 

Rowland,  Henry  C.     Duds.     1920.      F 17519 

An  interesting,  improbable,  swiftly  moving  and 
very  entertaining  tale  of  mystery  and  intrigue. 

Serao,  Matilde,  (Signora  M.  S.  Scarfoglip). 
Souls  divided.  1920.  F  17731 

A    translation    from    the    Italian    telling    a    love 

story  in  the  form  of  a  man's  impassioned  letters  to 

'    the  object  of  his  hopeless  adoration.     They  breathe 

an    old    world    atmosphere   and    are    very    romantic. 

Sidgwick,  Mrs.  Alfred.     Iron  cousins.    1919. 

F  18083 

A  young  English  governess  who  is  in  Germany 
just  before  the  war  tells  her  story.  It  is  a  de-

with  an  international  love  interest.

F  18216

Short  stories  much  above  the  average.  The

YOUNG  PEOPLE'S  BOOKS 
Miscellaneous 

Carpenter,  Frank  G.  Around  the  world  with 
the  children.  1917.  H  3409 

A  very  simple  geographical  reader,  in  which  the 
child  visits  his  little  brothers  and  sisters  in  other 
parts  of  the  world,  to  see  their  homes  and  to 
learn  how  their  wants  are  supplied.  The  illustra- 
tions, of  which  there  are  many,  tell  a  most  inter- 
esting story. 

Haines,  Donal  Hamilton.  The  dragon-flies; 
a  tale  of  the  flying  service.  1919.  H  4289 

An  interesting,  live  account  of  the  experiences 
of  an  American  college  athlete  who  becomes  a 
member  of  the  Aviation  Corps  and  sees  active 
service  overseas.  The  book  would  appeal  to  boys 
from  the  age  of  twelve  upwards. 

Rowell,  Cora  W.  Leaders  of  the  great  war. 
1919.  H  6052 

Contents:  Marshal  Joffre. — Marshal  Petajn. — 
Marshal  Foch. — Lord  Kitchener. — Marshal  Haig. — 
Admiral  Beatty. — David  Lloyd  George. — Georges 
Clemenceau. — Admiral  Sims. — General  Pershing. — 
Woodrow  Wilson. 

Interesting  and  instructive  accounts  of  the 
youth,  manhood,  and  international  achievements  of 
the  great  leaders  of  the  war.  Many  intimate 
touches  and  anecdotes  make  the  stories  very  hu- 
man.

Psychical  Research 

Carrington,  Hereward.  Your  psychic  pow- 
ers and  how  to  develop  them.  1920. 

L  13524 

Dresser,  Horatio  W.  The  open  vision;  a 
study  of  psychic  phenomena.  1920. 

L  13545 

A  mystical  study  of  the  inner  vision,  daily  im- 
pressions, and  premonitions  which  may  be  ascribed 
to  the  psychic  faculties. 

Spiritualism 

De  Koven,  Anna,  (Mrs.  Reginald  De  Ko- 
ven).  A  cloud  of  witnesses;  with  an  in- 
troduction by  James  H.  Hyslop.  1920. 

L  13507 

A  sincere  testimony  to  the  possibility  of  commu- 
nication between  this  world  and  the  next.  It  con- 
tains a  verbatim  record  of  conversations  between 
Mrs.  De  Koven  and  her  sister,  Mrs  H.  C.  Chatfield- 
Taylor,  through  a  medium  vouched  for  by  Professor 
Hyslop. 

Denis,  Leon.  Life  and  destiny;  tr.  into 
English  by  Ella  Wheeler  Wilcox.  L  13544 

A  Frenchman's  presentation  of  the  philosophy  of 
spiritualism  as  deduced  from  his  own  mature  ex- 
periences. It  devotes  considerable  attention  to  the 
theory  of  reincarnation. 

Henslow,  George.  The  proofs  of  the  truths 
of  spiritualism.  1919.  L  13506 

A  wealth  of  brief  quotations  from  authorities  and 
records  of  notable  experiences,  with  some  personal 
adventurings  of  this  aged  and  earnest  believer. — 
Booklist. 

Thy  son  liveth;  messages  from  a  soldier  to 
his  mother.  1918.  L  13509 

The  theme  of  the  messages  is  "There  is  no  death." 

Psychology 

Beyle,  Henri,  (Stendahl).  On  love,  tr.  from 
the  French.  1915.  L  13963 

Dumont,  Theron  Q.  The  power  of  concen- 
tration. 1916.  L 13898 

Hunter,  Walter  S.  General  psychology. 
1919.  L 13901 

An  introductory  psychology  distinguished  by  its 
rapid  survey  of  animal  psychology,  individual,  ap- 
plied, abnormal,  social  and  racial  psychology.  A 
good  book  for  general  readers  or  students  who  do 
not  care  to  specialize  in  the  subject. — Booklist. 

MacMurchy,  Helen.  The  almosts;  a  study 
of  the  feeble-minded  in  history  and  fic- 
tion. 1920.  L  13335 
A  unique  and  sympathetic  treatment  beginning 
with  the  court  fools  of  the  fifteenth  century, 
through  Shakespeare's  fools,  Bunyan's  Mr.  Feeble 
Mind,  Scott's  Mr.  Gellatly  and  Wamba,  and  Dick- 
ens' Edmond  Sparkler  and  Maggy,  Mr.  Toots, 
Smike,  and  Barnaby  Rudge,  to  Marm  Lisa  and  Alice 
Hegan  Rice's  Mr.  Opp. 

Trabue,  M.  R.,  and  Stockbridge,  F.  P.  Meas- 
ure your  mind;  the  mentimeter  and  how 
to  use  it.  1920.  L  13730 

"A  popular  treatise  on  the  measurement  of  in- 
telligence by  scientific  methods,"  written  by  a  pro- 
fessor of  Columbia,  who  was  at  the  head  of  the 
psychological  examinations  in  two  U.  S.  A.  camps, 
assisted  by  a  journalist. 


Watson,  John  B.  Psychology,  from  the 
standpoint  of  a  behaviorist.  1919.  L  13957 

New  Thought,  Psychoanalysis 

Blanchard,  Phyllis.  The  adolescent  girl;  a 
study  from  the  psychoanalytic  viewpoint, 
with  a  preface  by  Dr.  G.  Stanley  Hall. 
1920.  L 13889 

_A  valuable  _  summary  of  the  main  theories  of 
Fichte,  Schelling,  Von  Hartmann,  Bergson,  Freud, 
Trotter,  Adler,  Jung,  Maeder,  and  others;  and  has 
interest  for  a  wider  audience  than  the  title  sug- 
gests.— Booklist. 

Evans,  Elida.  The  problem  of  the  nervous 
child;  introduction  by  C.  G.  Jung.  1920. 

L 13687 

A  veritable  compend  of  the  workable  principles 
of  the  psychoanalytic  theories  as  applied  to  child 
life. — Dr.  Harriet  F.  Coffin  in  The  Survey. 

Holmes,  Ernest  Shurtleff.  Creative  mind 
and  success.  1919.  L  13221 

Holmes,  Fenwicke  L.  The  law  of  mind  in 
action;  daily  lessons  and  treatments  in 
mental  and  spiritual  science.  1919. 

L  13222 

The  chapters  are  optimistic,  tending  to  give  poise 
and  control,  and  are  built  upon  the  creative  power 
of  thought.  Their  chief  value  is  their  simplicity  of 
statement. — Booklist. 

Randall,  John  Herman.  The  spirit  of  the 
new  philosophy.  1919.  L 13228 

An  after-the-war  book  of  consolation. — Booklist. 

Tridon,  Andre.  Psychoanalysis;  its  history, 
theory  and  practice.  1919.  L  13954 

This  book  is  more  valuable  than  the  usual  popu- 
lar exposition  of  psychoanalysis,  since  it  summarizes 
on  all  important  points  the  views  of  Freud,  Jung, 
Adler,  White,  Jelhffe,  Kempf,  Jones  and  others  who 
are  working  in  this  unsettled  field. — Booklist. 


Cheley,   F.   H. 
1920. 


Ethics 

Stories    for   talks   to   boys. 
L 14102 


Chesterton,  Gilbert  K.     The  superstition  of 
divorce.    1920.  L 14298 

Clever  and  witty  articles  deploring  the  frequency 
of  divorce  and  remarriage  in  England,  where  this 
book  of  Mr.  Chesterton's  has  been  one  of  the  most 
popular  of  the  season. 

La   Motte,   Ellen   N.   The  opium  monopoly. 
1920.  L 14562 

From  her  intimate  acquaintance  with  the  Far 
East  the  author  pleads  for  the  protection -of  subject 
nations  from  the  evils  of  drugging. 


RELIGION 

Carpenter,   Edward.      Pagan   and    Christian 
creeds:     their  origin  and  meaning.     1920. 

M7972 

The  evolution  of  religion  from  the  era  of  "simple 
consciousness,"  through  the  stage  of  "self-conscious- 
ness" typified  by  Cain  and  Abel  and  culminating  in 
the  world  war,  to  the  stage  of  "universal  conscious- 
ness" on  which  we  are  now  entering.  The  Nation 
says  he  draws  more  radical  conclusions  from  the 
researches  of  Tyler  and  Frazer  than  those  authors 
themselves  would  have  thought  of  drawing.

Eliot,  Charles  W.  The  road  to  unity  among 
the  Christian  churches.  1920.  M  8893 

Dr.  Eliot  advises  abolishing  the  sectarian  training 
of  ministers,  removing  from  rituals  the  phrases  nc 
longer  believed,  allowing  the  admission  of  members 
without  subscription  to  a  creed,  and  cooperating  ef- 
fectively against  physical  evils.

Graves,  Frank  Pierreppnt.  What  did  Jesus 
teach?  An  examination  of  the  educational 
material  and  methods  of  the  Master.  1919. 

M7132 

Approaches  the  subject  from  the  viewpoint  of  the 
confused  or  doubting  person,  and  without  attempt- 
ing great  depth  of  research,  comes  to  common  sense 
appreciation  of  the  character  of  Jesus  stripped  of 
the  sentimentality  and  legend  which  has  grown  up 
about  him. — Booklist.

lows. 1920.  M  9160

SOCIOLOGY 

General  Works 

Barker,  John  Marshall:  The  social  gospel 
and  the  new  era.  1919.  M  8606 

Discusses  community  surveys,  public  health,  the 
social  problems  of  city  and  country  in  a  cursory 
but  very  helpful  and  suggestive  way. 


Brasol,  Boris  L. 
1920. 


Socialism  vs.   civilization. 
L  17568 


A  critical  study  of  the  socialistic  tendencies  of  to- 
day with  reference  to  Marxian  theories.  The  book 
shows  evidence  of  wide  reading  and  personal  knowl- 
edge of  Russian  affairs. 

Butler,  Nicholas  Murray.  Is  America  worth 
saving?  Addresses  on  national  problems 
and  party  policies.  1920.  L  15403 

An  optimistic  answer  to  the  question  founded  on 
the  author's  belief  in  the  power  of  education  to 
build  up  a  world  that  shall  be  based  on  moral  and 
spiritual  values. 

Cory,  Herbert  Ellsworth.  The  intellectuals 
and  the  wage  workers;  a  study  in  educa- 
tional psychoanalysis.  1919.  L  15407 

Contents  :  Equality.  —  Proletarianism.  —  Religion.  —  • 
Criticism.  —  History  and  freedom.  —  Liberty.  —  The 
class  struggle  and  fraternity.  —  Education,  a  program 
for  the  American  university. 

An  attempt  to  present  the  terms  upon  which  in- 
tellectuals and  wage  earners  should  unite  in  the 
task  of  social  reconstruction.  ...  It  is  evident  that 
he  has  written  for  scholars  rather  than  for  students. 
—  Nation. 

Davies,  George  R.    National  evolution.  1919. 

L  15532 

(The    national    social    science    series.) 
The  book  is  a  condensation  of  social  theories,  the 
only  original   point   being  "an.  attempt  to    harmonize 
the    cultural    theory    of    history    with    the    concrete 
workings    of    economic    law."  —  Booklist.

Barton,    Clarence   W.     A    world    remaking;

The  dramatic  role  played  by  finance  in  the  world

tional credit,  and  from  conditions  in  Russia  to  the

chinery.— Reedy's  Mirror.

Douglas, 

1920. 


C.     H. 


Economic     democracy. 
L 16621 


The  author  advocates  "administration  of  credit 
by  a  decentralized  local  authority;  the  control  of 
process  entirely  by  the  organized  producer,  and  the 
fixing  of  prices,  on  the  principle  of  use-value,  by 
the  community  as  a  whole  operating  by  the  most 
flexible  representation  possible." 

Thompson,  Charles  Manfred.  Elementary 
economics,  with  special  reference  to 
social  and  business  conditions  in  the 
United  States.  1919.  L  16679 

Written  for  high  school  students  with  the  aim  of 
spreading  among  the  masses  an  understanding  of  the 
underlying  principles  of  our  economic  life. — Book- 
list. 

Usher,  Abbott  Payson.  An  introduction  to 
the  industrial  history  ef  England.  1920 

L  16678 

_  By  considering  the  rise  of  crafts  in  antiquity  and 
t  £  *  guild  in  France,  the  industrial  development 
of  England  is  made  a  part  of  a  general  European 
trend.  Tne  facts  are  presented  with  scholarly  care, 
but  the  style  is  not  too  technical. — Booklist. 

Labor 

Beard,  Mary.  A  short  history  of  the  Amer- 
ican labor  movement.  1920.  L  16955 
A  brief  popular  statement  of  the  origin  and  de- 
velopment of  the  A.  F.  of  L.,  the  rise  of  the  more 
radical  organizations  now  hostile  to  it  and  the  lat- 
est currents  of  thought  and  action. 

Bloomfield,  Daniel,  ed.  Selected  articles  on 
problems  of  labor.  1920.  L  16956 

(The    handbook    series.) 


A  compilation  of  articles  covering  causes  of  in- 
dustrial unrest,  cost  of  living,  compensation,  hours, 
trade  unionism,  adjustment  of  disputes,  housing,  oc- 
cupational hygiene,  industrial  insurance,  etc. 

Carlton,  Frank  Tracy.     Organized  labor  in 
American  history.     1920.  L  16954 

Contains  much  general  discussion  of  a  historical 
character.  While  differing  somewhat  from  the  au- 
thor's judgment  in  the  selection  of  material,  the 
Survey  grants  that  "his  temper  is  tolerant  and  his 
mood  understanding." 

Crowther,  Samuel.  Common  sense  and 
labour.  1920.  L  16757 

Mr.  Crowther  has  no  faith  in  "profit-sharing  de- 
lusions" and  the  "fetish  of  industrial  democracy," 
lie  sees  that  today's  unrest  is  caused  by  the  "di- 
vorce of  labor  from  work";  that  production  must 
exceed  consumption,  and  that  there  must  be  open 
dealings  between  labor  and  capital. 

Gleason,  Arthur.  What  the  workers  want; 
a  study  of  British  labor.  1920.  L  16959 
It  is  vivid,  singularly  intimate  in  its  knowledge, 
and  with  a  frank  recognition  of  the  problems  in- 
volved that  gives  it  an  objectivity  rare  in  books  of 
the  kind. 

Hammond,  John  L.,  and  Hammond,  Barbara. 
The  skilled  labourer,  1760-1832.  1919. 

L  16952 

Companion  volume  to  The  village  labourer,  1760- 
1832,  L  16942,  and  The  town  labourer,  1760-1832, 
L  16941. 

This  history  incidentally  produces  striking  analo- 
gies with  present  conditions,  showing  the  industrial 
and  social  results  of  war,  the  conflict  between  capi- 
tal's zeal  for  productivity  and  labor's  anxiety  for 
traditional  liberties,  and  the  struggle  over  compul- 
sory arbitration. 

Holmes,  John  Haynes.  Is  violence  the  way 
out  of  our  industrial  disputes?  1920. 

L  16758 

Johnson,  F.  Ernest.  The  new  spirit  in  in- 
dustry. 1919.  L  16748 

Mr.  Johnson  seems  to  follow  the  lines  laid  down 
by  Carleton  Parker  in  emphasizing  the  psychology 
of  labor.  The  introduction  thus  explains  the  "new 
spirit":  "Production,  distribution,  and  consumption 
are  being  evaluated  in  terms  of  the  mind  of  men 
which  the  processes  produce." 

Orth,  Samuel  P.  The  armies  of  labor;  a 
chronicle  of  the  organized  wage-earners. 
1919.  B  5086,  40 

(The  chronicles  of  America  series.) 

Safety  Institute  of  America.  Safety  funda- 
mentals; lectures  given  for  the  benefit  of 
factory  inspectors  employed  by  the  city 
of  New  York,  the  states  of  New  York  and

Housing 

Aronovici,  Carol.  Housing  and  the  housing 
problem.  1920.  L  17042 

(The  national  social   science  series.) 

Regards  the  difficulties  of  the  housing  problem  as 

an  important  aspect  of  reconstruction  and  advocates 

a    constructive    national   program. 

Whitaker,  Charles  Harris.  The  joke  arbout 
housing.  1920.  '  L  17058 

The  "joke"  is  the  paradox  that  the  more  .houses 
we  buildf  the  poorer  we  build  and  the  higher  the 
rents,  and  Mr.  Whitaker  sets  about  suggesting  ways 
out  of  the  difficulty.

ironmasters,  and    Standard   Oil,  the  development  of 
railroads  and  the  relation  of  Wall  Street  to  the  war.

Browne,  Waldo  R.,  ed.  Man  or  the  state? 
A  group  of  essays  by  famous  writers. 
1919.  L 15728 

Contents:  Kropotkin:  The  state,  its  historic  role. 
• — Buckle:  Inquiry  into  the  influence  exercised  by 
government. — Emerson  :  Politics. — Thoreau  :  On  the 
duty  of  civil  disobedience. — Spencer :  The  right  to 
ignore  the  state. — Tolstoy:  Appeal  to  social  reform- 
ers.— Wilde:  The  soul  of  man  under  socialism. 

Seven  essays  by  as  many  distinguished  men,  the 
general  plea  of  which  is  for  a  renascent  individual- 


ism. .  .  .  They  seek  release  from  the  pressure  of 
social  institutions  by  making  the  individual  free  of 
the  state. — Nation.

1919.  L 15731 

(The  Stafford  Little  lectures  for  1919.)

ism which  is  coming  to  be  a  mark  of  our  more  im- 
portant bank  presidents,  to  the  amazement  and  con- 
fusion,  no  doubt,  of  their  editorial  satellites. — Dial.

made.  1920.  L 15933

dential choice  and  reveals  the  machinery  of  the 
two  great  political  parties.  •

An  original  study  of  shore  changes  to  determine

Turnbull,  Arthur.  The  life  of  matter;  an 
inquiry  and  adventure.  1919.  K  18554 

A  mystical  scientist  who  solves  the  problem  of 
unity  by  asserting  that  every  atom  is  alive.  He 
discredits  causality,  believes  action  and  reaction  to 
be  unequal,  and  derives  motion  from  a  change 
within  matter.

Camp,  Walter.  Handbook  on  health,  and 
how  to  keep  it.  1920.  N  1287 

An  authoritative  book  which  outlines  a  system  of 
setting-up  exercises  to  benefit  the  internal  organs 
rather  than  to  develop  muscle.  The  health  talks 
on  breathing,  dress,  rest,  sports,  etc.,  though  not 
new,  offer  valuable  suggestions. — Booklist. 

Mathews,  Albert  P.  Physiological  chem- 
istry, a  text-book  and  manual  for  stu- 
dents; 1919.  N  186 

Rummler,  Susan  Harding.  Natural  food  and 
care  for  child  and  mother;  presentation  of 
a  system  which  makes  safe  and  healthful 
the  gratification  of  the  natural  appetite,  in- 
cluding questions  and  answers  for  the  aid 
of  mothers  and  children's  nurses.  1919. 

N2184 

Mrs.  Rummler  lays  emphasis  on  the  importance 
of  natural  food  and  the  danger  involved  in  violat- 
ing nature's  laws.  She  thinks  most  childhood  dis- 
eases and  irregularities  are  due  to  defective  diet, 
and  she  especially  denounces  frequent  eating  of  arti- 
ficial sweets. 

Waldron,  William  H.  Army  physical  train- 
ing, based  on  the  system  of  physical  train- 
ing employed  in  the  United  States  army. 
1919.  N  1316 

Walsh,  William  S.     Yours  for  sleep.     1920. 

N1315

Aviation 

Brown,  Sir  Arthur  Whitten.  Flying  the  At- 
lantic in  sixteen  hours;  with  a  discussion 
of  aircraft  in  commerce  and  transporta- 
tion. 1920.  K  19405 

A  record  by  the  only  survivor  of  the  first  trans- 
Atlantic  flight.  There  is  a  description  of  the  tedious 
preparations  and  delays  in  Newfoundland,  then  a 
log  of  the  journey  itself  and  a  report  of  the  tri- 
umphal advance  to  London,  followed  by  a  prophecy 
for  air  travel  in  the  future.

Huneker,  James.  Bedouins:  Mary  Garden, 
Debussy,  Chopin  or  the  circus,  Botticelli, 
Poe,  Brahmsody,  Anatole  France,  Mir- 
beaii,  Caruso  on  wheels,  Calico  cats,  The 
artistic  temperament;  Idols  and  ambergris; 
with  The  supreme  sin,  Grindstones,  A 
masque  of  music,  and  The  vision  malefic. 
1920.  K 29588 

Essays  in  Huneker's  usual  exotic  style.  At  least 
four  of  the  Bedouin  chapters  are  on  Mary  Garden, 
whom  he  regards  as  the  "superwoman." 

Jean-Aubry,  G.  French  music  of  today; 
with  a  preface  by  Gabriel  Faure.  1919. 

K  29589 

(Library  of  music   and  musicians.) 

French  music  has  the  same  qualities  as  the  French 
character:  "The  fear  of  emphasis  in  expression,  the 
avoidance  of  all  that  is  redundant,  knowledge  with- 
out the  desire  to  display  it,  a  horror  of  pedantry,  a 
taste  for  pleasantry  and  wit." 

Lang,  Edith,  and  West,  George.  Musical 
accompaniment  of  moving  pictures.  1920. 

K  29914 

'  A  wise  and  helpful  guide  for  the  amateur  accom- 
panist. The  reader  is  impressed  with  the  peculiar 
gifts  required — the  large  repertoire,  and  a  thorough 
knowledge  of  musical  principles  to  enable  improvisa- 
tion and  of  the  psychology  of  harmony  to  avoid 
jarring  keys  or  speed. 

Rosenfeld,  Paul.  Musical  portraits;  inter- 
pretations of  twenty  modern  composers. 
1920.  K  29652 


Contents:  _  Wagner.  —  Strauss.  —  Moussorgsky. — 
Liszt.  —  Berlioz.  —  Franck.  —  Debussy.  —  Ravel. — 
Borodin. — Rimsky.  —  Korsakoff.  —  Rachmaninoff. — 
Scriabine.  —  Strawinsky.  —  Mahler.  —  Reger.  — 
Schoenberg. — Sibelius. — Loeffler.— -Ornstein. —  Bloch. 

LITERATURE 

Center,  Stella  Stewart,  comp.  The  worker 
and  his  work;  readings  in  present-day 
literature  presenting  some  of  the  activities 
by  which  men  and  women  the  world  over 
make  a  living.  1920.  J  6267 

(Lippincptt's  school  text  series.) 

A  compilation  of  poems  and  narrative  extracts 
intended  to  inspire  a  realization  of  the  romance 
of  different  kinds  of  labor  in  young  people  who  are 
still  undecided  what  work  to  take  up.  The  best 
are  from  Bennett,  Wells,  Phillpotts,  Maeterlinck, 
Garland,  and  Margaret  Deland. 

De  La  Mare,  Walter.  Rupert  Brooke  and 
the  intellectual  imagination.  1920.  E  10698 
This  essay  is  short  but  full,  both  of  wise  judg- 
ments and  beautiful  sayings.  It  conveys  a  sense 
not  only  of  the  value  of  Brooke's  poetry,  but  also 
of  the  charm  of  his  personality. — London  Mercury. 

Goldberg,  Isaac.  Studies  in  Spanish-Amer- 
ican literature.  1920.  J  5485 

Contents:  The  "modernists"  renovation. — Ruben 
Dario. — Jose  Enrique  Rodo. — Jose  Santos  Chocano. 
— Jose  Maria  Eguren. — Rufino  Blanco-Fombona. — 
Translations. 

These  five  men  are  selected  as  best  representing 
the  latest  important  literary  movement  of  Spanish 
America,  which,  however,  seems  to  indicate  French 
influence  rather  than  Spanish. 

Grozier,  Edwin  A.,  and  others,  ed.  One  hun- 
dred best  novels  condensed.  1920.  4  v. 

*R  1464 

Olgin,  Moissaye  J.  A  guide  to  Russian  lit- 
erature (1820-1917).  1920.  J  5576 

Includes  information  on  recent  writers  not  avail- 
able elsewhere,  covering  all  who  have  significantly 
influenced  the  social,  economic,  and  political  life  of 
Russia,  besides  the  authors  of  belles  lettres. 

Schofield,  William  Henry.  Mythical  bards 
and  The  life  of  William  Wallace.  1920. 

J4850,  5 

(Harvard   studies    in    comparative    literature.) 

Smith,  Logan  Pearsall,  ed.  A  treasury  of

Sinclair,  Upton.  The  brass  check;  a  study 
of  American  journalism.  1919.  J  7263 
An  accusation  that  the  entire  American  press  is 
corrupted  in  the  interests  of  powerful  financial 
forces.  The  indictment  seems  to  be  largely  based 
on  the  author's  own  difficulties  in  finding  a  market 
for  his  radical  productions.

ful  to    those   who   wish   to  practice   writing.

Graves,  Robert.     Country  sentiment.     1920. 

E  10985 

The  verse  of  Robert  Graves  charms  you  with  a 
whimsical  tenderness  that  is  appealing,  but  you 
feel  all  the  time  a  hidden  sense  of  something  for 
which  the  whimsey  is  protection.  That  something 
is  the  stern  reality  of  life. — Boston  Transcript.

Drama,  Theater 

Chandler,    Frank   Wadleigh.      The    contem- 
porary drama  of  France.     1920.        E  11914 
(The   contemporary   drama  series.) 
A    history    of    French    drama    from    Scribe    and 
Augier    to    the    "war    exploiters."      It    characterizes 
movements  and   playwrights,  and   gives  brief   analy- 
•jwes  of  their  works. — Booklist. 

Hill,  Wycliffe  A.  Ten  million  photoplay 
plots;  master  key  to  all  dramatic  plots. 
1919.  E  11774 

Hornblow,  Arthur.  A  history  of  the  theatre 
in  America  from  its  beginning  to  the  pres- 
ent time.  1919.  2  v.  E  12346 
A  detailed  and  informing  account  of  theaters, 
managers,  and  actors,  beginning  with  the  mummers 
of  colonial  days.  We  can  see  how  the  development 
of  American  drama  has  been  shaped  by  the  varying 
conditions  of  production. 

Joseph,  Helen  Haiman.  A  book  of  marion- 
ettes. 1920.  E  12437 
A  fascinating,  well-illustrated  volume  tracing 
puppet-history  from  its  shadowy  beginnings  in  _ an- 
tiquity, through  its  later  development  in  the  Orient 
and  Eurone,  down  to  the  marionettes  formerly 
brought  here  by  the  Little  Theater  and  those  of 
Tony  Sarg. 

MacClintock,  Lander.  The  contemporary 
drama  of  Italy.  1920.  E14084 

(The  contemporary  drama  series.) 
An  excellent  book  for  the  student  of  drama  and 
for  general  references  purposes.  It  covers  the  Ital- 
ian drama  of  to-day  from  its  foundations  in  Man- 
zoni  to  the  latest  production  of  Benelli  with  com- 
prehensive lists  and  outlines.

are  delightfully  funny  and  help  the  humor  of  the 
text. — Booklist.

James,  George  Wharton.     New  Mexico,  the 
land    of   the    delight    makers.    1920.    14412 
(See   America  First   series.) 

"The  history  of  its  ancient  cliff  dwellings  and 
pueblos,  conquest  by  the  Spaniards,  Franciscan  mis- 
sions; personal  accounts  of  the  ceremonies,  games, 
social  life  and  industries  of  its  Indians;  a  descrip- 
tion of  its  climate,  geology,  flora  and  birds,  its 
rivers  and  forests;  a  review  of  its  rapid  develop- 
ment, land-reclamation  projects  and  educational  sys- 
tem; with  full  and  accurate  accounts  of  its  pro- 
gressive counties,  cities  and  towns." 

Morley,  Christopher.  Travels  in  Philadel- 
phia. 1920.  111583 

"Not  intended  as  a  formal  portrait,  merely  as 
snapshots  of  vivacious  phases  of  the  life  of  to-day," 
these  articles  combine  a  happy,  not  too  studied  de- 
scription with  pleasant  humor  into  a  congenial  guide- 
book.— Philadelphia  Ledger.

Van  Dyke,  John  C.  The  Grand  Canyon  of 
the  Colorado;  recurrent  studies  in  im- 
pressions and  appearances.  1920.  1 4437 
It  is  not  a  guide  book;  it  is  a  very  simple  and 
direct  series  of  pictures,  personally  colored  but  not 
overcolored,  of  scenes  of  which  every  American  is 
proud. — New  York  Times. 

Wood,  Frederic.  The  turnpikes  of  New 
England  and  evolution  of  the  same 
through  England,  Virginia  and  Maryland. 
1919.  14811 

Most  valuable  to  engineers  and  to  those  inter- 
ested in  the  history  of  transportation,  but  the  de- 
lightful illustrations,  the  anecdotes,  and  the  maps 
make  it  interesting  to  the  general  reader  and  the 
traveler. — Booklist.

Experiences  of  the  Bishop  of  Carpentaria  as  a

country.  One  of  the  best  books  of  travel  of  recent 
times. — Boston  Transcript.

Individual  Biography 

Bispham,  David.  A  Quaker  singer's  recol- 
lections. 1920.  C  20874 

An  interesting  book  full  of  the  writer's  engaging 
personality.  His  Quaker  parentage  kept  him  long 
from  singing  in  opera.  He  studied  in  Europe  for 
concert  work,  knew  Huxley,  and  sang  before  Brown- 
ing in  Italy. 

Campbell,  John  Archibald.  Connor,  Henry 
G.  John  Archibald  Campbell,  associate 
justice  of  the  United  States  Supreme  court, 
1853-1861.  1920.  C  21141 

The  biography  of  a  southern  lawyer  who  was  con- 
nected with  the  famous  Dred  Scott  case. 

Clark,  Champ.  My  quarter  century  of  Amer- 
ican politics.  1920.  2  v.  C  21265 

Chatty  reminiscences,  with  many  anecdotes  of 
public  men. 

Clemens,  Samuel  L.  Brooks,  Van  Wyck. 
The  ordeal  of  Mark  Twain.  1920.  C  21292 
This  study  of  Mark  Twain  explains  the  pessi- 
mistic tone  of  his  philosophy  as  caused  by  "a  cer- 
tain miscarriage  of  his  creative  life,  a  balked  per- 
sonality, an  arrested  development  of  which  he  was 
himself  wholly  unaware." 

Clevenger,    Dr.    S.    V.       Robinson,    Victor. 

The  Don  Quixote  of  psychiatry.  1919.' 

C  21290 
Debs,  Eugene  V.  Karsner,  David.  Debs; 

his  authorized  life  and  letters.  1919.  C  21552 


With  a  modesty  becoming  the  true  biographer, 
Mr.  Karsner  has  permitted  Debs  to  speak  for  him- 
self and  to  show  us,  through  his  letters  and  ad- 
dresses, that  a  man  may  grow  to  maturity  without 
permitting  the  cowardices  and  compromises  of  life 
to  corrupt  him. — Nation.

Gladstone,  Mrs.  IV,  E.  Drew,  Mary.  Mrs. 
Gladstone,  by  her  daughter.  1920.  C  22113 
More  a  series  of  impressions  and  reminiscences

Hamilton,  Alexander.  Ford,  Henry  Jones. 
Alexander  Hamilton.  1920.  C  22286 

(Figures  from  American   history.) 

An  account  of  Hamilton's  political  ideals  and 
career,  including  also  his  personal  life  and  char- 
acter. 

Hankey,  Donald.  Letters  of  Donald  Hankey, 
"a  student  in  arms";  with  introduction  and 
notes  by  Edward  Miller,  M.  A.  1920. 

C  22295 

_  Through  his  letters  run  his  love  of  life  and  of 
his  fellow  men,  his  confidence  in  mankind,  his 
whole-souled  humanity.  They  reflect  the  spirit  with 
which  the  best  of  English  youth  went  to  war.— 
Daily  News. 

James,  Henry.  The  letters  of  Henry  James, 
selected  and  ed.  by  Percy  Lubbock.  1920. 
2  v.  C 22600 

These  four  hundred  letters  to  distinguished  rela- 
tives and  friends  give  a  continuous  story  of  James's 
life  from  his  twenty-sixth  to  his  last  year.  They 
contain  interesting  passages  of  criticism,  but  little 
on  his  own  work,  and  reveal  an  affectionate  and 
sympathetic  friend. 

Lafayette,  Marquis  de.  •  Roberts,  Octavia, 
(Mrs.  Barton  Corneau).  With  Lafayette 
in  America.  1919.  C  22835 

A ,  picturesque  portrait  of  (1)  the  marquis,  when 
he  came  to  help  America  at  the  age  of  nineteen, 
and  '(2)  the  nation's  guest,  when  almost  fifty  years 
afterward  he  made  a  triumphal  journey  through  the 
United  States.

1909.  C23017 

Merrier,  Cardinal.  Kellogg,  Charlotte.  Mer- 
cier,  the  fighting  cardinal  of  Belgium; 
foreword  by  Brand  Whitlock.  1920.  C  23368 
Mrs.  Kellogg's  work  for  the  relief  of  Belgium 
brought  her  constantly  in  close  and  sympathetic 
touch  with  Cardinal  Mercier,  and  her  sketch  of  his 
whole  career  completes  one  of  the  most  admirable 
and  touching  figures  of  the  war. — Nation.

HISTORY 

General  Works 

Harding,  Samuel  Bannister.  New  me- 
diaeval and  modern  history;  based  upon 
the  author's  "Essentials  in  mediaeval  and 
modern  history"  prepared  in  consultation 
with  Albert  Bushnell  Hart.  1913.  A  5947 
(Essentials  in  history.) 

Partridge,  G.  E.  The  psychology  of  na- 
tions; a  contribution  to  the  philosophy  of 
history.  1919.  A  2336 

The  conclusion  with  regard  to  war  is  that  it  can 
be  avoided  in  the  future  only  by  education,  for 
political  readjustment  is  not  sufficient. 

Tuell,  Harriet  E.  The  study  of  nations;  an 
experiment  in  social  education,  with  chap- 
ters on  China  and  Japan  by  Kenneth 
Scott  Latourette.  1920.  A  2360 

(Riverside   educational   monographs.) 
Each   nation   is   studied    from    the   point   of   view 
of    its_  present    individuality,    of    how    history    has 
made  it  what  it  is,  and  of  what  it  may  be  expected 
to    contribute   to   civilization. — Booklist. 


Europe 

Brown,  Carroll  N.  ir.  Hellas  and  unre- 
deemed Hellenism;  The  policy  of  victory 
in  the  East,  and  its  results,  by  Georges 
Bourdon. — Smyrna,  a  Greek  city,  by 
Charles  Vellay. — Hellenism  in  Asia  Minor 
in  the  middle  ages,  by  Charles  Diehl. 
1920.  A  5148 

(American-Hellenic    Society    publications.) 


Cole,  Grenville  A.  J.     Ireland,  the  outpost. 

1919.  A  5508 

Mr.  Cole  emphasizes  the  part  played  in  Ireland's 
history  by  her  _  geography,  her_  physical  structure, 
and  the  successive  waves  of  migration  which  have 
brought  varied  races  and  civilizations  to  her  shores. 

Cresson,  W.  P.    The  Cossacks;  their  history 
and  country.     1919.  A  6021 

This  is  probably  the  first  book  in  English  on  the 
history^  of  these  people.  It  relates  _their  legendary 
beginnings,  the  development  of  their  efforts  for  a 
free  government,  and  the  present  administration  of 
Ukraine. 

Gibbons,  Herbert  Adams.     France  and  our- 
selves;   interpretative    studies,    1917-1919. 

1920.  A  5750 

Dr.  Gibbons  describes  in  some  detail  the  indus- 
trial and  political  problems  that  are  confronting 
France.  He  would  have  us  realize  that  the  prin- 
ciple of  balance  of  power  is  as  important  and 
eternal  as  the  law  of  gravity. 

Kalpaschnikov,  Andrew.  A  prisoner  of  Trot- 
sky's. 1920.  A  6022 
A  partisan  account  of  revolutionary  events  in 
Russia.  The  author  blames  _  Col.  Raymond  Robins 
for  his  imprisonment,  and  is  strongly  opposed  to 
Kerensky.  Among  many  interesting  fellow  prison- 
ers was  the  slayer  of  Rasputin,  from  whom  he 
learned  the  details  of  the  monk's  death. 

Pons,  A.  A.  The  holocaust,  1919.        A  5110 

Bryce's  introduction  says  that  in  her  struggle 
with  the  H'apsburgs  Italy's  leaders  were  "thinking 
not  of  territorial  extensions,  but  of  the  rule  of  jus- 
tice in  a  world  set  free  for  peace  in  which  nation- 
alism was_to  be  subordinated  to  the  common  welfare 
of  humanity." 

Power,  Rhoda.     Under  the  Bolshevik  reign 
of    terror.       1919.  A  6024 

Contents:  From  Newcastle  to  Rostov-on-Don. — A 
Russian  bourgeois  household. — The  revolution. — A 
criminal's  paradise. — A_  journey  and  a  holiday. — An 
autumn  of  anxiety. — Civil  war. — Under  Cossack  pro- 
tection.— Enter  the  Bolsheviks. — Under  Bolshevik 
rule. — The  anarchists  and  departure. — From  Rostov 
to  the  Murman  coast. — Refugees  in  Murmansk.

navy.     1919.

Vinogradoff,  Sir  Paul,  ed.  The  reconstruc-

Contents:  The  situation  in  Russia,  by  _Sir  P. 
Vinogradoff. — Russia  as  an  economic  organism,  by

A  plea  for  the  League  of  Nations  to  assist  in  fur-
