Young,  George. 


The  new  Germany.     1920. 
A  5578 


A  vivid  account  of  events  and  conditions  in  Ger- 
many from  the  Armistice  to  the  Treaty  of  Ver- 
sailles. One  chapter  is  devoted  to  an  analysis  of 
the  new  constitution  and  one  to  comment  on  the 
treaty,  which  the  author  regards  as  "the  worst  of 
peaces."

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


The  European  War 

Czernin,  Count  Ottokar.     In  the  world  war. 
1920.  A  7082 

Memoirs  and  reflections  which  present  Austria  in 
the  leading  strings  of  Germany,  exonerate  the 
Kaiser,  denounce  the  peace  of  Versailles,  but  say 
nothing  of  the  League. — Booklist. 

Doyle,   Arthur    Conan.      A    history    of    the 
great  war.     1916-1920.     6v.  A  6313 

The  British  campaign  in  France  and  Flanders: 
v.  1,  1914.— v.  2,  1915.— v.  3,  1916.— v.  4,  1917.— 
v.  5,  Jan.  to  July.  1918. — v.  6,  July  to  November, 
1918. 


Falkenhayn,  General  von.  The  German  gen- 
eral staff  and  its  decisions,  1914-1916.  1920. 

A  7084 


Gibbs,  Philip.     Now  it  can   be   told.     1920. 

A  7064 

A  colleague  of  the  author's,  Arthur  Gleason,  tells 
in  The  Freeman  how,  as  early  as  the  fall  of  1914, 
Mr.  Gibbs  had  declared  his  purpose  to  spend  the 
rest  of  his  life  "telling  what  war  really  is," — and 
it  is  not  the  glory  of  war  that  he  celebrates. 

Hornung,  E.  W.    Notes  of  a  camp-follower 
on  the   western  front.     1919.  A  7070 

The  creator  of  Raffles  has  written  his  experience 
as  a  Y.  M.  C.  A.  canteen  worker.  There  are  vivid 
pictures  of  many  scenes  and  places  and  men,  some 
thrilling  adventures  and  many  humorous  incidents. 
— Booklist. 


Lovejoy,'  Esther  Pohl.  The  House  of  the 
Good  Neighbor.  1919.  A  7060 

An  account  of  a  social  center  in  France.  Dr. 
Lovejoy  describes  the  devastations  of  war,  the  hun- 
ger and  diseases  of  the  women  and  children,  and 
tells  of  some  of  the  things  that  were  done  to  make 
these  sufferings  less  cruel. 

Ludendorff,  Erich  von.  LudendorfPs  own 
story,  August  1914-NoVember  1918;  the 
great  war  from  the  siege  of  Liege  to  the 
signing  of  the  armistice  as  viewed  from 
the  Grand  Headquarters  of  the  German 
army.  1920.  2  v.  A  7071 

He  sees  himself  as  a  sort  of  Gulliver  bound 
down  by  the  politicians  at  home,  and  finally  ruined 
by  them.  He  was  never  wrong.  He  alone  really 
understood  the  war,  and  while  the  victories  are  to 
his  credit,  the  defects  are  due  to  others. — New  Re- 
public. 

Mayo,  Katherine.  "That  damn  Y";  a  rec- 
ord of  overseas  service.  1920.  A  7086 
A  very  timely  and  readable  book  which  may  well 
be  read  by  those  who  give  snapshot  denunciations 
of  the  "Y."  The  history  of  its  work  in  the  war 
very  ably  presented,  answering  some  of  the  com- 
monest charges  with  facts. — Booklist. 

Red  Cross.  American  National  Red  Cross. 
The  work  of  the  American  Red  Cross  dur- 
ing the  war;  a  statement  of  finances  and 
accomplishments  for  the  period  July  1, 
1917  to  February  28,  1919.  Civics  Dept. 


Causes 

Gordy,  Wilbur  F.  The  causes  and  mean- 
ing of  the  great  war.  1919.  A  7080 
Beginning  with  Germany  under  Bismarck  its  aim 
is  to"  show  the  reasons  for  the  struggles  following, 
and  to  develop  an  ideal  of  democracy  from  the  part 
played  by  America.  Intended  for  junior  high 
schools. — Booklist. 

Goricar,  Joseph,  and  Stowe,  Lyman  Beecher. 
The  inside  story  of  the  Austro-German 
intrigue;  or,  How  the  world  war  was 
brought  about.  1920.  A  7076 

The  views  of  a  Slav  with  a  strong  Russian  bias, 
who  holds  the  evils  of  secret  diplomacy  responsible 
for  the  war,  and  recommends  the  establishment  of 
a  United  States  of  Slavia  as  a  solution. 

Jonescu,  Take.  Some  personal  impressions, 
with  an  introduction  by  Viscount  Bryce. 
1920.  A  7079 

Moments  in  European  history  with  the  former 
prime  minister  of  Roumania.  Through  them  one 
receives  rather  striking  and  interesting  characteriza- 
tions of  such  figures  as  Poincare,  Lichnowsky,  Veni- 
zelos,  the  Kaiser,  and  others. — Booklist. 

Loreburn,  Earl.     How  the  war  came.     1920. 

A  7081 

A  keen,  though  kindly,  analysis  of  Sir  Edward 
Grey's  qualifications  for  the  post  of  Foreign  Secre- 
tary, the  excellence  of  his  motives,  and  the  errors 
in_  his  management  of  the  crisis  in  1914,  together 
with  a  strong  denunciation  of  secret  diplomacy. 


Personal  Narratives 

Albertson,  Ralph.  Fighting  without  a  war; 
an  account  of  military  intervention  in 
north  Russia.  1920.  A  7077 

The  British-American  expedition  into  North  Rus- 
sia and  the  reaction  of  the  Russian  people  during 
the  year  it  lasted.  It  is  a  tragic  story  of  good 
intentions,  stupidity,  suffering,  and  misunderstand- 
ing— of  utter  failure. — Survey. 

Bacon,  Sir  Reginald.  The  Dover  patrol 
1915-1917.  1919.  A  7074 

Written  particularly  to  give  the  student  of  naval 
history  the  underlying  reasons  for  action  or  inac- 
tion, combining  narrative  with  highly  technical 
treatment. 

Callwell,  Sir  C.  E.     The  Dardanelles.     1919. 

A  7075 

(Campaigns  and  their  lessons.) 

A  very  careful  and  son-etimes  technical  descrip- 
tion of  the  Dardanelles  campaign  from  the  military 
point  of  view,  which  goes  into  great  detail  about 
the  purpose,  the  expedition,  the  attack,  •  and  the 
military  organization. — Booklist. 

Domville-Fife,  Charles.  Submarines  and  sea 
power.  1919.  K  23273 

Records  of  many  romantic  events  on  England's 
sea  frontier,  1914  to  1918;  preparation  of  the  new 
navy  to  meet  the  submarine  menace,  and  new  wea- 
pons devised;  an  examination  of  the  effect  of  the 
submarine  on  naval  strategy. — Booklist. 

Evans,  E.  R.  G.  R.    Keeping  the  seas.    1920. 

A  7083 

Caotain  Evans'  account  of  the  Dover  patrol  which 
the  Booklist  calls  "a  clear  record  of  events  enliv- 
ened by  much  anecdote  and  many  humorous  inci- 
dents."

Freeman,    Lewis    R.        Sea-hounds.        1919. 

A  7073 

Contents:  The  men  who  changed  ships. — "Fire- 
brand."— "Back  from  the  jaws.  — Hunting. — The 
convoy  game.— Yank  boat  versus  U-boat. — Adriatic 
patrol. — -Patrol. — "Q." — The  whack  and  the  _  smack. 
— Bombed. — Against  odds. — Rounding  up  Fritz. 

These  accounts  are  breathless  with  daring  and 
also  very  instructive.  They  are  written  by  _  a 
British  navy  correspondent  who  has  a  keen  appetite 
for  the  dramatic  side  of  every  incident  and  who 
also  knows  the  ways  of  submarines. — Booklist. 

Hogan,  Martin  J.  The  Shamrock  battalion 
of  the  Rainbow;  a  story  of  the  "fighting 
sixty-ninth."  1919.  A  7069 

A  corporal's  personal  narrative  of  experiences  in 
camp,  on  transport  and  at  the  front  at  Luneville, 
Chateau  Thierry,  St.  Mihiel,  and  the  Argonne. — 
Booklist. 

Mabry,  Gregory,  comp.  Recollections  of  a 
recruit;  an  official  history  of  the  Fifty- 
fourth  U.  S.  Infantry.  1919.  A  7067 

MacVeagh,  Ewen  Cameron,  and  Brown,  Lee 
D.  The  Yankee  in  the  British  zone; 
with  a  foreword  by  Leonard  Wood.  1920. 

A  7055 

It  includes  many  anecdotes  of  the  characteristic 
failings  of  both  Briton  and  Yank  resulting  in  the 
growth  of  mutual  respect.  The  authors'  sense  of 
humor  will  save  them  from  any  serious  charge  of 
propagandism. 

Rainsford,  W.  Kerr.  From  Upton  to  the 
Meuse  with  the  307th  Infantry;  a  brief 
history  of  its  life  and  of  the  part  it 
played  in  the  great  war.  1920.  A  7061 
If  is  illustrated  by  striking  photographs  and 
drawings  which,  as  the  Booklist  says,  suggest 
a  beauty  that  is  the  antithesis  of  war. 

Stringer,  Harry  R.,  ed.  Heroes  all!  A 
compendium  of  the  names  and  official  cita- 
tions of  the  soldiers  and  citizens  of  the 
United  States  and  of  her  allies  who  were 
decorated  by  the  American  government 
for  exceptional  heroism  and  conspicuous 
service  above  and  beyond  the  call  of 
duty  in  the  war  with  Germany,  1917-1919. 
1919.  *A  7058 

Military  and  Naval  Operations 

Jones,  Elias  H.  The  road  to  En-Dor;  be- 
ing an  account  of  how  two  prisoners  of 
war  at  Yozga'd  in  Turkey  won  their  way 
to  freedom.  1920.  A  7085 

An  amazing  combination  of  ouija  boards  and 
British  wits  used  to  cheat  credulous  Turks  out  of 
two  war  prisoners. — Booklist. 

Keable,  Robert.  Standing  by;  war  time  re- 
flections in  France  and  Flanders.  1919. 

A  7059 

A  book  of  war  reminiscences  which  is  not  out 
of  date  because  its  author  is  constantly  putting 
frank,  fearless  questions,  which  came  to  him  as  a 
Catholic  chaplain  at  the  front,  before  himself  and 
the  reader. — Booklist.

A  3893 

The  story  of  a  native  Hawaiian  chieftain  who

civilized races.  Hawaii  celebrated  the  centenary  of

Jayne  Hill.  1920.  A  3889 

With  the  traveler's  eye  for  the  picturesque,  the 
author  describes  the  Philippines,  Hawaii,  Australia, 
Korea,  and  Japan;  but  underneath  this  interesting 
writing  is  the  purpose  of  the  diplomatist  and  sug- 
gestions for  a  careful  foreign  policy. — Condensed 
from  Booklist.

Irwin,  Wallace.     Trimmed  with  red.     1920. 

F  14272 

A  humorous  tale  of  the  meetings  and  absurd- 
ities of  the  parlor  Bolsheviks  and  the  still  greater

Kaye-Smith,  Sheila.     Tamarisk  town.     1920. 

F  14454 

An  unusual  story  of  a  man's  devotion  to  his

no  other  interest,  and  so  he  sacrifices  the  life  of  the

F  14605

Lynde,     Francis.       The     wreckers.        1920. 

F  14678 

The  story  of  a  western  railroad  and  big  business 
ventures.  An  able  road-builder  fights  the  exploit- 
ers and  grafters  and  saves  the  property  and  a  large 
community  from  loss. 

MacGill,  Patrick.     Maureen.     1920.     F 14970 

A  careful  study  of  peasant  life  in  Ireland.     The 

lives  it  portrays  are  poor  and  sordid,  but  there  is 

food  character  drawing  and  it  brings  before  us  the 
reland  of  today. 

Mackay,  Helen.     Chill  hours.     1920.    F 14944 
Sketches  of  life  and   character  in   a   French   hos- 
pital during  the  war.     They  are  beautifully  written 
and  unutterably  sad. 

MacNamara,  Brinsley.  The  clanking  of 
chains.  1919.  F 14954 

Modern  conditions  in  Ireland  and  the  Sinn  Fein 
rebellion  are  dealt  with  in  this  story. 

Marshall,  Edison.  The  voice  of  the  pack. 
1920.  F  15098 

Dan  Failing  is  given  six  months  to  live  in  his 
struggle  with  consumption.  He  goes  into  the_  moun- 
tains of  Oregon  and  his  many  adventures  give  him 
a  new  lease  on  life. 

Merwin,  Samuel.  Hills  of  Han;  a  romantic 
incident.  1920.  F 15298 

The  author  gives  a  picture  of  missionary  life  in 
China  and  records  some  events  during  the  time 
of  the  Boxer  rebellion. 

Mix,    Jennie    Irene.       At    fame's    gateway; 
the  romance  of  a  pianiste.     1920.     F  15417 
A   young   girl   from   a    small    Ohio    town    goes    to 
New    York    to    study    music.      She    relates    her    ex- 
periences with  the  musical  set  in  New  York. 

Montgomery,  L.  M.  Further  chronicles  of 
Avonlea  which  have  to  do  with  many  per- 
sonalities and  events  in  and  about  Avon- 
lea.  1920.  F  15467

Miss  Emily. — Sara's  way. — TTie  son  of  his  mother. 
— The  education  of  Betty. — In  her  selfless  mood. — 
The  conscience  case  of  David  Bell. — Only  a  com- 
mon fellow. — Tannis  of  the  flats.

1920.  F 15748 

A  story  of  Dakota  ranch  life  and  adventure  in

dagger.     1920.  F  15991 

South     American     revolutionists,     Bolsheviki,     and 

thieves   of   the  good   old-fashioned   kind   furnish   this

Porter,    Eleanor    H.      Mary    Marie.       1920. 

F 16537 

Mary-Marie  is  the  child  of  divorced  parents  and 
spends  half  her  time  with  each.  The  story  is  the 
diary  of  her  every-day  life. 

Raine,  William  MacLeod.  Oh,  you  Tex! 
1920.  F  16807 

Readers  who  crave  western  adventures  will  en- 
joy this  story  of  a  Texas  Ranger  and  his  encount- 
ers with  Mexicans,  Apache  Indians  and  outlaws  in 
the  stirring  times  following  the  Civil  War.

1919.  F 16906

Sedgwick,  Anne  Douglas,  (Mrs.  Basil  de 
Selincourt).  The  third  window.  1920. 

F 17905 

A  fine  piece  of  writing  on  the  theme  of  spirit- 
ualism. It  is  the  story  of  a  woman's  struggle  be- 
tween loyalty  for  her  dead  husband  and  love  for

This  diary  is  the  humorous  recital  of  a  judge's

-The  real  Charlotte.  1918.  F  18306

This  story  is  a  continuation  of  The  prairie  wife,

F 18537

russet.     1920.  F 18596 

A    pastoral    adventure    in    contentment    by    two

F  18583 

A  group  of  stories  relating  some  of  the  cases 
of  a  firm  of  shyster  lawyers. 

Vachell,  Horace  Annesley.    Whitewash.  1920. 

F  18813 

A  sociological  story  showing  the  extreme  com- 
placency of  English  landlords  regarding  the  housing 
conditions  among  the  tenantry  in  England  and  the 
difficulties  to  be  encountered  in  reforming  them. 

Vance,  Louis  Joseph.  The  dark  mirror.  1920. 

F  18824 

Weston,  George.    Mary  minds  her  business, 
1920.  F 19205 

Mary's  business  concerns  a  factory  of  which  she 
is  owner  and  manager.  She  attacks  new  labor  prob- 
lems and  trys  out  new  schemes  for  benefiting  her 
employees. 


White,  Stewart  Edward.     The  killer.     1920. 

F 19314 

Six  short  stories  dealing  chiefly  with  Arizona 
ranch  life  in  the  old  days. 

Williams,  Ben  Ames.  The  great  accident. 
1920.  F 19517 

A  story  of  political  intrigue  on  the  theme  of 
prohibition,  in  a  small  mid-western  town.  It  tells 
how  Winthrop  Chase  made  good  after  a  turn  of 
fate  had  made  him  mayor  of  Hardiston. 

Williamson,  C.  N.,  and  A.  M.  The  second 
latchkey.  1920.  F 19528 

The  romance  of  an  English  girl  and  an  Ameri- 
can millionaire  over  whom  hangs  a  mystery. 

Witwer,  H.  C.  There's  no  base  like  home. 
1920.  F 19671 

Ed  Harman  writes  these  letters  to  his  friend  Joe 
telling  of  his  varied  and  humorous  experiences  in 
baseball  and  matrimony. 

Wylie,  Ida  A.  R.  Holy  fire,  and  other 
stories.  1920.  F 19896 

Contents:    Holy  fire. — Thirst. — The  bridge  across. 

-"Tinker — tailor — "—Colonel    Tibbit    comes    home. 

— "  'Melia,    no    good." — A   gift    for    St.    Nicholas. — 

John   Prettyman  s   fourth   dimension. — An   Episcopal 

scherzo. 

A  collection  of  well  written  short  stories  rather 
psychological  in  tone,  which  are  told  with  keen 
insight  into  character.

combined. — Booklist.

1918.  H  8269 

_  A  thoroughly  interesting  story  of  high-school

The  everyday  life  of  a  Morq  boy  with  a  great 
deal  of  information  on  the  origin  and  development 
of  the  southern  Philippines.

story  of  Plymouth  colony  in  1620.  1920. 

H6719 

A  mixture  of  fact  and  fancy  about  the  Plymouth 
colony  in  the  first  three  years  of  its  existence.  De- 
lightful story  for  girls  between  the  ages  of  twelve 
and  fourteen. 

Thompson,  William.  Wigwam  wonder  tales; 
illustrated  by  Carle  Michel  Boog.  1919.

Vimar,    Auguste. 
1917.

illustrated  and  written,  for  very  young  children. 

Walden,  Walter.     The  hidden  islands.  1920. 

H6960 

An  adventure  story  for  boys.  Eight  boy  scouts 
have  a  treasure  hunt  which  turns  out  successfully 
after  an  exciting  encounter  with  a  gang  of  pirates.

PHILOSOPHY 

Bohme,  Jacob.     Six   theosophic  points   and 

other   writings.      1920.  L  14608 

Contents:      Six    theosophic    points. — Six    mystical 

points. — On  the  earthly  and  heavenly  mystery. 

A  new  translation  of  a  sixteenth  century  Germ 
mystic,  occasioned  doubtless  by  the  revived  interi 
in  mystical  thought. 

Harrison,   Frederic.     On   society.     1918. 

L  13817 

Contents:  pt.  I.  The  ethic  of  humanism. — 
Family  life. — The  functions  of  the  state. — A  state 
church. — Economic  duties. — The  economic  problem. 
— The  future  of  the  working  classes. — The  gospel 
of  industry. — Comte  and  Mill. — The  morals  of  trade. 
— The  morals  of  the  intellect. — Note:  on  Newton 
hall.  —  II.  Inaugural  address,  1881.  —  Mediaeval 
chivalry,  1881. — Fiist  annual  address,  1882. — An- 
nual address,  1884. 

Essays  on  positivism  intended  to  some  extent  as 
a  reply  to  John  Stuart  Mills'  criticisms  of  Comte 
in  his  book  "On  liberty." 

Hoernle,  R.  F.  Alfred.  Studies  in  contem- 
porary metaphysics.  1920.  L  12967 
Sets  forth  the  ideals  of  philosophy  with  refer- 
ence to  the  reality  of  the  world,  mechanism  and 
vitalism,  the  soul  and  _the  self,  and  then  discusses 
contemporary  tendencies  in  philosophy  (Dewey, 
Bertrand  Russell  and  others),  concluding  with  an 
epilogue  on  religion.

Sensible  little  talks  on  how  the  mind  and  nervous

and  action.  Good  for  the  discouraged  person  who 
is  capable  of  taking  himself  in  hand. — Booklist.

Luccock,  Halford  E.  Five-minute  shop-talks. 
1916.  M  9491 

Terse,  pointed  talks  on  practical  ethics  and 
.religion,  addressed  to  workingmen.  Their  manly, 
democratic  tone  has  appealed  to  workingmen,  who 
are,  the  author  says,  the  first  to  detect  and  despise 
two  things  in  a  speaker — "patronage  and  cant." — 
Booklist.

Theology  and  its  application  to  present  day  needs

to  ministers  and  laymen.

Discusses  church  efficiency,  inter-church  programs,

mixed  ungraded  groups  of  girls,  but  her  sugges- 
tions are  helpful  for  any  volunteer  club  leaders.

L  15292 


Laidler,    Harry   W. 
and  action.     1920. 


Socialism    in    thought 
L  17598 


Mr.  Laidler  presents  a  vivid  account  of  the 
socialist  theory  of  economic  development  and  a 
future  social  state,  as  well  as  the  achievements  and 
present  activities  of  organized  socialists  in  all  parts 
of  the  world. 

Scott,  J.  W.  Syndicalism  and  philosophical 
realism;  a  study  in  the  correlation  of  con- 
temporary social  tendencies.  1919.  L  17211 
In  contemporary  syndicalism  and  in  the  phil- 
osophies of  Bergson  and  Russell  he  diagnoses  the 
same  spiritual  sickness  to  which  he  gives  the  name 
of  realism. — Nero  Republic.

Presents  an  account  of  actual  methods  of  appro-

Hobson,  J.  A.     Taxation  in  the  new  state. 
1920.  L  17749 

An  endeavor  to  outline  a  new  philosophy  of  taxa- 
tion which  shall  provide  a  more  unified  system  and 
produce  a  permanently  sufficient  revenue.  The 
author  advocates  a  proportionate  tax  on  surplus 
income  without  :ecourse  to  direct  taxation.

Political  Science 

Government,  Democracy 

Goode,    William    T.      Bolshevism    at    work. 


1920. 


L  18501 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


119 


Mr.  Goode  was  at  first  prejudiced  against  the 
Bolsheviki.  but  is  surprised  to  find  himself  admir- 
ing everything,  their  political  machinery,  industrial 
or-.ianization,  tudiciary,  educational  system,_  and  art. 
The  Nation  thinks  him  sincere  but  uncritical. 

Macdonald,  T.  Ramsay.  Parliament  and  rev- 
olution. 1920.  L 15899 

(The  new  library  of  social  science.) 
A  defense  of  the  democratic  and  parliamentary 
method  of  socialist  reform  as  contrasted  with  the 
method  of  which  Lenin  is  the  most  conspicuous 
example.  It  may  convert  some  revolutionists  but 
it  is  not  intended  to  convert  them  to  the  ranks 
of  social  content. — Nation. 

Peterson,  Samuel.  Democracy  and  govern- 
ment. 1919.  L  15796 

A  lawyer's  suggestion  for  the  improvement  of 
our  present  governmental  machinery.  It  advocates, 
among  other  reforms,  restricting  suffrage  to  intelli- 
gent voters,  and  a  program  that  will  bring  the 
government  in  closer  touch  with  the  people. 

Webster,  Nesta  H.  (Mrs.  Arthur  Webster). 
The  French  revolution;  a  study  in  de- 
mocracy. 1919.  A  5749 

An  attempt  to  throw  light  on  the  part  played  by 
the  people  in  the  French  revolution,  which  the 
author  regards  as  having  been  unnecessary  and 
disastrous.

Army  and  Navy 

Crowder,  E.  H.  The  spirit  of  selective  serv-

Major-General  Crowder  compares  the  English  and

of  the  United  States  navy.  B  6818 

Social  Life  and  Customs 

Clark,  Alice.  Working  life  of  women  in  the 
seventeenth  century.  1920.  L  20964 

The  motive  of  Miss  Clark's  work  is  to  show  how 
the  rise  of  capitalism  affected  the  productive  capac- 
ity of  women.  With  considerable  elaboration  she 
traces  the  history  of  women  in  the  professions,  in 
the  crafts  and  trades,  in  textiles,  in  agriculture  and 
among  the  upper  classes. — Survey. 

Clephan,  R.  Coltman.  The  tournament;  its 
periods  and  phases.  1919.  *P  4580 

Mr.  Clephan  rudely  strips  the  tournament  of  the 
deceitful  trappings  woven  around  it  by  poets  and 
story-tellers.  He  traces  its  decline  _  from  a  primi- 
tive combat  through  stages  of  undeniable  usefulness 
to  that  last  period  when  it  had  become  a  clumsy 
and  very  expensive  sport. — Times  (London). 

Dunn,    Courtenay.     The   natural    history   of 


the  child;  a  book  for  all  sorts  and  condi- 
tions of  men,  women  and  children.     1920. 

L  20778 

A  desultory  assortment  of  facts  and  allusions 
with  reference  to  the  child's  ancestry,  early  life, 
environment,  schooling,  religion,  play. 

Reynolds,  Myra.  The  learned  lady  in  Eng- 
land, 1650-1760.  1920.  L  20992 

(Vassar  semi-centennial  series.) 
_We  begin  to  see  that  these  women,  fluttering  and 
disparate  as  they  seem,  actually  moved  with  steady 
progress  toward  the  goal  of  liberty  of  thought  and 
action  which  the  women  of  today  congratulate  them- 
selves on  having  almost  reached.  An  interesting 
and  original  piece  of  work. — Bookman.

Lanchester,  F.  W.  Aircraft  in  warfare;  the

Domestic  Economy 

Cooley,  Anna  M.,  and  Spohr,  Wilhelmina 
H.  Household  arts  for  home  and  school. 
1920.  2v.  K  24579 

Contents:  v.  1.  The  family  budget. — Care  of  the

Practice  problems  for  school  are  outlined  and  sug- 
gestions for  the  daily  routine  of  home  work  are 
thoroughly  discussed  in  these  volumes  which  are 
written  for  the  use  of  household  arts  classes. 


Presents  scientific  information  in  an  informal 
manner  and  especially  discusses  the  proper  feeding 
of  the  child.  Although  it  contains  recipes  it  a 
more  properly  a  food  manual  than  a  cook  book.

Commerce,  Business 

Lee,  James  Melvin.  Business  writing. 
1920.  L  20575,  2 

(Language    for   men   of   affairs.) 

Direct  and  specific  information  about  business 
writing  for  business  men  of  every  status.  It  dis- 
cusses the  essentials  of  writing  in  general,  and  the 
special  needs  for  writing  letters,  reports,  adver- 
tising copy,  and  publicity  articles.

Recreation

ation. 1920.  K  30088 

Dignifies  the  joys  of  leisure  by  quiet  insistence 
on  their  value  as  consolation.  The  first  part  out- 
lines the  forms  of  recreation  that  have  no  slight 
or  trivial  influence  in  a  well-ordered  life.  'I  he 
second  part  shows  how  leisure  may  be  laid  out  to 
advantage. — Condensed  from  The  Nation.

life-saving  are  included.

He  really  does  care  for  art  as  an  end  in

kind  of  artists  he  cares  about  in  letters  as  well  as

seriously.  .  .  .  Comes  from  a  man  who  knows

reflections.  His  partisanship  is  clearly  evident.

devoid  of  the  flavor  of  Wilde's  personality  as  they 
are  free  from  his  customary  affectation.

Guinea;  a  record  of  the  travels,  adven-

Posthumous  papers  of  a  man  who  fell  in  the

An  admirable  guide  to  old  London  written  with 
enthusiasm  and  a  sense  of  humor.  It  deals  mostly 
with  those  antiquities  which  are  a  household  word 
and  yet  are  practically  unknown  to  most  people, 
as  the  Domesday  Rook,  the  Roman  baths,  the  bells 
of  St.  Clements,  etc.

I  10658 

Charming  gossipy  family  letters  with  the  all- 
around  interest  in  Eastern  questions  one  would 
expect  from  the  highest  type  of  American  mind 
in  the  presence  of  these  ancient  civilizations. 


Franck,    Harry    A. 
Germany.      1920. 


Vagabonding    through 
I  7517 


Adventures  on  a  walking  trip  through  unoccupied 
Germany,  relating  the  docile  and  conciliatory  atti- 
tude of  the  German  today.  Gives  full  reports  of 
the  food  scarcity  in  the  cities  and  the  profiteering 
in  the  villages  and  farms.

among  the  Essex  waterways  near  such  station, 
school,  or  friendly  village  as  fancy  or  necessity 
dictates.  The  story  is  told  with  a  delight  that  is 
infectious. — Times  (London).

by  H.  D.  Hazeltine. — State  and  municipal  govern-

Social  legislation  and  administration,  by  Lord  E. 
Percy. — Characteristics  of  American  industrial  con- 
ditions, by  P.  B.  Kennedy. — The  relation  of  the 
American  government  to  business,  by  P.  B.  Ken- 
nedy.— Some  aspects  of  recent  party  history  in  the 
United  States,  by  J.  D.  Greene. — American  univer- 
sities, their  beginnings  and  development,  by  J.  W. 
Cunliffe. — State  universities,  schools  systems,  and 
colleges,  in  the  United  States  of  America,  by  G. 
E.  MacLean. — Literature  in  contemporary  America, 
by  H.  S.  Canby. — Two  American  philosophers,  Wil- 
liam James  and  Josiah  Royce,  by  G.  Santayana. — 
The  position  of  women  in  America,  by  Mrs. 
Bowlker. 

A  series  of  lectures  on  various  phases  of  life 
in  America,  establishing  her  kinship  with  England 
in  traditions,  institutions  and  ideals.

Bridge,  Sir  Frederick.  A  Westminster  pil-

An  entertaining  volume  of  reminiscences  by  the 
man  who  has  lately  resigned  after  forty-four  years 
as  organist  at  Westminster  Abbey. 

Grcsham,  Walter  Q.  Gresham,  Matilda. 
Life  of  Walter  Quintin  Gresham,  1832- 
1895.  1919.  2  v.  C  22180 

An  intimate  account  of  political  events  centering 
largely  around  Chicago.  Gresham  was  Arthur's 
secretary  of  the  treasury,  was  a  republican  candi- 
date for  presidential  nomination  in  1888,  and  in 
1892  became  Cleveland's  secretary  of  state. 

Hoover,  Herbert.  Kellogg,  Vernon  Lyman. 
Herbert  Hoover,  the  man  and  his  work. 
1920.  C  22472 

The  author,  who  is  well  versed  in  relief  work 
in  Belgium,  sketches  briefly  the  early  life  of  Hoover 
and  dwells  fully  on  his  career  as  food  administrator. 

Kitchener,  H.  H.  Lord.    Arthur,  Sir  George.

Official    biography    by    Lord    Kitchener's    private 

secretary,  who  presents  the  concrete  facts  in  a  plain 

narrative. 


Locker-Lampson,  Frederick.  Birrell,  Au- 
gustine, comp.  and  ed.  Frederick  Locker- 
Lampson;  a  character  sketch  with  a

Nowhere  has  he  gossiped  more  charmingly;  and

ever. — Spectator.

A  journalist's  record  of  a  five-week  visit.  He

The  Russian  diary  of  an  Englishman, 
Petrograd,  1915-1917.  1919.  A  6025 

This  diary  is  as  full  of  trivial  personal  details  as 
Pepys's  own.  It  is  written  from  the  point  of  view 
of  a  friend  of  the  court,  who  as  the  Athenaeum 
says,  regards  the  greatest  revolution  in  history  as 
an  extraordinarily  inconvenient  riot.

A  book  remarkable  for  its  succinct  analysis  of

phere, clever  character  portrayals,  plots  and  coun- 
terplots; illumined  throughout  with  vivid  flashes  of

Thompson,  Charles  T.  The  Peace  Confer- 
ence day  by  day;  a  presidential  pilgrimage 
leading  to  the  discovery  of  Europe,  with 
an  introductory  letter  by  Colonel  E.  M. 
House.  1920.  A  7094 

Diary  of  events  centering  about  the  activities  of 
Mr.  Wilson  ...  all  about  how  the  President 
breakfasted,  lunched,  dined,  attended  receptions, 
and  official  functions,  and  drove  out;  whom  he  saw, 
what  was  said,  what  he  did,  and  what  others 
thought  about  him. — Nation.

Crawford,  Mary  Caroline.  In  the  days  of 
the  Pilgrim  fathers.  1920.  B  5516 

A  readable  narrative  of  the  historical  facts  con- 
nected with  the  Mayflower  voyage.  Cambridge  as 
"the  cradle  of  the  Pilgrim  idea"  and  the  whole 
political  life  of  England  at  that  period  is  described, 
as  is  also  the  Dutch  mode  of  life  and  government 
which  influenced  the  Pilgrims  in  organizing  their 
colony.

esting way. — Booklist. 

N'colay,  Helen.  Our  nation  in  the  building. 
1916.  B  5909, 

A  good  history  for  the  man  or  woman  who  wants*

Bulletin. 

Panama 

Bunau-Var'lla,  Philippe.  The  great  adven- 
ture of  Panama;  wherein  are  exposed  its 
relation  to  the  great  war  and  also  the 
luminous  traces  of  the  German  conspir- 
acies against  France  and  the  United 
States.  1920.  B  4905 

The  author  regards  himself  as  solely  responsible 
for  the  revolution  of  Panama  when  she  proclaimed 
her  independence  of  Colombia.  He  also  lays  the 
blame  for  all  our  troubles  with  Colombia,  and 
prejudicial  opinion  with  regard  to  the  transaction, 
on  German  propaganda.

Bobby  lives  on  a  highway  leading  to  town.  His

A  group  of  city  boys  and  girls  spend  their  va- 
cation in  New  York  state  and  form  a  club  for

Opera  Scores,  Librettos,  and  Plots 

IN  the  Music  Room    on  the  fifth  floor  of  the  Main  Library   building  are  to 
be  found  librettos  together  with   from  ten  to  twenty  copies  of  the  vocal 
scores  of  all  the  favorite  operas  and  a  smaller  number  of  the  less  familiar  ones. 
They  may  be  borrowed  on  special  music  cards  issuable  to  all  registered  book 
borrowers  on  application. 

Much  difficulty  has  been  experienced  in  trying  to  secure  scores  of  the  new 
operas  announced  for  production  in  the  Chicago  Opera  repertory  for  this 
season,  and  not  all  have  proved  to  be  obtainable.  Below  is  a  list  of  references 
to  books  containing  plots  of  the  operas  in  the  program  for  the  coming  season. 
The  figures  refer  to  the  pages  and  the  letters  to  the  books  according  to  the 
following  key :

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those 

marked  Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

SPIRITUALISM 


O'Donnell,    Elliot, 
ualism.     1920. 


The    menace 


of    spirit- 
L  13512 


From  the  point  of  view  of  orthodox  Christianity 
it  [spiritualism]  menaces  faith  and  morality  alike; 
from  that  of  the  medical  profession  it  is  injurious 
to  health;  from  that  of  the  greater  number  of  most 
eminent  scientists  it  is  a  sham;  and  from  the  point 
of  view  of  common  sense  it  is  a  hotch-potch  of  im- 
becility, gullibility,  and  roguery. — Book  Review 
Digest. 

PSYCHOLOGY 

Mental  efficiency 

Goddard,     Henry     Herbert.       Human     effi- 
ciency and  levels  of  intelligence.     1920. 

L 13899 

(The  Louis  Clark  Vanuxem  foundation  lectures.) 
Four  lectures  explaining  how  the  recognition  of 
different  degrees  of  intelligence  among  children  and 
adults  can  effect  greater  social  efficiency  by  aiding 
each  person  to  train  for  the  work  and  responsibility 
which  his  mental  equipment  warrants.— -Booklist. 

Hall,     G.     Stanley.     Morale;     the     supreme 
standard   of    life    and    conduct.     1920. 

L  14106 

The  author  holds  that  the  war  itself  revealed  the 
bankruptcy  of  the  old  criteria  and  that  our  human 
standards  and  values  must  now  be  subjected  to  a  re- 
definition. This  the  book  sets  out  to  do,  using 
morale  with  a  psychophysic  connotation. — Book  Re- 
view Digest. 

Yoakum.    Clarence    S..    and    Yerkes.    Robert 
M.    ed.    Army  mental  tests.     1920.  L  13731 

A  description  of  the  methods  and  results  of  the 
psychological  tests  used  in  the  army.  Besides  giv- 
ing a  record  of  what  has  been  accomplished,  the 
book  is  intended  to  stimulate  similar  practices  in 
schools  and  industry.

RELIGION 

Elliott,  Ernest  Eugene.  How  to  advertise 
a  church.  1920.  M  8900 

Inge,  William  Ralph.  Outspoken  essays. 
1920.  M  9255 

Contents:  Our  present  discontents. — Patriotism. 
— The  birth-rate. — The  future  of  the  English  race. — 
Bishop  Gore  and  the  Church  of  England. — Roman 
Catholic  modernism. — Cardinal  Newman. — St.  Paul. 
— Institutionalism  and  mysticism. — The  indictment 
against  Christianity. — Survival  and  immortality. 

_Keen  rapierlike  satire_  by  the  Dean  of  St.  Paul's 
directed  against  everything  in  church  and  state  that 
is  not  Tory  and  Established  Church.  Preserved 
Smith,  writing  in  The  Nation,  calls  the  author 
(among  other  epithets)  "a  Christian  gentleman  with 
a  tongue  like  the  sting  of  a  wasp." 

La  Rue,  William  Earl.  The  foundations  of 
Mormonism;  a  study  of  the  fundamental 
facts  in  the  history  and  doctrines  of  the 
Mormons  from  original  sources.  1919. 

M7950 

Moulton,  Richard  G.,  ed.  The  modern 
reader's  Bible  for  schools:  the  New  Tes- 
tament. 1920.  M  6773 

An  abridgment  for  schools  of  Dr.  Moulton's  fa- 
mous work.  The  sayings  of  Jesus  in  the  Gospels 
(which  are  given  entire)  are  distinguished  by  dif- 
ferent type.  The  Acts  and  Epistles  are  consider- 
ably abridged. 


SOCIOLOGY 


Hapgood, 
1920. 


Norman. 


The    advancing    hour. 
L 15418 


Contents:  In  time  of  revolution. — The  storm 
cellar. — The  blockade  of  thought. — What  the  issues 
are. — Without  a  party. — Facing  bolshevism:  our

in  Russia. — Is  socialism  needed? — The  _  answer  of

An  attempt_  to  analyze  the  present  transition

O'Brien,  George.  The  economic  history  of 
Ireland  in  the  seventeenth  century.  1919.

Economics 

Feld,    R.    C.      Humanizing    industry.      1920. 

L 17196 

In  the  form  of  a  story,  schemes  are  worked  out 
for  accident  prevention,  health  measures,  educa- 
tion, _  pensions,  disability  funds  and  death  benefits, 
housing,  profitsharing,  and  industrial  representa- 
tion. 

Labor,  Wages 

Brooks,  John  Graham.  Labor's  challenge 
to  the  social  order;  democracy  its  own 
critic  and  educator.  1920.  L 16760 

A  discriminating  study  of  the  problem  involved 
in  labor's  rapid  accession  of  power,  in  what  it  is 
to  do  with  its  mastership,  and  in  the  readjustments 
of  the  social  order  required  by  present  conditions. 

Cole,  G.  D.  H.  The  payment  of  wages;  a 
study  in  payment  by  results  under  the 
wage-system"  1918.  L 16846 

(Trade  union  series.) 


Litchfield,  Paul  W.  The  industrial  repub- 
lic; a  study  in  industrial  economics.  1920. 

L  16761 

The  manager  of  the  Goodyear  Tire  and  Rubber 
factory  outlines  the  representation  plan  in  use 
there,  stating  that  on  the  analogy  of  republican 
government  it  is  necessary  that  labor  be  given  the 
management  of  an  industry  on  the  condition  that 
the  interests  of  capital  be  safeguarded. 

Parker,  Carleton  H.  The  casual  laborer, 
and  other  essays,  with  introduction  by 
Cornelia  Stratton  Parker.  1920.  L 16960 

Contents:  Toward  understanding  labor  unrest. — 
The  casual  laborer. — The  I.  W.  W. — Motives  in  eco- 
nomic life. 

These  four  essays  are  about  all  the  writings  we 
have  from  which  to  restore  Professor  Parkers  in- 
dustrial theories  and  ideals.  His  method  of  _  ap- 
proach was  from  the  psychology  of  the  working- 
man,  and  much  of  the  labor  unrest  is  held  to  be 
the  explosion  resulting  from  instincts  thwarted  by 
the  present  economic  environment. 

Selley,  Ernest.  Village  trade  unions  in  two 
centuries.  1919.  L 17198 

Treats  of  conditions  in  Great  Britain. 

Conference  of  Working-Class  Associations. 
(London,  1920).  The  trade  unions  and 
output;  papers  by  J.  T.  Brownlie  and  H. 
A.  Grimshaw;  with  a  report  of  the  dis- 
cussions to  which  they  gave  rise.  1920. 

L  16661,  7 

(The  reorganization  of  industry  series.) 
Contents:      The    workers'    interest    in    output,    by

An  account  of  the  development  of  banking  the-

Political  Science 

Bonjour,  Felix.  Real  democracy  in  opera- 
tion; the  example  of  Switzerland.  1920. 

L 18491 

An  exposition  of  Swiss  government  in  which  the 
twenty-five  separate  cantons  test  different  details  in 
forms  and  methods  of  administration  and  borrow 
from  one  another  those  that  prove  most  successful. 

Butts,    Alfred    Benjamin.      Public    adminis- 
tration in  Mississippi.     1919.       *B  1706,  3 
(Missisippi  Historical  Society  publications.) 

Gaston,  Herbert  E.  The  Nonpartisan 
League.  1920.  L 16496 

The  story  of  the  League's  successful  effort  to 
free  the  market  from  abuses,  to  liberate  the  state 
(N.  Dak.)  from  thralldom  to  the  big  business  in- 
terests, to  stimulate  agriculture,  to  make  rural  life 
more  agreeable,  and  to  conserve  the  wealth  and 
production  of  the  state  for  the  people  who  live 
in  it. — Freeman.

CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Hearnshaw,  F.  J.  C.  Democracy  at  the 
crossways;  a  study  in  politics  and  his- 
tory, with  special  reference  to  Great 
Britain.  1919.  L 15898 

A  conservative  view  defending  representative  gov- 
ernment founded  on  the  two-party  system,  and  re- 
garding the  increasing  political  influence  of  labor 
as  an  unwarranted  encroachment  by  a  powerful 
minority.

ment of  India.     1920.  L 18581 
An  analysis  of  the  form  of  English  administration 
in  India.     The  author  desires  its  reconstruction  with 
self-government  as  the  goal.

(Collections  of  the  Illinois  state  historical  li-

The   Negro 

Fox,  Early  Lee.  The  American  coloniza- 
tion society,  1817-1840.  1919.  *L  3201,  37 

(Johns  Hopkins  university  studies  in  historical

Landon,  Fred.  Canada's  part  in  freeing  the 
slave.  1920.  L 16178 

Seligmann,  Herbert  J.  The  negro  faces 
America.  1920.  L  16192 

An  attempt  to  enlighten  the  prejudice  of  unin- 
structed  white  people  showing  that  riots  are  caused 
by  economic  and  political  conditions  and  not  by 
instinctive  racial  antagonisms.  The  cruelty  and  in- 
justice arising  from  the  denial  of  social  equality  are 
described  and  the  questions  of  race  absorption  and 
the  "anthropological  myth"  discussed.

League  of  Nations 

Gumming,  C.  K.,  and  Pettit,  Walter  W., 
comp.  and  cd.  Russian-American  rela- 
tions, March,  1917-March,  1920;  docu- 
ments and  papers.  1920.  L 16334 

A  singularly  dramatic  record.  It  has  its  ensem-

ments. .  .  .  The  collected  telegrams  of  those 
days  offer  a  liberal  education  in  the  way  American 
foreign  affairs  are  handled. — New  Republic.

McMillan,  Margaret.  The  nursery  school. 
1919.  L  19741 

The  theory  is  that  the  various  current  devices 
for  the  care  of  the  child  fail  of  their  object  because 
they  deal  with  specific  symptoms  instead  of  with 
the  child  as  a  person.  The  author's  own  school 
tries  to  supply  all  the  needs  of  childhood-  in  their 
proper  relation  to  a  normal  development.

preface,  in  such  a  way  "that  a  man  or  boy  with  a 
common-school  education  can  easily  master  it."

A  dispassionate  critique,   apparently  written  more 
for    the    benefit    of    ardent    admirers    of    Miss    Aus- 
ten's novels  than   to   allure  others  to  their  perusal. 

Fiske,  George  Converse.  Lucilius  and  Hor- 
ace; a  study  in  the  classical  theory  of 
imitation.  1920.  J  4855,  7 

(University  of  Wisconsin  studies  in  language  and 
literature.) 

Moore,  T.  Sturge.  Some  soldier  poets. 
1920.  J  6364 

Contents:  Introduction. — Julian  Grenfell. — Rupert 
Brooke. — A  half  Pleiade:  Robert  Nichols,  Siegfried 
Sassoon  and  Robert  Graves. — R.  E.  Vernede. — Sor- 
ley. — Francis  Ledwidge. — Edward  Thomas. — F.  W. 
Harvey. — Richard  Aldington. — Alan  Seeger. — The 
best  poetry. 

In  the  opinion  of  the  New  York  Times,  these 
essays  "furnish  the  most  compact  and  pleasurable 
volume  put  together  so  far  about  these  men,"  and 
the  one  on  Alan  Seeger  is  especially  recommended. 

Perry,  Bliss.     A  study  of  poetry.     1920. 

J4804 

A  study  of  poetry  in  general  and  the  lyric  in 
particular  intended  for  the  use  of  the  teacher  and 
to  enable  the  average  reader  to  get  more  enjoy- 
ment from  the  reading  of  verse. 

Polti,  Georges.  The  thirty-six  dramatic 

situations.  1917.  E 14194 

Robertson,  J.  M.  The  problem  of  Hamlet. 

1920.  E  10211 

Seeks  to  explain  many  of  the  critic-baffling  prob- 
lems of  Hamlet  by  the  fact  that  in  writing  it 
Shakespeare  was  adapting  an  old  play  whose  main 
features  he  was  compelled  to  preserve. 

Three  priests  of  Peblis.  The  thre  prestis 
of  Peblis,  how  thai  tald  thar  talis;  ed. 
from  the  Asloan  and  Charteris  texts  by 
T.  D.  Robb,  M.  A.  1920.  *J  1101,  8 

(The   Scottish  text  society.) 

Van  Doren,  Carl,  ed.  Benjamin  Franklin 
and  Jonathan  Edwards;  selections  from 
their  writings.  1920.  E  6228 

(The   modern   student's  library.) 

Authorship,  Journalism 

Cook,  Sir  Edward.  The  press  in  war-time, 
with  some  account  of  the  official  press 
bureau.  1920.  J  7245 

A  brief  essay  describing  the  functions  of  Eng- 
land's press  1'ureau  during  the  war,  explaining  the 
principles  of  censorship  it  followed  and  presenting 
some  of  the  chief  problems  with  which  it  was  con- 
cerned. 

Gushing,  Charles  Phelps.  If  you  don't 
write  fiction.  1920.  J  7382 

Useful  suggestions  for  general  journalistic  work, 
including  the  preparation  of  manuscripts,  the  tak- 
ing of  photographs  for  publication,  and  finding  a 
market. 

Gay,  Robert  M.  Writing  through  reading; 
a  suggestive  method  of  writing  English, 
with  directions  and  exercises.  1920. 

J7176 

The  author  advocates  imitation  of  different  au- 
thors of  dissimilar  styles  to  give  the  student  ex- 
perience in  the  use  of  his  tools  and  a  conscious 
striving  after  definite  effects.  He  believes  such  a 
practice  will  also  tend  to  produce  habits  of  more 
effective  reading.

Poetry 

Baker,   Karle   Wilson.     Blue  Smoke.     1919. 

E 10635 

She  draws  down  trees,  birds,  stars,  prints  them 
on  her  page  witl1  a  diamond  delicacy,  heats  and 
lights  them  into  a  tender,  fiery  transparency. — Na- 
tion. 

Bridges,  Robert.  October,  and  other  poems, 
with  occasional  verses  on  the  war.  1920. 

E  10697 

Shows  the  poet  laureate's  reactions  to  the  war. 
There  are  dignified  apostrophes  to  England,  hands- 
across-the-seas  poems  to  America  and  an  arraign- 
ment of  Germany  in  clever  and  intricate  verse 
forms. 

Glaenzer,  Richard  Butler.  Literary  snap- 
shots, impressions  of  contemporary  au- 
thors. 1920.  E  10951 

Brief  clever  sketches  in  imagist  verse  portray- 
ing the  literary  features  of  about  fifty  of  the  best 
known  English,  American  and  foreign  prose  writ- 
ers, and  more  than  half  as  many  poets. 

Huxley,  Aldous.      Leda.      1920.  El  1073 

The  subject  is  Jove's  courtship  of  Leda,  and  the 
London  Mercury  regards  the  poem  as  a  remarkable 
work  for  a  young  man  and  far  ahead  of  any  of 
Mr.  Huxley's  previous  work.  A  few  of  his  earlier 
poems  are  reprinted  in  this  small  volume. 

Kemp,  Harry.  Chanteys  and  ballads;  sea- 
chanteys,  tramp-ballads  and  other  ballads 
and  poems.  1920.  E  7675 

Kreymborg,  Alfred.  Blood  of  things;  a 
second  book  of  free  forms.  1920.  E  11153 

Companion  •volume  to  Mushrooms;  a  book  of  free 
forms,  E  11152. 


McLeod,   Irene    Rutherford. 
1918. 


Before    dawn. 
E  14856 


_A  series  of  love  lyrics  that  rise  from  the  con- 
ditions of  the  war,  a  sonnet  sequence  also  inter- 
preting love  from  the  woman's  side,  a  dramatic 
poem,  a  number  of  independent  lyrics,  and  a  nar- 
rative monologue  in  what  may  be  called  the  Mase- 
field  manner. — Nation. 

Mallarme,  Stephane.  L'apres-midi  d'un 
faune;  tr.  from  the  French,  by  Joseph 
Twadell  Shipley.  E  13168 

Masefield,  John.     Enslaved.     1920.     E 14862 

Contents:  Enslaved.- — The  hounds  of  hell. — Cap 
on  head. — Sonnets. — The  passing  stranger. — Ani- 
mula. — The  Lemmings. — Forget. — On  growing  old. 
— Lyric. 

A  long  narrative  poem  in  which,  as  the  London 
Times  says,  Masefield  has  justified  the  use  of  verse 
in  the  telling  of  a  story  which  is  always  exciting 
and  often  moving.  Some  critics  think  it  may  have 
allegorical  intention,  representing  the  trials  and  suc- 
cesses of  England  in  the  adventures  of  the  hero. 

Simmons,  Lucretia  Van  Tuyl.  Goethe's 
lyric  poems  in  English  translation  prior 
to  1860.  1919.  J4855,  6 

(University  of  Wisconsin  studies  in  language  and 
literature.) 

Symons,  Arthur.  Lesbia  and  other  poems. 
1920.  E  11557 

Contents:  Lesbia. — Intermezzo. — Birds  in  the 
night. — Silhouettes. 

Fugitive  poems  gathered  together  from  many 
scattered  corners.  In  the  era  of  Oscar  Wilde  and 
Beardsley,  while  in  his  twenties,  Symons  was  a 
dominant  figure,  and  was  said  to  have  given  the 
most  perfect  psychology  of  love.  But  novelty-de- 
manding critics  are  now  classing  him  as  unmodern 
and  out  of  date. 


Tanner,  Aleph,  comp.  Homage  to  Robert 
Browning.  1920.  E  14347 

Collection  of  poems  written  in  honor  of  Robert 
Browning. 

Thorley,  Wilfrid,  ed.  Fleurs-de-lys;  a  book 
of  French  poetry  freely  translated  into 
English  verse,  with  an  introduction  and 
notes.  1920.  E 14331 

From  the  twelfth  century  to  the  present  time. 

The  introduction  contains  a  treatise  on  translat- 
ing and  a  historical  survey  of  French  verse.  The 
poems  are  grouped  chronologically  and  the  English 
of  the  free  translations  is  adapted  to  the  period  of 
the  original. 

Untermeyer,    Louis,    ed.      Modern    British 

poetry.      1920.  E 14328 

A  companion  volume  to  Modern  American  poetry. 

Modern  British  poetry  covers  the  same  period  (from 

.  about  1870  to  1920),  follows  the  same  chronolog- 
ical scheme,  but  it  is  more  amplified  and  goes  into 
far  greater  detail. — Introduction.

pastoral  with  songs.  1905.  E 13496 

Clements,  Colin  Campbell.  Seven  plays  of

Poet   Lore  plays. 

Contents:  The  cherry-blossom  river. — By  the

Dixon,  Thomas,  Jr.  A  man  of  the  people; 
a  drama  of  Abraham  Lincoln.  1920. 

E  13659 

The  play  itself  is  a  closely  knit  story  dramatizing 
an  incident  in  Lincoln's  career.  It  is  built  on  the 
discontent  with  Lincoln's  second  candidacy,  mani- 
fested by  the  Republican  national  committee,  which 
proposed  that  Lincoln  resign  and  Thaddeus  Stevens 
run  in  his  place. 

Downey,  June  E.  A  study  in  the  nude. 
1920.  E  13168 

Poet   Lore  plays.

(Plays  for  a  people's  theater.) 

A  dramatic  portrayal  of  English  middle-class  so- 
ciety in  1918. 

Lawrence,  D.  H.  Touch  and  go;  a  play 
in  three  acts.  1920.  E  12520 

(Plays  for  a  people's  theater.) 

Deals  with  the  relations  of  labor  and  capital  in 
an  English  mining  community.

Amusing  it  unquestionably  is,  but  a  great  deal

TRAVEL  AND  DESCRIPTION 

Curie,  Richard.  Wanderings;  a  book  of 
travel  and  reminiscence.  1920.  I  11365 
An  autobiographical  record  dedicated  to  Joseph 
Conrad  and  portraying  with  an  impressionistic  touch 
the  atmosphere  and  scenes  of  the  author's  travels 
over  four  continents.  His  effects  are  considered 
the  best  in  descriptions  of  the  least  trodden  paths. 

Europe 

Garey,   E.   B.,  and   others.     American   guide 
book  to  France  and  its  battlefields.     1920. 

16643 

Chapters  on  Paris,  provincial  French  life,  and 
battle-field  localities,  are  preceded  by  valuable  in- 
structions and  advice  for  the  inexperienced  trav- 
eler with  regard  to  passports  and  the  multitude  of 
necessary  details  belonging  to  an  ocean  passage. 

Maugham,  William  Somerset.  The  land  of 
the  Blessed  Virgin;  sketches  and  impres- 
sions in  Andalusia.  1920.  I  10481 
Andalusian  sketches  showing  Moorish  influence 
in  Spanish  architecture  and  local  color.  The  people 
are  represented  as  indolent,  truthless,  and  proud 
but  picturesque  and  fascinating  to  read  about. 

Zimmern,  Helen,  and  Agresti,  Antonio.  New 
Italy.     1920.  I  10445 

A  very  helpful  up-to-date  study  of  modern  Italy: 
its  history  from  1866  to  the  present  time;  its  po- 
litical parties  and  government;  its  home  and  for- 
eign policy;  its  educational  system,  economic  de- 
velopment, and  artistic  and  literary  life. 

Russia 

Davis,   Malcolm  W.     Open   gates   to   Rus- 
sia.    1920.  1 10526 

Mr.  Davis  is  anxious  to  arouse  America  to  her 
opportunity  in  the  new  Russia.  Not  only  does  he 
show  how  America  can  be  of  efficient  service  to 
Russia,  but  he  also  creates  a  healthy  _respect  for 
what  Russia  might  contribute  to  America. 

Hindus,   Maurice   G.     The   Russian  peasant 
and  the   revolution.      1920.  1 10529 

The  author  was  a  Russian  peasant  and  he  has 
written  a  book  which  Prof.  E.  A.  Ros_s  calls  "so 
concrete,  so  careful  of  fact,  so  impartial,  and  so 
free  from  propaganda  that  a  hundred  years  hence 
historians  will  treat  it  with  respect." 

United  States 

Moran,  George  E.  Moran's  official  auto- 
mobile route  and  trail  guide.  1920.  12768 
"A  conveniently  arranged  automobile  pocket  guide 
comprising  many  delightful  auto  tours  within  easy 
driving  distance  of  Chicago,  comprehensive  route 
and  state  maps,  descriptions  of  marked  highways 
and  trails,  and  much  information  of  value  to  the 
motorist." 

Rabb,  Kate  Milner,  ed.  A  tour  through 
Indiana  in  1840;  the  diary  of  John  Par- 
sons of  Petersburg,  Virginia.  1920.  I  4355 
The  picture  of  early  Indiana  is  remarkably  in- 
teresting and  complete,  including,  among  other 
things,  visits  -to  the  colleges  in  the  state,  with  amus- 
ing comment  on  their  faculties  and  curriculums. — 
Daily  News.

names,    their    origin    and    historic    signifi- 
cance.    1920.  *B  1520,  17 
(Collections   of  the  Minnesota  historical  society.)

Lincoln,  Abraham.  Barton,  William  E.  The 
Soul  of  Abraham  Lincoln.  1920.  C  23018 
The  first  part  of  Dr.  Barton's  work  reviews  the 
various  backgrounds  against  which_  Lincoln's  life 
was  lived;  the  second  examines  previous  biographies 
and  the  evidence  as  to  his  religious  beliefs;  the 
third  constructs  his  religious  belief  from  his  life 
and  words.

Intimate  personal  reminiscences  of  Swinburne  and 
his  associates,  notably  Watts-Dunton.  Mr.  Kerna- 
han calls  Swinburne  "the  knightliest  poet-_figure_  to 
pass  this  way  since  the  Elizabethan  days  in  which, 
surely,  he  should  have  been  born."

Aumonier,  Stacy. 


One  after  another.     1920. 
F 10735 


An    unusual    portrayal-  of   the    lives    of  a   brother 
and  sister,  and  a  vivid  panorama  of  life  itself. 


Benoit,     Pierre. 
1920. 


Atlantida     (L'Atlantide). 
F  11021 


A  tale  of  mystery  and  romance  relating  the  ad- 
ventures of  two  men  in  a  country  of  the  Sahara 
which  is  ruled  by  a  voluptuous  queen  supposed  to 
be  the  local  Atlantis. 

Brackett,   Charles.     The  counsel  of  the  un- 
godly.    1920.  F 11262 
A  simple  story  told  in  a  fascinating  style,  of  an 
impoverished  aristocrat  who  becomes  a  butler  in  the 
household   of   a   newly-rich   American. 

Curwood,  James  Oliver.  The  Valley  of 
Silent  Men;  a  story  of  the  Three  River 
country.  1920.  F 12219 

Another  stirring  tale  of  the  northwest  mounted 
police,  with  a  heroine  who  can  use  a  gun  with 
skill. 

Day,  Holman.  All-wool  Morrison;  time, 
today;  place,  the  United  States;  period  of 
action,  twenty-four  hours.  1920.  F  12355 
A  swiftly  moving  political  story  built  around  the 
proposed  consolidation  of  the  state's  water  power 
in  private  ownership.  It  conveys  a  warning  to 
the  United  States  to  guard  her  water  rights  on 
the  Canadian  border. 

Dehan,  Richard,  pseud,  of  Clotilde  I.  M. 
Graves.  The  eve  of  Pascua  and  other 
stories.  1920.  F 13220 

Contents:  The  eve  of  Pascua. — The  mother  of 
turquoise. — The  tribute  of  the  kiss. — A  sight  cure. 
— Wanted — a  king. — How  Carey  came  back  to  the 
mines. — The  pretender. — The  king's  catarrh. — A 
game  of  ecarte. — Johnny's  case. — An  impression. — 
The  end  of  the  cotillion. — His  deceased  wife's  sis- 
ter.— A  perfect  cure. — An  Ascot  hat. — A  maker  of 
comedies. 

Easton,  Dorothy.  The  golden  bird,  and

Galsworthy.  1920.  F 12628 

Fletcher,  J.   S.     Dead  men's  money.     1920. 

F 12855 

Drummond,  Hamilton.  A  maker  of  saints. 
1920.  F 12545 

A  very  readable  and  picturesque  romance  with 
thirteenth-century  Italy  as  a  background.  A  fa- 
mous sculptor  who  tries  to  interpret  the  message 
of  the  church  to  the  people  by  means  of  his  statues, 
is  the  "maker  of  saints."

1920.

Egan  returns  from  the  war  to  find  his  business

King,   Basil.     The  thread  of  flame.      1920. 

F 14461 

The  story  of  a  man  who,  having  lost  his  memory 
by  shell  shock,  is  compelled  to  accept  manual  em- 
ployment, and  develops  from  a  narrow  and  snobbish 
character  into  a  man  of  larger  sympathies  and  wider 
understanding. 

Lee,    Jennette,      (Mrs.    G.    S.    Lee).      The 
Chinese  coat.     1920.  F  14674 

The  mysterious  influence  of  an  oriental  coat  of 
marvelous  hue  and  texture  furnishes  the  plot  of 
this  charming  story.  It  is  a  symbol  of  the  one 
thing,  just  beyond  our  grasp,  which  would  make  life 
complete.

some outlaws  and  helps  to  rout  them.

Christmas  Music 

Library  calls  attention  once  more  to  its  musical  resources  by  means  of  the 
__  following  lists  of  compositions  appropriate  to  the  observance  of  Christmas. 
Any  of  the  thousands  of  music  books  and  pieces  of  sheet  music  comprising  the 
Music  Collection  in  the  Music  Room  on  the  fifth  floor  of  the  main  building  may 
be  borrowed  by  the  registered  holders  of  book  cards.  One  bound  volume  and 
five  sheets  of  music  will  be  issued  as  a  single  loan  for  four  and  two  weeks  re- 
spectively without  renewal. 


Oratorios,  Sacred  Cantatas 

Adams,  Mrs.  Crosby.  The  birth  of  Christ 
told  in  song;  a  cantata  for  church  or 
school,  suitable  for  voices  of  children  or 
adults.  Words  by  Edith  Hope  Kinney. 
Arr.  for  entertainment  by  Letha  L.  Mc- 
Clure.  Summy.

oratorio,  in  vocal  score  (with  accompani- 
ment for  the  organ  or  pianoforte). 
Novello. 

Bisdee,  A.  G.  The  holy  night;  a  masque 
to  be  performed  by  young  children  at 
Christmas-tide,  written  by  Florence  Con- 
verse. Curwen.

mer.

and  chorus),  with  accompaniment  of  or-

tra ad  lib.).  Schirmer.

Magnussen,  Julius.     God's  smile. 


1920. 
L 13515 


Tells  how  a  Danish  dramatist,  an  agnostic,  per- 
sonally received  spirit  writing  and  was  influenced 
to  play  the  piano  under  spirit  control;  deeply  sin- 
cere.— Booklist.

sleeping;  with  an  introduction  by  Booth 
Tarkington.  1920.  L 13513 

Mrs.  Sewali  records  the  beneficent  services  of  the 
spirits  (especially  her  husband)  in  teaching  and 
helping  her  to  receive  messages  and  to  spread  the 
good  news  abroad.

dence, theoretical  and  practical,  of  the

Temperate,  sincere  and  dignified,  this  transla- 
tion from  the  French  contains  fresh  testimonial 
matter  on  the  subject  of  survival.

Psychoanalysis 

Freud,  Sigmund.  A  general  introduction  to 
psychoanalysis;  authorized  translation, 
with  a  preface  by  G.  Stanley  Hall.  1920. 

L  13890 

Lectures,  almost  conversational  in  tone,  by  the 
proponent  of  the  psychoanalytic  theory.  The 
book  is  not  only  authoritative — it  is  very  clearly 
written  and  easy  of  comprehension  and  will  nat- 
urally supersede  all  other  introductions  to  the 
subject.

A  clear  and  stimulating  presentation  for  the  gen-

Woodworth,  Robert  Sessions.  Dynamic 
psychology.  1918.  L  13959 

(Columbia  university  lectures.) 

Occasionally  one  encounters  a  thinker  who  can 
be  both  sane  and  stimulating,  at  once  clear  and 
suggestive.  That  Professor  Woodworth  is  such  a 
thinker  is  perhaps  more  apparent  than  ever  before 
in  this  little  volume.  Withal  it  has  great  charm 
of  style. — W.  F.  Washburn  in  Science.

M7716 

The  history  of  how,  from  an  evangelical  ances- 
try which  included  an  Episcopal  father,  he  was 
driven  first  into  Anglicanism  and  then  into  the  Ro- 
man Catholic  church.  His  book  is  one  of  the  most 
fascinating,  because  one  of  the  most  candid,  in 
recent  years. — H.  J.  Liski  in  The  New  Republic.

A  book,  simply  and  logically  written,  explaining

SOCIOLOGY 

Emerson,  Guy.  The  new  frontier;  a  study 
of  the  American  liberal  spirit,  its  frontier 
origin,  and  its  application  to  modern 
problems.  1920.  L  15424 

An  exposition  of  the  dominant  character  of  tht 
American  spirit,  disclosing  its  source  in  the  suc- 
cessive struggles  of  the  people  in  pushing  further 
westward  the  boundaries  of  its  material  prosperity. 

Erskine,  John.  Democracy  and  ideals;  a 
definition.  1920.  L 15423 

Contents:  Democracy  and  ideals. — American  char- 
acter.— French  ideals  and  American.— Society  as  a 
university. — Universal  training  for  national  service. 
— University  leadership. 

"I  have  tried  to  express  a  central  conviction 
that  we  in  the  United  States  are  detached  from 
the  past,  and  that  this  detachment  is  the  striking 
fact  in  all  our  problems;  that  if  in  the  future  we 
are  to  become  and  to  remain  a  nation  we  must 
collaborate  for  common  ends." — Preface.

Annals. 

Budish,  J.  M.,  and  Soule,  George.  The  new 
unionism  in  the  clothing  industry.  1920. 

L  17190 

The  "new"  unions,  best  exemplified  by  the  cloth- 
ing workers,  are  those  which  are  conscious  of  their 
desire  for  a  new  order  and  base  their  demands 
on  more  fundamental  considerations  than  immediate 
relief. 

Crowther,  Samuel.     Why  men  strike.   1920. 

LI  698  5 

Mr.  Crowther  thinks  that  the  reason  is  because 
they  are  dissatisfied  with  working  for  someone  else, 
"capital";  and  that  if  they  would  invest  their  sur- 
plus instead  of  spending  it  extravagantly  they 
would  become  capitalists  and  be  working  for  them- 
selves. 

Gompers,  Samuel.    Labor  and  the  employer. 

1920.  L 16762 

He  deals  with  the  company  store,  the  right  of  col- 
lective bargaining,  the  eight-hour  day,  the  open 
shop,  the  strike,  picketing,  profit  sharing,  compul- 
sory arbitration.  _  He  leaves  the  great  solutions  to 
the  future. — Nation.

A  scientific  study  of  each  phase  of  the  steel  strike 
which  .  .  .  shows  the  steel  industry  to  be  auto- 
cratically managed,  imposing  intolerant  hours  and 
insufficient  wages — the  issues  of  the  strike  still  un- 
settled.— Booklist.

1920.  L  17567 

A  ^critique  of  certain  phases  of  m  Marxianism  in 
the  light  of  present-day  scientific  conclusions.  Most 
of  the  familiar  dogmas  are  passed  over,  and  atten- 
tion is  directed  to  an  examination  of  socialism  as  a 
promise  of  effective  social  reform. — Nation.

Political  Science 

Bigelow,  Melville  M.  Papers  on  the  legal 
history  of  government;  difficulties  funda- 
mental and  artificial.  1920.  L 15726 

Contents:  Unity  in  government. — The  family  in 
English  history:  an  inquiry. — Medieval  English 
sovereignty. — The  old  jury. — Becket  and  the  law. 

Mr.  Bigelow  undertakes  to  study  the  past  with 
reference  to  the  fundamental  principles  underlying 
society  rather  than  the  multiplicity  of  facts,  for 
these  may  not  be  rightly  known  and  the  consensus 
of  opinion  with  regard  to  their  significance  may 
vary  "from  time  to  time. 

Laski,  Harold  J.  Political  thought  in  Eng- 
land from  Locke  to  Bentham.  1920. 

L 15786 

(Home  university  library  of  modern^  knowledge.) 

It    is    Mr.    Laski's    opinion    that    this    apparently 

stagnant    century    was    really    productive    of    much 

philosophical    political    thought   which    bore   fruit   in 

the   more  active   period   that   followed.

U.  S.  Politics  and  Government 

Becker,  Carl.  The  United  States;  an  ex- 
periment in  democracy.  1920.  L 15897 

The  author  thinks  that  the  United  States  is  in 
danger  of  mistaking  the  form  of  democracy  for  its 
substance  and  so  ceasing  to  stand  as  a  "fruitful  ex- 
periment in  democracy.  The  menace  lies  in  what 
lie  regards  as  the  "habitual  dislike  of  thinking"  of 
Americans.

Clark,  Thomas  Arkle.  The  high  school  boy 
and  his  problems.  1920.  •  L  20065 

It  is  concrete  in  every  paragraph,  reminiscent, 
replete  with  glimpses  of  real  boys  facing  actual  sit- 
uations. Almost  as  important  as  is  its  content  is 
the  fact  that  it  promises  to  win  a  reading  from  the 
high-school  boy  to  whom  it  is  addressed. — School 
Review.

Unbound  in  Art  Room. 

Clutton-Brock,    A.      Essays    on    art.      1920. 

K  26691 

Contents:  "The  adoration  of  the  Magi." — Leo- 
nardo da  Vinci. — The  Pompadour^  in  art. — An  un- 
popular master. — A  defense  of  criticism. — The  artist 
and  his  audience. — Wilfulness  and  wisdom. — "The 
magic  flute." — Process  or  person? — The  artist  and 
the  tradesman. — Professionalism  in  art. — Waste  or 
creation? 

The  author  discovers  a  mean  between  Tolstoy's 
esthetic  negation  and  Whistler's  art  for  esthetes 
only,  and  he  sustains  some  provokingly  original 
ideas  on  various  aspects  of  the  new  artistic  move- 
ments^ He  is  apt  and  striking  in  metaphor  and  his 
style  is  a  delight. — Chicago  Tribune.

on  golf-course  architecture.  1920.  K  30594 
A  brief  exposition  of  the  elements  of  a  new  de- 
parture in  landscape  gardening  by  an  expert  in 
golf  courses.  It  also  suggests  to  the  golfer  addi- 
tional possibilities  of  his  game. 

Roper,  William  W.  Winning  football. 
1920.  K  30686 

An  exposition  of  the  newer  way  to  play  by  "think- 
ing football,"  which  the  author  regards  as  an  ex- 
cellent preparation  for  life  and  superior  in  every 
way  to  the  old  game  that  was  mainly  a  "trial  of 
speed  and  strength  and  weight  and  courage." 

LITERATURE 
Journalism,  Criticism 

Lewisohn,  Ludwig,  ed.  A  modern  book  of 
criticism.  1919.  J  4805 

(The  modern  library  of  the  world's  best  books.) 
This  book  is  made  up  of  extracts  from  the  com- 
ments of  twenty-five  of  the  best  critics  of  the  past 
ten  years  in  France,  England,  Germany,  and  Amer- 
ica, including  Anatole  France,  Shaw,  Bennett, 
George,  Powys,  Huneker,  Mencken,  and  Hackett. 

Looney,  J.  Thomas.  "Shakespeare"  iden- 
tified in  Edward  De  Vere,  the  seventeenth 
earl  of  Oxford.  1920.  E 10154 

Critics  differ  on  the  value  of  this  book.  The 
Times  says  it  is  "unencumbered  by  any  knowledge 
at  first  hand";  the  Bookman,  "Impossible  to  do  jus- 
tice to  the  wealth  of  evidence  collected  .  .  .,  and 
it  is  to  be  hoped  that  his  leads  will  be  followed 
up";  the  Nation  questions  the  validity  of  the  evi- 
dence. 

Muir,  Edwin.  We  moderns;  enigmas  and 
guesses.  1920.  L  14711 

(The  free   lance  books.) 

Contents:  Introduction. — The  old  age. — Original 
sin. — What  is  modern? — Art  and  literature. — Crea- 
tive love. — The  tragic  view. 

About  two  hundred  paragraphs  on  criticism  of 
life  and  literature  from  a  somewhat  Nietzschean 
point  of  view.  The  author  is  a  voting  Scotchman, 
the  Edward  Moore  of  The  New  Age. 

Payne,  George  Henry.  History  of  jouralism 
in  the  United  States.  1920.  J  7258 

A  history  not  only  of  journalism,  but  also  of  the 
parallel  development  of  democracy  in  connection 
with  politics  and  public  events  from  1690,  the  date 
of  the  first  newspaper  here,  down  to  the  present 
time. 

Russell,  Frances  Theresa.  Satire  in  the 
Victorian  novel.  1920.  J  7223 

An  interpretative  study  of  Victorian  fiction  and 
survey  of  the  methods  and  purposes  of  modern  sa- 
tire. Satire  is  discussed  in  its  use  by  different  nov- 
elists as  directed  against  individuals,  institutions 
and  types. 


Thayer,  William  Roscoe. 
phy.      1920. 


The  art  of  biogra- 
C 20052 


Three  lectures  by  the  biographer  of  Cavour,  John 
Hay,  and  Roosevelt,  dividing  the  subject  chrono- 
logically into  Biography  in  antiquity,  From  medieval 
to  modern,  and  Biography  in  the  nineteenth  cen- 
tury. 

Poetry 

Allen,  Morse  S.  The  satire  of  John  Mar- 
ston.  1920.  J  6382 

A  university  thesis  on  the  satirical  poetry  of  a 
contemporary  of  Shakespeare  who  was  a  dramatist 
and  divine  as  well  as  a  satirist. 

Erskine,  John.  The  kinds  of  poetry,  and 
other  essays.  1920.  J  4803 

Contents:  The  kinds  of  poetry. — The  teaching  of 
poetry. — The  new  poetry. — Scholarship  and  poetry. 

These  four  essays  reveal  a  lover  of  poetry  who 
accepts  the  best  in  the  new  verse  and  the  old, 
and  thinks  the  world  is  the  better  for  the  existence 
of  both. 

Gayley,  Charles  Mills,  and  Kurtz,  Benjamin 
Putnam.  Methods  and  materials  of  lit- 
erary criticism;  lyric,  epic  and  allied  forms 
of  poetry.  1920.  J  4734 

A  better  book  for  reference  than  for  general 
reading.  It  gives  an  analysis  and  history  of  lyric 
and  epic  poetry  by  characterizing  the  leading  theo- 
ries of  the  more  important  earlier  critics. 

Poems 

Hardy,  Thomas.  Collected  poems,  v.  1. 
Lyrical,  narratory,  and  reflective.  1919. 

E  11038 

Kerr,  R.  Watson.    War  daubs.  1919.  E  11137 

These  poems  reveal  a  poet's  agony  amid  the  hor- 
rors of  war  shorn  of  all  its  glamour.  They  are  as 
sincere  as  Sassoon's  •verse,  but  lack  the  satirical 
touch  that  gives  it  vigor. 

Millen,  William  A.  Songs  of  the  Irish 
revolution  and  songs  of  the  newer  Ire- 
land. 1920.  E 14870 

The  author  is  an  Irish-American  who  spent  eight 
student  years  in  Ireland,  of  which  he  is  very  fond. 
He  sympathizes  with  her  modern  spirit,  and  be- 
lieves that  her  salvation  will  come  through  her  na- 
tional university  and  her  men  of  learning.

meyer. — Louis  Untermeyer. 

Nichols,  Robert.     Aurelia  and  other  poems. 

1920.  El  1339 

A  sequence   of   Shakespearean  sonnets  telling  the 

story   of  a  disappointed  lover  whose   passion  grows 

with    the    falseness   of    his    mistress.

E  11499 

A  third  volume  of  Mr.  Sandburg's  poems  con- 
taining some  of  his  best  work.  Many  of  the  verses 
have  Chicago  for  their  theme.

Baker,  George  P.  Modern  American  plays.

E  12133 

An  amusing  and  clever  farce  containing  many

Milne,  A.  A.     First  plays.     1920.       E 14070 

Contents:  Wurzel-Flummery. — The  lucky  one. — 
The  boy  comes  home. — Belinda. — The  red  feathers. 

This  volume  is  "not  the  work  of  a  professional 
writer,  but  the  recreation  of  a  (temporary)  profes- 
sional soldier."  It  contains  one  war  play  and  one 
operetta  (The  red  feathers).  Ethel  Barrymore 
played  Belinda  in  London  and  New  York.

Collective 

Dombrowski,  Eric.  German  leaders  of  yes- 
terday and  today.  1920.  C  19459 

The  political  editor  of  the  Berliner  Tageblatt  pre- 
sents a  vivid  portrait  of  forty-five  Germans  of  all 
opinions  who  have  played  a  prominent  part  in  the 
history  of  the  last  six  years. — Ebert,  Ludendorf, 
Tirpitz,  Haase,  Liebknecht,  Rosa  Luxemburg,  the 
Kaiser,  etc. 

Who  was  who;  a  companion  to  Who's  who, 
containing  the  biographies  of  those  who 
died  during  the  period,  1897-1916.  1920. 

*R556 

Individual  Biography 

Bok,  Edward.  The  Americanization  of  Ed- 
ward Bok;  the  autobiography  of  a  Dutch 
boy  fifty  years  after.  1920.  C  20917 

The  fascination  of  success  hangs  about  this  ac- 
count of  how  the  Dutch  immigrant  boy  became  edi- 
tor of  the  Ladies  Home  Journal,  of  the  work  he 
did,  and  of  the  people  he  knew — Roosevelt,  Kipling,. 
Holmes,  Gladstone,  and  many  others. — Booklist. 

Clough,  Arthur  Hugh.  Osborne,'  James 
Insley.  Arthur  Hugh  Clough.  1920. 

C 21294 

Mr.    Osborne    has    written    a    judicious    and    bal- 
anced   essay    on    Clough.      He    does    not    claim    for 
him  that  he  was  a  great  poet;  but  he  rightly  insists 
"on  his  interest  in  his  period. — London  Mercury. 

Fisher,   John   Arbuthnot,   Lord.     Memories 

and  records.     1920.  C  21909 

An   old   man's  reminiscences   of  a   long   career  in 

the    British    navy — disjointed    and    jerky,    but    with 

plenty  of  punch  and  little  respect  for  personages.

A  biographical  and  critical  essay,  .including  a 
short  note  on  Holberg  literature  and  Holberg  stu- 
dents. 

Lloyd  George,  David.  Spender,  Harold.  The 
prime  minister.  1920.  C  23022 

The  first  thirteen  chapters,  devoted  to  Lloyd 
George's  childhood,  youth  and  early  career,  are 
particularly  interesting.  The  author  claims  "twenty- 
seven  years  of  unbroken  friendship"  and  the  tone 
is  eulogistic  throughout. 

Moton,  Robert  Russa.  Finding  a  way  out; 
an  autobiography.  1920.  C  23491 

The  life  story  of  Booker  T.  Washington's  suc- 
cessor told  with  a  sense  of  humor  and  betraying  a 
disposition  of. sweetness  and  dignity.  The  author's 
object  is  to  contribute  something  to  a  better  under- 
standing of  his  race. 

Roosevelt,  Theodore.  Bishop,  Joseph  Buck- 
lin.  Theodore  Roosevelt  and  his  time 
shown  in  his  own  letters.  1920.  2  v. 

C  23982 

These  volumes  supplement  the  Autobiography  and 
with  it  form  a  valuable  history  of  our  own  times. 
The  work  was  undertaken  with  Mr.  Roosevelt's  en- 
tire approval  and  five-sixths  of  it  are  devoted  to 
his  administration  ?nd  *he  years  following. 

Tussaud,  Mme.  Marie.  Tussaud,  John  Theo- 
dore. The  romance  of  Madame  Tus- 
saud's.  1920.  C  24608 

Madame  Tussaud,  for  a  time  the  companion  of 
Louis  XVI's  sister,  was  the  originator  of  the  wax 
works  of  characters  of  the  French  Revolution 
which,  augmented  by  succeeding  members  of  her 
family  with  late  historical  characters,  still  forms  a 
unique  historical  collection  in  London. 

Whiteley,  Opal.  The  story  of  Opal;  the 
journal  of  an  understanding  heart.  1920. 

C  24827 

With  its  human  interest,  unconscious  humor  and 
minor  tragedies,  its  self-taught  wisdom  and  naive 
charm,  as  well  as  its  keen  observant  record  of  things 
usually  unseen  and  unfelt  by  the  ordinary  child  or 
adult  in  wood  and  field,  the  book  is  truly  the  "jour- 
nal of  an  understanding  heart." — Boston  Transcript. 

Wilson.  William  B.  Babson,  Roger  W.  \V. 
B.  Wilson  and  the  Department  of  Labor. 
1919.  C  24845 

Mr.  Wilson's  'ife  is  followed  from  his  boyhood 
in  Scotland,  his  early  coming  to  America,  vyork  in 
a  cobbler's  ihop,  in  coal  mines,  and  on  railroads; 
the  six  years  in  Congress,  the  creation  o_f  the  De- 
partment of  Labor  and  Mr.  Wilson's  appointment  as 
the  first  secretary. — American  Economic  Review.

Hutton,  S.  K.  By  Eskimo  dog-sled  and 
kayak;  a  description  of  a  missionary's

1919.  1 4212 

Kent,  Rockwell.  Wilderness;  a  journal  of 
quiet  adventure  in  Alaska,  with  drawings 
by  the  author  and  an  introduction  by 
Dorothy  Canfield.  1920.  111790 

The  Alaskan  diary — simple,  virile,  interesting — of 
Rockwell  Kent,  an  artist  who  went  into  the  wilder- 
ness for  the  sake  of  his  art.  He  took  his  small 
son  as  a  companion,  and  together  they  lived  in  a 
cabin,  and  worked,  played,  read,  end  painted. 

Malone,  Colonel  C.  L.  The  Russian  re- 
public. 1920.  1 10535 

Colonel  Malone  is  an  aviator  and  an  M.  P.,  but 
not  a  socialist.  He  reports  certain  facts  from  an 
objective  and  somewhat  skeptical  point  of  view  and 
reaches  the  conclusion  that  the  only  reasonable  way 
to  avoid  a  possible  military  dictatorship  in  Russia 
is  to  recognize  the  soviet  republic  and  give  it  a 
chance. 

Safroni-Middleton,  A.  South  Sea  foam;  the 
romantic  adventures  of  a  modern  Don 
Quixote  in  the  southern  seas.  1920.  1 1640 
A  tale  of  adventure  and  romance  replete  with 
the  legendary  lore  of  a  tropical  people.  The  au- 
thor's experience  has  brought  him  in  contact  with 
friends  of  Stevenson  in  Samoa  rather  than  the 
exotic  haunts  of  Gauguin. 

HISTORY 

Wells,  H.  G.  The  outline  of  history;  being 
a  plain  history  of  life  and  mankind,  writ- 
ten with  the  advice  and  editorial  help  of 
Ernest  Baker,  H.  H.  Johnston,  E.  Ray 
Lankester  and  Gilbert  Murray.  1920.  2v. 

A  2228 

From  the  beginning  of  the  world  to  1920. 

The  book  shows  wonderful  vision  and  _  the  keen 
power  of  analysis  and  swift  characterization  which 
is  H.  G.  Welis.  An  atmosphere  of  disillusionment 
and  gentle  skepticism  envelops  the  narrative  which 
is  remarkably  fluid  and  easy  to  read.
