Dawson,  Richard.  Red  terror  and  green; 
the  Sinn-Fein-Bolshevist  movement.  1920. 

A  5512 

A  study  of  the  Irish  nationalist  movement  which 
the  Nation  describes  as  "as  thorough  as  it  is  mer- 
ciless." Mr.  Dawson  believes  that  "Sinn  Fein  has 
obeyed  the  law  of  revolutionary  evolution  and  no 
longer  controls  the  force  it  sets  in  motion." 

Good,  james  Winder.     Ulster  and   Ireland. 

1919.  A  5509 

The  author  holds  Carson  responsible  for  the 
"triumph  of  the  influences  that  make  for  division 
in  Ireland."  He  feels  that  Ireland  must  decide  her 
own  destiny,  l>ut  Ulster  is  only  to  be  won  by  rea- 
son and  argument.  Mr.  Good  is  a  native  of  Mun- 
ster  who  has  spent  his  "happiest  years"  in  Belfast 
and  professes  to  "boast  himself  an  Ulsterman." 

Hibben,  Paxton.  Constantine  I  and  the 
Greek  people.  1920.  A  6150 

Mr.  Hibben  was  the  Associated  Press  correspond- 
ent in  Greece  through  most  of  1915,  1916,  and 
into  1917.  He  dedicates  his  book  to  King  Con- 
stantine and  claims  the  popular  sentiment  was  with 
the  throne  'in  desiring  to  remain  neutral  and  not 
with  Venizelos  and  the  Allied  cause. 

O'Hegarty,  P.  S.  Sinn  Fein,  an  illumina- 
tion. 1919  A  5516 

Tells  the  story  of  Sinn  Fein  from  the  inside, 
tracing  its  evolution  from  the  establishment  of  the 
Gaelic  League  in  1893,  but  looking  backward  to 
the  prc-Norman  period  in  a  historical  survey  of 
Irish  nationalism. 

Spargo,  John.  "The  greatest  failure  in  all 
history;"  a  critical  examination  of  the 
actual  workings  of  bolshevism  in  Russia. 

1920.  A  6027 

"A  part  of  the  indisputable  evidence  upon  which 
I  base  my  condemnation  of  Bolshevism  as  a  mad 
attempt,  by  a  brutal  and  degrading  tyranny,  to 
carry  out  an  impossible  program." 

Walling,  William  English.  Sovietism;  the 
A  B  C  of  Russian  bolshevism — according 

-  to  the  Bolshevists.  1920.  A  6028 

An  arraignment  of  Bolshevism  substantiated  by 
fragmentary  extracts  from  speeches  and  official 
manifestoes. 

European  War 

Bellairs,  Carlyon.  The  battle  of  Jutland; 
the  sowing  and  the  reaping.  1920. 

A  7112 

This  story  of  Jutland  is  by  a  critic  of  Lord  Jel- 
licoe's  policy,  and  readers  of  the  Admiral's  o\v'n  ac- 
count will  be  interested  in  comparing  the  two. 


Cornford,  L.  Cope.  The  paravane  adven- 
ture. 1919.  A  7103 

The  true  _and  romantic  story  of  the  adventures 
of  an  English  vessel  fighting  against  submarines, 
told  with  the  consent  of  the  admiralty  Board. 

Creel,  George.  How  we  advertised  Amer- 
ica; the  first  telling  of  the  amazing  story 
of  the  Committee  on  Public  Information 
that  carried  the  gospel  of  Americanism 
to  every  corner  of  the  globe.  1920. 

A  7113 

Mr.  Creel  accuses  Congress  of  trying  to  keep 
from  the  public  any  final  statement  of  what  was 
accomplished,  and  says  that  be  has  written  the  book 
in  order  to  defeat  that  purpose  and  chronicle  the 
facts  in  detail. 

The  war,  the  world,  and  Wilson.     1920. 

A  7 107 

A  defense  of  President  Wilson  and  a  plea  for 
the  ratification  of  the  peace  treaty  and  acceptance 
of  the  league  of  nations.  It  was  our  pledges  that 
won  the  war,  the  author  states,  and  our  repudia- 
tion of  those  pledges  that  is  losing  the  peace. — 
Book  Review  Digest. 

Folks,  Homer.  The  human  costs  of  the 
war.  1920.  A  7110 

What  we  have  is  not  a  succession  of  harrowing 
scenes,  but  a  real  explanation  of  what  happened  to 
the  people.  It  is  the  closing  chapters  on  War  and 
the  children,  War  the  best  friend  of  disease,  and 
Civilization's  indictment  of  war,  that  reveal  the 
author's  personality  and  special  experience. — Sur- 
vey. 

Gaines,  Ruth.  Ladies  of  Grecourt;  the 
Smith  College  relief  unit  in  the  Somme. 
1920.  A  71 11 

A  continuation  of  the  record,  begun  in  Helping 
France  (A  7022),  of  the  relief  work  of  the  Smith 
College  unit  in  sixteen  villages,  where  from  a 
population  of  nearly  5600  only  174O  were  left,  six 
hundred  of  whom  were  children  under  fifteen. 

Gordon-Smith,  Gordon.  From  Serbia  to 
Jugoslavia;  Serbia's  victories,  reverses  and 
final  triumph,  1914-1918.  1920.  A  7116 
•  A  "story  of  Serbia's  crucifixion"  by  a  war  cor- 
respondent who  followed  the  campaign  personally 
from  the  headquarters  of  the  Serbian  army. 

Hayes,  Carlton  J.  H.  A  brief  history  of 
the  great  war.  1920.  A  7108 

In  mastery  of  detail,  in  perspective,  in  propor- 
tion, in  perspicuity,  in  philosophic  grasp  of  his 
subject  as  a  whole  he  outclasses  all  rivals  .  .  . 
Apparently  innocent  of  literary  artifice,  he  has 
drawn  a  picture  that  has  not  the  color  and  poetry 
of  a  painting  but  the  fidelity  of  a  photograph. — 
Nation. 

Kluck,  Alexander  von.  The  march  on 
Paris  and  the  battle  of  the  Marne,  1914, 
with  portrait  and  maps  and  notes  by  the 
historical  section  (military  branch)  of  the 
Committee  of  Imperial  Defense.  1920. 

A  7115 

Observations  of  a  commanding  general  who  re- 
views bis  own  actions  in  the  quietude  of  his  study 
and  illustrates  them  with  the  orders  issued  to  him 
and  by  him,  but  with  very  little  information  be- 
yond the  manceuvers  of  his  own  army  in  the  field 
and  almost  none  of  the  enemy. — New  York  Times. 

Mercier,    D.    J.,    Cardinal.      Cardinal     Mer- 

cier's  own  story.     1920.  A  7097 

Not   a   first   person    narrative,    as   the   title   would 

indicate,  but  a  collection   of  the  letters  that  passed 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


161 


between  the  Cardinal  _and  the  German  authorities, 
connected  often  by  brief  explanatory  notes  written 
by  the  editor. 

Murray,  Gilbert.  Our  great  war  and  the 
great  war  of  the  ancient  Greeks.  1920. 

A  2615 

(The  Creighton  lectures.) 

A  comparison  with  the  Peloponnesian  war,  show- 
ing many  striking  similarities  as  betrayed  in  Greek 
history  and  literature.  It  points  a  lesson  from  the 
peace  that  was  no  peace  but  was  followed  by  other 
wars  resulting  in  the  destruction  of  both  combat- 
ants. 

Paine,  Ralph  D.  The  Corsair  in  the  war 
zone.  1920.  A  71 14 

The  story  of  the  yacht  which  J.  Pierpont  Morgan 
put  at  the  service  of  the  United  States  government 
as  soon  as  we  entered  the  war.  It  patrolled  the 
coast  from  Lisbon  to  Queenstown  and  its  story  is 
one  of  courage  and  patriotism. 

Pollard,  A.  F.  A  short  history  of  the  great 
war.  1920.  A  7109 

A  critical  and  interpretative  one-volume  history 
by  a  professor  of  history  in  London  University. 
Critics  consider  it  as  reliable  a  presentation  as  we 
can  have  at  so  short  a  perspective  of  time. 

Repington,  C.  a  Court.  The  first  world  war, 
1914-1918;  personal  experiences.  1920. 
2v.  A  7122 

The  diary  of  a  military  expert  who  writes  with 
extreme  self-confidence  and  from  the  aristocratic 
point  of  view. 

Schreiner,  George  Abel.  The  craft  sinister; 
a  diplomatico-political  history  of  the  great 
war  and  its  causes — diplomacy  and  inter- 
national politics  and  diplomatists  as  seen 
at  close  range  by  an  American  news- 
paperman who  served  in  Central  Europe 
as  war  and  political  correspondent.  1920. 

A  7099 

Scott,  Arthur  Pearson.  An  introduction  to 
the  peace  treaties.  1920.  A  7104 

_An  analysis  of  the  treaty  of  Versailles  which 
will  greatly  simplify  the  subject  to  all  who  find  the 
text  hard  reading.  The  author  begins  with  the 
causes  of  the  war  and  is  careful  to  present  both 
sides  of  disputed  points  of  the  treaty,  though  in 
general  his  own  attitude  is  favorable. 


Seton- Watson,  R.  W. 
ing  pot.     1919.

A  collection  in  book  form  of  a  number  of  essays,

and  Turkey.

1920.  A  2687 

Contents:  Religion  and  nationality. — Hebraism

Smith,  Vincent  A.  The  Oxford  history  of 
India,  from  the  earliest  times  to  the  end 
of  1911.  1920  A  3852 

This  comprehensive  survey  of  India  was  written 
upon  the  conclusion  of  many  years  spent  in  the  In- 
dian Civil  Service,  during  which  Mr.  Smith  was 
an  earnest  and  sympathetic  student  of  Indian  an- 
tiquity and  later  history.

Bogart,  Ernest  Ludlow,  and  Thompson,

(Illinois  centennial  publications.  The  centennial

Three  chapters  are  contributed  by  other  authors:

Haworth,  Paul  L.  The  United  States  in 
our  own  times,  1865-1920.  1920.  B  5708 

A  straightforward  chronological  account  .  .  .  pre- 
sented in  an  easy  flowing  style  which  carries  the 
reader  along  in  the  current  of  narration. — Nation. 

Henderson,  Archibald.  The  conquest  of  the 
old  Southwest;  the  romantic  story  of  the 
early  pioneers  into  Virginia,  the  Carolinas, 
Tennessee,  and  Kentucky,  1740-1790. 
1920.  B  7568 

A  very  condensed  history,  with  a  great  number 
of  witness-references  showing  the  care  with  which 
Mr.  Henderson  has  done  his  work.  He  has  added 
a  valuable  and  convenient  treatise  concerning  a 
somewhat  overlooked  section  to  the  history  of  the 
formation  of  the  United  States. — Boston  Transcript. 

Schnure,  William  Marion,  comp.  Selins- 
grove,  Penna.,  chronology.  (1700-1850). 
1918.  B  8188 

Violette,  Eugene  Morrow.  A  history  of 
Missouri.  1918.  B  3647 

Mexico,   South  America 

Blasco  Ibanez,  V.  Mexico  in  revolution. 
1920.  B  8567 

Frankly  criticizes  the  Mexican  leaders  and  con- 
trasts the  poverty  and  misery  prevailing  there  (in 
spite  of  great  wealth  of  national  resources)  with 
the  prosperity  of  the  rest  of  the  continent.  He  lays 
the  deplorable  conditions  to  the  lack  of  an  educa- 
tional system  and  Mexico's  policy  of  isolation. 

Bulnes,  Don  Gonzalo.  Chile  and  Peru;  the 
causes  of  the  war  of  1879.  1920.  B  4943 

O'Shaughnessy,     Edith,      (Mrs.       Nelson 
O'Shaugnessy).     Intimate  pages  of  Mex- 
ican history.     1920.  B  8578 
Contents:     Porfirio    Diaz. — Francisco    Leon    de    la 
Barra. — Francisco    I.    Madero. — Victoriano    Huerta. 
The  author  thinks  Mexico  needs  a  dictator.     She 
calls  the  administration  of  Diaz  Mexico's  Augustan 
age    and    portrays    his    successors    with    their    succes- 
sive -victories   and   defeats.      She   has   little  sympathy 
with    Wilson's   Mexican   policy.

fighters.    1919. 


Boys'    book    of    frontier 
H  6329 


The  deeds  of  American  pioneers  in  defense  of 
their  lives  and  homes.  The  account  commences 
with  pioneers  and  hunters  in  the  east,  and  continues 
on  to  the  frontiersmen  and  soldiers  of  the  west. 

St.  Nicholas.  Travel  stories.  1920.  I  11395 
Contents:  The  Grand  Canon  of  Arizona,  by  W. 
H.  Simpson.  —  In  rainbow  land,  by  Amy  Suther- 
land. —  Traveling  in  India,  by  Mabel  A.  Spicer.— 
Where  the  sunsets  of  all  the  yesterdays  are  found, 
by  O.  D.  Wheeler.  —  -Firecrackers,  by  E.  Pomeroy. 
—  Curious  clocks,  by  C.  A.  Brassier.  —  Motoring 
through  the  golden  age,  by  A.  B.  Paine.  —  Letter- 
boxes in  foreign  lands,  by  A.  R.  Roy.  —  Lost  Rheims, 
by  Louise  E.  Prickitt.  —  Where  Dorothy  Vernon 
dwelt,  by  Minna  B.  Noyes.  —  Glimpses  of  foreign 
fire  brigades,  by  C.  T.  Hill.  —  Dutch  cheeses,  by 
H.  M.  Smith.  —  A  geography  city  "come  alive,"  by 
L.  Harbeson.  —  The  giant  and  the  genie,  by  G.  F. 
Stratton.  —  Out  in  the  big  game  country,  by  C.  H. 
Rowe. 


Stories 

Aldon,  Adair.  At  the  sign  of  the  Two 
Heroes.  1920.  H  7149 

A  band  of  French-Canadian  smugglers  carry  on  a 
thriving  business  on  Lake  Champlain  until  they  are 
discovered  by  some  adventure-seeking  boys.  The 
story  is  filled  with  exciting  episodes. 

Baldwin,  James,  and  Livengood,  W.  W. 
Sailing  the  seas;  the  log  of  Tom  Darke. 
1920.  H  7286 

A  book  for  the  boy  who  is  interested  in  ships 
and  sailing.  A  lad  begins  his  sea-faring  life  as  a 
cabin  boy  and  soon  climbs  to  the  captaincy.  The 
educational  value  of  this  book  is  especially  signifi- 
canj. 

Gordon,  Mary   D.     The  crystal  ball.     1920. 

H8272 

Prince  Jock  and  Princess  Joan  set  out  from  the 
palace  in  search  of  the  magical  crystal  ball  which 
is  to  serve  as  a  birthday  present  for  the  King. 
After  various  experiences  with  people  and  animals, 
the  ball  is  found  and  presented,  much  to  the  sur- 
prise and  pleasure  of  their  royal  father. 

Gregor,  Elmer  Russell.    White  Otter.     1920. 

H8284 

White  Otter,  a  gallant  young  Indian  warrior, 
has  many  perilous  adventures,  including  buffalo 
hunts  and  battles  with  hostile  tribes.  For  boys  be- 
tween the  ages  of  twelve  and  fourteen. 


Griffis,   William    Elliot. 
1920. 


Swiss    fairy   tales. 
H8287 


These  tales  tell  of  the  coming  of  the  Swiss  to 
colonial  America,  and  other  stories  of  Swiss  life, 
including  some  variations  of  well-known  fairy  tales. 

Knipe,    Emilie    Benson,    and    Knipe,    Alden 
Arthur.    A  Mayflower  maid.    1920.    H  4574 

A  story  of  the  Plymouth  colony  and  a  new  ver- 
sion of  the  "courtship  of  Miles  Standish."  A  good 
book  for  older  girls. 


Murray, 

1920. 


Gilbert. 


The*    airplane     spider. 
H5265 


The  social  life  of  a  spider  written  for  very  young 
children. 

Remick,  Grace  M.     The  Sheldon  six — Anne. 

1920.  H  5982 

The  >first   of   a   new   series  by  the  author   of   the 

Jane   Stuart   books.     Anne  learns  to  make  the  best 

of  her  opportunities  even  in  the  face  of  a  handicap. 

Sabin,   Edwin   L.      On   the   overland   stage; 
or,  Terry  as  a  king  whip  cub     1918. 

Hughes  Room 

Smith,  Harriet  Lummis.     Peggy  Raymond's 
school  days;  or,  Old  girls  and  new.     1916. 

H6508 

(The    Friendly    Terrace   series.) 

The  ordinary  type  of  boarding  school  story. 

Turpin,    Edna.      Treasure    Mountain.      1920. 

H6878 

A  party  of  friends  have  a  camping  trip  in  the 
Virginia  Mountains.  As  a  result  of  an  exploring 
expedirion  two  of  the  party  are  lost.  When  they 
are  found  by  searchers  they  make  known  the  dis- 
covery of  a  great  treasure.

GOVERNMENT     PUBLICATIONS 
OF  SPECIAL  INTEREST 

Circulating  copies  may  be  issued  on  the  regular 
card  in  the  Civics  and  Documents  Department,  first 
floor,  Randolph  Street  entrance. 

Churchill,  E.  P.  The  oyster  and  the  oyster 
industry  of  the  Atlantic  and  Gulf  Coasts. 
1920.  C6.1.  1919-8 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Fisheries.  Appendix  8  to  re- 
port for  1919.) 

Extremely  interesting  material  relative  to  the 
most  valuable  fishery  of  the  U.  S.  It  discusses 
geographical  distribution,  description  and  anatomy, 
life  history,  environment,  cultivation,  and  market- 
ing of  the  oyster.  Illustrated.  Value  for  school 
work. 

Grant,  C  V.,  and  Hansen,  A.  A.  Poison  ivy 
and  poison  sumac  and  their  eradication. 
1920.  A1.9  -1166 

(Farmers'  bulletin.) 

Gives  simple  methods  of  recognizing  the  plants, 
and  destroying  them;  also  tells  what  remedies  to 
apply  when  poisoned  and  how  to  avoid  poisoning. 

International  Association  of  Industrial  Ac- 
cident Boards  and  Commissions,  Toronto, 

1919.  Proceedings;   6th    annual    meeting. 

1920.  L2.3    273 

(U.   S.   Bureau  of  Labor   Statistics.      Bulletin.) 
With  special  emphasis  on  medical  care. 

Lee,  Frederic  E.  The  Russian  co-operative 
movement.  1920.  C18.15  101 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Foreign  and  Domestic  Com- 
merce. Misc.  ser.) 

A  general  history  and  detailed  description  of  the 
55  years  of  this  movement,  with  a  membership  of 
20.000.GOO,  representing  100,000,000  people. 

Lloyd,  W.  A.  Status  and  results  county 
agent  work;  northern  and  western  states, 

1919.  1920.  Al.14/2     106 

(U.  S.  Dept.  of  Agriculture.  Department  circu- 
lar.) 

The  need  of  trained,  competent,  contented,  in- 
terested men  to  carry  on  the  alreadv  successful 
work  of  the  county  agricultural  agent  is  the  prob- 
lem now  confronting  this  organization. 

Lundquist,  R.  A.,  and  Williams,  C.  S.  South 
African  markets  for  American  hardware. 

1920.  C18.11     198 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Foreign  and  Domestic  Com- 
merce. Special  agents  ser.) 

Interesting  survey  of  the  territory  covered,  going 
into  detail  regarding  markets  for  particular  lines 
of  hardware. 

McCaskey,  H.  D.,  and  Burchard,  E.  F.  Our 
mineral  supplies.  1919.  119.3  666 

(U.  'S.   Geological  Survey.     Bulletin.) 
A  valuable  collection  of  reports  published  during 
the  war  showing  the  source  of  supply  of  each  min- 
eral, its  uses,   domestic  production,  importation  and 
exportation. 

Moore,  Elaine  F.  Economic  aspects  of  the 
commerce  and  industry  of  The  Nether- 
lands, 1912-1918.  1919.  C18.15  91 

(Bureau  of  Foreign  and  Domestic  Commerce. 
Misc.  series.) 

This  report  is  one  of  a  series  of  economic  studies 
of  different  countries  during  the  war,  prepared  by 
the  War  Trade  Board. 


Ontario.     Bureau  of  Municipal  Affairs.     Re- 
port rehousing  for  1919.     1920. 

Includes  reports  of  officials,  statements  as  to  op- 
erations of  housing  commissions,  plans,  etc.  Illus- 
trated. 

Rosenthal,    S. '  W.     Jewelry   and   silverware 
in    Chile,    Bolivia,    and    Peru.      1919. 

C18.11     187 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Foreign  and  Domestic  Com- 
merce. Special  agents  ser.) 

Gives  insight  into  conditions  of  west-coast  coun- 
tries, with  opportunities  for  trade  opened  up  by 
the  Panama  Canal  since  the  European  war;  treats 
of  necessary  credit  extension,  and  the  market  de- 
mands in  style,  quality  and  finish.  Illustrated. 

Rufener,   Louis   A.     The  economic  position 
of  Switzerland  during  the  war.     1919. 

C18.15     90 

(Bureau  of  Foreign  and  Domestic  Commerce. 
Misc.  series.) 

Prepared  by  the  Bureau  of  Research  and  Statis- 
tics of  the  War  Trade  Board.  Outlines  the  com- 
mercial situation  of  Switzerland  and  shows  changes 
in  trade  and  industry  during  the  war. 

Shaw,  J.   B.     Enamels   for   sheet  iron   and 

steel.     1920.  C13.8     165 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Standards.     Technologic  paper.) 

Reliable  and  authentic  information  shedding  light 

on  the  problems  encountered  by  workers  in  enamel. 

Shrader,  J.    H.      Castor-oil  industry.     1920. 

A1.3     867 

(U.   S.   Dept.   of  Agriculture.     Bulletin.) 

Covers    sources,    trade   and    commerce,    inspection 

and  valuation  of  castor  beans  and  the  manufacture, 

properties,  and   uses   of  the  oil. 

Smith,    Philip    S.      Electrical    goods    in    Ar- 
gentina, Uruguay,  and   Brazil.     1919. 

C18.ll     184 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Domestic  and  Foreign  Com- 
merce. Special  agents  ser.) 

Shows  opportunities  for  the  introduction  of  Amer- 
ican goods  with  a  review  of  the  use  of  electricity  in 
these  countries.

BRANCHES 

Independent  collections  of  3,000  to  20,000 
volumes  in  quarters  owned  or  rented  by  the 
Library,  or  occupied  by  arrangement  with  the 
Park  Boards,  and  administered  by  a  librarian 
and  staff. 

Branches  marked  *  are  open  from  9  a.  m. 
to  10  p.  m.;  those  marked  t  are  open  from 
9  a.  m.  to  9  p.  m. ;  those  marked  t  are  open 
from  9  a.  m.,  to  5  p.  m. ;  all  others  are  open 
from  1  to  9  p.  m.;  open  holidays  from  2  to 
6  p.  m.

DELIVERY  STATIONS 

Points  of  call  for  Library  delivery  auto- 
mobiles. Located  in  shops  of  various  sorts 
and  have  no  collections  of  books.  Patrons 
may  leave  orders  which  will  be  filled  at  the 
Main  Library  and  delivered  to  the  station.

CENTRAL  LIBRARY  BUILDING 
Michigan    Boulevard,   Washington    and    Randolph    Streets 

WEEKDAY  HOURS  :  Circulation  Dept.,  9  a.  m.  to  8 :30  p.  m. 

Reference,    Periodicals,  and  Civics  Depts.,  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m 
Art,   Music,   Patent  Reports,  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m 
Children's    Dept.,  9:30  a.  m.  to  6:00  p.  m. 

SUNDAY  AND  HOLIDAY  HOURS:     Reference  and  Periodicals    Depts.    only,    9   a.    m.    to   6   p.    m. 

Holidays  comprise  New  Year's  Day,  Lincoln's  Birthday,  Washington's  Birthday,  Memorial 
Day,  Independence  Day,  Labor  Day,  Thanksgiving  Day,  Christmas  Day. 
All  departments  are  closed  on  Independence  Day  and  Christmas  Day. 


BRANCHES 

Independent  collections  of  3,000  to  20,000  volumes  in  quarters  owned  or  rented  by  the 
Library,  or  occupied  by  arrangement  with  the  Park  Boards,  and  administered  by  a  librarian 
and  staff. 

Branches  marked  *  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m. ;  those  marked  t  are  open  from 
9  a.  m.  to  9  p.  m. ;  those  marked  $  are  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  5  p.  m. ;  those  marked  fl  are 
open  from  2  to  7  p.  m. ;  all  others  are  open  from  1  to  9  p  m. ;  and  all  are  open  holidays  from 
2  to  6  p.  m.
